> A language **L** is called a **Regular Language** if and only if it can be described by a **Regular Expression (RE)**, accepted by a **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)**, or accepted by a **Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA)** including its variants. Regular languages form the **lowest tier (Type-3)** of the **Chomsky Hierarchy** and are precisely the class of languages recognized by finite-state machines with no auxiliary memory.

Formally, let $\Sigma$ be a finite, non-empty set of symbols called an **alphabet**. A **string** (or word) over $\Sigma$ is a finite sequence of symbols from $\Sigma$. The set of all possible strings over $\Sigma$ (including the empty string $\varepsilon$) is denoted $\Sigma^*$.

A **language** $L$ over $\Sigma$ is any subset of $\Sigma^*$:

$$L \subseteq \Sigma^*$$

A language **L** is **regular** if there exists a finite automaton $M$ such that:

$$L = L(M) = \{ w \in \Sigma^* \mid M \text{ accepts } w \}$$

## 1.2 The Three Equivalent Characterizations

KTU examiners love this triangle of equivalence. A language is regular if and only if it satisfies **any one** of the following three conditions:

| # | Characterization | Description |
|---|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Regular Expression** | Can be expressed using $\cup$, $\cdot$, $*$ operators |
| 2 | **Finite Automaton** | Accepted by some DFA / NFA / $\varepsilon$-NFA |
| 3 | **Regular Grammar** | Generated by a right-linear or left-linear grammar |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Vending Machine**
> Think of a regular language as the set of all valid "button-press sequences" on a vending machine.
>
> - The **alphabet** $\Sigma = \{A, B, C\}$ represents the three available buttons (e.g., Cola, Bisleri, Coffee).
> - A **finite automaton** is the internal logic chip that tracks which state the machine is in (Idle → Coin Inserted → Selection Made → Dispensing).
> - **Crucially**, the machine has **finite memory** — it cannot count how many coins you inserted in the past; it can only remember the *current* state.
>
> Because the memory is bounded (a fixed number of states), the machine can only recognize patterns where "what matters" is the **current position/state**, not the entire history. This is exactly what regular languages are: patterns with **bounded memory requirements**.

## 1.4 The Formal Language Hierarchy (Chomsky Hierarchy)

Regular Languages sit at the **bottom** of the Chomsky Hierarchy. Each higher level strictly subsumes the one below:

$$\text{Regular} \subsetneq \text{Context-Free} \subsetneq \text{Context-Sensitive} \subsetneq \text{Recursively Enumerable}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick: "Reg Fish CF CSS RE"**
> Regular ⊂ Context-Free ⊂ Context-Sensitive ⊂ Recursively Enumerable.
> Each step UP the hierarchy adds **more computational power** (and more memory).

## 1.5 Why Are Regular Languages Important?

- **Lexical Analysis**: Compilers use regular expressions (regex) for tokenizing source code.
- **Pattern Matching**: `grep`, `sed`, text editors, DNA sequence matching in bioinformatics.
- **Network Protocols**: TCP/IP state machines, regex-based intrusion detection.
- **Model Checking**: Hardware verification (sequential circuits are essentially DFAs).
- **Search Engines**: URL routing and query parsing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Regular Expressions — The Algebraic Description

A **Regular Expression (RE)** over an alphabet $\Sigma$ is built recursively using the following rules. KTU frequently asks for "the language denoted by an RE", so memorize these operators.

### 2.1.1 Base Cases
- $\varnothing$ denotes the **empty language** (no strings).
- $\varepsilon$ denotes the language $\{\varepsilon\}$ — the language containing only the **empty string**.
- For each $a \in \Sigma$, the RE $a$ denotes the language $\{a\}$.

### 2.1.2 Inductive Cases
Let $r$ and $s$ be regular expressions denoting languages $L(r)$ and $L(s)$:

| Operation | Notation | Language Denoted |
|-----------|----------|------------------|
| **Union** | $r \mid s$ or $r + s$ | $L(r) \cup L(s)$ |
| **Concatenation** | $r \cdot s$ or $rs$ | $\{ xy \mid x \in L(r), y \in L(s) \}$ |
| **Kleene Star** | $r^*$ | $\displaystyle\bigcup_{i=0}^{\infty} L(r)^i$ |
| **Plus** | $r^+$ | $L(r) \cdot L(r)^*$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Precedence Order (Highest to Lowest):** Kleene Star $(^*)$ → Concatenation $(\cdot)$ → Union $(+)$. Always parenthesize when in doubt!

## 2.2 Finite Automaton — The Machine Description

A **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)** is a 5-tuple:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$

Where:
- $Q$ — finite, non-empty set of **states**.
- $\Sigma$ — finite **input alphabet** (no $\varepsilon$ allowed in DFA).
- $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ — **transition function** (total, deterministic).
- $q_0 \in Q$ — **start state**.
- $F \subseteq Q$ — set of **final/accepting states**.

A **Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA)** is also a 5-tuple $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$, but:

$$\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^Q$$

The transition returns a **set of states**, and $\varepsilon$-transitions are permitted. An NFA accepts a string $w$ if **at least one** sequence of choices leads to a final state.

## 2.3 Kleene's Theorem (The Fundamental Bridge)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kleene's Theorem (1956):**
> A language $L$ is regular **if and only if** there exists a finite automaton (DFA/NFA) that recognizes it **if and only if** it can be denoted by a regular expression.

This three-way equivalence (RE ↔ NFA ↔ DFA) is the heart of Module 1.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Quick-Reference Table**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---------|----------------|-------|
| **DFA → RE** | State Elimination Method | Solve equations of form $X_i = \sum a_{ij} X_j + c_i$ |
| **RE → NFA** | Thompson's Construction | Base cases: $a$, $\varepsilon$, $\varnothing$; combine with $\cup, \cdot, *$ |
| **NFA → DFA** | Subset Construction | Each DFA state = subset of NFA states; max $2^n$ states |
| **DFA Minimization** | Table-Filling Algorithm | Distinguishable states merged; complexity $O(n^2 \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert)$ |
| **Pumping Lemma** | $\forall L$ regular, $\exists p \geq 1$ such that $\forall w \in L, \vert w \geq p$ | Write $w = xyz$ with $\vert xy \leq p$, $\vert y \geq 1$, $xy^i z \in L$ |
| **# of Strings** | $\vert \Sigma \vert^n$ | Number of strings of length exactly $n$ over $\Sigma$ |
| **Star of Sigma** | $\vert \Sigma^* \vert = \infty$ | Countable infinity |
| **Union Cardinality** | $\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert$ | Inclusion-Exclusion |

## 2.5 Closure Properties of Regular Languages

If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular, then the following are **also regular**:

$$\text{Union}, \text{Concatenation}, \text{Kleene Star}, \text{Intersection}, \text{Complement}, \text{Reverse}, \text{Difference}, \text{Homomorphism}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Regular languages are **closed under all Boolean operations**. This is unique to regular languages — context-free languages are NOT closed under intersection or complement!

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lexers** in compilers (GCC, Clang) use RE-driven DFAs for token recognition.
- **Network Firewalls** employ DFAs for fast packet-header inspection.
- **Bioinformatics pipelines** (BLAST) use RE matching for motif detection in DNA.
- **Hardware Design**: Sequential circuits in VLSI are modeled as DFAs for synthesis.
- **Digital Forensics**: Regex engines scan petabytes of logs for IOCs (Indicators of Compromise).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: NFA → DFA Conversion (Subset Construction)

> **Problem:** Convert the following NFA over $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ to an equivalent DFA.
>
> - States: $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
> - Start state: $q_0$
> - Final states: $F = \{q_2\}$
> - Transitions:
>   - $\delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\}$
>   - $\delta(q_0, 1) = \{q_0\}$
>   - $\delta(q_1, 0) = \varnothing$
>   - $\delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_2\}$
>   - $\delta(q_2, 0) = \{q_2\}$
>   - $\delta(q_2, 1) = \{q_2\}$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Initialize DFA.** Start state of DFA = $\varepsilon$-closure of NFA start state.

Since this NFA has no $\varepsilon$-transitions, the start state is simply $\{q_0\}$. Mark as "unvisited".

**Step 2: Process $\{q_0\}$ on input symbols.**

Compute the next state for each input symbol:

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{q_0\}, 0) = \bigcup_{q \in \{q_0\}} \delta_{NFA}(q, 0) = \delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\}$$

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{q_0\}, 1) = \bigcup_{q \in \{q_0\}} \delta_{NFA}(q, 1) = \delta(q_0, 1) = \{q_0\}$$

Add $\{q_0, q_1\}$ and $\{q_0\}$ to the list of unvisited DFA states.

**Step 3: Process $\{q_0, q_1\}$.**

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{q_0, q_1\}, 0) = \delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_1, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \varnothing = \{q_0, q_1\}$$

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{q_0, q_1\}, 1) = \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\}$$

Add $\{q_0, q_2\}$.

**Step 4: Process $\{q_0, q_2\}$.**

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{q_0, q_2\}, 0) = \delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_2, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$$

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{q_0, q_2\}, 1) = \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_2, 1) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\}$$

Add $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$.

**Step 5: Process $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$.**

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \varnothing \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$$

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, 1) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\}$$

**Step 6: Mark Final States.** Any DFA state containing an NFA final state ($q_2$) is final:

$$F_{DFA} = \{\{q_0, q_2\}, \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}\}$$

### Final DFA Transition Table

| State | On 0 | On 1 | Final? |
|-------|------|------|--------|
| $\rightarrow A = \{q_0\}$ | $B = \{q_0, q_1\}$ | $A = \{q_0\}$ | No |
| $B = \{q_0, q_1\}$ | $B = \{q_0, q_1\}$ | $C = \{q_0, q_2\}$ | No |
| $C = \{q_0, q_2\}$ | $D = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ | $C = \{q_0, q_2\}$ | **Yes** |
| $D = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ | $D = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ | $C = \{q_0, q_2\}$ | **Yes** |

> [!TIP]
> **Maximum DFA states from NFA with $n$ states:** $2^n$ subsets. Here $n = 3$, so $\leq 8$ — we got 4.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: RE → NFA (Thompson's Construction)

> **Problem:** Construct an NFA for the regular expression $r = (a \mid b)^* a b b$.

### Recursive Decomposition

**Step 1:** Parse $r$ as a syntax tree:

$$r = \Big( (a \mid b)^* \Big) \cdot a \cdot b \cdot b$$

**Step 2:** Build base NFAs for atoms $a$, $b$, $\varepsilon$ (each has 2 states).

**Step 3:** Combine using standard Thompson rules:
- **Star** of $(a \mid b)$: New start/final state with $\varepsilon$-transitions.
- **Concatenation** of three sub-expressions: Chain via $\varepsilon$.

### Construction Steps

$$\text{NFA for } a: \quad q_0 \xrightarrow{a} q_1$$

$$\text{NFA for } b: \quad p_0 \xrightarrow{b} p_1$$

$$\text{NFA for } (a \mid b): \quad \text{branching start} \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} \text{(a-sub-NFA)} \cup \text{(b-sub-NFA)}$$

$$\text{NFA for } (a \mid b)^*: \quad \text{add new } q_s \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} (a \mid b), \text{ and } q_s \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} q_f$$

$$\text{Final NFA: chain } ((a \mid b)^*) \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} a \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} b \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} b$$

**Final Result:** An NFA with at most **14 states** (each base contributes 2, plus operator overhead). KTU's algorithm guarantees at most $2 \cdot \vert r \vert$ states, where $\vert r \vert$ is the number of symbols in $r$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Pumping Lemma Application

> **Problem:** Prove that $L = \{ a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0 \}$ is **NOT regular**.

### Proof Structure (Contradiction)

**Step 1: Assume** $L$ is regular. Then by the Pumping Lemma, there exists a pumping length $p \geq 1$ such that for every string $w \in L$ with $\vert w \geq p$, we can write $w = xyz$ satisfying:
- $\vert xy \leq p$
- $\vert y \geq 1$
- $xy^i z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$

**Step 2: Choose** a specific "killer" string:

$$w = a^p b^p$$

Clearly $\vert w \vert = 2p \geq p$, so the lemma applies.

**Step 3: Analyze the decomposition.**

Since $\vert xy \leq p$ and $w$ starts with $p$ copies of $a$, the substring $xy$ lies entirely within the $a$-block. Therefore:

$$y = a^k \quad \text{for some } k \geq 1$$

So $x = a^{p-k-\ell}$ and $z = a^\ell b^p$ for some $\ell \geq 0$. Specifically:

$$x = a^{p-k}, \quad z = b^p$$

**Step 4: Pump with $i = 2$.**

$$xy^2 z = a^{p-k} \cdot a^{2k} \cdot b^p = a^{p+k} b^p$$

**Step 5: Show Contradiction.**

For $xy^2 z$ to be in $L$, we would need the number of $a$'s to equal the number of $b$'s:

$$p + k = p \implies k = 0$$

But this contradicts $\vert y \geq 1$, which requires $k \geq 1$.

**Step 6: Conclude.**

Our assumption that $L$ is regular is **false**. Therefore $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ is **not regular**. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting to verify that the killer string satisfies $\vert w \geq p$. Also, students often forget to apply pumping lemma in **BOTH directions** when required (i.e., checking $i = 0$ AND $i = 2$).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

> [!TIP]
> **"Which Technique Should I Use?" — KTU Exam Strategy**

| Given | Goal | Use This Technique | Complexity |
|-------|------|--------------------|-----------|
| RE | NFA | Thompson's Construction | $O(\vert r \vert)$ states |
| NFA | DFA | Subset Construction | Up to $2^n$ states |
| DFA | RE | State Elimination / Arden's Theorem | Algebraic |
| DFA | Minimal DFA | Table-Filling (Myhill-Nerode) | $O(n^2 \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert)$ |
| RE / FA | Prove NOT regular | Pumping Lemma (contradiction) | Pick $w = a^p b^p$ style |
| Two Regular Languages | Show $L_1 \cap L_2$ regular | Product Construction (DFA × DFA) | $O(n_1 \cdot n_2)$ states |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — DFA for "Strings ending in 'ab'"

```mermaid
graph LR
    A((q0)) -- "a" --> B((q1))
    A -- "b" --> A
    B -- "a" --> B
    B -- "b" --> C((q2))
    C -- "a" --> B
    C -- "b" --> A

    classDef startState fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    classDef finalState fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    class A startState
    class C finalState
```

> **Interpretation:** Start at `q0`. Stay at `q0` on `b`, go to `q1` on `a`. From `q1`, on `b` transition to the accepting state `q2`. The string is accepted **only if it ends exactly with "ab"**.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — NFA for RE $(a \mid b)^* a b b$

```mermaid
graph LR
    startS((S)) -- epsilon --> starA
    starA((X1)) -- epsilon --> branchTop
    starA -- epsilon --> acceptF((F))
    branchTop((X2)) -- epsilon --> nfaA
    branchTop -- epsilon --> nfaB
    nfaA((X3)) -- a --> mid1((X5))
    nfaB((X4)) -- b --> mid1
    mid1 -- epsilon --> starA
    mid1 -- epsilon --> nextA
    nextA((X6)) -- a --> nextB((X7))
    nextB -- b --> nextC((X8))
    nextC -- b --> acceptF

    classDef startNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef finalNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    class startS startNode
    class acceptF finalNode
```

> **Interpretation:** The start state `S` jumps to `X1` (the loop entry) via $\varepsilon$. `X1` can either exit to the final `F` (representing zero repetitions) or feed back to `X2` for one or more repetitions. Each repetition consumes either `a` or `b`, and then exactly `a`, `b`, `b` must follow.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Regular Language Tools

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Alphabet Sigma] --> B[Lexical Specification]
    B --> C{Choose Method}
    C --> D[Regular Expression RE]
    C --> E[Finite Automaton FA]
    D --> F[Thompson Construction]
    F --> G[NFA]
    G --> H[Subset Construction]
    H --> I[DFA]
    I --> J[Minimization Algorithm]
    J --> K[Minimal DFA]
    E --> G
    I --> L[State Elimination]
    L --> M[Simplified RE]
    K --> N[Lexer / Scanner Module]
    M --> N

    classDef inputNode fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outputNode fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px
    classDef processNode fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#A16207,stroke-width:2px
    class A,B inputNode
    class N outputNode
    class C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,L,M processNode
```

> **Interpretation:** This is the **compiler-construction pipeline**. Engineers start with an alphabet, choose either a regex or FA, transform it through the formal pipeline, and feed the minimized DFA into a production lexer (e.g., `lex`, `flex`).

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Chomsky Hierarchy (Set Inclusion)

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[REGULAR LANGUAGES] --> CF[CONTEXT FREE LANGUAGES]
    CF --> CS[CONTEXT SENSITIVE LANGUAGES]
    CS --> RE[RECURSIVELY ENUMERABLE]

    R -.->|RE, DFA, NFA| D1[Description Tools]
    CF -.->|CFG, PDA| D2[Description Tools]
    CS -.->|CSG, LBA| D3[Description Tools]
    RE -.->|Unrestricted Grammar, TM| D4[Description Tools]

    classDef baseClass fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef toolClass fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#6B21A8,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    class R,CF,CS,RE baseClass
    class D1,D2,D3,D4 toolClass
```

> **Interpretation:** Arrows indicate **strict subset** relationships. Regular languages sit at the base — the simplest class with the weakest descriptive power but the fastest decision algorithms ($O(n)$ for membership testing).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a regular language. State Kleene's Theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

A language $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ is called a **regular language** if it can be accepted by some finite automaton (DFA/NFA) or denoted by a regular expression.

**Kleene's Theorem:** A language $L$ is regular **if and only if** the following three conditions are equivalent:
1. $L$ can be denoted by a **Regular Expression**.
2. $L$ is accepted by some **Finite Automaton** (DFA or NFA).
3. $L$ is generated by some **Regular Grammar** (right-linear or left-linear).

This three-way equivalence is the cornerstone of automata theory. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for theorem statement with all three conditions)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the difference between DFA and NFA with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | DFA | NFA |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Transition function | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ (single state) | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma_\varepsilon \rightarrow 2^Q$ (set of states) |
| Determinism | Unique next state for each input | Multiple next states possible |
| $\varepsilon$-transitions | Not allowed | Allowed |
| Acceptance | Only one path matters | **At least one** path must lead to a final state |
| Example | Vending machine logic | Pattern matcher with backtracking |

**Example:** A DFA recognizing strings ending in "ab" has exactly one transition per state-symbol pair, whereas an NFA for $(a \mid b)^* a$ allows multiple branches at the start state on input $a$. *(3 marks: 1 for tabular distinction + 2 for examples)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Construct a DFA that accepts all strings over $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ containing the substring "**001**" as a substring. Draw the transition diagram and provide the formal 5-tuple definition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Convert the following NFA to an equivalent DFA using subset construction. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

| State | On 0 | On 1 |
|-------|------|------|
| $\rightarrow p$ | $\{p, q\}$ | $\{p\}$ |
| $q$ | $\{r\}$ | $\varnothing$ |
| $r$ | $\{r\}$ | $\{r\}$ |
| $\leftarrow *r$ | (final) | |

*(Here $\leftarrow *r$ denotes the final state.)*

---

### **Model Solution for Question A(a)**

**Approach:** We need to track how much of "001" we've matched so far.

**State Design:**
- $A$ — initial state (matched nothing yet).
- $B$ — matched "0".
- $C$ — matched "00".
- $D$ — matched "001" (ACCEPTING — we can stay here forever).
- $E$ — dead/trap state (optional, but explicit DFAs prefer it).

**Transition Logic (Reading left-to-right):**

| Current State | Read 0 | Read 1 |
|---------------|--------|--------|
| $A$ (start) | $B$ | $A$ |
| $B$ (have "0") | $C$ | $A$ |
| $C$ (have "00") | $C$ | $D$ |
| $D$ (have "001", final) | $D$ | $D$ |

**Formal 5-tuple:**

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$

where:
- $Q = \{A, B, C, D\}$
- $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$
- $q_0 = A$
- $F = \{D\}$
- $\delta$ as per the table above.

**Verification:** Test string "1001":
- $A \xrightarrow{1} A \xrightarrow{0} B \xrightarrow{0} C \xrightarrow{1} D$ ✅ Accepted.

**Mermaid Visualization:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A((A)) -- "0" --> B((B))
    A -- "1" --> A
    B -- "0" --> C((C))
    B -- "1" --> A
    C -- "0" --> C
    C -- "1" --> D((D))
    D -- "0" --> D
    D -- "1" --> D

    classDef start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef accept fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    class A start
    class D accept
```

---

### **Model Solution for Question A(b)**

**Step 1: Identify NFA parameters.**
- $Q = \{p, q, r\}$
- $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$
- Start state: $p$
- Final states: $F = \{r\}$
- No $\varepsilon$-transitions.

**Step 2: Initialize DFA.**
- DFA start state = $\{p\}$ (since no $\varepsilon$-closure needed).
- Subsets found so far: $\{\{p\}\}$ (unvisited).

**Step 3: Compute transitions from $\{p\}$.**

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{p\}, 0) = \delta_{NFA}(p, 0) = \{p, q\}$$

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{p\}, 1) = \delta_{NFA}(p, 1) = \{p\}$$

Add $\{p, q\}$ to unvisited list.

**Step 4: Process $\{p, q\}$.**

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{p, q\}, 0) = \delta(p, 0) \cup \delta(q, 0) = \{p, q\} \cup \{r\} = \{p, q, r\}$$

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{p, q\}, 1) = \delta(p, 1) \cup \delta(q, 1) = \{p\} \cup \varnothing = \{p\}$$

Add $\{p, q, r\}$ to unvisited list.

**Step 5: Process $\{p, q, r\}$.**

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{p, q, r\}, 0) = \{p, q\} \cup \{r\} \cup \{r\} = \{p, q, r\}$$

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{p, q, r\}, 1) = \{p\} \cup \varnothing \cup \{r\} = \{p, r\}$$

Add $\{p, r\}$ to unvisited list.

**Step 6: Process $\{p, r\}$.**

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{p, r\}, 0) = \{p, q\} \cup \{r\} = \{p, q, r\}$$

$$\delta_{DFA}(\{p, r\}, 1) = \{p\} \cup \{r\} = \{p, r\}$$

**Step 7: Identify Final States.**
Any DFA state containing $r$ (the NFA final state) is final in DFA:

$$F_{DFA} = \{\{p, q, r\}, \{p, r\}\}$$

**Final DFA Transition Table:**

| DFA State | On 0 | On 1 | Final? |
|-----------|------|------|--------|
| $\rightarrow A = \{p\}$ | $B = \{p, q\}$ | $A = \{p\}$ | No |
| $B = \{p, q\}$ | $C = \{p, q, r\}$ | $A = \{p\}$ | No |
| $C = \{p, q, r\}$ | $C = \{p, q, r\}$ | $D = \{p, r\}$ | **Yes** |
| $D = \{p, r\}$ | $C = \{p, q, r\}$ | $D = \{p, r\}$ | **Yes** |

The DFA has **4 states**, less than the maximum $2^3 = 8$. ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** State and prove the **Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages**. Use it to prove that $L = \{ w \in \{a, b\}^* \mid n_a(w) = n_b(w) \}$ is **not regular**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Thompson's Construction**, build an NFA for the regular expression:

$$r = (a \mid b)^* (a b \mid b a)^*$$

Draw the NFA clearly and explain each construction step. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B(a)**

**Statement of Pumping Lemma:**

> If $L$ is a regular language, then there exists a constant $p \geq 1$ (the pumping length) such that every string $w \in L$ with $\vert w \geq p$ can be decomposed as $w = xyz$ satisfying:
> 1. $\vert xy \leq p$
> 2. $\vert y \geq 1$
> 3. $xy^i z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$

**Proof Idea:** Since $L$ is regular, it has a DFA with $p$ states ($p = $ number of states). For any $w = w_1 w_2 \dots w_n$ with $n \geq p$, by the **Pigeonhole Principle**, the DFA must revisit some state within the first $p+1$ transitions. The loop between revisits forms the "pumpable" substring $y$.

**Application to $L = \{w \mid n_a(w) = n_b(w)\}$:**

**Step 1:** Assume $L$ is regular. Then $\exists p$ as above.

**Step 2:** Choose $w = a^p b^p \in L$. Clearly $\vert w \geq p$.

**Step 3:** By the Pumping Lemma, $w = xyz$ with $\vert xy \leq p$ and $\vert y \geq 1$.

Since the first $p$ symbols are all $a$, the substring $xy$ consists entirely of $a$'s. So $y = a^k$ with $k \geq 1$.

**Step 4:** Pump down with $i = 0$:

$$xz = a^{p-k} b^p$$

For $xz \in L$, we need $n_a(xz) = n_b(xz)$, i.e., $p - k = p$, which gives $k = 0$.

**Step 5:** Contradiction: $k \geq 1$ but $k = 0$ is impossible.

Hence $L$ is **not regular**. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Model Solution for Question B(b)**

**Parsing $r$:**

$$r = (a \mid b)^* \cdot (a b \mid b a)^*$$

This is a **concatenation of two Kleene-starred expressions**. We use Thompson's Construction recursively.

**Step 1: Build base NFAs** for $a$, $b$, $ab$, $ba$.

**NFA for $a$:** States $\{s_1, f_1\}$, transition $s_1 \xrightarrow{a} f_1$.

**NFA for $b$:** States $\{s_2, f_2\}$, transition $s_2 \xrightarrow{b} f_2$.

**NFA for $ab$ (concatenation):** Add $\varepsilon$-transition from $f_1$ to $s_2$.

**NFA for $ba$ (concatenation):** Add $\varepsilon$-transition from $f_2'$ to $s_1'$.

**NFA for $(ab \mid ba)$ (union):** New start $S_1$ with $\varepsilon$-edges to both, merge finals into single $F_1$.

**NFA for $(a \mid b)$ (union):** New start $S_2$ with $\varepsilon$-edges to NFAs of $a$ and $b$, merge finals into $F_2$.

**NFA for $(a \mid b)^*$ (Kleene star):** New start $S_3$ and final $F_3$, with $\varepsilon$-edges:
- $S_3 \to S_2$ (enter the loop)
- $F_2 \to S_2$ (repeat)
- $S_3 \to F_3$ (skip the loop, $\varepsilon$ repetitions)
- $F_2 \to F_3$ (exit after repetitions)

**NFA for $(ab \mid ba)^*$:** Analogous construction with new start $S_4$ and final $F_4$.

**Step 2: Concatenate the two stars** via a new $\varepsilon$-edge from $F_3$ to $S_4$.

**Final Result:** An NFA with approximately **16–18 states** and at most $2 \cdot \vert r \vert = 2 \cdot 13 = 26$ states (Thompson's bound).

**Mermaid Visualization:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    startN((START)) -- epsilon --> starAB
    starAB((P1)) -- epsilon --> starABinner
    starABinner((P2)) -- epsilon --> endAB
    endAB((F1)) -- epsilon --> startN
    endAB -- epsilon --> starABAB
    starABAB((P3)) -- epsilon --> starABABinner
    starABABinner((P4)) -- epsilon --> endABAB
    endABAB((F2)) -- epsilon --> starABAB
    endABAB -- epsilon --> finalN((FINAL))

    starABinner -- epsilon --> branchAorB1
    branchAorB1((B1)) -- epsilon --> subNFAa1
    subNFAa1((A1)) -- a --> subNFAf1((A2))
    branchAorB1 -- epsilon --> subNFAb1
    subNFAb1((B2)) -- b --> subNFAfb1((B3))
    subNFAf1 -- epsilon --> endAB
    subNFAfb1 -- epsilon --> endAB

    starABABinner -- epsilon --> branchABorBA
    branchABorBA((B4)) -- epsilon --> subNFAab
    subNFAab((AB1)) -- a --> ab2
    ab2((AB2)) -- b --> ab3((AB3))
    branchABorBA -- epsilon --> subNFAba
    subNFAba((BA1)) -- b --> ba2
    ba2((BA2)) -- a --> ba3((BA3))
    ab3 -- epsilon --> endABAB
    ba3 -- epsilon --> endABAB

    classDef startNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef finalNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    class startN startNode
    class finalN finalNode
```

> [!TIP]
> **Construction Tip:** Always label $\varepsilon$-transitions explicitly. In KTU exams, drawing them as unlabeled arrows often leads to marks deducted for ambiguity.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Module 1:**
>
> 1. **Pumping Lemma Misuse:** Many students try to **prove** a language is regular using the Pumping Lemma. ❌ WRONG! The Pumping Lemma is **only used to prove languages are NOT regular** (by contradiction). To prove regularity, construct a DFA, NFA, or RE.
>
> 2. **Subset Construction Errors:** Forgetting to take the **union** of transitions from multiple NFA states when computing DFA transitions. Each element of the subset must contribute.
>
> 3. **Final State Identification:** Marking a DFA state as final just because it "looks right" instead of checking if it **contains** at least one NFA final state.
>
> 4. **RE Precedence:** Writing $a b^*$ as $(ab)^*$ instead of $a(b^*)$. KTU values **parenthesization discipline**.
>
> 5. **Empty Set vs Empty String:** Confusing $\varnothing$ (the empty set, RE for empty language) with $\varepsilon$ (the empty string). The string $\varepsilon$ has length 0 but is **not** the same as the empty set.
>
> 6. **Missing Transition Functions:** DFAs require the transition function to be **total** (defined for every state-symbol pair). Forgetting one entry costs full marks.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Regular Languages**

- **Definitions to Memorize:**
  - Alphabet $\Sigma$, String, Language $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$, Regular Language.
  - DFA 5-tuple $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ — deterministic, total, no $\varepsilon$.
  - NFA 5-tuple — nondeterministic, may use $\varepsilon$.
  - Regular Expression — built from $\varnothing, \varepsilon, a, \cup, \cdot, *$.

- **Three Equivalent Views of Regular Languages:**
  - Regular Expressions (algebraic)
  - Finite Automata (mechanical)
  - Regular Grammars (generative)

- **Key Theorems:**
  - Kleene's Theorem (RE ↔ FA equivalence)
  - Pumping Lemma (negative test for regularity)
  - Myhill-Nerode Theorem (minimal DFA uniqueness)

- **Algorithms to Master:**
  - RE → NFA (Thompson's Construction)
  - NFA → DFA (Subset Construction)
  - DFA → Minimal DFA (Table-Filling)
  - DFA → RE (State Elimination / Arden's Theorem)
  - Product Construction for Intersection

- **Closure Properties (Memorize All):**
  - Closed under: Union, Intersection, Complement, Concatenation, Kleene Star, Reverse, Difference, Homomorphism, Inverse Homomorphism.
  - **Open questions** (where it is unknown / not closed): Regular languages are closed under **ALL** Boolean operations — this is one of their defining strengths!

- **Pumping Lemma Template for Proofs:**
  1. Assume $L$ is regular; let $p$ be the pumping length.
  2. Choose $w \in L$ with $\vert w \geq p$ — usually $w = a^p b^p$ or $w = (ab)^p$.
  3. Decompose $w = xyz$ with $\vert xy \leq p$, $\vert y \geq 1$.
  4. Show that some $xy^i z$ (typically $i = 0$ or $i = 2$) is **not** in $L$.
  5. Conclude contradiction; $L$ is not regular.

- **Engineering Applications (for viva/long-answer context):**
  - Compiler lexers (RE → DFA via `lex`/`flex`).
  - Network intrusion detection (regex on packet headers).
  - DNA sequence matching in bioinformatics.
  - Hardware verification (sequential circuits = DFA).

- **Quick Decision Rule:**
  - *"Can this be recognized with bounded memory?"* → YES → Regular.
  - *"Does it require counting or matching pairs across the string?"* → NO → Not regular.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formal Definition of a Nondeterministic Finite Automaton
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Formal Definition of a Nondeterministic Finite Automaton # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Nondeterministic Finite Automaton - NFA):** > A **Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA)** is formally defined as a **5-tuple** mathematical structure $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$, where each component has a precise, distinct role. The components of the 5-tuple are: | Symbol | Name | Mathematical Description | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $Q$ | Finite Set of States | A finite, non-empty set of internal configurations. | | $\Sigma$ | Input Alphabet | A finite, non-empty set of input symbols (alphabet). $\Sigma = \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$ may include $\varepsilon$ for $\varepsilon$-NFA. | | $\delta$ | Transition Function | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma_{\varepsilon} \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ (Maps a state and symbol to a **set** of next states). | | $q_0$ | Start State | $q_0 \in Q$ (The single designated initial state). | | $F$ | Set of Accept States | $F \subseteq Q$ (Subset of states where acceptance occurs). | The **2** in the expression $2^Q$ is the **powerset operator** — it represents *all possible subsets* of $Q$. This single symbol is what makes the automaton *nondeterministic*. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reading a Map" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: Navigating a City with Multiple Road Choices** > > Imagine you are a traveler in a city. You have a **map** of intersections (states) and roads (transitions). Each road is labeled with a letter (the input symbol). > > - In a **DFA**, at every intersection, you have **exactly one road** labeled with each letter. The path is rigidly forced. > - In an **NFA**, at some intersections, you might have **zero, one, or many roads** labeled with the *same* letter. You get to "clone" yourself and explore **all possible paths in parallel**. If **even one clone** reaches the destination (an accept state) by the end of the journey, the input is **accepted**. This "parallel cloning" or "guessing" capability is the intuitive essence of **nondeterminism**. It is not magic — it is a *mathematical abstraction* that simply checks whether *some* valid path exists. ## 1.3 Why Nondeterminism Matters in Theory of Computation > [!IMPORTANT] > Nondeterminism is a **theoretical construct** introduced to: > 1. **Simplify construction** of automata for complex languages (e.g., regular expressions $\rightarrow$ NFA is far easier than directly to DFA). > 2. **Model parallelism** and "guessing" — fundamental in algorithms, parsing (LR parsers), and search problems. > 3. **Bridge to advanced models** like pushdown automata and Turing machines. > 4. Prove the **closure properties** of regular languages under union, concatenation, and Kleene star elegantly. A celebrated theorem (Rabin & Scott, 1959) states: > **Every NFA can be converted to an equivalent DFA.** NFAs and DFAs recognize the **same class of languages** — the *Regular Languages*. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of the Transition Function $\delta$ The transition function is the *heart* of the NFA. Let us dissect it carefully. For a standard NFA (no $\varepsilon$-moves): $$\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^{Q}$$ For each state $q \in Q$ and each input symbol $a \in \Sigma$, the function returns a **set of states** $S \subseteq Q$ — that is, an element of the powerset $2^Q$. ### Three Critical Properties (The "Three Liberties" of Nondeterminism) > [!NOTE] > **Property 1: Multiple Destinations** > $\delta(q, a)$ may contain **more than one** state. > *Example*: $\delta(q_1, 0) = \{q_2, q_3, q_5\}$ > > **Property 2: Missing Transitions** > $\delta(q, a)$ may be the **empty set** $\emptyset$. > *Example*: $\delta(q_2, 1) = \emptyset$ (no 1-transition from $q_2$). > > **Property 3: Non-deterministic Branching** > Multiple states can transition on the same symbol, creating *parallel* computation paths. ## 2.2 Formal Acceptance of a String > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Acceptance):** > A string $w = w_1 w_2 \dots w_n$ is **accepted** by NFA $M$ if there exists *at least one* sequence of states $r_0, r_1, \dots, r_n$ in $Q$ such that: > > 1. $r_0 = q_0$ (starts at the start state) > 2. $r_{i+1} \in \delta(r_i, w_{i+1})$ for $i = 0, 1, \dots, n-1$ > 3. $r_n \in F$ (ends in an accept state) The **language accepted** by $M$ is: $$L(M) = \{ w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid \text{there exists an accepting path for } w \text{ in } M \}$$ This is called **existential acceptance** — *existence* of one good path is enough. ## 2.3 Extended Transition Function $\hat{\delta}$ Just like in DFA, we extend $\delta$ to operate on *strings*: $$\hat{\delta} : Q \times \Sigma^{*} \rightarrow 2^{Q}$$ with the recursive definition: - **Base case**: $\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = \{q\}$ - **Recursive case**: $\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \bigcup_{p \in \hat{\delta}(q, w)} \delta(p, a)$ The union is crucial — we collect all possible states reachable after reading $w$ and then following the $a$-transitions from each. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **Memorize the following table for KTU exams:** | Concept | NFA Expression | DFA Equivalent | Remarks | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Automaton Tuple | $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | Identical 5-tuple structure | | Transition Map | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ | Key difference: **powerset** vs **single state** | | Accept Criterion | $\exists$ at least one accepting path | All paths lead to $F$ | Existential vs Universal | | Max Transitions per Cell | Up to $\vert Q \vert$ | Exactly $1$ (or $0$ if partial) | NFA can have **none**, **one**, or **many** | | Worst-Case States after Conversion | $2^{\vert Q \vert}$ (DFA equivalent) | $\vert Q \vert$ | Exponential blow-up possible | | String Acceptance | $\hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \emptyset$ | $\hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \in F$ | NFA: intersection non-empty | | Equivalence Theorem | $L(NFA) = L(DFA)$ | — | Both recognize **Regular Languages** | | Relationship to Regex | $Regex \rightarrow \varepsilon$-NFA $\rightarrow$ NFA $\rightarrow$ DFA | Direct conversion is harder | NFA is the **standard intermediate** | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - **Lexical Analyzers** in compilers (e.g., `lex`, `flex`) internally convert regex $\to$ NFA $\to$ DFA. > - **Network Protocol Verification**: NFAs model network packet filters efficiently. > - **Pattern Matching** in text editors, bioinformatics (DNA sequence matching). > - **Hardware Design**: Finite state machines for digital circuits (though DFAs are more common in hardware). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Constructing a NFA and Verifying Acceptance **Problem:** Construct an NFA that accepts the language $L = \{ w \in \{0, 1\}^{*} \mid w \text{ contains "11" as a substring} \}$. ### Step 1: Design the States We need to track whether we have seen: - Nothing yet - A single "1" (potential start of "11") - The substring "11" already So $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$. ### Step 2: Identify the Alphabet $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$. ### Step 3: Define the Transition Function We deliberately allow **missing transitions** (nondeterminism is *optional* — we only use it when convenient). | State | On '0' | On '1' | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $q_0$ | $\{q_0\}$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | | $q_1$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{q_2\}$ | | $q_2$ | $\{q_2\}$ | $\{q_2\}$ | **Transition Logic Explained:** - From $q_0$ on '0': stay in $q_0$ (reset search for "11"). - From $q_0$ on '1': **nondeterministically** either stay in $q_0$ (ignore the '1') **or** move to $q_1$ (we *guess* this '1' is the start of "11"). - From $q_1$ on '0': the guess failed; no transition (dead end — but other paths may survive). - From $q_1$ on '1': we found "11" — go to $q_2$ (accepting sink). - From $q_2$ on either: remain in $q_2$ (once "11" is found, it stays found). ### Step 4: Specify Start and Accept States - $q_0 = q_0$ - $F = \{q_2\}$ ### Step 5: Formal 5-Tuple $$M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{0, 1\}, \delta, q_0, \{q_2\})$$ ### Step 6: Verify Acceptance of $w = 0110$ We trace the computation tree to demonstrate nondeterministic branching. $$\begin{aligned} \text{After } \varepsilon &: \text{ current states} = \{q_0\} \\ \text{Read '0' &: \delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0\} \rightarrow \text{current} = \{q_0\} \\ \text{Read '1' &: \delta(q_0, 1) = \{q_0, q_1\} \rightarrow \text{current} = \{q_0, q_1\} \\ \text{Read '1' &: \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \\ \text{Read '0' &: \delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_1, 0) \cup \delta(q_2, 0) = \{q_0\} \cup \emptyset \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\} \end{aligned}$$ **Final state set**: $\{q_0, q_2\}$. **Acceptance Check**: $\{q_0, q_2\} \cap F = \{q_2\} \cap \{q_2\} = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$. ✅ **String $w = 0110$ is ACCEPTED** because the path reaching $q_2$ survives. ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DFA vs NFA > [!TIP] > **Use this matrix to write comparison answers in KTU exams (a very common 7-mark question).** | Criterion | DFA | NFA | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Transition Output | A single state | A **set of states** | | Number of transitions | $\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert$ (complete) | $0$ to $\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert$ (variable) | | Computational Model | Deterministic (unique path) | Nondeterministic (parallel paths) | | Power | Regular languages | Regular languages (same class) | | Implementation | Direct (hardware-friendly) | Requires subset construction to simulate | | Construction from Regex | Difficult | **Easy and natural** | | States for $L$ | May be large | Often **fewer states** | | Conversion | — | Subset construction gives DFA with up to $2^{\vert Q \vert}$ states | | Closure Properties | Inherited from NFA | Proven directly for NFA, transferred to DFA | | Acceptance Definition | $\hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \in F$ | $\hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \emptyset$ | | Graphical Notation | Arrow per symbol | Arrow per symbol, multiple arrows allowed | ## 3.3 Powerset Calculation (For Subset Construction Reference) If $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$, then: $$2^{Q} = \{\emptyset, \{q_0\}, \{q_1\}, \{q_2\}, \{q_0, q_1\}, \{q_0, q_2\}, \{q_1, q_2\}, \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}\}$$ Total elements: $2^{3} = 8$. In general, the DFA equivalent of an NFA can have up to $2^{\vert Q \vert}$ states. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 State Diagram of the Worked NFA Example The NFA for $L = \{w \mid w \text{ contains "11" as a substring}\}$: ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> q0 q0 --> q0 : 0 q0 --> q0 : 1 q0 --> q1 : 1 q1 --> q2 : 1 q2 --> q2 : 0 q2 --> q2 : 1 q2 --> [*] note right of q0 Start state. Reading 1 branches to q0 (ignore) and q1 (start guess). end note note right of q1 One '1' seen. Next '1' confirms substring '11'. end note note right of q2 Final / accepting sink state. Stays in q2 forever. end note ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation of the Diagram:** > - The **double self-loop** of $q_0$ on input '1' is the visual signature of **nondeterminism**: the NFA can *either* stay in $q_0$ *or* move to $q_1$ on the same input. > - The empty cell $\delta(q_1, 0) = \emptyset$ has no outgoing arrow — that path *dies*, but other parallel paths may continue. ## 4.2 Transition Table Representation | State | Input '0' | Input '1' | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\rightarrow q_0$ | $\{q_0\}$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | | $q_1$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{q_2\}$ | | $*q_2$ | $\{q_2\}$ | $\{q_2\}$ | Legend: $\rightarrow$ = start state, $*$ = accept state. ## 4.3 Computation Tree for $w = 011$ (Visualizing Nondeterminism) ```mermaid graph TD A0["q0"] -->|"read 0"| A1["q0"] A1 -->|"read 1
branches"| A2a["q0"] A1 -->|"read 1
branches"| A2b["q1"] A2a -->|"read 1"| A3a["q0"] A2a -->|"read 1"| A3b["q1"] A2b -->|"read 1"| A3c["q2
(ACCEPT)"] style A3c fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px style A0 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Tree:** > The tree **branches** at state $q_0$ on input '1' — this is nondeterminism visualized. The string is **accepted** because the green-shaded path reaches $q_2$ (an accept state). The KTU exam answer should explicitly mention *at least one* accepting path. ## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: NFA Acceptance Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR IN[Input String w] --> READ[Symbol Reader] READ --> CUR[Current State Set S] CUR --> DELTA[Apply delta on symbol] DELTA --> UNION[Union all target states] UNION --> CHECK{Is S intersect F non-empty?} CHECK -->|Yes| ACC[ACCEPT w] CHECK -->|No| MORE{More symbols?} MORE -->|Yes| READ MORE -->|No| REJ[REJECT w] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q1.** Define a Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA). List the components of its 5-tuple representation. **Model Answer:** A Nondeterministic Finite Automaton is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where: - $Q$ is a finite set of states. - $\Sigma$ is a finite input alphabet. - $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ is the transition function mapping to a *set* of states. - $q_0 \in Q$ is the start state. - $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of final/accept states. The key feature is the **powerset** $2^{Q}$ in the transition function's codomain, allowing nondeterministic branching. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2.** How does the acceptance condition of an NFA differ from that of a DFA? **Model Answer:** In a DFA, the *unique* computation path must end in a final state. In an NFA, a string is accepted if **there exists at least one** computation path that ends in a final state. Mathematically: - DFA: $\hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \in F$ - NFA: $\hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \emptyset$ This is **existential** acceptance in NFA. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Module 1]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Construct an NFA that accepts the language $L = \{ w \in \{a, b\}^{*} \mid w \text{ ends with "ab"} \}$. Provide the formal 5-tuple and a clear state diagram. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Design Intuition:** We need to track the last symbol read because we are looking for the suffix "ab". **States:** - $q_0$: Initial state (no relevant suffix seen). - $q_1$: The last symbol read was "a" (potential start of "ab"). - $q_2$: Accepting state — we have just seen "ab". **Transitions (showing nondeterminism-friendly structure):** | State | On 'a' | On 'b' | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\rightarrow q_0$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ | | $q_1$ | $\{q_1\}$ | $\{q_2\}$ | | $*q_2$ | $\{q_1\}$ | $\{q_2\}$ | **Explanation:** - From $q_0$ on 'a': nondeterministically *stay* in $q_0$ (ignore the 'a') or *move* to $q_1$ (guess this 'a' starts the suffix). - From $q_0$ on 'b': stay in $q_0$ (still no relevant suffix). - From $q_1$ on 'a': stay in $q_1$ (overwrite previous 'a'). - From $q_1$ on 'b': move to $q_2$ (we found "ab"). - From $q_2$ on 'a': go to $q_1$ (last symbol now is 'a' again). - From $q_2$ on 'b': stay in $q_2$ (last symbol is still 'b' — suffix "ab" not formed anew, but a path persists). **Formal 5-Tuple:** $$M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b\}, \delta, q_0, \{q_2\})$$ **State Diagram:** ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> q0 q0 --> q0 : a q0 --> q1 : a q0 --> q0 : b q1 --> q1 : a q1 --> q2 : b q2 --> q1 : a q2 --> q2 : b ``` **Verification:** For $w = aab$: - Path: $q_0 \xrightarrow{a} q_1 \xrightarrow{a} q_1 \xrightarrow{b} q_2$ ✓ **Accepted**. **(7 marks)** --- **(b)** Prove that the language $L = \{ w \in \{0, 1\}^{*} \mid w \text{ has "01" as a substring} \}$ is regular by constructing an NFA. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **States:** - $q_0$: Start (no "0" seen recently). - $q_1$: Have just seen a "0". - $q_2$: Accept state — "01" has occurred. **Transitions:** | State | On '0' | On '1' | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\rightarrow q_0$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ | | $q_1$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{q_2\}$ | | $*q_2$ | $\{q_2\}$ | $\{q_2\}$ | **Intuition:** From $q_0$, on seeing a '0', we *guess* it might be the start of "01", so we move to $q_1$. If the next symbol is '1', we confirm by moving to $q_2$. If it is '0', that guess path dies — but other parallel paths (still in $q_0$) survive. **5-tuple:** $M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{0, 1\}, \delta, q_0, \{q_2\})$. Since we have constructed an NFA $M$ with $L(M) = L$, and NFAs accept exactly the **regular languages**, $L$ is regular. $\blacksquare$ **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **(a)** Define an NFA formally. Explain each component of the 5-tuple with a suitable example. Compare NFA with DFA in tabular form. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Formal Definition:** An NFA is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$: 1. **$Q$**: Finite non-empty set of states. 2. **$\Sigma$**: Finite non-empty input alphabet. 3. **$\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^{Q}$**: Transition function (key: maps to *sets* of states). 4. **$q_0 \in Q$**: Initial/start state. 5. **$F \subseteq Q$**: Set of final/accept states. **Example:** NFA for $L = \{ \varepsilon, a \}$: $Q = \{q_0, q_1\}$, $\Sigma = \{a\}$, $q_0 = q_0$, $F = \{q_1\}$, $\delta(q_0, a) = \{q_1\}$, $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_0\}$ (via NFA semantics). **Comparison Table** — see Analytical Matrix in Section 3.2 above. **(7 marks)** --- **(b)** Construct an NFA over the alphabet $\{0, 1\}$ that accepts all strings containing the substring "101". Draw the transition diagram and formally specify the automaton. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **States:** - $q_0$: Initial (no progress). - $q_1$: Have seen "1" (first symbol of "101"). - $q_2$: Have seen "10". - $q_3$: Accept state — "101" found. **Transitions:** | State | On '0' | On '1' | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\rightarrow q_0$ | $\{q_0\}$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | | $q_1$ | $\{q_2\}$ | $\{q_1\}$ | | $q_2$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{q_3\}$ | | $*q_3$ | $\{q_3\}$ | $\{q_3\}$ | **5-tuple:** $M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3\}, \{0, 1\}, \delta, q_0, \{q_3\})$. **Diagram:** ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> q0 q0 --> q0 : 0 q0 --> q0 : 1 q0 --> q1 : 1 q1 --> q1 : 1 q1 --> q2 : 0 q2 --> q3 : 1 q3 --> q3 : 0 q3 --> q3 : 1 ``` **Verification:** For $w = 0101$: - Path: $q_0 \xrightarrow{0} q_0 \xrightarrow{1} q_1 \xrightarrow{0} q_2 \xrightarrow{1} q_3$ ✓ **Accepted**. **(7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting the powerset $2^{Q}$**: Writing $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ for an NFA. This is the *DFA* definition — **zero credit** for that component. > > 2. **Confusing the start state notation**: Use $q_0$ (subscript zero), not $q^{0}$ or $q_1$. The start state is *uniquely* designated. > > 3. **Using universal acceptance instead of existential**: Writing "all paths must end in $F$" — this is the NFA *complement* acceptance and is **wrong** for standard NFA. > > 4. **Omitting empty-set transitions**: A cell with **no arrow** in the diagram *must* be shown as $\emptyset$ in the table for full marks. > > 5. **Missing the start state arrow or double-circle for accept states**: In diagrams, always mark start state with a small incoming arrow and accept states with double circles (or a `*` in the table). > > 6. **Calling NFA "non-deterministic" without justification**: Briefly state that the transition function maps to a *set* — that is the mathematical source of nondeterminism. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — NFA Formal Definition** - ✅ An NFA is a **5-tuple** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$. - ✅ The **transition function** signature is $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ — this is the *defining* difference from DFA. - ✅ **Acceptance** is **existential**: $\hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \emptyset$. - ✅ $\hat{\delta}$ is defined recursively with $\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = \{q\}$. - ✅ NFA may have **zero, one, or many** next states per input symbol. - ✅ NFAs and DFAs accept the **same class** of languages (Regular Languages). - ✅ NFA equivalent of an NFA with $n$ states can have up to $2^{n}$ states (**exponential blow-up**). - ✅ NFA construction from **regular expressions** is much *simpler* than direct DFA construction. - ✅ For every state in the diagram, draw *all* outgoing transitions or explicitly mark missing ones as $\emptyset$. - ✅ Start state: single arrow from nowhere. Accept state(s): double circle. - ✅ Standard notation: $q_0$ = start, $F$ = accept set, $\Sigma$ = alphabet, $Q$ = state set. ---

topics.title: Equivalence of NFAs and DFAs - Subset Construction, Minimization of DFA, NFA with Epsilon Transitions
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equivalence of NFAs and DFAs - Subset Construction, Minimization of DFA, NFA with Epsilon Transitions

# Equivalence of NFAs and DFAs — Subset Construction, Minimization of DFA, NFA with Epsilon Transitions

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)

A **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)** is a 5-tuple

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$

where

- $Q$ is a finite, **non-empty** set of states,
- $\Sigma$ is a finite **input alphabet** (does **not** include $\varepsilon$),
- $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ is the **transition function** (total, single-valued),
- $q_0 \in Q$ is the unique **start state**,
- $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of **final (accepting) states**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Determinism Property:** For every state $q \in Q$ and every symbol $a \in \Sigma$, exactly one transition $\delta(q,a)$ is defined. The machine has **no choice** and **no spontaneity**.

### 1.2 Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA)

An **Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA)** is also a 5-tuple

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$

with the only difference being the transition function:

$$\delta : Q \times \Sigma_\varepsilon \rightarrow 2^Q$$

where $\Sigma_\varepsilon = \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$ and $2^Q$ is the power set of $Q$. The transition returns a **set of possible next states** (possibly empty).

> [!NOTE] **Three Sources of Nondeterminism:**
> 1. Multiple transitions on the same symbol ($|\delta(q,a)| > 1$).
> 2. Spontaneous $\varepsilon$-transitions.
> 3. Missing transitions (dead states implicit).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The GPS vs. The Maze"

Imagine you are inside a maze with multiple exits:

- **DFA = A strict GPS app.** At every junction, the GPS gives you **exactly one** turn instruction based on the street name. There is no ambiguity — you either reach the exit or get stuck.
- **NFA = A magic compass.** At every junction, the compass *guesses* which path might lead to the exit. It "clones" itself into multiple parallel explorers; if **any one clone** finds the exit, the original input is accepted.
- **Subset Construction = Translating the magic compass back into a deterministic GPS** by tracking the *set* of all clones simultaneously — converting parallel guessing into a single deterministic simulation.
- **Minimization = A cleanup crew** that walks through the GPS map and merges two stations (states) that are *behaviorally identical* — the traveller cannot tell them apart no matter what road is taken.

### 1.4 The Equivalence Theorem (KTU High-Yield Result)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Theorem (Rabin–Scott, 1959):** Every language recognized by an NFA is also recognized by some DFA, and vice versa. Formally:
> $$\mathcal{L}(\text{NFA}) = \mathcal{L}(\text{DFA})$$
> Hence the class of **regular languages** is closed under both models.

### 1.5 Epsilon-NFA ($\varepsilon$-NFA)

An **$\varepsilon$-NFA** is an NFA that is permitted to make **spontaneous transitions** without consuming any input symbol. The transition function becomes:

$$\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^Q$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why introduce $\varepsilon$?** It enormously simplifies the construction of automata for union, concatenation, and Kleene star — three core regular operations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Subset Construction (Rabin–Scott Algorithm)

**Goal:** Convert an NFA $N = (Q_N, \Sigma, \delta_N, q_0, F_N)$ into an equivalent DFA $D = (Q_D, \Sigma, \delta_D, \{q_0\}, F_D)$ that accepts exactly the same language.

**Construction Logic (step by step):**

- **Step 1 — Define new state set.** Each state of $D$ is a **subset** of $Q_N$:
$$Q_D \subseteq 2^{Q_N}$$
- **Step 2 — Start state.** It is the singleton containing $q_0$: $q_{0,D} = \{q_0\}$.
- **Step 3 — Transition rule.** For a DFA-state $S \subseteq Q_N$ and symbol $a \in \Sigma$:
$$\delta_D(S, a) = \bigcup_{q \in S} \delta_N(q, a)$$
- **Step 4 — Final states.** A DFA-state $S$ is accepting iff it contains at least one NFA-accepting state:
$$F_D = \{\, S \subseteq Q_N \mid S \cap F_N \neq \emptyset \,\}$$
- **Step 5 — Iterate** until no new subsets are generated (closure under $\delta_D$).

> [!NOTE] In the worst case, $|Q_D| = 2^{|Q_N|}$. The blow-up is **exponential** but unavoidable in general.

### 2.2 DFA Minimization (Table-Filling Algorithm)

**Goal:** Given a DFA $D$, produce the **unique** (up to isomorphism) minimum-state DFA $D_{\min}$ that accepts the same language.

**Core Idea — Distinguishability:** Two states $p, q$ are **distinguishable** if there exists *some* string $w$ such that exactly one of $\hat\delta(p,w), \hat\delta(q,w)$ is a final state. Otherwise they are **equivalent** (mergeable).

**Algorithm (Hopcroft's Trace / Table-Filling):**

- **Step 1 — Initialize.** For every pair of states $\{p,q\}$ where exactly one is in $F$, **mark** the pair as *distinguishable*.
- **Step 2 — Iterative propagation.** For every unmarked pair $\{p,q\}$ and every symbol $a \in \Sigma$:
    - If the pair $\{\delta(p,a), \delta(q,a)\}$ is already marked, then mark $\{p,q\}$ as distinguishable (because they lead to distinguishable states).
- **Step 3 — Converge.** Repeat Step 2 until no new marks are added in a complete pass.
- **Step 4 — Partition.** Two states $p, q$ are equivalent iff the pair $\{p,q\}$ is **never marked**. Merge each equivalence class into a single state of $D_{\min}$.

### 2.3 Epsilon-Closure ($\varepsilon$-closure)

For any set of states $S \subseteq Q$, the **$\varepsilon$-closure** is the set of all states reachable from any state in $S$ using **only $\varepsilon$-transitions** (including $S$ itself):

$$\text{E-closure}(S) = S \cup \big\{ q \mid \exists p \in S \text{ such that } p \xRightarrow{\varepsilon^*} q \big\}$$

Recursive definition:
$$\text{E-closure}(S) = S \cup \bigcup_{q \in S} \text{E-closure}\big(\delta(q, \varepsilon)\big)$$

### 2.4 Converting $\varepsilon$-NFA to DFA

The transition for a DFA-state $S$ on symbol $a$ is:
$$\delta_D(S, a) = \text{E-closure}\!\left(\bigcup_{q \in S} \delta(q, a)\right)$$

The start state is $\text{E-closure}(\{q_0\})$ and the final state condition remains $S \cap F \neq \emptyset$.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NFA transition | $\delta_N : Q \times \Sigma_\varepsilon \to 2^Q$ | Always remember $\Sigma_\varepsilon = \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$ |
| 2 | Subset construction transition | $\delta_D(S,a) = \bigcup_{q \in S} \delta_N(q,a)$ | Subscript $D$ for DFA, $N$ for NFA — never mix |
| 3 | $\varepsilon$-closure | $\text{E-closure}(S) = S \cup \bigcup_{q \in S} \text{E-closure}(\delta(q,\varepsilon))$ | Apply iteratively until stable |
| 4 | $\varepsilon$-NFA $\to$ DFA transition | $\delta_D(S,a) = \text{E-closure}(\bigcup_{q \in S} \delta(q,a))$ | Wrap the union with E-closure — examiners check this |
| 5 | Final-state condition (both) | $F_D = \{S \mid S \cap F_N \neq \emptyset\}$ | A subset is accepting iff **it contains at least one** old final state |
| 6 | Worst-case blow-up | $\vert Q_D \vert \leq 2^{\vert Q_N \vert}$ | Often asked as a 3-mark question |
| 7 | Distinguishability | $p \not\equiv q \iff \exists w \in \Sigma^* : \delta^*(p,w) \in F \oplus \delta^*(q,w) \in F$ | The $\oplus$ symbol means "exactly one" |
| 8 | Minimization guarantee | $D_{\min}$ is unique up to state renaming | Myhill–Nerode theorem basis |
| 9 | Number of pairs in table | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ for $n$ states | Common in calculation questions |
| 10 | Regex $\to \varepsilon$-NFA | Union, concat, star via $\varepsilon$-transitions | Always ask "what is the smallest DFA?" |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Real-World Utility:** Subset construction underlies **lexical analyzers** (Lex/Flex), **regex engines** (RE2, PCRE), **network packet filters**, and **model-checking** tools. DFA minimization is the foundation of **compiler optimization** and **hardware logic synthesis** (state minimization in FSMs).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Subset Construction (NFA $\to$ DFA)

**Given NFA** $N = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a,b\}, \delta_N, q_0, \{q_2\})$ with transition table:

| State | $a$ | $b$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\to q_0$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ |
| $q_1$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{q_2\}$ |
| $*q_2$ | $\{q_2\}$ | $\{q_2\}$ |

We construct the equivalent DFA step-by-step.

**Iteration 1 — Process start state $S_0 = \{q_0\}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_D(\{q_0\}, a) &= \delta_N(q_0, a) = \{q_0, q_1\} \;\triangleq\; A \\
\delta_D(\{q_0\}, b) &= \delta_N(q_0, b) = \{q_0\} \;\triangleq\; B
\end{aligned}$$

New states discovered: $A = \{q_0, q_1\}$, $B = \{q_0\}$.

**Iteration 2 — Process $A = \{q_0, q_1\}$ on $a$ and $b$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_D(A, a) &= \delta_N(q_0, a) \cup \delta_N(q_1, a) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \emptyset = \{q_0, q_1\} = A \\
\delta_D(A, b) &= \delta_N(q_0, b) \cup \delta_N(q_1, b) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\} \;\triangleq\; C
\end{aligned}$$

New state: $C = \{q_0, q_2\}$ — note $C \cap F_N = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$, so $C$ is **accepting**.

**Iteration 3 — Process $B = \{q_0\}$ on $a$ and $b$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_D(B, a) &= \delta_N(q_0, a) = \{q_0, q_1\} = A \\
\delta_D(B, b) &= \delta_N(q_0, b) = \{q_0\} = B
\end{aligned}$$

No new states.

**Iteration 4 — Process $C = \{q_0, q_2\}$ on $a$ and $b$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_D(C, a) &= \delta_N(q_0, a) \cup \delta_N(q_2, a) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \;\triangleq\; D \\
\delta_D(C, b) &= \delta_N(q_0, b) \cup \delta_N(q_2, b) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\} = C
\end{aligned}$$

New state: $D = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$. It contains $q_2$, so $D$ is **accepting**.

**Iteration 5 — Process $D = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ on $a$ and $b$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_D(D, a) &= \delta_N(q_0,a) \cup \delta_N(q_1,a) \cup \delta_N(q_2,a) \\
&= \{q_0,q_1\} \cup \emptyset \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0,q_1,q_2\} = D \\
\delta_D(D, b) &= \delta_N(q_0,b) \cup \delta_N(q_1,b) \cup \delta_N(q_2,b) \\
&= \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0,q_2\} = C
\end{aligned}$$

No new states. **Algorithm terminates.**

**Resulting DFA:**

| DFA-State | $a$ | $b$ | Accepting? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\to \{q_0\}$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ | No |
| $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0, q_2\}$ | No |
| $\{q_0\}$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ | No |
| $\{q_0, q_2\}$ | $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ | $\{q_0, q_2\}$ | **Yes** |
| $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ | $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ | $\{q_0, q_2\}$ | **Yes** |

Renaming: $A = \{q_0,q_1\}, B = \{q_0\}, C = \{q_0,q_2\}, D = \{q_0,q_1,q_2\}$.

> [!NOTE] **Verification — Language:** $L = \{w \in \{a,b\}^* \mid w \text{ contains } ab \text{ as a substring}\}$. The NFA "guesses" when to switch from reading $a^*$ to reading $b$; the DFA deterministically tracks "have I seen an $ab$ yet?" via state $C$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — DFA Minimization (Table-Filling)

**Given DFA** with states $\{p, q, r, s\}$, start $= p$, $F = \{r, s\}$, alphabet $\{a, b\}$:

| State | $a$ | $b$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\to p$ | $q$ | $p$ |
| $q$ | $r$ | $p$ |
| $*r$ | $r$ | $s$ |
| $*s$ | $r$ | $r$ |

**Step 1 — Initial Marking.** Mark all pairs $\{X, Y\}$ where exactly one is final.

Number of pairs: $\frac{4 \cdot 3}{2} = 6$ pairs: $\{p,q\}, \{p,r\}, \{p,s\}, \{q,r\}, \{q,s\}, \{r,s\}$.

Pairs initially marked (one final, one non-final): $\{p,r\}, \{p,s\}, \{q,r\}, \{q,s\}$. So $r \sim s$ and $p \sim q$ remain unmarked.

**Step 2 — Iterative Distinguishing Pass 1:**

- Check $\{p,q\}$ on $a$: $\delta(p,a)=q, \delta(q,a)=r \Rightarrow$ pair $\{q,r\}$ is marked $\Rightarrow$ **mark $\{p,q\}$**.
- Check $\{p,q\}$ on $b$: $\delta(p,b)=p, \delta(q,b)=p \Rightarrow$ pair $\{p,p\}$ trivially equivalent, no new info.
- Check $\{r,s\}$ on $a$: $\delta(r,a)=r, \delta(s,a)=r \Rightarrow$ pair $\{r,r\}$ trivial.
- Check $\{r,s\}$ on $b$: $\delta(r,b)=s, \delta(s,b)=r \Rightarrow$ pair $\{r,s\}$ itself (unmarked) $\Rightarrow$ no new mark.

After Pass 1: $\{p,q\}$ marked. Only $\{r,s\}$ remains unmarked.

**Step 3 — Pass 2 on $\{r,s\}$:**

- On $a$: $\{r,r\}$ trivial.
- On $b$: $\{s, r\}$ — same pair, still unmarked.
- No new marks.

**Step 4 — Convergence reached.** Algorithm terminates.

**Step 5 — Equivalence Partitioning:**

- $\{p\}$, $\{q\}$, $\{r,s\}$ (merged).

**Resulting Minimum DFA** $D_{\min}$ with states $\{[p], [q], [rs]\}$:

| State | $a$ | $b$ | Accepting? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\to [p]$ | $[q]$ | $[p]$ | No |
| $[q]$ | $[rs]$ | $[p]$ | No |
| $*[rs]$ | $[rs]$ | $[rs]$ | **Yes** |

> [!NOTE] **Reduction:** From 4 states down to 3 states. The minimum DFA is **unique up to renaming**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — $\varepsilon$-NFA to DFA Conversion

**Given $\varepsilon$-NFA** with states $\{p, q, r\}$, start $= p$, $F = \{r\}$, alphabet $\{a, b\}$:

| State | $a$ | $b$ | $\varepsilon$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\to p$ | $\{q\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{r\}$ |
| $q$ | $\{q\}$ | $\{r\}$ | $\emptyset$ |
| $*r$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ |

**Step 1 — Compute $\varepsilon$-closures:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{E-closure}(\{p\}) &= \{p\} \cup \delta(p,\varepsilon) = \{p, r\} \\
\text{E-closure}(\{q\}) &= \{q\} \\
\text{E-closure}(\{r\}) &= \{r\} \\
\text{E-closure}(\{p, r\}) &= \{p, r\} \\
\text{E-closure}(\{p, q\}) &= \{p, q, r\} \quad (\text{via } p \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} r)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — DFA Start State.** $A = \text{E-closure}(\{p\}) = \{p, r\}$. Since $r \in A$, $A$ is **accepting**.

**Step 3 — Transitions from $A = \{p, r\}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_D(A, a) &= \text{E-closure}\big(\delta(p,a) \cup \delta(r,a)\big) = \text{E-closure}(\{q\} \cup \emptyset) = \text{E-closure}(\{q\}) = \{q\} = B \\
\delta_D(A, b) &= \text{E-closure}\big(\delta(p,b) \cup \delta(r,b)\big) = \text{E-closure}(\emptyset \cup \emptyset) = \emptyset \;\triangleq\; \emptyset
\end{aligned}$$

New state: $B = \{q\}$ (non-accepting). Trap state $\emptyset$ discovered.

**Step 4 — Transitions from $B = \{q\}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_D(B, a) &= \text{E-closure}(\delta(q,a)) = \text{E-closure}(\{q\}) = \{q\} = B \\
\delta_D(B, b) &= \text{E-closure}(\delta(q,b)) = \text{E-closure}(\{r\}) = \{r\} = C
\end{aligned}$$

New state: $C = \{r\}$ — **accepting**.

**Step 5 — Transitions from $C = \{r\}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_D(C, a) &= \text{E-closure}(\delta(r,a)) = \text{E-closure}(\emptyset) = \emptyset \\
\delta_D(C, b) &= \text{E-closure}(\delta(r,b)) = \text{E-closure}(\emptyset) = \emptyset
\end{aligned}$$

No new states. **Algorithm terminates.**

**Resulting DFA:**

| DFA-State | $a$ | $b$ | Accepting? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\to \{p, r\}$ | $\{q\}$ | $\emptyset$ | **Yes** |
| $\{q\}$ | $\{q\}$ | $\{r\}$ | No |
| $\{r\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | **Yes** |
| $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | No |

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation:** The $\varepsilon$-transition $p \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} r$ makes $\{p\}$ and $\{r\}$ *initially simultaneously active* — captured precisely by $A = \{p, r\}$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | DFA | NFA | $\varepsilon$-NFA |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Transition function | $Q \times \Sigma \to Q$ | $Q \times \Sigma \to 2^Q$ | $Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \to 2^Q$ |
| Deterministic? | Yes | No | No |
| $\varepsilon$-moves? | No | No | Yes |
| Typical state count | Smaller (after minimization) | Smaller (natural) | Smallest of all three |
| Conversion to DFA | Trivial | Subset construction | $\varepsilon$-closure + subset |
| Acceptance semantics | Single path | $\exists$ accepting path | $\exists$ accepting path |
| Equivalence to other models? | Yes (by Myhill–Nerode) | Yes | Yes |

### 4.2 Subset Construction Pipeline (Architectural Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input NFA N] --> B[Enumerate reachable subsets]
    B --> C{For each subset S and symbol a}
    C --> D[Compute union of delta_N on a]
    D --> E[Apply epsilon closure if needed]
    E --> F{Is target subset new?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Add to DFA state set Q_D]
    F -- No --> H[Skip]
    G --> C
    H --> C
    C --> I{All subsets processed?}
    I -- No --> C
    I -- Yes --> J[Mark subsets containing final states]
    J --> K[Output equivalent DFA D]
```

flowchart TD

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define the **subset construction** algorithm. State one situation in which a DFA obtained via subset construction may have fewer states than $2^{|Q_N|}$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **subset construction** is an algorithm that converts any NFA $N = (Q_N, \Sigma, \delta_N, q_0, F_N)$ into an equivalent DFA $D = (2^{Q_N}, \Sigma, \delta_D, \{q_0\}, F_D)$ where

$$\delta_D(S, a) = \bigcup_{q \in S} \delta_N(q,a), \quad F_D = \{S \mid S \cap F_N \neq \emptyset\}.$$

The number of reachable subsets is at most $2^{|Q_N|}$ but is typically far fewer because **only subsets actually reachable from $\{q_0\}$ via $\delta_D$ are constructed**. Many subsets are unreachable and hence discarded. **Example:** An NFA with 5 states often yields a DFA with only 8–10 reachable subsets, not the worst-case 32.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for transition rule, 1 mark for the "reachable subsets" justification.)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] What is the **$\varepsilon$-closure** of a set of states? Why is it required when converting an $\varepsilon$-NFA to a DFA?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **$\varepsilon$-closure of a state set $S$**, denoted $\text{E-closure}(S)$, is the set of all states reachable from any state in $S$ using **zero or more $\varepsilon$-transitions**, including the states of $S$ itself:

$$\text{E-closure}(S) = \{\, q \in Q \mid \exists p \in S \text{ with } p \xRightarrow{\varepsilon^*} q \,\}.$$

It is **required** during $\varepsilon$-NFA $\to$ DFA conversion because an $\varepsilon$-NFA may move from state $p$ to $q$ without consuming any input. To preserve the exact language accepted, the DFA must treat $p$ and $q$ as **simultaneously active** whenever $p$ is active. The $\varepsilon$-closure bundles all such spontaneous successors into a single DFA state, ensuring that no accepting path is missed.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mathematical notation, 1 mark for justification.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Subset Construction + Minimization

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Convert the following NFA into an equivalent DFA using the **subset construction** algorithm. Show all intermediate subsets and clearly identify the accepting states. **(7 marks)**

| State | $a$ | $b$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\to p$ | $\{p, q\}$ | $\{p\}$ |
| $q$ | $\{r\}$ | $\emptyset$ |
| $r$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{r\}$ |
| $*s$ | $\{s\}$ | $\{s\}$ |

**(b)** Minimize the resulting DFA using the **table-filling** (equivalence partitioning) algorithm. Draw the state-transition diagram of the minimum DFA. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Start subset:** $A = \{p\}$. Since $s \notin A$, $A$ is non-accepting.

**Step 2 — Transitions from $A$:**

$$\delta_D(A, a) = \delta(p,a) = \{p, q\} = B, \quad \delta_D(A, b) = \{p\} = A$$

**Step 3 — Process $B = \{p,q\}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_D(B, a) &= \delta(p,a) \cup \delta(q,a) = \{p,q\} \cup \{r\} = \{p,q,r\} = C \\
\delta_D(B, b) &= \delta(p,b) \cup \delta(q,b) = \{p\} \cup \emptyset = \{p\} = A
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Process $C = \{p,q,r\}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_D(C, a) &= \{p,q\} \cup \{r\} \cup \emptyset = \{p,q,r\} = C \\
\delta_D(C, b) &= \{p\} \cup \emptyset \cup \{r\} = \{p, r\} = D
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Process $D = \{p, r\}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_D(D, a) &= \{p,q\} \cup \emptyset = \{p,q\} = B \\
\delta_D(D, b) &= \{p\} \cup \{r\} = \{p,r\} = D
\end{aligned}$$

No new states. **Accepting states:** those containing $s$ — but $s$ never appears in any reachable subset because the NFA cannot reach $s$ from $p$. Therefore **no state is accepting**, meaning $L = \emptyset$.

| DFA-State | $a$ | $b$ | Accepting? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\to A = \{p\}$ | $B$ | $A$ | No |
| $B = \{p,q\}$ | $C$ | $A$ | No |
| $C = \{p,q,r\}$ | $C$ | $D$ | No |
| $D = \{p,r\}$ | $B$ | $D$ | No |

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Pair count:** $\frac{4 \cdot 3}{2} = 6$ pairs: $\{A,B\}, \{A,C\}, \{A,D\}, \{B,C\}, \{B,D\}, \{C,D\}$.

**Step 2 — Initial marking.** None of the states is accepting, so **no pair is initially marked**.

**Step 3 — Distinguishability propagation.** Two states are distinguishable only if some string leads one of them to an accepting state and the other to a non-accepting state. Since **all states are non-accepting** and all reachable states are also non-accepting, **no pair can ever be marked**. 

**Step 4 — Result.** All four states $A, B, C, D$ belong to a **single equivalence class** $\{A, B, C, D\}$.

**Step 5 — Minimum DFA:**

| State | $a$ | $b$ | Accepting? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\to [ABCD]$ | $[ABCD]$ | $[ABCD]$ | No |

The minimum DFA has just **one state** — a non-accepting trap. This confirms $L = \emptyset$.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in Subset Construction:**
> 1. **Forgetting to identify accepting subsets** — always check $S \cap F_N \neq \emptyset$ after every new subset is created.
> 2. **Stopping too early** — the algorithm must run until **no new subsets** appear in a complete iteration. Many students declare termination after the first pass.
> 3. **Confusing $F_D$ with $F_N$** — the final set of the DFA is **a set of subsets**, not the original NFA's final states.
> 4. **Forgetting the trap state $\emptyset$** in $\varepsilon$-NFA conversions — if no transition exists, the DFA *must* still have a deterministic transition (typically to a dead state).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — $\varepsilon$-NFA Conversion + Minimization

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Consider the $\varepsilon$-NFA below. Compute the $\varepsilon$-closure of every state, then convert it into an equivalent DFA without $\varepsilon$-transitions. **(7 marks)**

| State | $a$ | $b$ | $\varepsilon$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\to A$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{B\}$ |
| $B$ | $\{C\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ |
| $C$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{D\}$ | $\{B\}$ |
| $*D$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ |

**(b)** Minimize the DFA obtained in part (a). State the minimum number of states and draw the transition diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Compute $\varepsilon$-closures:**

- $\text{E-closure}(\{A\}) = \{A\} \cup \delta(A,\varepsilon) \cup \delta(B,\varepsilon) \cup \delta(C,\varepsilon) = \{A, B, C\}$
- $\text{E-closure}(\{B\}) = \{B\}$
- $\text{E-closure}(\{C\}) = \{C, B\} = \{B, C\}$
- $\text{E-closure}(\{D\}) = \{D\}$

**Step 2 — Start state of DFA:** $S_0 = \text{E-closure}(\{A\}) = \{A, B, C\}$. Since $D \notin S_0$, it is **non-accepting**.

**Step 3 — Transitions from $S_0 = \{A, B, C\}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_D(S_0, a) &= \text{E-closure}\big(\delta(A,a) \cup \delta(B,a) \cup \delta(C,a)\big) \\
&= \text{E-closure}(\emptyset \cup \{C\} \cup \emptyset) = \text{E-closure}(\{C\}) = \{B, C\} = S_1 \\
\delta_D(S_0, b) &= \text{E-closure}\big(\delta(A,b) \cup \delta(B,b) \cup \delta(C,b)\big) \\
&= \text{E-closure}(\emptyset \cup \emptyset \cup \{D\}) = \text{E-closure}(\{D\}) = \{D\} = S_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Transitions from $S_1 = \{B, C\}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_D(S_1, a) &= \text{E-closure}(\{C\} \cup \emptyset) = \{B, C\} = S_1 \\
\delta_D(S_1, b) &= \text{E-closure}(\emptyset \cup \{D\}) = \{D\} = S_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Transitions from $S_2 = \{D\}$ (accepting):**

$$\delta_D(S_2, a) = \text{E-closure}(\emptyset) = \emptyset = S_3, \quad \delta_D(S_2, b) = \text{E-closure}(\emptyset) = \emptyset = S_3$$

**Step 6 — Trap state $S_3 = \emptyset$:**

$$\delta_D(S_3, a) = \delta_D(S_3, b) = \emptyset = S_3$$

**Resulting DFA:**

| DFA-State | $a$ | $b$ | Accepting? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\to \{A,B,C\}$ | $\{B,C\}$ | $\{D\}$ | No |
| $\{B,C\}$ | $\{B,C\}$ | $\{D\}$ | No |
| $\{D\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | **Yes** |
| $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | No |

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Initialize table.** Pairs: $\frac{4 \cdot 3}{2} = 6$. Initial marks (one is final): $\{S_0, S_2\}, \{S_1, S_2\}, \{S_2, S_3\}$.

**Step 2 — Pass 1 on unmarked pair $\{S_0, S_1\}$:**

- On $a$: $\{S_1, S_1\}$ — trivial.
- On $b$: $\{S_2, S_2\}$ — trivial.
- No mark.

**Pass 1 on $\{S_0, S_3\}$:**

- On $a$: $\{S_1, S_3\}$ — not marked yet.
- On $b$: $\{S_2, S_3\}$ — **already marked** $\Rightarrow$ **mark $\{S_0, S_3\}$**.

**Pass 1 on $\{S_1, S_3\}$:**

- On $a$: $\{S_1, S_3\}$ — being processed (no mark yet, but we will revisit).
- On $b$: $\{S_2, S_3\}$ — marked $\Rightarrow$ **mark $\{S_1, S_3\}$**.

**Step 3 — Pass 2:** only $\{S_0, S_1\}$ remains unmarked. Recheck:

- On $a$: $\{S_1, S_1\}$ — trivial.
- On $b$: $\{S_2, S_2\}$ — trivial.
- No change. **Converged.**

**Step 4 — Equivalence classes:**

- $\{S_0, S_1\}$ (merged)
- $\{S_2\}$ alone
- $\{S_3\}$ alone

**Minimum DFA:**

| State | $a$ | $b$ | Accepting? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\to [S_0 S_1]$ | $[S_0 S_1]$ | $[S_2]$ | No |
| $[S_2]$ | $[S_3]$ | $[S_3]$ | **Yes** |
| $[S_3]$ | $[S_3]$ | $[S_3]$ | No |

Reduction: 4 states → 3 states.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes in $\varepsilon$-NFA / Minimization:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply $\varepsilon$-closure AFTER the union of $\delta$ values** — this is the most common 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting to take the $\varepsilon$-closure of the start state** — the initial DFA state is never simply $\{q_0\}$ but $\text{E-closure}(\{q_0\})$.
> 3. **Not propagating marks iteratively** in the table-filling algorithm. A pair becomes distinguishable only if its successors on *some* symbol are distinguishable — this requires **multiple passes**.
> 4. **Over-merging** — states can be merged only if no string distinguishes them; an off-by-one error here costs full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **DFA** transition function is total and single-valued: $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \to Q$.
> - **NFA** transition returns a set of states: $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \to 2^Q$.
> - **$\varepsilon$-NFA** adds spontaneous $\varepsilon$-transitions to the NFA.
> - **Subset construction** creates a DFA whose states are subsets of NFA states; final = subset intersects $F_N$.
> - **$\varepsilon$-closure** = all states reachable via $\varepsilon$-transitions (transitive closure).
> - **$\varepsilon$-NFA $\to$ DFA transition rule:** $\delta_D(S,a) = \text{E-closure}(\bigcup_{q \in S} \delta(q,a))$.
> - **Worst-case blow-up:** $|Q_D| \leq 2^{|Q_N|}$ (often not reached).
