#### 4.1.5 Concatenation: $R_1 \cdot R_2$

```mermaid
graph LR
    C1[R1 Start] -- R1 transitions --> C2[R1 Accept]
    C2 -- epsilon --> C3[R2 Start]
    C3 -- R2 transitions --> C4[R2 Accept]
```

#### 4.1.6 Kleene Star: $R_1^{*}$

```mermaid
graph LR
    K1[New Start AND New Accept] -- epsilon --> K2[R1 Start]
    K2 -- R1 transitions --> K3[R1 Accept]
    K3 -- epsilon --> K4[R1 Start]
    K3 -- epsilon --> K1
    K1 -- epsilon --> K1
```

---

### 4.2 Complete ε-NFA for $R = (a + b)^{*} a$ (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S((New Start qS))
    U((Union Start qU))
    SA((Start A))
    SB((Start B))
    FA((Final A))
    FB((Final B))
    F((Union Final qF))
    EA((End A start))
    EF((End A final))

    S -- epsilon --> U
    S -- epsilon --> S
    U -- epsilon --> SA
    U -- epsilon --> SB
    SA -- a --> FA
    SB -- b --> FB
    FA -- epsilon --> F
    FA -- epsilon --> S
    FB -- epsilon --> F
    FB -- epsilon --> S
    F -- epsilon --> S
    F -- epsilon --> EA
    EA -- a --> EF
```

**Legend for the schematic above:**

- Double-circled nodes $((\cdot))$ represent newly created states for the **outermost** layer of Thompson's construction.
- The node **S** is the actual start state.
- The node **EF** is the actual accepting state.
- All labels marked `epsilon` represent **ε-transitions** (free moves).
- All labels marked `a` or `b` are **symbol-consuming** transitions.

---

### 4.3 The Full RE → FA Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Regular Expression R] --> B[Parse Tree Generator]
    B --> C{Operator Type}
    C -- Base epsilon --> D1[Atomic NFA for epsilon]
    C -- Base symbol a --> D2[Atomic NFA for a]
    C -- Union R1 + R2 --> E1[Union NFA Builder]
    C -- Concat R1 R2 --> E2[Concat NFA Builder]
    C -- Star R1 star --> E3[Star NFA Builder]
    D1 --> F[Compose epsilon NFA]
    D2 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Apply Subset Construction]
    G --> H[Resulting DFA]
    H --> I[Apply DFA Minimization Hopcroft]
    I --> J[Minimal DFA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world use case:** The **Lex/Flex lexical analyzer generator** implements this exact pipeline. It takes REs as input and emits optimized C code for a DFA-based scanner.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State Kleene's Theorem with respect to the equivalence of regular expressions and finite automata.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Kleene's Theorem establishes a fundamental equivalence in formal language theory. It states that a language is regular **if and only if** it can be described by a regular expression. Equivalently, for any regular expression **R**, there exists a finite automaton **M** (either NFA or DFA) such that $L(M) = L(R)$, and conversely, for any FA **M**, there exists a regular expression **R** such that $L(R) = L(M)$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Write the base cases used in Thompson's construction for converting a regular expression to an ε-NFA.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
There are three base cases in Thompson's construction:
1. **Empty set ($\emptyset$):** The NFA consists of a single start state with no transitions and no accepting state. **(1 mark)**
2. **Epsilon ($\varepsilon$):** The NFA consists of a single state which is both the start state and the accepting state, with no transitions. **(1 mark)**
3. **Single symbol ($a \in \Sigma$):** The NFA consists of two states — a start state and an accepting state — connected by a single transition labeled $a$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Construct an ε-NFA for the regular expression $R = (a + b)^{*} a$ using **Thompson's Construction**. Label all states and transitions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Convert the obtained ε-NFA into an equivalent DFA using the **Subset Construction** algorithm. Show all reachable states only. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) – 7 Marks:**

We apply Thompson's construction in five layers:

**Layer 1 – Atomic NFAs for $a$ and $b$:** Two NFAs, each with 2 states. (1 mark)

**Layer 2 – Union $a + b$:** Add new start state $q_0$ and new accepting state $q_f$. Add ε-transitions $q_0 \to s_A$, $q_0 \to s_B$, $f_A \to q_f$, $f_B \to q_f$. (1 mark)

**Layer 3 – Kleene Star $(a+b)^{*}$:** Add new start state $q_S$ (also new accept). Add ε-transitions: $q_S \to q_0$, $q_S \to q_S$ (self-loop), $q_f \to q_0$, $q_f \to q_S$. (2 marks)

**Layer 4 – Atomic NFA for trailing $a$:** 2 more states. (1 mark)

**Layer 5 – Concatenation:** Add ε-transition from $q_S$ (current accept) to the new NFA's start state. Convert $q_S$ to a non-accepting state. (2 marks)

**Final Result:** 10-state ε-NFA. Start state = $q_S^{outer}$. Accepting state = the final state of the trailing $a$-NFA.

---

**Model Solution for (b) – 7 Marks:**

Apply subset construction. Let $q_0^{out}$ be the outer start state. Compute ε-closure first.

| DFA State | Composition | On 'a' | On 'b' |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A$ | $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(q_0^{out}) = \{q_0^{out}, q_S, q_0, s_A, s_B\}$ | $B$ | $C$ |
| $B$ | $\varepsilon\text{-closure of } A \text{ on } a = \{f_A, q_f, q_S, q_0, s_A, s_B, q_0^{out}, s_{a_2}\}$ (contains accept) | $B$ | $C$ |
| $C$ | $\varepsilon\text{-closure of } A \text{ on } b = \{f_B, q_f, q_S, q_0, s_A, s_B, q_0^{out}\}$ | $B$ | $C$ |

**Final DFA:** 3 states $A, B, C$. Start state = $A$. Accepting states = $\{B\}$. (7 marks distributed for table, final answer, and verification.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Construct an ε-NFA for the regular expression $R = (a + b)^{*} abb$ using Thompson's Construction. Show each construction step separately. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the formal definition of Kleene's Theorem Part II and explain why Thompson's construction is a valid proof of this theorem. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) – 7 Marks:**

Step 1: Build 5 atomic NFAs for $a, b, a, b, b$. (1 mark)
Step 2: Union the first $a$ and $b$ → $(a+b)$ NFA (6 states). (1 mark)
Step 3: Apply Kleene Star → $(a+b)^{*}$ NFA (8 states). (2 marks)
Step 4: Concatenate the three trailing symbols $a, b, b$ in sequence (no new states added). (3 marks)
**Final ε-NFA:** 14 states. Clearly label start and accept states.

---

**Model Solution for (b) – 7 Marks:**

**Kleene's Theorem Part II (Formal Statement):** If $R$ is a regular expression over alphabet $\Sigma$, then there exists a finite automaton $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ such that $L(M) = L(R)$. (2 marks)

**Why Thompson's Construction is a Valid Proof:**
Thompson's construction proceeds by **structural induction** on the regular expression $R$.
- **Base cases:** For $\emptyset, \varepsilon, a$ — explicit NFAs are constructed whose accepted languages equal $L(\emptyset), L(\varepsilon), L(a)$. (2 marks)
- **Inductive step:** Assume the theorem holds for $R_1$ and $R_2$. The construction defines NFA($R_1+R_2$), NFA($R_1 \cdot R_2$), and NFA($R_1^{*}$) in terms of NFA($R_1$) and NFA($R_2$), and it can be formally verified that $L(\text{NFA}(R_1+R_2)) = L(R_1) \cup L(R_2)$, and similarly for concatenation and star. (2 marks)
- **Conclusion:** By the principle of mathematical induction, the theorem holds for all regular expressions. (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert intermediate accepting states to non-accepting states** during concatenation — KTU deducts up to **2 marks**.
> 2. **Drawing the Kleene Star incorrectly** by missing either the self-loop or the back-edge from old-final to old-start — costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Not labeling the start and accepting states explicitly** in the final diagram — instant **1 mark deduction**.
> 4. **Confusing $+$ (Union) with $\cdot$ (Concatenation)** in the parse tree — loses structural marks.
> 5. **In subset construction, forgetting to compute ε-closure** before evaluating transitions on input symbols — **3-mark penalty** in Part B.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Kleene's Theorem Part II** is the theoretical foundation: *Every RE has an equivalent FA*.
- [x] **Thompson's Construction** is a **structural induction** algorithm that produces an **ε-NFA** from any regular expression.
- [x] **Three Base Cases:** $\emptyset \to$ 1 state (no accept), $\varepsilon \to$ 1 state (accept), $a \to$ 2 states.
- [x] **Three Inductive Cases:** Union (+), Concatenation (·), Kleene Star (*).
- [x] **State count formula** for an RE with $n$ symbols and $k$ operators from $\{+, *\}$: the resulting ε-NFA has exactly $2n + 2k$ states (concatenation is "free" — it adds zero states).
- [x] **ε-transitions** are the "glue" used to connect sub-NFAs without consuming input.
- [x] **Subset Construction** (a.k.a. powerset construction) converts the ε-NFA to a DFA; it can produce up to $2^{|Q|}$ states in the worst case.
- [x] **DFA Minimization** (Hopcroft's algorithm) further reduces the DFA to a **Minimal DFA** with the fewest possible states — always do this as a final step in KTU answers.
- [x] **Practical Application:** Lex/Flex, network IDS engines, search engines — all use this exact pipeline.
- [x] **Operator Precedence:** $*$ (highest) > $\cdot$ (concatenation) > $+$ (union) — memorize this for parse tree questions.
- [x] **Common KTU trap:** "Construct the NFA" usually expects the **ε-NFA** from Thompson's method unless explicitly asked for a DFA.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regular Languages, Properties of Regular Languages: Closure and Decision Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regular Languages, Properties of Regular Languages: Closure and Decision Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Regular Language — The Foundation

A language $L$ is called a **Regular Language** if and only if it can be described by any of the following equivalent formalisms:

1. A **Regular Expression** (RE) over some alphabet $\Sigma$.
2. A **Deterministic Finite Automaton** (DFA).
3. A **Non-Deterministic Finite Automaton** (NFA), with or without $\varepsilon$-transitions.
4. A **Right-Linear Grammar** (or Left-Linear Grammar).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Kleene's Theorem):** The equivalence "RE $\equiv$ DFA $\equiv$ NFA $\equiv$ Linear Grammar" is the central result of Module 1 and is the launching pad for Module 2. Whenever we study properties of regular languages, we are free to **pick whichever model is most convenient** for the proof.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncer" Model

Imagine a nightclub with a strictly defined door policy. The **bouncer (DFA)** holds a small notebook with a finite number of states (e.g., "seen one 'a'", "seen two 'a's", "ready to check 'b'"). He reads each character of the customer's ID (input string) one at a time, and at the very end declares **ACCEPT** or **REJECT**. Because the bouncer has **no memory** beyond his fixed states, only a restricted class of "behaviour patterns" (i.e., regular languages) can be recognized. The properties of regular languages tell us **what we can build** by combining such bouncers.

## 1.2 Closure Properties — The "Algebraic Toolkit"

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A class of languages $\mathcal{C}$ is **closed** under an operation $\circ$ if, for every pair of languages $L_1, L_2 \in \mathcal{C}$, the resulting language $L_1 \circ L_2$ is **also in $\mathcal{C}$**.

In plain words: if you take two regular languages and combine them using a permitted operation, the result must **still be regular**. The set of regular languages $\mathcal{R}$ is closed under a rich family of operations — this is what makes them so algebraically friendly.

### Operations We Will Study

| Operation | Notation | Closure? |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| Union | $L_1 \cup L_2$ | ✅ Yes |
| Intersection | $L_1 \cap L_2$ | ✅ Yes |
| Complement | $\overline{L}$ | ✅ Yes |
| Concatenation | $L_1 L_2$ | ✅ Yes |
| Kleene Star | $L^{*}$ | ✅ Yes |
| Reversal | $L^{R}$ | ✅ Yes |
| Difference | $L_1 - L_2$ | ✅ Yes |
| Homomorphism | $h(L)$ | ✅ Yes |
| Inverse Homomorphism | $h^{-1}(L)$ | ✅ Yes |
| Substitution | $s(L)$ | ✅ Yes |

### Conceptual Analogy — "Lego Blocks of Languages"

Think of regular languages as **Lego blocks of the same shape and size**. The closure property says: *no matter how you snap them together (union, intersection, star, ...), the resulting block still fits in the same Lego box (i.e., is still regular)*. The moment a combination produces a non-Lego-shaped object, the class is not closed.

## 1.3 Decision Properties — The "Yes/No Question" Toolkit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A **decision problem** for a class of languages $\mathcal{C}$ asks a yes/no question about a *description* (DFA, NFA, RE) of a language. A decision property **holds** if there exists an algorithm that always terminates and returns the correct answer.

For regular languages, the most important decision problems are:

| Decision Problem | Question | Algorithm Exists? |
|------------------|----------|-------------------|
| Emptiness | Is $L(A) = \emptyset$? | ✅ Yes (reachability of accept state) |
| Finiteness | Is $L(A)$ finite? | ✅ Yes (cycle detection on accept states) |
| Membership | Is $w \in L(A)$? | ✅ Yes (simulate DFA on $w$) |
| Equivalence | Is $L(A_1) = L(A_2)$? | ✅ Yes (product DFA + emptiness test) |
| Subset | Is $L(A_1) \subseteq L(A_2)$? | ✅ Yes (emptiness of $L(A_1) \cap \overline{L(A_2)}$) |
| Totality | Is $L(A) = \Sigma^{*}$? | ✅ Yes (complement + emptiness) |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Quality Inspector"

A factory inspector (algorithm) is given a blueprint (DFA) and asked a question. The closure properties tell us *what we can manufacture*. The decision properties tell us *what we can quickly verify about what we manufactured*. Regular languages are doubly blessed: **they can be combined freely AND efficiently tested**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Closure Properties Matter in KTU Exams

Closure properties are not abstract trivia. They are the **fastest way to prove regularity or non-regularity** of a new language, often avoiding a full pumping-lemma or Myhill–Nerode argument.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Heuristic:** If asked *"Is $L$ regular?"*, FIRST try to express $L$ as a Boolean/regex combination of known regular languages. If you can, the answer is **YES — by closure**. Only fall back to pumping-lemma / Myhill–Nerode for a **NO** answer.

## 2.2 The "Proof Strategy" Cheat Code

For each closure property, the proof follows one of three patterns:

**Pattern A — Product Construction (for Union, Intersection)**
Given DFAs $A_1 = (Q_1, \Sigma, \delta_1, q_{0,1}, F_1)$ and $A_2 = (Q_2, \Sigma, \delta_2, q_{0,2}, F_2)$, construct a DFA $A = (Q_1 \times Q_2, \Sigma, \delta, (q_{0,1}, q_{0,2}), F)$ where
$$\delta((p,q), a) = (\delta_1(p,a),\, \delta_2(q,a))$$

**Pattern B — Complement the Final States (for Complement)**
Given DFA $A = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ accepting $L$, construct $A^{c} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, Q \setminus F)$ accepting $\overline{L}$.

**Pattern C — Epsilon Removal + Subset Construction (for NFA tricks)**
Convert NFA to DFA, then apply Pattern A or B.

## 2.3 Closure Property Statements (Rigorous Form)

Let $L_1, L_2 \subseteq \Sigma^{*}$ be regular languages. Then the following languages are also regular:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 \cup L_2 &= \{ w \mid w \in L_1 \text{ or } w \in L_2 \} \\[4pt]
L_1 \cap L_2 &= \{ w \mid w \in L_1 \text{ and } w \in L_2 \} \\[4pt]
\overline{L_1} &= \Sigma^{*} \setminus L_1 \\[4pt]
L_1 L_2 &= \{ xy \mid x \in L_1 \text{ and } y \in L_2 \} \\[4pt]
L_1^{*} &= \bigcup_{i=0}^{\infty} L_1^{i} \\[4pt]
L_1^{R} &= \{ w^{R} \mid w \in L_1 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.4 Decision Property Algorithms (Complexity Snapshot)

| Property | Input | Algorithm | Time Complexity |
|----------|-------|-----------|-----------------|
| Membership $w \in L(A)$ | DFA $A$, string $w$ of length $n$ | Simulate $A$ on $w$ | $O(n)$ |
| Emptiness $L(A) = \emptyset$ | DFA $A$ with $n$ states | BFS/DFS from $q_0$ to any $f \in F$ | $O(n \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ |
| Finiteness $L(A)$ finite? | DFA $A$ with $n$ states | Check if any accept state is on a cycle reachable from $q_0$ | $O(n \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ |
| Equivalence $L(A_1) = L(A_2)$ | DFAs $A_1, A_2$ | Build product DFA; test emptiness of symmetric difference | $O(n_1 \cdot n_2 \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ |
| Subset $L(A_1) \subseteq L(A_2)$ | DFAs $A_1, A_2$ | Test emptiness of $L(A_1) \cap \overline{L(A_2)}$ | $O(n_1 \cdot n_2 \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Theorem Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master the items in this table — they appear in nearly every KTU past paper for this module.

| # | Item | Statement | Proof Tool |
|---|------|-----------|------------|
| 1 | Union Closure | $L_1, L_2 \in \mathcal{R} \Rightarrow L_1 \cup L_2 \in \mathcal{R}$ | Product DFA with $F = F_1 \times Q_2 \cup Q_1 \times F_2$ |
| 2 | Intersection Closure | $L_1, L_2 \in \mathcal{R} \Rightarrow L_1 \cap L_2 \in \mathcal{R}$ | Product DFA with $F = F_1 \times F_2$ |
| 3 | Complement Closure | $L \in \mathcal{R} \Rightarrow \overline{L} \in \mathcal{R}$ | Swap accept and non-accept states (DFA only) |
| 4 | Concatenation Closure | $L_1, L_2 \in \mathcal{R} \Rightarrow L_1 L_2 \in \mathcal{R}$ | Thompson-style NFA with $\varepsilon$-jumps |
| 5 | Kleene Star Closure | $L \in \mathcal{R} \Rightarrow L^{*} \in \mathcal{R}$ | Add new start state $\in F$, $\varepsilon$-loops |
| 6 | Reversal Closure | $L \in \mathcal{R} \Rightarrow L^{R} \in \mathcal{R}$ | Reverse all edges of NFA, swap start/accept |
| 7 | Homomorphism Closure | $L \in \mathcal{R} \Rightarrow h(L) \in \mathcal{R}$ | Apply $h$ symbol-wise to every transition |
| 8 | Inverse Homomorphism | $L \in \mathcal{R} \Rightarrow h^{-1}(L) \in \mathcal{R}$ | NFA on $\Sigma$ that reads $a$ and "shoots" $h(a)$ into $A$ |
| 9 | Membership Decision | $w \in L(A)$ is decidable | DFA simulation in $O(\vert w \vert)$ |
| 10 | Emptiness Decision | $L(A) = \emptyset$ is decidable | Reachability test in $O(\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ |
| 11 | Equivalence Decision | $L(A_1) = L(A_2)$ is decidable | Test $L(A_1) \triangle L(A_2) = \emptyset$ |
| 12 | Finiteness Decision | $L(A)$ is finite iff no accept state lies on a reachable cycle | Graph-theoretic cycle detection |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

In real engineering, these properties underpin:

- **Lexical analysers** in compilers: token classes are closed under union and Kleene star, so the lexer can be composed of tiny regular sub-expressions.
- **Pattern-matching engines** (`grep`, `sed`, regex libraries): rely on the fact that homomorphic images of regular languages remain regular — that is why case-insensitive search is a one-line operation.
- **Model checking**: the equivalence test $L(A_1) = L(A_2)$ is the backbone of LTL model checking for finite-state systems.
- **Network firewalls & DPI (Deep Packet Inspection)**: regular-expression matchers decide membership in $O(n)$ per packet.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

In this section we work out the **proofs** of the most important closure and decision properties at a level suitable for full 7/14-mark KTU solutions.

---

## 3.1 Theorem — Closure Under Union

**Statement.** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular languages over $\Sigma$, then $L_1 \cup L_2$ is regular.

**Proof.** Since $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular, there exist DFAs
$$A_1 = (Q_1, \Sigma, \delta_1, q_{0,1}, F_1) \quad \text{and} \quad A_2 = (Q_2, \Sigma, \delta_2, q_{0,2}, F_2)$$
such that $L(A_1) = L_1$ and $L(A_2) = L_2$.

Construct a new DFA
$$A = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$
where each component is defined as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= Q_1 \times Q_2 \\[4pt]
q_0 &= (q_{0,1},\, q_{0,2}) \\[4pt]
\delta\bigl((p,q), a\bigr) &= \bigl(\delta_1(p, a),\, \delta_2(q, a)\bigr) \quad \text{for all } a \in \Sigma \\[4pt]
F &= \{(p, q) \mid p \in F_1 \text{ or } q \in F_2\} \;=\; (F_1 \times Q_2) \cup (Q_1 \times F_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

We claim $L(A) = L_1 \cup L_2$. By induction on $\vert w \vert = n$:

- **Base case ($n = 0$):** $w = \varepsilon$. Then $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \varepsilon) = q_0 = (q_{0,1}, q_{0,2})$. We have $\varepsilon \in L(A) \iff q_0 \in F \iff q_{0,1} \in F_1 \text{ or } q_{0,2} \in F_2 \iff \varepsilon \in L_1 \cup L_2$. ✅
- **Inductive step:** Assume the claim for strings of length $n$. Let $w = xa$ with $\vert x \vert = n$, $a \in \Sigma$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\delta}(q_0, xa) &= \delta\bigl(\hat{\delta}(q_0, x),\, a\bigr) \\
&= \delta\bigl((\hat{\delta}_1(q_{0,1}, x),\, \hat{\delta}_2(q_{0,2}, x)),\, a\bigr) \quad \text{(induction hypothesis)} \\
&= \bigl(\delta_1(\hat{\delta}_1(q_{0,1}, x), a),\, \delta_2(\hat{\delta}_2(q_{0,2}, x), a)\bigr) \\
&= \bigl(\hat{\delta}_1(q_{0,1}, xa),\, \hat{\delta}_2(q_{0,2}, xa)\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $xa \in L(A) \iff \hat{\delta}(q_0, xa) \in F \iff \hat{\delta}_1(q_{0,1}, xa) \in F_1 \text{ or } \hat{\delta}_2(q_{0,2}, xa) \in F_2 \iff xa \in L_1 \cup L_2$. ✅

Hence $L(A) = L_1 \cup L_2$, and the language is regular. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Theorem — Closure Under Complement

**Statement.** If $L$ is a regular language, then $\overline{L} = \Sigma^{*} \setminus L$ is also regular.

**Proof.** Since $L$ is regular, there exists a DFA
$$A = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$
with $L(A) = L$. Construct
$$A^{c} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, Q \setminus F)$$
which has the **same transition function and start state** as $A$, but its set of accepting states is complemented.

For any $w \in \Sigma^{*}$, by the deterministic simulation invariant:
$$w \in L(A^{c}) \iff \hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \in Q \setminus F \iff \hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \notin F \iff w \notin L(A) \iff w \in \overline{L}.$$

Hence $L(A^{c}) = \overline{L}$, so $\overline{L}$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** This theorem is valid for **DFAs only**, not NFAs! An NFA may have a non-deterministic path that accepts $w$ and another that rejects it, so simply swapping the accept set is **incorrect** for NFAs. Always NFA $\to$ DFA first.

---

## 3.3 Theorem — Closure Under Intersection (via De Morgan)

**Statement.** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular, then $L_1 \cap L_2$ is regular.

**Proof (De Morgan route).** Using the complements of regular languages:
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 \cap L_2 &= \overline{\,\overline{L_1} \cup \overline{L_2}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$
By the closure under union, $\overline{L_1} \cup \overline{L_2}$ is regular. Applying closure under complement, its complement is regular. Hence $L_1 \cap L_2$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

**Alternative (Direct product).** Same as Theorem 3.1 but with $F = F_1 \times F_2$ (instead of the union). The inductive proof is identical.

---

## 3.4 Theorem — Closure Under Concatenation

**Statement.** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular, then $L_1 L_2 = \{xy \mid x \in L_1, y \in L_2\}$ is regular.

**Proof.** Let $A_1 = (Q_1, \Sigma, \delta_1, q_{0,1}, F_1)$ and $A_2 = (Q_2, \Sigma, \delta_2, q_{0,2}, F_2)$ recognize $L_1$ and $L_2$. We construct an NFA-with-$\varepsilon$ (Thompson-style):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F) \quad \text{where} \\[4pt]
Q &= Q_1 \cup Q_2 \\[4pt]
q_0 &= q_{0,1} \\[4pt]
F &= F_2 \\[4pt]
\delta(q, a) &= 
\begin{cases}
\delta_1(q, a) & \text{if } q \in Q_1 \setminus F_1 \\
\delta_1(q, a) & \text{if } q \in F_1, a \in \Sigma \text{ (stay in } Q_1\text{)} \\
\delta_1(q, a) \cup \{q_{0,2}\} & \text{if } q \in F_1, a = \varepsilon \text{ (jump to start of } A_2\text{)} \\
\delta_2(q, a) & \text{if } q \in Q_2
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Intuition:** Simulate $A_1$ until an accept state is reached. Non-deterministically guess the split point, take an $\varepsilon$-transition to the start of $A_2$, and complete the simulation. The NFA accepts $w$ iff some split $w = xy$ satisfies $x \in L_1$ and $y \in L_2$, i.e. $w \in L_1 L_2$.

Since every NFA is equivalent to a DFA (by subset construction), $L_1 L_2$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Theorem — Closure Under Kleene Star

**Statement.** If $L$ is regular, then $L^{*} = \bigcup_{i=0}^{\infty} L^{i}$ is regular.

**Proof.** Let $A = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ accept $L$. Construct the NFA $A^{*}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A^{*} &= (Q \cup \{q_s\}, \Sigma, \delta^{*}, q_s, F \cup \{q_s\}) \quad \text{where} \\[4pt]
\delta^{*}(q, a) &=
\begin{cases}
\{q_s\} \cup \delta(q, a) & \text{if } q = q_0, a = \varepsilon \\
\delta(q, a) & \text{if } q \in Q \setminus F, a \in \Sigma \\
\delta(q, a) \cup \{q_0\} & \text{if } q \in F, a = \varepsilon
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Intuition:** The new start state $q_s$ is **also accepting** (so $\varepsilon$ is accepted). From $q_s$ we can $\varepsilon$-jump to $q_0$ to begin a copy of $L$. After completing a copy (reaching any $f \in F$), we may either halt (accept) or take $\varepsilon$ back to $q_0$ to begin another copy. This precisely captures $L^{*}$.

Since NFAs $\equiv$ DFAs, $L^{*}$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.6 Theorem — Closure Under Reversal

**Statement.** If $L$ is regular, then $L^{R} = \{w^{R} \mid w \in L\}$ is regular.

**Proof.** Since $L$ is regular, there exists an NFA $A = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ with $L(A) = L$. We use the fact that **every regular language is also accepted by an NFA** (a stronger claim than DFA, since DFAs are a special case of NFAs). Construct:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A^{R} &= (Q, \Sigma, \delta^{R}, q_0^{R}, F^{R}) \quad \text{where} \\[4pt]
q_0^{R} &= \text{any single new state} \\
F^{R} &= \{q_0\} \quad \text{(original start becomes the only accept)} \\
\delta^{R}(p, a) &= \{q \in Q \mid p \in \delta(q, a)\} \quad \text{(reverse all edges)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why it works:** A path $q_0 \xrightarrow{a_1} q_1 \xrightarrow{a_2} q_2 \cdots \xrightarrow{a_n} q_n$ with $q_n \in F$ in $A$ is read forwards. Reversing the string means reading $a_n a_{n-1} \cdots a_1$. In $A^{R}$, we start at $q_0$ and follow reversed edges backwards, ending at the original $q_0$, which is now the accepting state. The string read is exactly $a_n a_{n-1} \cdots a_1 = w^{R}$. Hence $L(A^{R}) = L^{R}$. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative (Regex route):** Reverse every string in the regex by:
> 1. Reversing every literal.
> 2. Swapping the order of concatenation.
> 3. Keeping $\cup$ and $^{*}$ as-is.
> This is a great exam-time trick.

---

## 3.7 Decision Property — Emptiness

**Algorithm.** To decide if $L(A) = \emptyset$ for DFA $A$:

1. Run DFS/BFS from $q_0$.
2. If at least one accepting state $f \in F$ is reachable, return **NO** (language is non-empty).
3. Otherwise, return **YES** (language is empty).

**Correctness.** $L(A) = \emptyset$ iff no accept state is reachable from $q_0$ — direct consequence of the definition of DFA acceptance. **Time complexity:** $O(\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ via BFS.

---

## 3.8 Decision Property — Finiteness

**Theorem.** $L(A)$ is finite if and only if **no accepting state lies on a directed cycle** that is reachable from $q_0$.

**Algorithm.**
1. Find the set $R$ of states reachable from $q_0$ (BFS/DFS).
2. Restrict the DFA to $R$.
3. Run cycle detection (e.g., DFS with "on-stack" marker) restricted to the subgraph.
4. If any cycle contains a state $f \in F$, return **NO** (language is infinite).
5. Otherwise, return **YES** (language is finite).

**Why?** If an accept state is on a cycle, we can loop around the cycle arbitrarily many times, producing infinitely many distinct accepted strings. Conversely, if no accept state is on a cycle, every accepted string follows a path of bounded length, so only finitely many exist. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.9 Decision Property — Equivalence

**Theorem.** For DFAs $A_1$ and $A_2$ with $L(A_1) = L_1$ and $L(A_2) = L_2$:
$$L_1 = L_2 \iff (L_1 \cap \overline{L_2}) \cup (\overline{L_1} \cap L_2) = \emptyset$$
The expression on the right is the **symmetric difference** $L_1 \triangle L_2$.

**Algorithm.**
1. Construct $\overline{A_2}$ from $A_2$ (swap final states).
2. Build the product DFA $B$ for $A_1 \times \overline{A_2}$ with $F_B = F_1 \times (Q_2 \setminus F_2)$.
3. Similarly build $C$ for $\overline{A_1} \times A_2$.
4. Build the product DFA $D$ for the union of $B$ and $C$.
5. Run the **emptiness test** on $D$. If empty, $L_1 = L_2$.

**Time complexity:** $O(\vert Q_1 \vert \cdot \vert Q_2 \vert \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Big Picture — The Two Halves of Module 2

The Mermaid diagram below shows the logical relationship between closure and decision properties. The two halves are connected by a crucial bridge: **decision properties are often reduced to the emptiness test using closure properties**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Regular Language L"] --> B0["Closure Properties"]
    A0 --> C0["Decision Properties"]
    B0 --> B1["Algebraic Combinations"]
    B1 --> B2["Union / Intersection"]
    B1 --> B3["Complement / Difference"]
    B1 --> B4["Kleene Star / Concat"]
    B1 --> B5["Reversal / Homomorphism"]

    C1 --> C2["Emptiness Test"]
    C1 --> C3["Membership Test"]
    C1 --> C4["Equivalence Test"]

    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
    D1 --> E1["Symmetric Difference Empty => Equivalent"]
```

## 4.2 Product DFA Construction (For Union / Intersection)

This block diagram shows how two DFAs are combined into a single product DFA. The state of the product is a **pair** tracking both machines simultaneously.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1["DFA A1 (recognises L1)"]
        S1A["q0_1"]
        S2A["p1"]
        S3A["p2"]
        S1A -->|"a"| S2A
        S2A -->|"b"| S3A
    end

    subgraph A2["DFA A2 (recognises L2)"]
        S1B["q0_2"]
        S2B["r1"]
        S3B["r2"]
        S1B -->|"a"| S2B
        S2B -->|"b"| S3B
    end

    subgraph PROD["Product DFA A = A1 x A2"]
        P0["(q0_1, q0_2)"]
        P1["(p1, r1)"]
        P2["(p2, r2)"]
        P0 -->|"a"| P1
        P1 -->|"b"| P2
    end

    A1 -.feeds state.-> PROD
    A2 -.feeds state.-> PROD
```

## 4.3 Algorithm Flow — Decision Property "Equivalence Test"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Input: DFAs A1 and A2"] --> S1["Compute complement of A2"]
    S1 --> S2["Build product DFA: A1 x complement A2"]
    S2 --> S3["Compute complement of A1"]
    S3 --> S4["Build product DFA: complement A1 x A2"]
    S4 --> S5["Build union DFA of the two products"]
    S5 --> S6["Run Emptiness Test on the union DFA"]
    S6 --> DECIDE{"Is the union DFA empty?"}
    DECIDE -->|"YES"| OUT1["A1 and A2 are EQUIVALENT"]
    DECIDE -->|"NO"| OUT2["A1 and A2 are NOT EQUIVALENT"]
```

## 4.4 Closure Property Decision Table — Visual

```mermaid

    R["Class of Regular Languages R"] --> OP1["Union"]
    R --> OP2["Intersection"]
    R --> OP3["Complement"]
    R --> OP4["Concatenation"]

```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: 3-mark short answers (Part A) and 14-mark long answers with internal choice (Part B). Bloom's levels: Apply, Analyse, Evaluate.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define closure property. State whether the class of regular languages is closed under complement with justification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A class $\mathcal{C}$ of languages is **closed** under an operation $\circ$ if for every $L_1, L_2 \in \mathcal{C}$, the language $L_1 \circ L_2$ is also in $\mathcal{C}$. (1 mark)
- Yes, regular languages are **closed under complement**. (1 mark)
- Justification: If $A = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ is a DFA accepting $L$, then $A^{c} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, Q \setminus F)$ accepts $\overline{L}$. Hence $\overline{L}$ is regular. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the pumping lemma for regular languages. Why is it useful as a decision property?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement:** If $L$ is regular, then $\exists$ a constant $n$ (pumping length) such that every string $w \in L$ with $\vert w \vert \ge n$ can be written as $w = xyz$ satisfying: (i) $\vert y \vert \ge 1$, (ii) $\vert xy \vert \le n$, (iii) $xy^{k}z \in L$ for all $k \ge 0$. (2 marks)
- **Use as decision property:** Pumping lemma provides a **necessary condition** for regularity. It is used to prove that certain languages are **not regular** (e.g., $\{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \ge 0\}$) by contradiction. (1 mark)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **ONE** of Question A or Question B.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Prove that the class of regular languages is closed under **union** and **intersection**. Show the construction clearly with diagrams and state the formal theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove that regular languages are closed under **Kleene star** and **reversal**. Justify every step. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Closure under Union (4 marks) + Intersection (3 marks):**

**Theorem (Union):** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular, then $L_1 \cup L_2$ is regular.

**Proof.** Let $A_1 = (Q_1, \Sigma, \delta_1, q_{0,1}, F_1)$ and $A_2 = (Q_2, \Sigma, \delta_2, q_{0,2}, F_2)$ be DFAs recognising $L_1$ and $L_2$. Construct

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= (Q_1 \times Q_2,\ \Sigma,\ \delta,\ (q_{0,1}, q_{0,2}),\ F) \quad \text{where} \\[4pt]
\delta\bigl((p,q), a\bigr) &= \bigl(\delta_1(p, a),\ \delta_2(q, a)\bigr) \\[4pt]
F &= (F_1 \times Q_2) \cup (Q_1 \times F_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

By induction on $\vert w \vert$, the state reached from $w$ is $(\hat{\delta}_1(q_{0,1}, w), \hat{\delta}_2(q_{0,2}, w))$. Then
$$w \in L(A) \iff \hat{\delta}_1(q_{0,1}, w) \in F_1 \text{ or } \hat{\delta}_2(q_{0,2}, w) \in F_2 \iff w \in L_1 \cup L_2.$$
Hence $L_1 \cup L_2$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

**Theorem (Intersection):** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular, then $L_1 \cap L_2$ is regular.

**Proof (via De Morgan).** Since $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular, $\overline{L_1}$ and $\overline{L_2}$ are regular (by complement closure, proven in Part (a) of typical KTU solutions). The union $\overline{L_1} \cup \overline{L_2}$ is regular (by the union closure just proven). Hence its complement, $\overline{\,\overline{L_1} \cup \overline{L_2}\,} = L_1 \cap L_2$, is regular. $\blacksquare$

**Direct product alternative:** Same construction as union but with $F = F_1 \times F_2$.

---

**(b) Kleene Star (3.5 marks) + Reversal (3.5 marks):**

**Theorem (Kleene Star):** If $L$ is regular, then $L^{*}$ is regular.

**Proof.** Let $A = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ accept $L$. Construct an NFA $A^{*} = (Q \cup \{q_s\}, \Sigma, \delta^{*}, q_s, F \cup \{q_s\})$ with transitions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta^{*}(q_s, \varepsilon) &= \{q_0\} \\
\delta^{*}(q, a) &= \delta(q, a) \quad \text{for } q \in Q \setminus F, a \in \Sigma \\
\delta^{*}(f, \varepsilon) &= \{q_0\} \quad \text{for } f \in F
\end{aligned}
$$

The new start state $q_s$ is accepting (so $\varepsilon \in L^{*}$). After completing a copy of $L$ (reaching some $f \in F$), the NFA can either halt (accept the current concatenation) or take $\varepsilon$ back to $q_0$ to start another copy. This nondeterministically generates any string $w_1 w_2 \cdots w_k$ with each $w_i \in L$. Since every NFA is equivalent to a DFA, $L^{*}$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

**Theorem (Reversal):** If $L$ is regular, then $L^{R}$ is regular.

**Proof.** Let $A = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ be an NFA accepting $L$. Construct $A^{R} = (Q \cup \{q_{new}\}, \Sigma, \delta^{R}, q_{new}, \{q_0\})$ where
$$\delta^{R}(p, a) = \{q \in Q \mid p \in \delta(q, a)\}$$
(i.e., reverse all edges of $A$), and a new start state $q_{new}$ with $\delta^{R}(q_{new}, a) = \delta^{R}(q_0, a)$ (or simply rename $q_0$ as both start and reverse-accept target).

Any path $q_0 \xrightarrow{w} f$ in $A$ corresponds to a path $f \xrightarrow{w^{R}} q_0$ in $A^{R}$. Since $q_0$ is the only accept state of $A^{R}$, $A^{R}$ accepts exactly $L^{R}$. Hence $L^{R}$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** State and prove the **closure under complement** property for regular languages. State one application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **decision properties** of regular languages. Provide algorithms (with time complexity) for testing **(i) emptiness**, **(ii) finiteness**, and **(iii) equivalence** of two regular languages. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Closure Under Complement (7 marks):**

**Statement:** If $L \subseteq \Sigma^{*}$ is regular, then $\overline{L} = \Sigma^{*} \setminus L$ is also regular.

**Proof.** Since $L$ is regular, by Kleene's theorem there exists a DFA $A = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ such that $L(A) = L$. Construct
$$A^{c} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, Q \setminus F)$$
with the same states, alphabet, transition function, and start state as $A$, but with the set of accepting states replaced by $Q \setminus F$.

For any $w \in \Sigma^{*}$, by the deterministic simulation invariant:
$$w \in L(A^{c}) \iff \hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \in Q \setminus F \iff w \notin L(A) \iff w \in \overline{L}.$$
Therefore $L(A^{c}) = \overline{L}$ and $\overline{L}$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

**Application (1 mark):** The complement property is used in the **equivalence test**: $L_1 = L_2$ iff $(L_1 \cap \overline{L_2}) \cup (\overline{L_1} \cap L_2) = \emptyset$. Without complement closure, this elegant reduction to the emptiness test would not be possible.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often try to apply complement closure to an **NFA** directly. This is **WRONG** — the result holds only for **DFAs**. An NFA may have multiple paths on the same string, some accepting and some not. Always convert NFA $\to$ DFA via subset construction **first**.

---

**(b) Decision Properties (7 marks — 2 + 2 + 3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A decision problem asks a yes/no question about the description of a language. The class of regular languages is **decidable** for many important questions.

**(i) Emptiness (2 marks):**
**Question:** Given DFA $A$, is $L(A) = \emptyset$?
**Algorithm:** Run BFS/DFS from $q_0$. Return "YES" (empty) iff no state in $F$ is reachable.
**Time complexity:** $O(\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$.

**(ii) Finiteness (2 marks):**
**Question:** Given DFA $A$, is $L(A)$ finite?
**Algorithm:**
1. Compute reachable states $R$ from $q_0$.
2. Run cycle detection (e.g., DFS with "on-stack" markers) restricted to $R$.
3. Return "NO" (infinite) iff some $f \in F$ lies on a cycle.
4. Else return "YES" (finite).
**Time complexity:** $O(\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$.

**(iii) Equivalence (2 marks):**
**Question:** Given DFAs $A_1$ and $A_2$, is $L(A_1) = L(A_2)$?
**Algorithm:**
1. Construct $\overline{A_2}$ by complementing its accept set.
2. Build the product DFA $B$ for $A_1 \times \overline{A_2}$ with $F_B = F_1 \times (Q_2 \setminus F_2)$.
3. Symmetrically build $C$ for $\overline{A_1} \times A_2$.
4. Build the union DFA $D$ of $B$ and $C$.
5. Run the **emptiness test** on $D$.
6. Return "YES" (equivalent) iff $L(D) = \emptyset$.

**Time complexity:** $O(\vert Q_1 \vert \cdot \vert Q_2 \vert \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the DFA requirement for complement closure** — 1 mark lost if you say "NFA accept states can be swapped" without justification. Always say "first convert NFA to DFA".
> 2. **Confusing the Kleene star of a language with a single string** — $L^{*}$ is the set of **all finite concatenations** of zero or more strings from $L$, not the set of single repeated copies of one string.
> 3. **Mis-stating the pumping lemma conditions** — All three conditions ($\vert y \vert > 0$, $\vert xy \vert \le n$, $xy^{k}z \in L$ for all $k \ge 0$) must be listed. Missing one = 1 mark deduction.
> 4. **Decision property algorithm steps skipped** — Listing the algorithm without the time complexity or correctness justification will fetch only partial marks.
> 5. **Homomorphism vs Inverse Homomorphism confusion** — $h(L) = \{h(w) \mid w \in L\}$ applies $h$ to strings; $h^{-1}(L) = \{w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid h(w) \in L\}$ is the pre-image. Closure holds for both, but the constructions are different.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — print this before the exam!**

- ✅ A language is **regular** iff it has a DFA, NFA, regular expression, or linear grammar.
- ✅ **Closure under Boolean operations:** Union, Intersection, Complement — all three hold for regular languages.
- ✅ **Closure under concatenation and Kleene star:** Constructed via Thompson-style NFAs.
- ✅ **Closure under reversal:** Reverse edges of an NFA, swap start/accept.
- ✅ **Closure under homomorphism & inverse homomorphism:** Both hold for regular languages (true even for context-free languages, except the inverse).
- ✅ **Closure under substitution:** If $s(a)$ is regular for every literal $a$, then $s(L)$ is regular for every regular $L$.
- ✅ **Decision: Emptiness** $\Rightarrow$ BFS reachability, $O(\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$.
- ✅ **Decision: Finiteness** $\Rightarrow$ no accept state on a reachable cycle.
- ✅ **Decision: Membership** $\Rightarrow$ simulate DFA on $w$, $O(\vert w \vert)$.
- ✅ **Decision: Equivalence** $\Rightarrow$ reduce to emptiness of the symmetric difference.
- ✅ **Decision: Subset** $\Rightarrow$ reduce to $L_1 \cap \overline{L_2} = \emptyset$.
- ✅ **Decision: Totality** $\Rightarrow$ $\overline{L} = \emptyset$ ?
- ✅ **Exam trick:** "Is $L$ regular?" → try closure properties **first**; only use pumping lemma / Myhill–Nerode to prove **non-regularity**.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** "Regular $\Rightarrow$ Context-Free" (true), but "Context-Free $\Rightarrow$ Regular" (false — counterexample: $\{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \ge 0\}$).
- ✅ **DFA vs NFA:** Use DFA for complement closure proofs; use NFA for concatenation, star, reversal, and homomorphism constructions.
- ✅ **Time complexity table** is the most-asked theoretical question on KTU Module 2 papers — memorize it verbatim.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Pumping Lemma

The **Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages** is a fundamental property that every regular language must satisfy. It is a **necessary condition** (not sufficient) used primarily to **prove that a language is NOT regular**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Statement (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard Formulation):**
> If $L$ is a regular language, then there exists a **constant integer** $n \geq 1$ (called the *pumping length*) such that for every string $s \in L$ with $\lvert s \rvert \geq n$, we can write $s = xyz$ satisfying:
>
> 1. $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$ (i.e., $y \neq \varepsilon$)
> 2. $\lvert xy \rvert \leq n$
> 3. $xy^{i}z \in L$, for every $i \geq 0$

Here, $y^{i}$ denotes $y$ repeated $i$ times. When $i=0$, $y$ is "pumped out"; when $i \geq 1$, $y$ is "pumped in".

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Garden Hose Analogy:**
> Imagine a long, flexible garden hose of length $n$. Now take a string $s$ that is **longer** than the hose (i.e., $\lvert s \rvert \geq n$). As you thread this string through a machine (a DFA with $n$ states), some **section of the hose must be visited twice** (by the **Pigeonhole Principle**). That repeated section is our substring $y$ — the "pumpable" part.
>
> Because the machine has no memory of *how many times* it visited that section, you can stretch $y$ (pump it up) or shrink it (pump it down), and the machine will still accept the resulting string. The string behaves like a *looped rope*: pull the loop tight (i = 0), keep it as is (i = 1), or stretch it many times (i = 2, 3, ...), and the rope remains intact.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Pumping Length** | $n$ | Equals the number of states in the minimal DFA |
| **String Length Lower Bound** | $\lvert s \rvert$ | Must satisfy $\lvert s \rvert \geq n$ |
| **Pumpable Substring** | $y$ | Non-empty, located within first $n$ characters |
| **Pumpable Region** | $xy$ | Length of prefix $\leq n$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The Pumping Lemma is an **IF–THEN** statement, not an **IF AND ONLY IF**. Its contrapositive is what we use in proofs:
> *If no such decomposition exists for some $s$ of length $\geq n$, then $L$ is not regular.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Conditions Explained

The Pumping Lemma rests on three inseparable conditions. Violating **any one** of them invalidates the decomposition.

### Condition 1: $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$ (The Non-Empty Pump)
- Ensures we are actually **pumping something**.
- Without this, the lemma would be trivially true for any split (just pump the empty string forever).

### Condition 2: $\lvert xy \rvert \leq n$ (The Localized Pump)
- The "pumpable" part $y$ must lie **within the first $n$ symbols** of $s$.
- This restricts the location of pumping, making the lemma useful for refutation.

### Condition 3: $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$ (The Closure Property)
- The language must be **closed under pumping** of $y$ in both directions:
  - **Pumping Out** ($i = 0$): $xz \in L$
  - **Pumping In** ($i = 2$): $xyyz \in L$
  - **General** ($i = k$): $xy^{k}z \in L$

## 2.2 The Underlying Logic: Why the Pumping Lemma Holds

**Step-by-step logical structure:**

1. Let $L$ be a regular language accepted by a DFA $M$ with $n$ states.
2. Consider any string $s \in L$ with $\lvert s \rvert = m \geq n$.
3. As $M$ reads $s$, it traverses a sequence of $m+1$ states: $q_{0}, q_{1}, q_{2}, \ldots, q_{m}$.
4. Since there are $m+1 > n$ states in this sequence and only $n$ distinct states available, by the **Pigeonhole Principle**, **at least two states must be identical**.
5. Let the first repeated state occur at position $j$ and the next at position $k$ where $0 \leq j < k \leq n$.
6. Define:
   - $x = s_{1}s_{2}\ldots s_{j}$  (the part before the first repeat)
   - $y = s_{j+1}s_{j+2}\ldots s_{k}$  (the loop — the "pump")
   - $z = s_{k+1}s_{k+2}\ldots s_{m}$  (the tail after the loop)
7. The substring $y$ corresponds to a cycle in the DFA. We can traverse this cycle any number of times (including zero), and the DFA will still end in the same accepting state.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
> - The DFA has finite memory, so revisiting a state with the same input character means the future behavior is deterministic.
> - The cycle (loop) is repeatable because automata lack a "counter" — they cannot count how many times the cycle has been traversed.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Reference Table for KTU 2024 Exams:**

| Element | Formal Expression | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Pumping Length** | $n = $ number of states in minimal DFA | The "memory capacity" of the automaton |
| **String Decomposition** | $s = xyz$ | Splitting a long accepted string into three parts |
| **Non-Empty Pump** | $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$ | Pumped substring is never the empty string |
| **Pump Localization** | $\lvert xy \rvert \leq n$ | Loop occurs within the first $n$ symbols |
| **Closure Under Pumping** | $\forall i \geq 0, \ xy^{i}z \in L$ | Language is invariant under repetition/removal of $y$ |
| **Contrapositive Form** | $\exists s \in L, \ \forall xyz, \ \neg(\text{conditions}) \Rightarrow L \notin \text{REG}$ | The standard proof-by-contradiction template |
| **Pumping In (i = 2)** | $xyyz \in L$ | Doubling the pumped substring |
| **Pumping Out (i = 0)** | $xz \in L$ | Removing the pumped substring entirely |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Pumping Lemma has profound applications in:

1. **Compiler Design:** Verifying that token patterns (regular expressions) are correctly defined. If a proposed pattern fails the Pumping Lemma, it cannot be implemented as a finite-state scanner.
2. **Network Protocol Verification:** Regular languages model simple protocols; the Pumping Lemma helps prove when a protocol *cannot* be expressed as a finite-state machine.
3. **Pattern Matching Engines:** Tools like `grep` rely on regular expressions. The lemma explains why certain pattern-matching operations (e.g., matching balanced parentheses) **cannot** be done with regex.
4. **Digital Circuit Design:** Sequential circuits with finite memory correspond to DFAs. The lemma bounds the repetitive behaviors of such circuits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Proof of the Pumping Lemma (For-Regular Direction)

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem:** If $L$ is regular, then the Pumping Lemma holds for $L$.

### Proof:

Let $L$ be a regular language. By definition, there exists a DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_{0}, F)$ that recognizes $L$, with $\lvert Q \rvert = n$ states.

Choose the **pumping length** $n$ (this is the same $n$ as the number of states).

Let $s = s_{1}s_{2}\ldots s_{m}$ be any string in $L$ with $m \geq n$.

Consider the sequence of states traversed by $M$ while processing $s$:

$$
q_{0}, \ q_{1}, \ q_{2}, \ \ldots, \ q_{n}, \ \ldots, \ q_{m}
$$

where each $q_{i+1} = \delta(q_{i}, s_{i+1})$.

Since this sequence has $m+1$ states and $m+1 > n$ (because $m \geq n$), by the **Pigeonhole Principle**, there must exist indices $j$ and $k$ with $0 \leq j < k \leq n$ such that:

$$
q_{j} = q_{k}
$$

Now define the three parts of $s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= s_{1}s_{2}\ldots s_{j} \\
y &= s_{j+1}s_{j+2}\ldots s_{k} \\
z &= s_{k+1}s_{k+2}\ldots s_{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of the three conditions:**

**Condition 1: $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$**

Since $j < k$, the substring $y$ contains at least one character. Therefore:

$$
\lvert y \rvert = k - j \geq 1
$$

**Condition 2: $\lvert xy \rvert \leq n$**

Since $k \leq n$, the prefix $xy$ has length exactly $k$:

$$
\lvert xy \rvert = k \leq n
$$

**Condition 3: $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$**

For any $i \geq 0$, consider the string $xy^{i}z$. The DFA processes $x$ and reaches state $q_{j}$. Then it processes $y$ a total of $i$ times, each time starting and ending at $q_{j}$ (because the loop from $q_{j}$ to $q_{k}$ satisfies $q_{j} = q_{k}$). Finally, it processes $z$ and reaches the same final state as the original string $s$. Since $s \in L$, the original final state is accepting. Therefore, $xy^{i}z$ is also accepted. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 The Analytical Matrix: Applying the Pumping Lemma (Not-Regular Direction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Proof Template — Proving a Language is NOT Regular:**

Given a language $L$, to prove $L$ is not regular, we use the **contrapositive** of the Pumping Lemma:

| Step | Action | Formal Expression |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Assume Regular** | Suppose $L$ is regular. | $L \in \text{REG}$ |
| **2. Get Pumping Length** | Then there exists $n$ such that... | $\exists n \geq 1$ |
| **3. Adversary Picks String** | Choose $s \in L$ with $\lvert s \rvert \geq n$ | $s = \ldots$ (cleverly chosen) |
| **4. Show All Splits Fail** | For every decomposition $s = xyz$ satisfying conditions 1 & 2 | $\forall xyz$ |
| **5. Exhibit Violation** | Find some $i \geq 0$ such that $xy^{i}z \notin L$ | $\exists i, \ xy^{i}z \notin L$ |
| **6. Contradiction** | Therefore our assumption is false. | $\therefore L \notin \text{REG}$ |

## 3.3 Worked-Out Example: $L = \{ 0^{k}1^{k} \mid k \geq 0 \}$

**Claim:** $L$ is not regular.

**Step 1: Assume for contradiction** that $L$ is regular. Let $n$ be the pumping length.

**Step 2: Choose the adversary string:**

$$
s = 0^{n}1^{n}
$$

Clearly, $s \in L$ and $\lvert s \rvert = 2n \geq n$. ✓

**Step 3: Analyze all possible decompositions.** By condition 2, $\lvert xy \rvert \leq n$, so the substring $y$ consists only of $0$s (since the first $n$ characters of $s$ are all $0$s).

**Step 4: Apply pumping.** Let $i = 2$. The pumped string is:

$$
xy^{2}z = 0^{n + \lvert y \rvert}1^{n}
$$

**Step 5: Check membership in $L$.** For $xy^{2}z$ to be in $L$, we need:

$$
n + \lvert y \rvert = n
$$

which implies $\lvert y \rvert = 0$, contradicting Condition 1 ($\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$).

**Step 6: Conclusion.** Our assumption leads to a contradiction. Therefore, $L = \{0^{k}1^{k} \mid k \geq 0\}$ is **not regular**. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Pumping Process in a DFA

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram visualizes how a long string traverses a DFA and exposes a loop (the pumpable substring $y$).

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["q0 Start"] -->|"x prefix"| B["qj First Visit"]
    B -->|"y loop character 1"| C["q(j+1)"]
    C -->|"y loop character 2"| D["q(j+2)"]
    D -->|"y loop continues"| E["q(k-1)"]
    E -->|"y loop ends here"| F["qk Repeat of qj"]
    F -->|"z suffix continues"| G["qm Accept State"]
    F -.->|"loop can repeat infinitely"| B

    style A fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Green node** ($q_0$): Initial state.
- **Yellow nodes** ($q_j$, $q_k$): The two identical states — the "loop entry" and "loop exit".
- **Pink node** ($q_m$): Accepting state (final state after reading the full string).
- The **dashed back-edge** from $q_k$ to $q_j$ represents the optional repetition of the loop (the pump).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Pumping Lemma Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["Step 1 Input Language L"] --> P2["Step 2 Assume L is regular"]
    P2 --> P3["Step 3 DFA M exists with n states"]
    P3 --> P4["Step 4 Pick string s in L with length at least n"]
    P4 --> P5["Step 5 Apply Pigeonhole Principle"]
    P5 --> P6["Step 6 Identify repeated states qj and qk"]
    P6 --> P7["Step 7 Decompose s into xyz"]
    P7 --> P8["Step 8 Verify three conditions"]
    P8 --> P9{"Step 9 Are all conditions satisfied?"}
    P9 -->|"Yes"| P10["Step 10 Conclude L is regular"]
    P9 -->|"No contradiction arises"| P11["Step 11 Conclude L is not regular"]

    style P1 fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C,color:#000
    style P4 fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C,color:#000
    style P5 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style P6 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style P7 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style P8 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style P9 fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    style P10 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000

```

**Engineering Interpretation:** This flowchart encapsulates the **decision procedure** a student or verification engineer follows when applying the Pumping Lemma. The teal blocks (Steps 1–4) are the **setup**, the yellow blocks (Steps 5–8) are the **technical core**, the orange block (Step 9) is the **decision point**, and the green/red blocks (Steps 10–11) are the **conclusions**.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Adversary vs. Prover Game

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Adv as Adversary
    participant Prov as Prover
    participant L as Language L

    Prov->>L: Assume L is regular
    L-->>Prov: Returns pumping length n
    Adv->>Prov: Chooses challenging string s
    Prov->>Prov: Tries all splits s = xyz
    loop For every valid split
        Prov->>Prov: Check xy to the power i times z membership
    end
    Prov->>Adv: Finds a split that fails
    Adv->>L: Contradiction established
    L-->>Prov: L is not regular
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**State the Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages. Mention all three conditions clearly.**

**Model Answer:**

> The Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages states that:
>
> If $L$ is a regular language, then there exists a constant $n \geq 1$ (the pumping length) such that every string $s \in L$ with $\lvert s \rvert \geq n$ can be decomposed as $s = xyz$ where:
>
> 1. $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$
> 2. $\lvert xy \rvert \leq n$
> 3. $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$
>
> The Pumping Lemma is used as a tool to prove that certain languages are **not regular**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Why is the Pumping Lemma considered a necessary but not sufficient condition for regularity?**

**Model Answer:**

> The Pumping Lemma is **necessary** because every regular language must satisfy it (this is a proven theorem).
>
> However, it is **not sufficient** because there exist non-regular languages that also satisfy the Pumping Lemma. For instance, the language $L = \{ a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 0 \}$ is not regular, but it satisfies the Pumping Lemma for $n = 3$.
>
> Therefore, the Pumping Lemma can only be used to **disprove** regularity (via the contrapositive), not to **prove** regularity. To prove regularity, one must construct a DFA, NFA, or regular expression.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse the pumping lemma (which proves non-regularity) with a method to prove regularity. **Marks will be deducted** if you attempt to "prove" regularity using the Pumping Lemma.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to check **all possible decompositions** of the string. You must show that *no matter how* the string is split (respecting conditions 1 and 2), the pumping fails. Examiners expect a case analysis.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Choosing a weak adversary string (e.g., one too short or one that trivially pumps). The string must be carefully selected to expose the limitation of finite automata.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Failing to explicitly state the value of $i$ that causes the violation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q3(a) [7 Marks]** Prove that the language $L = \{ a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1 \}$ is not regular using the Pumping Lemma.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Assume for contradiction** that $L$ is regular. Then by the Pumping Lemma, there exists a pumping length $n$ (we can reuse the symbol $n$, though it is a different $n$ from the language's parameter — to avoid confusion, let the pumping length be $p$).

So there exists $p \geq 1$ such that every string $s \in L$ with $\lvert s \rvert \geq p$ can be written as $s = xyz$ with $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$, $\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$, and $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$.

**Step 2: Choose the adversary string.** We select:

$$
s = a^{p}b^{p}
$$

Clearly, $s \in L$ (with $n = p$ in the language definition), and $\lvert s \rvert = 2p \geq p$. ✓

**Step 3: Analyze the structure of $y$.**

By Condition 2, $\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$, so the substring $y$ lies entirely within the first $p$ characters of $s$. Since the first $p$ characters of $s$ are all $a$'s, we have:

$$
y = a^{k} \quad \text{for some} \quad k \geq 1
$$

**Step 4: Apply pumping with $i = 0$.**

The string $xy^{0}z = xz$ is obtained by removing $y$ from $s$:

$$
xz = a^{p-k}b^{p}
$$

**Step 5: Check membership in $L$.**

For $xz$ to be in $L$, we would need:

$$
p - k = p
$$

which implies $k = 0$. This contradicts Condition 1 ($\lvert y \rvert = k \geq 1$).

**Step 6: Conclude.**

The contradiction shows that our initial assumption is false. Therefore, $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ is **not regular**. $\blacksquare$

---

**Q3(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the significance of the condition $\lvert xy \rvert \leq n$ in the Pumping Lemma. What would happen if this condition were removed?

**Model Answer:**

**Significance of $\lvert xy \rvert \leq n$:**

This condition ensures that the pumpable substring $y$ is **localized** to the first $n$ characters of the string. This restriction is what makes the Pumping Lemma useful for proving non-regularity.

By confining $y$ to a known region of the string, we can control the structure of $y$ when choosing the adversary string. For example, if we pick $s = a^{n}b^{n}$, then $y$ must consist entirely of $a$'s, which lets us pump $y$ and break the balance of $a$'s and $b$'s.

**What happens if $\lvert xy \rvert \leq n$ is removed:**

If we remove this condition, the Pumping Lemma becomes **trivially true for all languages**, and hence **useless**.

**Proof of Triviality:** Suppose we drop the condition $\lvert xy \rvert \leq n$. Then for any string $s \in L$ with $\lvert s \rvert \geq n$, we can simply set $x = \varepsilon$, $y = s$, and $z = \varepsilon$. Then:

- $\lvert y \rvert = \lvert s \rvert \geq 1$ ✓
- $xy^{i}z = s^{i+1}$ 

Wait — this would require $s^{i+1} \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$, which is **not** guaranteed. So the trivial decomposition $x = \varepsilon$, $y = s$, $z = \varepsilon$ doesn't automatically work.

**Corrected Insight:** Without the localization condition, an adversary could place $y$ in a region that makes it difficult or impossible to choose a violating $i$. The condition $\lvert xy \rvert \leq n$ is what gives the lemma its **proving power** by ensuring the pumpable region is constrained.

In essence, the localization condition is the **bridge** between the abstract existence of a pumping decomposition and the practical ability to construct counterexamples for non-regular languages.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q4(a) [7 Marks]** Using the Pumping Lemma, prove that $L = \{ ww \mid w \in \{0,1\}^{*} \}$ (the language of even-length palindromes over $\{0,1\}$... actually, $ww$ is the language of strings formed by concatenating a string with itself) is not regular.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Assume for contradiction** that $L$ is regular. Let $p$ be the pumping length.

**Step 2: Choose the adversary string.** We select:

$$
s = 0^{p}1^{p}0^{p}1^{p}
$$

Clearly, $s \in L$ because $s = ww$ where $w = 0^{p}1^{p}$. Also, $\lvert s \rvert = 4p \geq p$. ✓

**Step 3: Analyze the structure of $y$.**

By Condition 2, $\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$, so $y$ lies in the first $p$ characters of $s$, which are all $0$'s. So:

$$
y = 0^{k} \quad \text{for some} \quad 1 \leq k \leq p
$$

**Step 4: Apply pumping with $i = 2$.**

$$
xy^{2}z = 0^{p+k}1^{p}0^{p}1^{p}
$$

**Step 5: Show this is not in $L$.**

For $xy^{2}z$ to be in $L$, it must equal $ww$ for some $w$. This means the string must be of the form (some string)(same string), i.e., the second half must equal the first half.

The first $p+k$ characters of $xy^{2}z$ are: $0^{p+k}$.

The remaining $3p - k$ characters are: $1^{p}0^{p}1^{p}$.

For $xy^{2}z = ww$, we need $0^{p+k}$ to be the first half, so the second half would also need to be $0^{p+k}$. But the second half starts with $1$'s, so this is impossible unless $p + k = 0$, which contradicts $p, k \geq 1$.

**Step 6: Conclude.**

This contradiction shows $L$ is **not regular**. $\blacksquare$

---

**Q4(b) [7 Marks]** State and prove the Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages. Use a DFA with $n$ states in your proof.

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Already covered in Section 1.1 above.

**Proof:**

Let $L$ be a regular language. Then there exists a DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_{0}, F)$ with $\lvert Q \rvert = n$ such that $L = L(M)$.

Set the pumping length to be $n$. Let $s = s_{1}s_{2}\ldots s_{m}$ be any string in $L$ with $m \geq n$.

Consider the state sequence when $M$ processes $s$:

$$
q_{0}, \ q_{1}, \ q_{2}, \ \ldots, \ q_{m}
$$

This sequence has $m+1 \geq n+1$ states, but $M$ has only $n$ states. By the Pigeonhole Principle, there exist indices $j, k$ with $0 \leq j < k \leq n$ such that $q_{j} = q_{k}$.

Define:

$$
x = s_{1}\ldots s_{j}, \quad y = s_{j+1}\ldots s_{k}, \quad z = s_{k+1}\ldots s_{m}
$$

**Verification:**

1. $\lvert y \rvert = k - j \geq 1$ since $k > j$. ✓
2. $\lvert xy \rvert = k \leq n$. ✓
3. For any $i \geq 0$, the DFA processes $x$ to reach $q_{j}$, then processes $y$ exactly $i$ times, returning to $q_{j}$ each time (since $q_{j} = q_{k}$). It then processes $z$ to reach the original accepting state. Thus $xy^{i}z \in L$. ✓

Therefore, the Pumping Lemma holds for $L$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ The **Pumping Lemma** is a **necessary** condition for regularity — it holds for ALL regular languages.
- ✅ It is **not sufficient** — some non-regular languages also satisfy it.
- ✅ The Pumping Lemma is used almost exclusively to **prove non-regularity** via the contrapositive.
- ✅ The **pumping length** $n$ equals the number of states in the minimal DFA (a hint for choice in proofs).
- ✅ **Three conditions** must be verified: $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$, $\lvert xy \rvert \leq n$, and $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$.
- ✅ The proof relies on the **Pigeonhole Principle**: a string of length $\geq n$ traversing an $n$-state DFA must repeat a state.
- ✅ In **adversary proofs**, the chosen string $s$ must be **cleverly constructed** to exploit the structure of the language.
- ✅ The substring $y$ is **localized** to the first $n$ characters — this is what makes the lemma practically useful.
- ✅ **Classic non-regular examples**: $L = \{a^{n}b^{n}\}$, $L = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n}\}$, $L = \{ww \mid w \in \Sigma^{*}\}$, $L = \{w \mid w \text{ is a palindrome}\}$.
- ✅ **Always check all decompositions** in your proof — show that *every* valid split $s = xyz$ leads to a violation for some $i$.
- ✅ **Choose $i$ strategically**: usually $i = 0$ (pump out) or $i = 2$ (pump in) is sufficient to break the language structure.
- ✅ **A common pitfall**: using a string that is too short (less than $n$) or one that allows easy pumping. Always ensure $\lvert s \rvert \geq n$.
- ✅ **KTU exam tip**: explicitly mention the **contrapositive** form of the lemma in your answer to demonstrate conceptual clarity — examiners value this.
- ✅ Remember: pumping $y$ **changes the length** of the string by a multiple of $\lvert y \rvert$. Use this to break balanced structures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Myhill-Nerode Theorem (MNT) without Proof
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Myhill-Nerode Theorem (MNT) without Proof

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Myhill–Nerode Equivalence Relation

Let $L \subseteq \Sigma^{\star}$ be a language over alphabet $\Sigma$. The **Myhill–Nerode relation**, denoted $\equiv_L$ (or $\sim_L$), is defined on $\Sigma^{\star}$ as:

$$\forall\, x,\, y \in \Sigma^{\star} :\quad x \equiv_L y \iff \bigl(\forall\, z \in \Sigma^{\star}\bigr)\bigl[\, x z \in L \iff y z \in L\,\bigr]$$

Read in plain English: **two strings $x$ and $y$ are equivalent if and only if every possible continuation $z$ leads both of them to agree on membership in $L$.** If some suffix $z$ causes $x z$ and $y z$ to disagree, $x$ and $y$ are inequivalent.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEITT525 / Module 2)**
> The Myhill–Nerode Theorem connects **regular languages**, **DFAs**, and **equivalence relations of finite index**. It is the canonical tool for (i) proving **non-regularity** and (ii) constructing the **minimum-state DFA** for any regular language.

### 1.2 Right-Invariant Equivalence Relation

An equivalence relation $R$ on $\Sigma^{\star}$ is called **right-invariant** if for all $x, y, z \in \Sigma^{\star}$:

$$x\, R\, y \;\implies\; x z\, R\, y z$$

In words: appending the *same* suffix to two equivalent strings yields strings that are still equivalent. The Myhill–Nerode relation $\equiv_L$ is automatically right-invariant by construction.

### 1.3 Index of an Equivalence Relation

The **index** of an equivalence relation $R$ is the **cardinality (number)** of the partition of $\Sigma^{\star}$ induced by $R$ — i.e., the number of distinct equivalence classes $\Sigma^{\star}/R$. Denote it as:

$$[R] \;=\; \mid \Sigma^{\star} / R \mid$$

> [!NOTE] **Core Definition — Equivalence Class**
> The equivalence class of a string $x$ is $[x] = \{ y \in \Sigma^{\star} \mid x \equiv_L y \}$. The set of all such classes partitions $\Sigma^{\star}$ into disjoint, non-empty blocks.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine every string $x \in \Sigma^{\star}$ is a **traveller arriving at a decision desk** before a hidden machine (the language $L$). The desk official does not know $L$, but the traveller can shout any continuation $z$, and the machine responds **YES (in $L$)** or **NO (not in $L$)**.

- Two travellers $x$ and $y$ are given the **same badge** iff, no matter what suffix $z$ they shout, the machine answers identically for both.
- The number of distinct badges handed out is the **index of $\equiv_L$**.
- If infinitely many distinct badges are needed, no finite DFA can simulate the machine → $L$ is not regular.

> **Real-world parallel:** consider *fingerprinting* an input stream. Two prefixes are "behaviourally indistinguishable" w.r.t. the language $L$ if the future of the computation cannot tell them apart — they share the same fingerprint.

### 1.5 Why MNT Matters (Engineering Utility)

| Application | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Non-regularity proofs** | Exhibit an *infinite family* of inequivalent strings. |
| **Minimum DFA synthesis** | Each equivalence class maps to **one DFA state**. |
| **Lower bound on DFA size** | Any DFA for $L$ needs at least $[\equiv_L]$ states. |
| **Minimisation algorithm** (Hopcroft) | Iteratively refines partitions until stable; converges to $\equiv_L$. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Properties of $\equiv_L$

Let $L \subseteq \Sigma^{\star}$. The Myhill–Nerode relation $\equiv_L$ satisfies three structural properties:

1. **Reflexivity** — $\forall x \in \Sigma^{\star},\; x \equiv_L x$.  
   *Reason:* For every $z$, $x z \in L \iff x z \in L$ (trivially true).

2. **Symmetry** — $x \equiv_L y \Rightarrow y \equiv_L x$.  
   *Reason:* The biconditional $\forall z\,(xz \in L \iff yz \in L)$ is symmetric in $x$ and $y$.

3. **Transitivity** — $x \equiv_L y$ and $y \equiv_L z \Rightarrow x \equiv_L z$.  
   *Reason:* $\forall w\,(xw \in L \iff yw \in L)$ and $\forall w\,(yw \in L \iff zw \in L)$ chain together.

4. **Right-invariance** — $x \equiv_L y \Rightarrow \forall w,\; xw \equiv_L yw$.  
   *Reason:* For any $u$, $(xw)u = x(wu) \in L \iff y(wu) \in L \iff (yw)u \in L$.

> [!IMPORTANT] Because $\equiv_L$ is reflexive, symmetric, transitive, **and** right-invariant, it partitions $\Sigma^{\star}$ into equivalence classes that are *closed under right-concatenation* — the exact algebraic structure needed to define a DFA.

### 2.2 The Myhill–Nerode Theorem (Statement)

Let $L \subseteq \Sigma^{\star}$. The following three statements are equivalent:

| # | Statement |
|---|---|
| **(a)** | $L$ is **regular** (i.e., accepted by some DFA). |
| **(b)** | $L$ is the **union of some equivalence classes** of a **right-invariant** equivalence relation of **finite index** on $\Sigma^{\star}$. |
| **(c)** | The Myhill–Nerode relation $\equiv_L$ has a **finite number of equivalence classes**. |

Furthermore, the **minimum-state DFA** for $L$ has exactly $[\equiv_L]$ states, and its transition function on the class $[x]$ under input symbol $a$ is:

$$\delta([x],\, a) \;=\; [x a]$$

The start state is $[\varepsilon]$ and the set of accepting states is $\{ [x] \mid x \in L \}$.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **(a) ⟹ (c):** If $L$ is accepted by a DFA $M$ with $n$ states, the Myhill–Nerode relation induced by $M$ (two strings are equivalent iff they drive $M$ to the same state) has at most $n$ classes and refines $\equiv_L$, so $\equiv_L$ is coarser or equal — hence also finite. (Sketch only — *proof is not in syllabus*.)

- **(c) ⟹ (a):** If $\equiv_L$ has finitely many classes, we can *build* a DFA whose states are exactly those classes; right-invariance guarantees the transition function is well-defined. (Sketch only — *proof is not in syllabus*.)

- **(b)** is the "algebraic" middle condition connecting the two views.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Myhill–Nerode relation | $x \equiv_L y$ | $x, y$ indistinguishable w.r.t. $L$ |
| Defining biconditional | $\forall z \in \Sigma^{\star} : (xz \in L \iff yz \in L)$ | Continuation test |
| Right-invariance | $x \equiv_L y \Rightarrow xz \equiv_L yz$ | Closing under right-concat |
| Index of $R$ | $\;[\!R]\; = \mid \Sigma^{\star}/R \mid$ | Number of equivalence classes |
| Equivalence class | $[x] = \{ y \in \Sigma^{\star} \mid x \equiv_L y \}$ | Block in the partition |
| Minimum DFA states | $n_{\min} = [\!\equiv_L\!]$ | Lower bound on DFA size |
| DFA start state | $[\varepsilon]$ | Class of empty string |
| DFA transition | $\delta([x], a) = [x a]$ | Class-merge rule |
| DFA accept states | $F = \{ [x] \mid x \in L \}$ | Classes intersecting $L$ |
| $L$ regular | $\iff [\!\equiv_L\!] < \infty$ | MNT criterion |
| $L$ not regular | $\iff [\!\equiv_L\!] = \infty$ | MNT non-regularity test |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Lexical analysers / tokenisers** in compilers use DFAs minimised via MNT to shrink memory and speed up scanning.
- **Network intrusion detection** engines build minimal DFAs over signature alphabets — fewer states mean faster packet inspection.
- **Hardware model checking** uses MNT-style partitions to abstract infinite state spaces into finite quotients.
- **Bioinformatics pattern matchers** rely on MNT-minimised automata to scan gigabyte-scale DNA streams in real time.
