**Step 1 — Pattern Analysis:**
This is similar to part (a) but now $i, j, k \geq 0$ (including the case $i=j=0, k=0$, i.e., the string $\varepsilon$).

**Step 2 — Productions:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aSc \mid B \\
B &\rightarrow bBc \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification:**

**Empty string $\varepsilon$ ($i=j=k=0$):**
$$S \Rightarrow B \Rightarrow \varepsilon \quad \checkmark$$

**String $ac$ ($i=1, j=0, k=1$):**
$$S \Rightarrow aSc \Rightarrow a(B)c \Rightarrow a(\varepsilon)c = ac \quad \checkmark$$

**String $bcc$ ($i=0, j=1, k=2$):**
$$S \Rightarrow B \Rightarrow bBc \Rightarrow b(\varepsilon)c = bc$$
This gives $bc$, but we want $bcc$. **Re-derivation:**
$$S \Rightarrow B \Rightarrow bBc \Rightarrow b(bBc)c \Rightarrow bb(\varepsilon)cc = bbcc$$
This gives $bbcc$ (i=0, j=2, k=2). For $bcc$ (i=0, j=1, k=2), we need a different rule. **Re-derivation for $bcc$:**
$$S \Rightarrow B \Rightarrow bBc \Rightarrow b(\varepsilon)c = bc$$
We need $bcc$ which has 2 $c$'s and 1 $b$. This means $i=0, j=1, k=2$ so $i+j=1 \neq 2=k$. **Correction:** $bcc$ has $i=0, j=1, k=2$, so $i+j=1 \neq 2$. The string $bcc$ is **not** in $L_4$. ✓ (The grammar correctly avoids generating it.)

**String $abc$ ($i=1, j=1, k=2$):**
$$S \Rightarrow aSc \Rightarrow a(B)c = aBc \Rightarrow a(bBc)c = abBcc \Rightarrow ab(\varepsilon)cc = abcc \quad \checkmark$$

**String $aacc$ ($i=2, j=0, k=2$):**
$$S \Rightarrow aSc \Rightarrow a(aSc)c = aaScc \Rightarrow aa(B)cc = aaBcc \Rightarrow aa(\varepsilon)cc = aacc \quad \checkmark$$

**Formal CFG Components:**
- $V = \{S, B\}$
- $T = \{a, b, c\}$
- $P = \{S \rightarrow aSc, \; S \rightarrow B, \; B \rightarrow bBc, \; B \rightarrow \varepsilon\}$
- $S$ is the start symbol.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Base Case:** Students often write $S \rightarrow aSb$ but forget $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ (or $S \rightarrow ab$). Without the base case, the grammar generates the **empty language**, not the intended language. This is the **#1 reason for losing marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing $n \geq 0$ vs $n \geq 1$:** The base case differs! If $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$, the base is $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$. If $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$, the base is $S \rightarrow ab$. The KTU examiner explicitly tests this distinction.
>
> 3. **Interleaving vs. Concatenation:** For $L = \{a^m b^n\}$, the $a$'s must all come **before** the $b$'s. Do not write a rule like $S \rightarrow aSb \mid bSa$ — that would generate a different (more complex) language.
>
> 4. **Insufficient Verification:** Always derive at least **two sample strings** (one short, one long) to convince the examiner your grammar works. A grammar without verification loses 2-3 marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to List CFG Components:** The KTU board expects you to **explicitly state** $V, T, P, S$ in your answer. Writing only the productions is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Designing CFGs":**

- **Definition**: A CFG is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where $V$ = non-terminals, $T$ = terminals, $P$ = productions, $S$ = start symbol.
- **Core Pattern 1** ($a^n b^n$): Use $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$ (or $S \rightarrow ab$ if $n \geq 1$).
- **Core Pattern 2** (Palindromes): Use $S \rightarrow aSa \mid bSb \mid a \mid b \mid \varepsilon$.
- **Core Pattern 3** ($a^m b^n$, independent counts): Use $S \rightarrow AB$ with two separate sub-grammars.
- **Core Pattern 4** (Balanced parens): Use $S \rightarrow SS \mid (S) \mid \varepsilon$.
- **Core Pattern 5** (Arithmetic with precedence): Use a 3-tier grammar $E \rightarrow E+T \mid T$, $T \rightarrow T*F \mid F$, $F \rightarrow (E) \mid id$.
- **Core Pattern 6** (Self-embedding): For $a^n c^m b^n d^m$, use $S \rightarrow aSd \mid A$, $A \rightarrow cAb \mid \varepsilon$.
- **Verification**: Always perform leftmost derivations for 2-3 sample strings to prove the grammar works.
- **Soundness Check**: Ensure your grammar does NOT generate strings outside the target language (e.g., $ba$ should not be in $L = \{a^n b^n\}$).
- **Operator Precedence Encoding**: In expression grammars, the **deeper** the operator in the parse tree, the **higher** its precedence.
- **Always State Components**: List $V, T, P, S$ explicitly in your KTU answer; do not write only the productions.
- **LaTeX Convention**: Use $\rightarrow$ for productions and $\Rightarrow$ for derivations — never interchange them.
- **Recursive Insight**: Most KTU CFG design problems hinge on recognizing the **self-referential pattern** in the language. If strings can be arbitrarily long, you need recursion.
- **Base Case Matters**: The base case determines whether the empty string (or smallest unit) is included; this directly maps to whether $n \geq 0$ or $n \geq 1$ in the language description.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parse Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parse Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2: PARSE TREES — DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 🎯 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Parse Tree)**
> Let $G = (V, T, P, S)$ be a context-free grammar. A **parse tree** (also called a **derivation tree** or **syntax tree**) for $G$ is a rooted, ordered tree in which:
> 1. Every leaf node is labeled with a **terminal symbol** (element of $T$) or with the empty string $\varepsilon$.
> 2. Every interior node is labeled with a **non-terminal symbol** (element of $V$).
> 3. The **root** of the tree is labeled with the **start symbol** $S$.
> 4. If an interior node labeled $A$ has children labeled $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ (from left to right), then the production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ must belong to $P$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Derivation Sequences:**
> A parse tree is a *graphical representation* of a derivation. It captures the *structure* of the string without committing to a specific order (leftmost vs. rightmost). The **yield** of a parse tree is the string of leaves read from left to right.

---

## 🌳 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Tree of a Sentence"

Imagine you are an English teacher analyzing the sentence: **"The boy reads a book."**

Just as a **family tree** shows how ancestors relate to descendants without forcing you to read the names in any particular order, a **parse tree** shows how a sentence is built up from the **start symbol** (the "ancestor") to the terminal words (the "descendants" / leaves).

- **Root** → The "great-grandparent" of the sentence, labeled $S$ (Sentence).
- **Internal nodes** → The "parents" and "children" — these are grammatical categories (Noun Phrase, Verb Phrase, etc.).
- **Leaves** → The actual words of the sentence — the "leaves on the tree."
- **Yield** → If you pluck the leaves from left to right and string them together, you get the original sentence.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Think **"Root = Start, Leaves = Terminals, Edges = Productions."**

---

## 🧠 1.3 Why Parse Trees Matter in KTU 2024 / Engineering

| Application Area | Role of Parse Trees |
|---|---|
| **Compilers** (Lex/Yacc, ANTLR) | Used to build the Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) for semantic analysis and code generation. |
| **Natural Language Processing** | Constituency parsing in linguistics models relies directly on parse tree structures. |
| **XML/HTML Parsers** | DOM trees are generalized parse trees over document grammars. |
| **Query Languages (SQL)** | SQL parsers construct parse trees before execution plan generation. |
| **Theorem Provers / AI** | Proof trees are mathematical parse trees over formal logic grammars. |

---

## 📐 1.4 Standard Terminology — Quick Glossary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Yield of a Parse Tree:** The string obtained by concatenating the leaf labels from **left to right**. Denoted as $yield(T)$.
> - **Subtree:** Any node together with all its descendants forms a subtree.
> - **Partial Parse Tree:** A subtree whose root is $S$ but whose leaves may include non-terminals.
> - **Pruning:** Removing all subtrees whose roots are leaves labeled with non-terminals.

topics.title: Ambiguous Grammars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ambiguous Grammars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ambiguous Grammar (Formal Definition):**
> A Context-Free Grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is said to be **ambiguous** if and only if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ such that $w$ possesses **two or more distinct leftmost derivations**. Equivalently, $w$ has **two or more distinct parse trees** rooted at the start symbol $S$.

### 1.2 Inherently Ambiguous Language

> [!NOTE]
> **Inherently Ambiguous CFL:**
> A Context-Free Language $L$ is called **inherently ambiguous** if **every** Context-Free Grammar $G$ that generates $L$ (i.e., $L(G) = L$) is ambiguous. In simple words, no unambiguous grammar can generate the language $L$.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Two Roads to the Same Village

Imagine you are driving from **City A** to **Village B**. Your GPS shows **two completely different valid routes** (Route 1: highway, Route 2: countryside road). Both routes legally lead to Village B, but the journeys are structurally distinct.

- **The driver** = the derivation sequence (step-by-step application of production rules)
- **The destination** = the generated string $w$
- **The map showing the route** = the parse tree (graphical structure)

If **two structurally different maps (parse trees)** can both correctly guide you to the **same village (string)**, the road network (grammar) is **ambiguous**.

### 1.4 Why Ambiguity Matters in Engineering

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Real-World Impact:** In compiler design (Lexical Analysis & Syntax Analysis phases), a programming language **must** have an unambiguous grammar. If a statement like `int a, b;` or `if (x) if (y) foo(); else bar();` has two valid parse trees, the compiler cannot decide which interpretation the programmer intended. This leads to **semantic errors, wrong code generation, and undefined behavior**. Languages like C, Java, and Python use carefully disambiguated grammars (often expressed via precedence and associativity rules).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Criteria to Test for Ambiguity (Step-by-Step Logic)

A grammar $G$ is tested for ambiguity using the following structured reasoning:

1. **Step 1 — String Selection:** Pick a candidate string $w$ from $L(G)$.
2. **Step 2 — First Derivation:** Construct a leftmost derivation $S \Rightarrow^* w$ using one sequence of production rules.
3. **Step 3 — Second Derivation:** Construct a *different* leftmost derivation $S \Rightarrow^* w$ using another sequence of production rules.
4. **Step 4 — Structural Difference Check:** Verify that the two derivations differ in the **order of rule application** (not merely in notation).
5. **Step 5 — Conclusion:**
   - If even **one** string $w$ admits $\geq 2$ leftmost derivations $\Rightarrow$ **Grammar is Ambiguous**.
   - If **every** string has a unique derivation $\Rightarrow$ **Grammar is Unambiguous**.

### 2.2 Equivalence Theorem (Why Parse Trees and Derivations Coincide)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** For any CFG $G$ and any string $w \in L(G)$:
> - Number of distinct **leftmost derivations** of $w$ = Number of distinct **parse trees** for $w$.
> - Number of distinct **rightmost derivations** of $w$ = Number of distinct parse trees for $w$.
>
> Therefore, ambiguity can be checked using **any one** of these three equivalent criteria.

### 2.3 Sources of Ambiguity (Common Causes)

| **Source** | **Description** | **Example Context** |
|---|---|---|
| Missing Precedence | Operator priorities not encoded | $+$ vs $*$ in arithmetic |
| Missing Associativity | Left/right grouping not enforced | $a - b - c$ ambiguity |
| Dangling Else | `else` clause ambiguity | Nested `if-then-else` |
| Underspecified Structure | Multiple ways to break string | String concatenation grammars |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Mathematical Statement** | **Verdict** |
|---|---|---|
| Grammar Ambiguity | $\exists\, w \in L(G)$ such that $w$ has $\geq 2$ parse trees | Ambiguous iff true |
| Inherent Ambiguity | $\forall\, G'$ with $L(G') = L$, $G'$ is ambiguous | Language is Inherently Ambiguous |
| Disambiguation | Construct $G'$ such that $L(G') = L(G)$ and $G'$ is unambiguous | Always possible (except inherent cases) |
| Leftmost Derivation Count | $N_{LMD}(w) = N_{PT}(w) = N_{RMD}(w)$ | All equal for any CFG |
| Chomsky Hierarchy Bound | Regular $\subset$ Deterministic CFL $\subset$ Unambiguous CFL $\subset$ CFL | Ambiguity only above DCFL |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Compiler generators such as **YACC (Yet Another Compiler Compiler)**, **ANTLR**, and **Bison** use precedence and associativity directives to **automatically resolve ambiguity** in arithmetic and logical expressions, ensuring deterministic parsing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Classic Example 1 — Arithmetic Expression Grammar (The Canonical Ambiguous Grammar)

#### 3.1.1 The Ambiguous Grammar $G_1$

Let the grammar $G_1 = (V, T, P, E)$ where:
- $V = \{E\}$
- $T = \{+, *, (, ), \text{id}\}$
- $P$:
  - $E \to E + E$
  - $E \to E * E$
  - $E \to (E)$
  - $E \to \text{id}$

#### 3.1.2 Target String $w$

$$w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$

#### 3.1.3 First Leftmost Derivation ($\Rightarrow$ Addition binds tighter than multiplication)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\Rightarrow E + E \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + E \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + E * E \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * E \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

This derivation makes the operator $+$ the **root operation**, meaning multiplication binds *tighter* (standard mathematical convention). The corresponding parse tree groups as $(\text{id} + (\text{id} * \text{id}))$.

#### 3.1.4 Second Leftmost Derivation ($\Rightarrow$ Multiplication binds tighter than addition)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\Rightarrow E * E \\
  &\Rightarrow E + E * E \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + E * E \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * E \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

This derivation makes the operator $*$ the **root operation**, meaning addition binds *tighter*. The corresponding parse tree groups as $((\text{id} + \text{id}) * \text{id})$.

#### 3.1.5 Conclusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single string $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ has **two structurally distinct parse trees** and **two distinct leftmost derivations**. Therefore, $G_1$ is **AMBIGUOUS**.

### 3.2 The Unambiguous Disambiguated Grammar $G_2$

We enforce **precedence** ($*$ over $+$) and **left-associativity** through layered non-terminals:

- $E \to E + T \mid T$
- $T \to T * F \mid F$
- $F \to (E) \mid \text{id}$

#### 3.2.1 Unique Derivation of $\text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\Rightarrow E + T \\
  &\Rightarrow T + T \\
  &\Rightarrow F + T \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + T \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + T * F \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + F * F \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * F \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

Every step is **forced** — only one rule applies at each stage. Thus $G_2$ is **unambiguous**, and $L(G_1) = L(G_2)$.

### 3.3 Classic Example 2 — The Dangling Else Problem

#### 3.3.1 The Ambiguous Grammar $G_3$ (If-Then-Else)

- $S \to \text{if } E \text{ then } S \mid \text{if } E \text{ then } S \text{ else } S \mid \text{other}$

#### 3.3.2 Target String $w$

$$w = \text{if } e_1 \text{ then if } e_2 \text{ then } s_1 \text{ else } s_2$$

#### 3.3.3 Two Parse Trees

- **Interpretation A:** `else` binds to the **inner** `if` (nearest unmatched then) — Standard C/Java convention.
- **Interpretation B:** `else` binds to the **outer** `if` — Different structural grouping.

Since both parse trees are valid, $G_3$ is **ambiguous**.

#### 3.3.4 Disambiguated Grammar $G_4$ (Forces else → nearest if)

- $S \to M \mid U$
- $M \to \text{if } E \text{ then } M \text{ else } M \mid \text{other}$
- $U \to \text{if } E \text{ then } S \mid \text{if } E \text{ then } M \text{ else } U$

> [!NOTE]
> $M$ denotes a *matched* statement (every `then` has a matching `else`), while $U$ denotes an *unmatched* statement. This guarantees each `else` is matched to the **nearest preceding unmatched `then`**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Ambiguity Comparison Table

| **Grammar** | **Language Generated** | **Ambiguous?** | **Inherently Ambiguous?** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $G_1$ (Flat arithmetic) | Arithmetic expressions | **Yes** | No (disambiguatable via $G_2$) |
| $G_2$ (Layered arithmetic) | Arithmetic expressions | No | N/A |
| $G_3$ (Dangling else) | Nested if-then-else | **Yes** | No (disambiguatable via $G_4$) |
| $G_4$ (Matched/Unmatched) | Nested if-then-else | No | N/A |
| $L = \{a^n b^n c^m d^m \mid n,m \geq 1\} \cup \{a^n b^m c^m d^n \mid n,m \geq 1\}$ | Mixed | Every grammar is ambiguous | **Yes (Inherently Ambiguous)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Two Distinct Parse Trees for `id + id * id`

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA["E"]
    nodeB["E"]
    nodeC1["+"]
    nodeD["E"]
    nodeE1["E"]
    nodeF1["*"]
    nodeG1["id"]
    nodeH1["id"]
    nodeI1["id"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC1
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeG1
    nodeD --> nodeE1
    nodeD --> nodeF1
    nodeD --> nodeI1
    nodeE1 --> nodeH1

    style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style nodeF1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
```

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeX["E"]
    nodeY["E"]
    nodeZ["*"]
    nodeP["E"]
    nodeQ["E"]
    nodeR1["+"]
    nodeS1["id"]
    nodeT1["id"]
    nodeU1["id"]

    nodeX --> nodeY
    nodeX --> nodeZ
    nodeX --> nodeP
    nodeY --> nodeP
    nodeY --> nodeR1
    nodeY --> nodeS1
    nodeP --> nodeT1
    nodeP --> nodeU1

    style nodeX fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeZ fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style nodeR1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - **Tree 1** (left): Root operator is `+`, meaning `*` binds tighter — yields `id + (id * id)`.
> - **Tree 2** (right): Root operator is `*`, meaning `+` binds tighter — yields `(id + id) * id`.
> - Both trees derive the **same string** but produce **different evaluation orders**, proving $G_1$ is ambiguous.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Dangling Else: Two Parse Trees

```mermaid
graph TB
    A1["S"]
    B1["if e1 then S"]
    C1["if e2 then s1 else s2"]

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> C1

    style A1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style C1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```

```mermaid
graph TB
    A2["S"]
    B2["if e1 then S else S"]
    C2["if e2 then s1"]
    D2["s2"]

    A2 --> B2
    B2 --> C2
    B2 --> D2

    style A2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style D2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Tree 1** binds `else` to the inner `if` (nearest convention).
> **Tree 2** binds `else` to the outer `if` (alternative grouping).
> Both yield the same string — hence $G_3$ is ambiguous.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Disambiguation Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    raw["Raw Ambiguous Grammar G"] --> analyze["Step 1: Identify Conflicting Productions"]
    analyze --> classify["Step 2: Classify Ambiguity Source"]
    classify --> prec["Step 3a: Enforce Precedence"]
    classify --> assoc["Step 3b: Enforce Associativity"]
    classify --> match["Step 3c: Apply Matching Rules"]
    prec --> merge["Step 4: Merge Equivalent Structures"]
    assoc --> merge
    match --> merge
    merge --> final["Unambiguous Grammar G-prime"]
    final --> verify["Step 5: Verify L(G-prime) equals L(G)"]

    style raw fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style final fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style verify fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define an **ambiguous grammar**. State the two equivalent criteria used to detect ambiguity in a context-free grammar.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A Context-Free Grammar $G$ is called **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ that has **more than one distinct leftmost derivation**.
>
> **Two Equivalent Criteria for Ambiguity Detection:**
> 1. **Multiple Leftmost Derivations:** Existence of $\geq 2$ distinct leftmost derivations for some $w \in L(G)$.
> 2. **Multiple Parse Trees:** Existence of $\geq 2$ distinct parse trees rooted at $S$ that yield the same string $w$.
>
> *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for both criteria = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between an **ambiguous grammar** and an **inherently ambiguous language** with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Ambiguous Grammar** | **Inherently Ambiguous Language** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | At least one string has two parse trees | **Every** grammar for the language is ambiguous |
| Scope | Property of a specific grammar $G$ | Property of the language $L$ itself |
| Disambiguation | May be possible by rewriting rules | **Impossible** — no unambiguous grammar exists |
| Example Grammar | $E \to E+E \mid E*E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$ | $L = \{a^n b^n c^m d^m\} \cup \{a^n b^m c^m d^n\}$ |
| Example Language | Arithmetic expressions (disambiguatable) | The union language above |

*(2 marks for tabular distinction + 1 mark for example = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**Q.A (a)** Consider the grammar $G$ with productions:
- $S \to A \mid B$
- $A \to aAb \mid ab$
- $B \to aBb \mid aBb$

Prove that $G$ is **ambiguous** by exhibiting two distinct parse trees for the string $w = aabb$. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.A (b)** For the ambiguous arithmetic grammar $E \to E+E \mid E*E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$, construct an **equivalent unambiguous grammar** that respects standard precedence ($*$ over $+$) and left-associativity. Show the unique parse tree for $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id} - \text{id}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify candidate string.** Take $w = aabb \in L(G)$.

**Step 2 — First Leftmost Derivation (via non-terminal $A$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow A \\
  &\Rightarrow aAb \\
  &\Rightarrow a\,ab\,b \\
  &= aabb
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Second Leftmost Derivation (via non-terminal $B$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow B \\
  &\Rightarrow aBb \\
  &\Rightarrow a\,ab\,b \\
  &= aabb
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Two Distinct Parse Trees:**

**Tree 1 (via $A$):**
$$S \to A \to aAb \to aabb$$

**Tree 2 (via $B$):**
$$S \to B \to aBb \to aabb$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** The string $aabb$ admits two structurally different parse trees. Therefore, $G$ is **ambiguous**. $\blacksquare$

*(Distribution: 2 marks derivation 1, 2 marks derivation 2, 2 marks parse tree sketches, 1 mark conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify ambiguity source:** The flat productions $E \to E+E$ and $E \to E*E$ allow any operator to be the root, causing precedence and associativity conflicts.

**Step 2 — Disambiguated Grammar $G'$ (Three-tier structure):**

- $E \to E + T \mid T$
- $T \to T * F \mid F$
- $F \to (E) \mid \text{id}$

**Step 3 — Precedence Enforcement:** Multiplication ($*$) is at a deeper level ($T \to T*F$), so it binds *tighter* than addition ($+$ at level $E$).

**Step 4 — Left-Associativity Enforcement:** Recursion on the **left** in $E \to E+T$ and $T \to T*F$ enforces left-grouping.

**Step 5 — Unique Derivation for $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id} - \text{id}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\Rightarrow E + T \\
  &\Rightarrow T + T \\
  &\Rightarrow F + T \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + T \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + T * F \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + F * F \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * F \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id} \\
&\text{(continuing for } - \text{id)} \\
  &\Rightarrow E - T \text{ at the outer level} \\
  &\Rightarrow \cdots \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id} - \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Unique Parse Tree (Mermaid Visualization):**

```mermaid
graph TB
    rootE["E"]
    subE["E + T"]
    leftT["T"]
    minusT["T"]
    minusF["F"]
    minusID["id"]
    starT["T * F"]
    starLeftT["T"]
    starLeftF["F"]
    starLeftID["id"]
    starRightF["F"]
    starRightID["id"]

    rootE --> subE
    subE --> leftT
    subE --> minusT
    leftT --> minusF
    minusF --> minusID
    minusT --> starT
    starT --> starLeftT
    starT --> starRightF
    starLeftT --> starLeftF
    starLeftF --> starLeftID
    starRightF --> starRightID

    style rootE fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

The grouping produced is: $\big((\text{id}) + ((\text{id}) * (\text{id})) - (\text{id})\big)$, equivalent to $((\text{id} + (\text{id} * \text{id})) - \text{id})$ with standard precedence.

**Step 7 — Verification:** $L(G') = L(G)$ (same language), but $G'$ is **unambiguous** (unique parse tree for every string).

*(Distribution: 2 marks identifying ambiguity, 2 marks new grammar, 2 marks derivation, 1 mark parse tree = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Q.B (a)** Define the **dangling else problem**. Show with parse trees why the grammar $S \to \text{if } E \text{ then } S \mid \text{if } E \text{ then } S \text{ else } S \mid \text{other}$ is ambiguous for the string `if e1 then if e2 then s1 else s2`. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.B (b)** Construct an **equivalent unambiguous grammar** that resolves the dangling else ambiguity. Prove its correctness by deriving the same string uniquely. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** The **dangling else problem** is a classic ambiguity in programming language grammars where an `else` clause can structurally attach to **more than one enclosing `if` statement**, producing multiple valid parse trees for the same conditional statement.

**Two Parse Trees for `if e1 then if e2 then s1 else s2`:**

**Parse Tree 1 — else binds to inner if:**
$$S \Rightarrow \text{if } e_1 \text{ then } S \Rightarrow \text{if } e_1 \text{ then } \big(\text{if } e_2 \text{ then } s_1 \text{ else } s_2\big)$$

**Parse Tree 2 — else binds to outer if:**
$$S \Rightarrow \text{if } e_1 \text{ then } S \text{ else } S \Rightarrow \text{if } e_1 \text{ then } (\text{if } e_2 \text{ then } s_1) \text{ else } s_2$$

Both parse trees yield the same string, hence the grammar is **ambiguous**.

*(Distribution: 2 marks definition, 3 marks parse trees, 2 marks conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Disambiguated Grammar (3 Marks):**

- $S \to M \mid U$
- $M \to \text{if } E \text{ then } M \text{ else } M \mid \text{other}$
- $U \to \text{if } E \text{ then } S \mid \text{if } E \text{ then } M \text{ else } U$

**Intuition:** $M$ = *matched* statement (every `then` paired with an `else`); $U$ = *unmatched* statement (has pending `then` awaiting future `else`).

**Unique Derivation (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow U \quad \text{(start symbol, unmatched begins)} \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{if } e_1 \text{ then } S \quad \text{(outer if-then)} \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{if } e_1 \text{ then } U \quad \text{(inner begins)} \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{if } e_1 \text{ then } \text{if } e_2 \text{ then } S \quad \text{(innermost)} \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{if } e_1 \text{ then } \text{if } e_2 \text{ then } s_1 \quad \text{(substitute s1)} \\
  &\Rightarrow \text{if } e_1 \text{ then } \text{if } e_2 \text{ then } s_1 \text{ else } s_2 \quad \text{(close with else s2 via M)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Every string has exactly **one derivation** under $G'$. The `else` is forced to bind to the **nearest unmatched `then`**, matching standard C/Java semantics.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Grammar Ambiguity with Language Ambiguity:** A language is ambiguous only if **every** grammar for it is ambiguous. Many students wrongly claim a language is inherently ambiguous just because *one* grammar is.
> 2. **Forgetting to Check Multiple Strings:** A grammar is ambiguous if **at least one** string is ambiguous. Students often try to prove ambiguity for all strings unnecessarily, wasting time.
> 3. **Ignoring Leftmost vs Rightmost:** KTU expects **leftmost derivations** as the standard proof technique. Mixing both without justification loses marks.
> 4. **Missing Equivalence Verification in Disambiguation:** When rewriting an ambiguous grammar, you **must** verify $L(G') = L(G)$. Examiners explicitly check this.
> 5. **Drawing Parse Trees Without Root-to-Leaf Order:** Parse trees must be drawn with $S$ at the root, terminals at leaves, and internal nodes as non-terminals. Sloppy diagrams cost 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Dangling Else Disambiguation Mistake:** Forgetting to distinguish *matched* ($M$) from *unmatched* ($U$) statements leads to an incorrect disambiguated grammar.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ambiguous Grammars**

- **Definition:** A CFG is ambiguous $\iff$ $\exists\, w \in L(G)$ with $\geq 2$ distinct parse trees (equivalently, $\geq 2$ leftmost derivations).
- **Inherent Ambiguity:** Property of a **language**, not a grammar. The union $\{a^n b^n c^m d^m\} \cup \{a^n b^m c^m d^n\}$ is the classic inherently ambiguous CFL example.
- **Equivalence Theorem:** $N_{LMD}(w) = N_{RMD}(w) = N_{PT}(w)$ for any CFG and any string.
- **The Big Ambiguous Grammar:** $E \to E+E \mid E*E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$ — used in nearly every KTU exam.
- **The Standard Disambiguation:** Introduce layered non-terminals ($E \to E+T \mid T$, $T \to T*F \mid F$, $F \to (E) \mid \text{id}$) to enforce precedence ($* > +$) and left-associativity.
- **Dangling Else Fix:** Split statements into **Matched** ($M$) and **Unmatched** ($U$) categories; force each `else` to attach to the nearest preceding unmatched `then`.
- **Undecidability Note (Advanced):** It is **undecidable** in general whether an arbitrary CFG is ambiguous — no algorithm exists that works for all grammars.
- **Compiler Relevance:** Ambiguity in programming language grammars is resolved via precedence declarations, associativity rules, and one-token lookahead (LL(1), LR(1)) parsing.
- **KTU Favorite Questions:** (1) Disambiguate $E \to E+E \mid E*E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$. (2) Show dangling else ambiguity and fix it. (3) Prove inherent ambiguity for the union language.
- **Verification Always Required:** After disambiguation, **always** confirm $L(G') = L(G)$ — this is a mandatory step examiners look for.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CYK Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** CYK Algorithm ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** The CYK (Cocke–Younger–Kasami) Algorithm is a **membership-testing algorithm** for Context-Free Grammars (CFG). It is a **decision procedure** that determines whether a given string $w \in \Sigma^*$ is generated by a CFG $G$, i.e., whether $w \in L(G)$. ### 1.1 Formal Definition Let $G = (V, T, P, S)$ be a Context-Free Grammar in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**. For every production in $P$, exactly one of the following must hold: $$A \rightarrow BC \quad \text{(where } A, B, C \in V \text{)}$$ $$A \rightarrow a \quad \text{(where } A \in V, \; a \in T \cup \{\varepsilon\} \text{)}$$ The **CYK Algorithm** is a dynamic programming procedure that, given an input string $w = a_1 a_2 \ldots a_n$ of length $n$, decides whether $S \Rightarrow^* w$ using a two-dimensional table $T[i, j]$ where $1 \leq i \leq n$ and $1 \leq j \leq n - i + 1$. > [!NOTE] > **Why CNF is Mandatory:** The CYK algorithm structurally relies on the fact that every derivation in CNF either **terminates in one step** ($A \rightarrow a$) or **branches into exactly two non-terminals** ($A \rightarrow BC$). This binary branching is what allows the elegant binary split used during table filling. ### 1.2 The Triangular Table $T[i, j]$ Each cell $T[i, j]$ stores the **set of non-terminals** that can derive the substring $w_i w_{i+1} \ldots w_{i+j-1}$ (the substring of length $j$ starting at position $i$). $$\text{Formal definition: } T[i, j] = \{A \in V \mid A \Rightarrow^* w_i w_{i+1} \ldots w_{i+j-1}\}$$ The string $w \in L(G)$ **if and only if** $S \in T[1, n]$. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Word Search Puzzle" > [!TIP] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are given a long word (the input string) and a dictionary of grammar rules (the CFG in CNF). You want to know if this word is "grammatically valid." > > - **Column index $i$** = the starting position of your substring puzzle piece. > - **Row index $j$** = how many letters long this piece is. > - **Each cell** = a "label box" listing all the grammatical categories (non-terminals) that could plausibly produce this piece. > - **Bottom-up filling:** You start with single letters (you know which categories produce a single 'a' or 'b'), then keep combining adjacent puzzle pieces. If two adjacent pieces can be labeled $B$ and $C$ respectively, and a rule says "$A \rightarrow BC$," then the combined piece can also be labeled $A$. > - **Final acceptance:** The whole word is valid only if the **starting symbol $S$** ends up in the topmost corner cell covering the entire string. This **piece-by-piece assembly** with **memorization of intermediate results** is exactly the spirit of dynamic programming that CYK exploits. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Algorithmic Foundation — The "Why" Behind the Binary Split For any CNF production $A \rightarrow BC$, a single derivation step always splits a non-terminal into **exactly two** children. If $A$ derives the substring $w_i \ldots w_{i+j-1}$, then there exists some split point $k$ ($1 \leq k < j$) such that: $$A \Rightarrow BC \Rightarrow^* w_i \ldots w_{i+k-1} \cdot w_{i+k} \ldots w_{i+j-1}$$ Therefore $B$ must derive the **left** substring of length $k$, and $C$ must derive the **right** substring of length $j - k$. The CYK algorithm enumerates every such split and uses **already-computed** smaller subproblems (the table entries below the current cell) to fill the current cell. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** This is precisely the **optimal substructure** property that makes dynamic programming applicable. Once $T[i, k]$ and $T[i+k, j-k]$ are known, computing $T[i, j]$ requires no further recursion — only a lookup against the productions of $G$. ### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Rules The algorithm fills the table **bottom-up** (increasing $j$, i.e., increasing substring length): - **Base Case ($j = 1$):** For each position $i$, examine every terminal production: $$T[i, 1] = \{A \in V \mid A \rightarrow a_i \in P\}$$ - **Recursive Case ($j \geq 2$):** For every split point $k$ with $1 \leq k < j$, and for every production $A \rightarrow BC$: $$T[i, j] = T[i, j] \cup \{A \mid \exists\, k,\; B \in T[i, k] \text{ and } C \in T[i+k, j-k]\}$$ - **Final Decision:** $$\text{Accepted if } S \in T[1, n]; \quad \text{Rejected otherwise.}$$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Mathematical Form | Constraint / Range | |---|---|---| | Grammar Form (CNF) | $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | $A, B, C \in V$; $a \in T \cup \{\varepsilon\}$ | | Cell Definition | $T[i, j] = \{A \mid A \Rightarrow^* w_i \ldots w_{i+j-1}\}$ | $1 \leq i \leq n$, $1 \leq j \leq n - i + 1$ | | Base Case | $T[i, 1] = \{A \mid A \rightarrow a_i\}$ | All positions $i$ with $a_i$ matching | | Recursive Case | $T[i, j] = \bigcup_{k=1}^{j-1} \{A \rightarrow BC \mid B \in T[i, k],\; C \in T[i+k, j-k]\}$ | Loop over all split points $k$ | | Membership Test | $w \in L(G) \iff S \in T[1, n]$ | Decision at top corner | | Time Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(n^3 \cdot \vert P \vert)$ | Where $n = \vert w \vert$ and $\vert P \vert$ = number of productions | | Space Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | Triangular table of size $\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | ### 2.4 Why $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$? — Complexity Derivation Intuition - There are $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ cells in the triangular table. - For each cell of length $j$, there are $j - 1$ split points — summed over all cells, this gives $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ total work. - The $\vert P \vert$ factor accounts for checking each production at each split. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Area | How CYK is Used | |---|---| | **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** | Parsing English sentences using probabilistic CFGs (e.g., PCFG parsers) | | **Compiler Design** | Syntax verification and ambiguity detection in programming language front-ends | | **Bioinformatics** | RNA secondary structure prediction (Stochastic CYK variant) | | **Formal Verification** | Model checking for context-free properties in software/hardware systems | | **Linguistic Typology** | Testing word membership in formal grammar research corpora | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — Complete CYK Trace **Given CFG in CNF:** $$S \rightarrow AB \;\vert\; BC$$ $$A \rightarrow BA \;\vert\; a$$ $$B \rightarrow CC \;\vert\; b$$ $$C \rightarrow AB \;\vert\; a$$ **Input String:** $w = aab$ (length $n = 3$) **Step 1 — Initialize Table for $j = 1$ (Length 1 substrings)** Each cell $T[i, 1]$ contains all non-terminals that produce the single terminal $a_i$ directly. $$T[1, 1]: a_1 = a \;\Rightarrow\; A \rightarrow a \text{ and } C \rightarrow a \;\Rightarrow\; T[1, 1] = \{A, C\}$$ $$T[2, 1]: a_2 = a \;\Rightarrow\; T[2, 1] = \{A, C\}$$ $$T[3, 1]: a_3 = b \;\Rightarrow\; B \rightarrow b \;\Rightarrow\; T[3, 1] = \{B\}$$ **Step 2 — Fill Row $j = 2$ (Length 2 substrings)** **Compute $T[1, 2]$ for substring "aa" (split point $k = 1$):** $$T[1, 2] = \{A \rightarrow BC \mid B \in T[1, 1],\; C \in T[2, 1]\}$$ - $T[1, 1] = \{A, C\}$, $T[2, 1] = \{A, C\}$ - Possible $(B, C)$ pairs: $(A, A), (A, C), (C, A), (C, C)$ - Scan productions: $B \rightarrow CC$ matches $(C, C)$ ✓ $$T[1, 2] = \{B\}$$ **Compute $T[2, 2]$ for substring "ab" (split point $k = 1$):** - $T[2, 1] = \{A, C\}$, $T[3, 1] = \{B\}$ - Possible $(B, C)$ pairs: $(A, B), (C, B)$ - Scan productions: - $S \rightarrow AB$ matches $(A, B)$ ✓ - $C \rightarrow AB$ matches $(A, B)$ ✓ $$T[2, 2] = \{S, C\}$$ **Step 3 — Fill Row $j = 3$ (Length 3 substring, the entire string)** **Compute $T[1, 3]$ for substring "aab" — enumerate ALL split points:** **Split $k = 1$:** Substrings of lengths 1 and 2 → $T[1, 1] = \{A, C\}$ and $T[2, 2] = \{S, C\}$ - Possible $(B, C)$ pairs: $(A, S), (A, C), (C, S), (C, C)$ - Scan productions: - $B \rightarrow CC$ matches $(C, C)$ ✓ **Split $k = 2$:** Substrings of lengths 2 and 1 → $T[1, 2] = \{B\}$ and $T[3, 1] = \{B\}$ - Possible $(B, C)$ pairs: $(B, B)$ - Scan productions: - No production has form $X \rightarrow BB$. ✗ **Combining both splits:** $$T[1, 3] = \{B\}$$ **Step 4 — Final Decision** Since $T[1, 3] = \{B\}$ and the start symbol $S \notin T[1, 3]$: $$\boxed{w = aab \notin L(G) \quad \text{(String REJECTED)}}$$ ### 3.2 Verification via Direct Derivation Attempt To confirm, can we derive "aab" from $S$? $$S \rightarrow AB \rightarrow \ldots$$ For the final $AB$ to yield "aab", we need: - $A \Rightarrow^* a$ (one terminal) and $B \Rightarrow^* ab$, OR - $A \Rightarrow^* aa$ and $B \Rightarrow^* b$ From the table, $T[1, 2] = \{B\}$ means $B$ is the **only** non-terminal derivable from "aa", and $B$ requires $CC$ which needs two $C$'s each producing a single terminal — but then $B \Rightarrow CC \Rightarrow aa$. ✓ So $S \rightarrow AB$ requires $A \Rightarrow aa$ (impossible, since $T[1,2] = \{B\}$ does not contain $A$) and $B \Rightarrow b$ (which is one terminal, not "ab"). **Conclusion confirmed:** No valid parse exists; "aab" is rejected. ✓ ### 3.3 Master Reference Table — CYK Trace Summary | Cell $T[i, j]$ | Substring | Splits Considered | Non-Terminals Added | Final Cell | |---|---|---|---|---| | $T[1, 1]$ | "a" | (base) | $A \rightarrow a$, $C \rightarrow a$ | $\{A, C\}$ | | $T[2, 1]$ | "a" | (base) | $A \rightarrow a$, $C \rightarrow a$ | $\{A, C\}$ | | $T[3, 1]$ | "b" | (base) | $B \rightarrow b$ | $\{B\}$ | | $T[1, 2]$ | "aa" | $k=1$ | $B \rightarrow CC$ via $(C, C)$ | $\{B\}$ | | $T[2, 2]$ | "ab" | $k=1$ | $S \rightarrow AB$, $C \rightarrow AB$ via $(A, B)$ | $\{S, C\}$ | | $T[1, 3]$ | "aab" | $k=1, k=2$ | $B \rightarrow CC$ via $(C, C)$ | $\{B\}$ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 CYK Algorithm Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start CYK Algorithm]) --> inputCheck{CFG in CNF?} inputCheck -- No --> convCNF[Convert CFG to CNF
Eliminate epsilon, unit,
and useless productions] convCNF --> readString inputCheck -- Yes --> readString[Read input string w
of length n] readString --> initTable[Initialize triangular
table T of size n x n] initTable --> baseLoop[For j = 1: Fill T i,1
with non-terminals that
produce terminal a_i] baseLoop --> rowLoop{j less than n?} rowLoop -- Yes --> splitLoop[For each cell T i,j
try all split points k
from 1 to j-1] splitLoop --> prodCheck{Search productions
A -> BC where
B in T i,k and
C in T i+k, j-k} prodCheck -- Match found --> addNT[Add A to T i,j] prodCheck -- No match --> nextSplit addNT --> nextSplit[Try next split point] nextSplit --> rowLoop rowLoop -- No, all rows done --> finalCheck{S in T 1,n?} finalCheck -- Yes --> accept([String ACCEPTED
w is in L G]) finalCheck -- No --> reject([String REJECTED
w is NOT in L G]) ``` ### 4.2 CYK Triangular Table Visual Representation ```mermaid graph TB subgraph BottomRow["j = 1: Single Terminals"] cell11["T[1,1]
w1 = a
{A, C}"] cell21["T[2,1]
w2 = a
{A, C}"] cell31["T[3,1]
w3 = b
{B}"] end subgraph MiddleRow["j = 2: Length-2 Substrings"] cell12["T[1,2]
'aa'
{B}"] cell22["T[2,2]
'ab'
{S, C}"] end subgraph TopRow["j = 3: Entire String"] cell13["T[1,3]
'aab'
{B}"] end cell11 --- cell12 cell12 --- cell13 cell21 --- cell22 cell22 --- cell13 cell31 --- cell13 style cell13 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px style cell12 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px style cell22 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px style cell11 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px style cell21 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px style cell31 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Functional Architecture — CNF Conversion Pipeline (Pre-Requisite) ```mermaid flowchart LR cfgInput[Arbitrary CFG G] --> step1[Step 1: Eliminate
epsilon-productions
A -> epsilon] step1 --> step2[Step 2: Eliminate
unit productions
A -> B] step2 --> step3[Step 3: Eliminate
useless symbols] step3 --> step4[Step 4: Convert terminals
in long productions
e.g. A -> BbC becomes
A -> B X C, X -> b] step4 --> step5[Step 5: Break long
productions to length 2
e.g. A -> BCD becomes
A -> B Y, Y -> CD] step5 --> cnfOut[Pure CNF Grammar
Ready for CYK] style cfgInput fill:#e1f5ff style cnfOut fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:3px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The red-highlighted cell `T[1, 3]` is the **decision cell**. Since it contains $\{B\}$ and not $S$, the string "aab" is rejected. The arrows visualize how each longer-substring cell is computed from two smaller adjacent cells below it (one to the lower-left, one to the lower-right). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** **What is Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)? Why is it a necessary pre-requisite for the CYK algorithm?** **Model Answer:** A Context-Free Grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)** if every production in $P$ is of one of the following two forms: $$A \rightarrow BC \quad \text{where } A, B, C \in V$$ $$A \rightarrow a \quad \text{where } A \in V, \; a \in T \cup \{\varepsilon\}$$ CNF is mandatory for CYK because: 1. **Binary Branching Constraint:** Every production has either one terminal or exactly two non-terminals on the RHS. This guarantees that **every parse tree is a binary tree**, enabling a clean recursive decomposition of any substring into two smaller substrings. 2. **Bounded Recursion Depth:** The recursive case $T[i, j]$ always splits into $T[i, k]$ and $T[i+k, j-k]$ for $1 \leq k < j$, with both substrings strictly shorter than $j$. This guarantees termination. 3. **Direct Terminal Lookup:** The base case $T[i, 1]$ is simply the set $\{A \mid A \rightarrow a_i\}$, which requires the "single terminal" form $A \rightarrow a$. Without CNF, productions like $A \rightarrow BCD$ or $A \rightarrow aB$ would require handling more than two children per node, breaking the elegant binary dynamic-programming structure. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** **State and explain the time and space complexity of the CYK algorithm.** **Model Answer:** For an input string $w$ of length $n$ and a CFG with $|P|$ productions: - **Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(n^3 \cdot |P|)$ - There are $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ cells in the triangular CYK table. - For each cell of length $j$, there are $(j - 1)$ split points. - Total splits across all cells: $\sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} k = \mathcal{O}(n^3)$. - Each split point checks all productions, contributing the $|P|$ factor. - **Space Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ - The triangular table has $\frac{n(n+1)}{2} = \mathcal{O}(n^2)$ cells. - Each cell stores a set of non-terminals from $V$, but $|V|$ is treated as a constant relative to $n$. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]** Consider the following CFG in CNF: $$S \rightarrow AB \;\vert\; BC$$ $$A \rightarrow BA \;\vert\; a$$ $$B \rightarrow CC \;\vert\; b$$ $$C \rightarrow AB \;\vert\; a$$ **(a)** Construct the CYK table and determine whether the string $w = baba$ belongs to $L(G)$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the role of the **base case** and **recursive case** in the CYK algorithm with reference to this example. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q3(a): Step-by-Step Trace for $w = baba$ **Step 1 — Fill Base Row $j = 1$ (Single Terminals)** $$T[1, 1]: b_1 = b \;\Rightarrow\; B \rightarrow b \;\Rightarrow\; T[1, 1] = \{B\}$$ $$T[2, 1]: b_2 = a \;\Rightarrow\; A \rightarrow a,\; C \rightarrow a \;\Rightarrow\; T[2, 1] = \{A, C\}$$ $$T[3, 1]: b_3 = b \;\Rightarrow\; T[3, 1] = \{B\}$$ $$T[4, 1]: b_4 = a \;\Rightarrow\; T[4, 1] = \{A, C\}$$ **Step 2 — Fill Row $j = 2$** **Cell $T[1, 2]$ for "ba":** Split $k = 1$: $T[1, 1] = \{B\}$, $T[2, 1] = \{A, C\}$ - Pairs: $(B, A), (B, C)$ - $A \rightarrow BA$ matches $(B, A)$ ✓ $$T[1, 2] = \{A\}$$ **Cell $T[2, 2]$ for "ab":** Split $k = 1$: $T[2, 1] = \{A, C\}$, $T[3, 1] = \{B\}$ - Pairs: $(A, B), (C, B)$ - $S \rightarrow AB$ matches $(A, B)$ ✓ - $C \rightarrow AB$ matches $(A, B)$ ✓ $$T[2, 2] = \{S, C\}$$ **Cell $T[3, 2]$ for "ba":** Same logic as $T[1, 2]$ $$T[3, 2] = \{A\}$$ **Step 3 — Fill Row $j = 3$** **Cell $T[1, 3]$ for "bab":** Try $k = 1, 2$ - $k = 1$: $T[1, 1] = \{B\}$, $T[2, 2] = \{S, C\}$ → No production starts with $B$ and produces an $S$ or $C$ child on the right. - $k = 2$: $T[1, 2] = \{A\}$, $T[3, 1] = \{B\}$ → $A \rightarrow BA$ matches ✓ $$T[1, 3] = \{A\}$$ **Cell $T[2, 3]$ for "aba":** Try $k = 1, 2$ - $k = 1$: $T[2, 1] = \{A, C\}$, $T[3, 2] = \{A\}$ → $C \rightarrow AB$ needs $B$ in $T[3, 2]$, but only $A$ is there. ✗ - $k = 2$: $T[2, 2] = \{S, C\}$, $T[4, 1] = \{A, C\}$ → $S \rightarrow AB$ needs $A$ in $T[2, 2]$, but $A \notin \{S, C\}$. $C \rightarrow AB$ similarly fails. ✗ $$T[2, 3] = \emptyset$$ **Step 4 — Fill Row $j = 4$ (Entire String)** **Cell $T[1, 4]$ for "baba":** Try $k = 1, 2, 3$ - $k = 1$: $T[1, 1] = \{B\}$, $T[2, 3] = \emptyset$ → No candidates possible. ✗ - $k = 2$: $T[1, 2] = \{A\}$, $T[3, 2] = \{A\}$ → $A \rightarrow BA$ needs $B$, not $A$. ✗ - $k = 3$: $T[1, 3] = \{A\}$, $T[4, 1] = \{A, C\}$ → $A \rightarrow BA$ needs $B$, not $A$. ✗ $$T[1, 4] = \emptyset$$ **Final Decision:** Since $S \notin T[1, 4] = \emptyset$: $$\boxed{w = baba \notin L(G) \quad \text{(REJECTED)}}$$ --- #### **Model Solution to Q3(b): Base Case vs Recursive Case Analysis** **Base Case ($j = 1$):** The base case initializes the table for length-1 substrings (single terminals). For each position $i$, $T[i, 1]$ is computed by inspecting **terminal productions** $A \rightarrow a_i$ in $P$. In our example: $T[2, 1] = \{A, C\}$ because both $A \rightarrow a$ and $C \rightarrow a$ apply to position 2 where $a_2 = a$. This step is **$\mathcal{O}(n \cdot |P|)$** and forms the **foundation** for all higher rows. **Recursive Case ($j \geq 2$):** The recursive case generalizes the base case to longer substrings using the **divide-and-conquer** principle. For each cell $T[i, j]$, we iterate over **all split points** $k$ from $1$ to $j - 1$, treating the substring as the concatenation of a left part (length $k$) and a right part (length $j - k$). For our example, when computing $T[2, 2]$ for "ab": - The split $k = 1$ decomposes "ab" into "a" (length 1) and "b" (length 1). - We reuse the already-computed values $T[2, 1] = \{A, C\}$ and $T[3, 1] = \{B\}$. - Any production $X \rightarrow YZ$ with $Y \in \{A, C\}$ and $Z = B$ qualifies. This yields $S$ and $C$. **Why this works:** Because CNF guarantees that any derivation splits into **two sub-derivations**, the recursion mirrors the syntactic structure exactly. Each cell $T[i, j]$ aggregates all "parse tree roots" that could yield the substring $w_i \ldots w_{i+j-1}$. **Overall algorithmic structure:** $$T[i, j] = \underbrace{\{A \mid A \rightarrow a_i\}}_{j=1 \text{ (base)}} \;\cup\; \underbrace{\bigcup_{k=1}^{j-1} \{A \rightarrow BC \mid B \in T[i, k],\; C \in T[i+k, j-k]\}}_{j \geq 2 \text{ (recursive)}}$$ This bottom-up accumulation ensures **no exponential recomputation** — every substring is analyzed exactly once, yielding polynomial time. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q4. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]** Consider the CFG in CNF: $$S \rightarrow AB \;\vert\; a$$ $$A \rightarrow BB \;\vert\; b$$ $$B \rightarrow AB \;\vert\; a$$ **(a)** Using the CYK algorithm, check whether the string $w = aab$ is accepted by this grammar. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** If the above grammar had a production $S \rightarrow ACD$ where $C, D$ are non-terminals, explain why CYK would fail to apply directly. How would you convert such a production for CYK compatibility? **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q4(a): CYK for $w = aab$** **Step 1 — Base Row ($j = 1$):** $$T[1, 1]: a_1 = a \;\Rightarrow\; S \rightarrow a,\; B \rightarrow a \;\Rightarrow\; \{S, B\}$$ $$T[2, 1]: a_2 = a \;\Rightarrow\; \{S, B\}$$ $$T[3, 1]: a_3 = b \;\Rightarrow\; A \rightarrow b \;\Rightarrow\; \{A\}$$ **Step 2 — Row $j = 2$:** **$T[1, 2]$ for "aa":** Split $k = 1$: $\{S, B\} \times \{S, B\}$ - Pairs: $(S, S), (S, B), (B, S), (B, B)$ - $A \rightarrow BB$ matches $(B, B)$ ✓ $$T[1, 2] = \{A\}$$ **$T[2, 2]$ for "ab":** Split $k = 1$: $\{S, B\} \times \{A\}$ - Pairs: $(S, A), (B, A)$ - $S \rightarrow AB$ needs $A$ first, then $B$. $(S, A)$ fails. $(B, A)$ — no production has $X \rightarrow BA$. ✗ $$T[2, 2] = \emptyset$$ **Step 3 — Row $j = 3$ (Entire String):** **$T[1, 3]$ for "aab":** Try $k = 1, 2$ - $k = 1$: $\{S, B\}$ and $\emptyset$ → No candidates. ✗ - $k = 2$: $\{A\}$ and $\{A\}$ → $A \rightarrow BB$ needs $(B, B)$, not $(A, A)$. ✗ $$T[1, 3] = \emptyset$$ **Final Decision:** $$\boxed{S \notin T[1, 3] \;\Rightarrow\; w = aab \notin L(G) \quad \text{(REJECTED)}}$$ --- #### **Model Solution to Q4(b): Handling Non-CNF Productions** **Why CYK Fails Directly:** The CYK algorithm's recursive case assumes every production has the form $A \rightarrow BC$ (exactly two non-terminals). A production like $S \rightarrow ACD$ has **three** non-terminals on the RHS, violating this constraint. The CYK table only stores pairs $(B, C)$ via split points $k$, so a triple split cannot be directly represented. **Conversion Procedure (Length Reduction):** Introduce a new non-terminal $E$ and replace: $$S \rightarrow ACD \quad \xrightarrow{\text{Introduce } E} \quad S \rightarrow AE, \;\; E \rightarrow CD$$ If $E \rightarrow CD$ is still too long (e.g., $C \rightarrow DEF$), repeat: $$E \rightarrow CD \quad \xrightarrow{\text{Introduce } F} \quad E \rightarrow CF, \;\; F \rightarrow DE$$ Continue until all productions have exactly two non-terminals on the RHS. **General Rule:** Any production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \ldots X_m$ with $m \geq 3$ is replaced by: $$A \rightarrow X_1 Z_1, \quad Z_1 \rightarrow X_2 Z_2, \quad Z_2 \rightarrow X_3 Z_3, \quad \ldots, \quad Z_{m-2} \rightarrow X_{m-1} X_m$$ where $Z_1, Z_2, \ldots, Z_{m-2}$ are **fresh non-terminals** not in $V$. Additionally, if any $X_i$ is a terminal (e.g., $A \rightarrow aBC$), introduce a fresh non-terminal $Y_i$ with $Y_i \rightarrow a$ and replace $X_i$ by $Y_i$ in the production. After these transformations, the grammar is in CNF and CYK applies directly. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting CNF Conversion (–3 marks typical):** Students often attempt CYK on a grammar that is *not* in CNF. If the grammar contains $\varepsilon$-productions, unit productions, or terminals mixed with non-terminals (e.g., $A \rightarrow aB$), the CYK table construction is **undefined**. Always verify CNF first. > > 2. **Missing Split Points (–2 marks per missed split):** For a cell $T[i, j]$ with $j \geq 2$, **every** split point $k = 1, 2, \ldots, j-1$ must be considered. Students frequently check only $k = 1$ and miss derivations that require mid-string splits. > > 3. **Incorrect Production Matching (–2 marks):** When checking $A \rightarrow BC$, the order matters! $B$ must come from the **left** substring $T[i, k]$ and $C$ from the **right** substring $T[i+k, j-k]$. Reversing them is a common error. > > 4. **Off-by-One in Cell Indices (–1 to 2 marks):** Remember $T[i, j]$ indexes *position* $i$ and *length* $j$, not position $i$ and *end-position* $j$. The end position is $i + j - 1$. > > 5. **Forgetting to Check Final Cell (–1 mark):** Always explicitly state the contents of $T[1, n]$ and check whether $S \in T[1, n]$ in the conclusion. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for CYK Algorithm (Module 2):** > > - **Definition:** CYK is a **membership-testing** algorithm for CFGs in CNF using **dynamic programming**. > - **Pre-requisite:** Grammar MUST be in **Chomsky Normal Form** (rules $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$ only). > - **Data Structure:** Triangular table $T[i, j]$ with $1 \leq i \leq n$ and $1 \leq j \leq n - i + 1$. > - **Cell Meaning:** $T[i, j]$ = set of non-terminals that derive the substring starting at position $i$ with length $j$. > - **Base Case ($j = 1$):** $T[i, 1] = \{A \mid A \rightarrow a_i\}$ — only terminal productions count. > - **Recursive Case ($j \geq 2$):** $T[i, j] = \bigcup_{k=1}^{j-1} \{A \rightarrow BC \mid B \in T[i, k],\; C \in T[i+k, j-k]\}$. > - **Decision:** Accept iff $S \in T[1, n]$. > - **Complexity:** Time $\mathcal{O}(n^3 \cdot |P|)$, Space $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$. > - **Order Matters:** $B$ comes from left substring, $C$ from right substring in $A \rightarrow BC$. > - **All Splits Mandatory:** Iterate over every $k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, j-1\}$ for each cell. > - **Derivation Tree Implication:** A cell containing a non-terminal $X$ implies there exists a parse tree rooted at $X$ yielding that substring. > - **Ambiguity Indicator:** If $T[1, n]$ contains multiple non-terminals besides $S$ (or $S$ appears via multiple split choices), the grammar may be **ambiguous**. > - **Empty Set Notation:** A cell with $\emptyset$ means no non-terminal can derive that substring — useful early signal of rejection. > - **Memorization Trick:** Think "**B**efore **C**" — in $A \rightarrow BC$, the **left** side of the split ($B$) is computed **first** in the table layout (lower-left cell), and $C$ is the lower-right cell.

topics.title: Formal Definition of a Pushdown Automaton (PDA)
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Formal Definition of a Pushdown Automaton (PDA) # 📘 MODULE 3 — FORMAL DEFINITION OF A PUSHDOWN AUTOMATON (PDA) ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pushdown Automaton (PDA)** — A Pushdown Automaton is a **7-tuple mathematical model of computation** that augments a Finite Automaton with an auxiliary **unbounded stack (Last-In-First-Out memory)**. It precisely accepts the class of **Context-Free Languages (CFLs)** generated by Context-Free Grammars, thereby bridging regular language theory and general recursive language theory. Formally, a PDA is defined as the **7-tuple**: $$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$$ | Component | Symbol | Type | Description | |---|---|---|---| | **State Set** | $Q$ | Finite non-empty set | Set of all internal control states of the PDA | | **Input Alphabet** | $\Sigma$ | Finite non-empty set | Set of symbols appearing on the input tape | | **Stack Alphabet** | $\Gamma$ | Finite non-empty set | Set of symbols that can be pushed onto the stack | | **Transition Function** | $\delta$ | $Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times (\Gamma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \to \mathcal{P}(Q \times \Gamma^{*})$ | Defines the computational behaviour of the PDA | | **Start State** | $q_0$ | $q_0 \in Q$ | The unique state in which computation begins | | **Initial Stack Symbol** | $Z_0$ | $Z_0 \in \Gamma$ | The symbol placed on the stack before any input is read | | **Final States** | $F$ | $F \subseteq Q$ | A subset of accepting states (used in acceptance-by-final-state) | > [!NOTE] > The notation $\mathcal{P}(Q \times \Gamma^{*})$ denotes the **power set** of $Q \times \Gamma^{*}$, meaning that a single transition may map to **multiple possible (state, stack-string) pairs** — this is what gives rise to **non-determinism** in PDAs. ## 1.2 The Transition Function $\delta$ — Expanded The transition function consumes **three inputs** at each step: 1. The **current state** $q \in Q$ 2. The **current input symbol** $a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$ (where $\varepsilon$ means "no input consumed") 3. The **top symbol of the stack** $X \in \Gamma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$ (where $\varepsilon$ means "stack is not consulted") And produces a **finite set of pairs** $(p, \gamma)$ where: - $p \in Q$ is the new state - $\gamma \in \Gamma^{*}$ is the string of symbols that **replaces** the top of the stack The meaning of a transition $\delta(q, a, X) \ni (p, \gamma)$ is: > *"When the PDA is in state $q$, the next input symbol is $a$, and the top of the stack is $X$, it may move to state $p$ and replace $X$ on the top of the stack with the string $\gamma$."* > [!IMPORTANT] > The **$\varepsilon$ in the third argument** is critical. It permits the PDA to **make stack-only transitions** (called $\varepsilon$-transitions or stack moves) without consuming any input — essential for accepting languages like $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ when the input is exhausted but matching still needs verification. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **postman with a tall, vertical stack of letter trays** standing in a busy sorting office. The postman has: - 🧠 **A brain (Finite Control)**: remembers only a few things — e.g., *"I am currently sorting inward mail"* or *"I am currently sorting outward mail"*. He cannot remember an arbitrarily long history. - 📬 **A conveyor belt (Input Tape)**: an endless ribbon of envelopes with addresses arrives, one by one, from the left. He can only read the **leftmost unread envelope** at any time. - 📚 **A stack of trays (Pushdown Stack)**: an infinitely tall stack of trays. He can either **push** a new tray on top, **pop** (remove) the topmost tray, or **peek** at the topmost tray to decide what to do. He **cannot** see or access the trays in the middle of the stack without removing those above them. At each moment, the postman looks at: 1. **His current mental state** (what phase he is in), 2. **The next envelope's address symbol** (input), and 3. **The topmost tray's label** (stack top). Based on these three pieces of information, he decides to: - Change his mental state, - Move to the next envelope (or skip one), and - Either push, pop, or replace the top tray. The postman **accepts** the mail if, after reading the last envelope, his mental state is one of the designated "happy states" (final states) — OR — his tray stack is empty (empty-stack acceptance). This is precisely a **Pushdown Automaton**. ## 1.4 Why is a Stack Needed? A finite automaton alone **cannot count**. It cannot distinguish the language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ from $L = \{a^* b^*\}$ because it has no unbounded memory. The **stack provides unbounded memory** (though restricted to LIFO access) sufficient to count matched nested structures — exactly what Context-Free Languages describe. | Capability | Finite Automaton | Pushdown Automaton | |---|---|---| | States | Finite | Finite | | Memory | None (only state) | Unbounded **LIFO stack** | | Recognizes | Regular Languages | **Context-Free Languages** | | Counting | Cannot count arbitrarily | Can count matching symbols | | Power | $\subset$ | Strictly greater | # 📘 SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Step-by-Step Logic The execution of a PDA can be understood as a sequence of **instantaneous descriptions (IDs)**. Each ID captures a complete "snapshot" of the PDA's configuration. ### Step 1: Initial Configuration $$ID_0 = (q_0, w, Z_0)$$ The PDA begins in state $q_0$, with the entire input string $w \in \Sigma^*$ on the input tape, and the stack containing only $Z_0$. ### Step 2: Reading a Transition Rule A transition rule has the form: $$\delta(q, a, X) = \{(p_1, \gamma_1), (p_2, \gamma_2), \ldots, (p_k, \gamma_k)\}$$ This means: from state $q$, reading input $a$, with $X$ on stack-top, the PDA may **non-deterministically** choose any of the $k$ possible actions. ### Step 3: Stack Replacement Operation The string $\gamma$ **replaces** $X$ at the top of the stack. Three special cases: | Stack Action | $\gamma$ Value | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Push** $Y$ onto stack | $\gamma = YX$ or $\gamma = Y\alpha$ | New symbol added on top | | **Pop** $X$ from stack | $\gamma = \varepsilon$ | Top symbol is removed | | **No stack change** | $\gamma = X$ | Stack-top remains unchanged | > [!NOTE] > The convention used in KTU-standard texts: **the leftmost symbol of the stack string represents the top of the stack**. So if the stack contains $AXZ_0$ (top-to-bottom), we read it as: $A$ is on top, $X$ below, $Z_0$ at the bottom. ### Step 4: The "Turnstile" Notation $\vdash$ A single computational step is denoted by $\vdash$ (a "turnstile" or "yields" symbol): $$(q, aw, X\beta) \vdash (p, w, \gamma\beta)$$ $$\text{iff } (p, \gamma) \in \delta(q, a, X)$$ Here: - $q$ is the current state - $a$ is the input symbol being read (or $\varepsilon$) - $w$ is the rest of the input - $X$ is the top of the stack - $\beta$ is the rest of the stack (below $X$) - $p$ is the new state - $\gamma$ replaces $X$ on the top of the stack The reflexive-transitive closure $\vdash^{*}$ denotes **zero or more moves**. ### Step 5: Acceptance Criteria A PDA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ **accepts** a string $w \in \Sigma^*$ by **final state** if: $$(q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \alpha) \text{ for some } q \in F \text{ and } \alpha \in \Gamma^{*}$$ The string $w$ is **accepted by empty stack** if: $$(q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon) \text{ for some } q \in Q$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A string is in the language $L(M)$ if there exists **at least one** sequence of moves leading to acceptance. Non-determinism means a string is accepted if **any computation path** accepts it — not all paths. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formal Expression / Rule | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | PDA 7-tuple | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ | Definitional form | | 2 | Transition Function (general) | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times (\Gamma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \to \mathcal{P}(Q \times \Gamma^{*})$ | Non-deterministic PDA | | 3 | Deterministic PDA (DPDA) | $\mid \delta(q, a, X) \mid + \mid \delta(q, \varepsilon, X) \mid \leq 1$ for all $(q,a,X)$ | At most one move per configuration | | 4 | Single Move | $(q, aw, X\beta) \vdash (p, w, \gamma\beta)$ if $(p, \gamma) \in \delta(q, a, X)$ | Standard KTU convention | | 5 | $\varepsilon$-Move | $(q, w, X\beta) \vdash (p, w, \gamma\beta)$ if $(p, \gamma) \in \delta(q, \varepsilon, X)$ | No input consumed | | 6 | Final-State Acceptance | $(q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \alpha)$, $q \in F$ | String in $L(M)$ | | 7 | Empty-Stack Acceptance | $(q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$ | Stack fully emptied | | 8 | Language Accepted | $L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \alpha), q \in F\}$ | Set of accepted strings | | 9 | Power of PDA | $\mathcal{L}(\text{PDA}) = \text{Class of Context-Free Languages}$ | Strictly contains Regular Languages | | 10 | DPDA Subset | $\mathcal{L}(\text{DPDA}) \subsetneq \mathcal{L}(\text{PDA})$ | DCFL $\subsetneq$ CFL | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Pitfall — Convention on Stack-Top Direction**: Always clarify which side of the stack string represents the top. KTU's standard textbook convention (Hopcroft/Ullman style) places the **leftmost symbol** as the top. Some other texts use the opposite. Confusing this will cause **every** transition to be evaluated incorrectly in your exam. ## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Component | Component | Why is it needed? | How does it function? | |---|---|---| | $Q$ | Finite memory of "phases" of computation | Holds current control state | | $\Sigma$ | The PDA must know what symbols can appear on input | Defines the input vocabulary | | $\Gamma$ | The PDA's stack can hold a possibly different set of symbols than the input | Defines the stack vocabulary (often $\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$) | | $\delta$ | The actual "program" of the PDA | Maps (state, input, stack-top) → (new-state, new-stack-top) | | $q_0$ | A well-defined starting point | All computations begin here | | $Z_0$ | The stack must have a sentinel at the bottom | Prevents accidental empty stack at start; demarcates stack base | | $F$ | Defines which states count as "accepting" | Used only in final-state acceptance model | ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering | Field | Application of PDA Concept | |---|---| | **Compiler Design** | **Parser** for context-free grammars (e.g., recursive descent, SLR, LALR parsers are essentially PDAs) | | **XML / HTML Processing** | Validating tag nesting — e.g., `` — requires matching balanced tags (a PDA-style check) | | **Programming Language Syntax** | Checking balanced parentheses, brackets, braces — the textbook CFL $\{a^n b^n\}$ is exactly this | | **Network Protocols** | Validating properly nested protocol messages in packet headers | | **Bioinformatics** | Modelling **RNA secondary structure** — nested base-pairing is a CFL property | | **Natural Language Processing** | Parsing nested clauses in sentences (e.g., "the cat [that chased the mouse [that ate the cheese]] slept") | # 📘 SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Acceptance Trace for $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ We will construct a PDA for the language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ and derive the full acceptance trace for the string $w = aabb$. ### Step 1: Design the PDA Intuition: For every $a$ read from input, push an $A$ onto the stack. For every $b$ read, pop one $A$ from the stack. If the input ends and the stack is back to $Z_0$, accept. Define the PDA: $$M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b\}, \{a, Z_0\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_2\})$$ Stack alphabet $\Gamma = \{a, Z_0\}$. We use lowercase $a$ as a stack symbol (different from input $a$ — common technique). Let's rename the stack alphabet as $\{A, Z_0\}$ for clarity. So we have: $$M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b\}, \{A, Z_0\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_2\})$$ ### Step 2: Define the Transition Function $\delta$ The PDA uses three states: - $q_0$: Reading $a$'s and pushing $A$ for each - $q_1$: Reading $b$'s and popping $A$ for each - $q_2$: Final accepting state The transitions are: $$\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, AZ_0)\} \quad \text{— first } a: \text{ push } A, \text{ keep } Z_0 \text{ at bottom}$$ $$\delta(q_0, a, A) = \{(q_0, AA)\} \quad \text{— subsequent } a: \text{ push } A \text{ on top of } A$$ $$\delta(q_0, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\} \quad \text{— first } b: \text{ switch to state } q_1, \text{ pop } A$$ $$\delta(q_1, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\} \quad \text{— subsequent } b: \text{ pop } A$$ $$\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_2, Z_0)\} \quad \text{— input empty and only } Z_0 \text{ on stack: accept}$$ ### Step 3: Derive the Instantaneous Description (ID) Sequence for $w = aabb$ **Initial ID** (configuration before any moves): $$ID_0: (q_0, aabb, Z_0)$$ State $= q_0$, remaining input $= aabb$, stack-top $= Z_0$ (the only symbol). --- **Move 1**: Apply $\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, AZ_0)\}$ - Read $a$ from input — input becomes $abb$ - Replace $Z_0$ with $AZ_0$ on stack — stack-top is now $A$ - Stay in state $q_0$ $$ID_1: (q_0, abb, AZ_0)$$ --- **Move 2**: Apply $\delta(q_0, a, A) = \{(q_0, AA)\}$ - Read $a$ from input — input becomes $bb$ - Replace top $A$ with $AA$ — stack-top is now $A$, second-from-top is also $A$ - Stay in state $q_0$ $$ID_2: (q_0, bb, AAZ_0)$$ --- **Move 3**: Apply $\delta(q_0, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$ - Read $b$ from input — input becomes $b$ - Replace top $A$ with $\varepsilon$ (pop!) — stack-top is now $A$ - Switch to state $q_1$ $$ID_3: (q_1, b, AZ_0)$$ --- **Move 4**: Apply $\delta(q_1, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$ - Read $b$ from input — input becomes $\varepsilon$ (empty) - Pop top $A$ — stack-top is now $Z_0$ - Stay in state $q_1$ $$ID_4: (q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0)$$ --- **Move 5**: Apply $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_2, Z_0)\}$ - Consume no input (input remains $\varepsilon$) - Replace $Z_0$ with $Z_0$ (no change) - Move to final state $q_2$ $$ID_5: (q_2, \varepsilon, Z_0)$$ --- **Final Verdict**: State $q_2 \in F = \{q_2\}$, input is empty, computation halts. **String $aabb$ is accepted.** $\blacksquare$ ### Step 3.1: Consolidated ID Sequence $$(q_0, aabb, Z_0) \vdash (q_0, abb, AZ_0) \vdash (q_0, bb, AAZ_0) \vdash (q_1, b, AZ_0) \vdash (q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) \vdash (q_2, \varepsilon, Z_0)$$ ## 3.2 The Generalized Derivation Pattern for $L = \{a^n b^n\}$ For an arbitrary string $a^n b^n$, the ID trace pattern is: $$ \begin{aligned} (q_0, a^n b^n, Z_0) &\vdash (q_0, a^{n-1} b^n, AZ_0) \\ &\vdash (q_0, a^{n-2} b^n, A^2 Z_0) \\ &\vdash \ldots \vdash (q_0, b^n, A^n Z_0) \\ &\vdash (q_1, b^{n-1}, A^{n-1} Z_0) \\ &\vdash (q_1, b^{n-2}, A^{n-2} Z_0) \\ &\vdash \ldots \vdash (q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) \\ &\vdash (q_2, \varepsilon, Z_0) \end{aligned} $$ Total number of moves $= 2n + 1$ for input length $2n$. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Three Equivalent Views of a PDA Transition A single transition $\delta(q, a, X) = (p, \gamma)$ can be equivalently described in three notations: | Notation | Form | Reading | |---|---|---| | **Mathematical** | $\delta(q, a, X) = \{(p, \gamma)\}$ | Function-style | | **State-Transition** | $q \xrightarrow{a, X \to \gamma} p$ | Arrow on state diagram | | **Standard Textbook** | $\delta(q, a, X) = \{(p, \gamma)\}$ or $(p, \gamma) \in \delta(q, a, X)$ | "In" relation | > [!NOTE] > In KTU diagrams, the state-transition arrow is **most commonly used**. The label takes the form "$a, X \to \gamma$" meaning *"read $a$, pop $X$, push $\gamma$"*. For $\varepsilon$-moves, the label is "$\varepsilon, X \to \gamma$" (no input symbol). ## 3.4 Derivation — Why DPDA is Weaker than NPDA A **Deterministic PDA (DPDA)** requires: $$\forall q \in Q, \forall a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}, \forall X \in \Gamma \cup \{\varepsilon\}: \mid \delta(q, a, X) \mid \leq 1$$ But also a more subtle condition — for each $(q, X)$ pair, the PDA cannot have **both** an $a$-transition and an $\varepsilon$-transition available: $$\text{If } \delta(q, a, X) \neq \emptyset \text{ for some } a \in \Sigma, \text{ then } \delta(q, \varepsilon, X) = \emptyset$$ This restriction makes DPDA strictly weaker. The classic example is $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{*}\}$ (palindromes of even length), which is a CFL but **not** a DCFL — no DPDA can accept it. ## 3.5 Block Diagram of PDA Computation (Textual Schematic) ``` +----------+ +--------------------+ +----------+ | INPUT | a/X | FINITE CONTROL | X/γ | STACK | | TAPE | ------->| (State q ∈ Q) | ------> | (Γ*) | | (Σ*) | | | | Top ↓ | +----------+ +--------------------+ +----------+ ^ | ^ | v | | +---------------+ | | | NEXT STATE | | | | p ∈ Q | | | +---------------+ | | | +----- Read Head moves right -------- Pop / Push --+ ``` The cycle is: **Read input symbol → Look at stack-top → Apply $\delta$ → Move to new state → Modify stack-top → Move input head right (or stay for $\varepsilon$-moves)**. # 📘 SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid State Diagram — PDA for $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> s0 s0 : q0 Reading a's pushing A s1 : q1 Reading b's popping A s2 : q2 Accepting Final State s0 --> s0 : a, Z0 to AZ0\npush first A s0 --> s0 : a, A to AA\npush another A s0 --> s1 : b, A to epsilon\npop A, switch phase s1 --> s1 : b, A to epsilon\npop next A s1 --> s2 : epsilon, Z0 to Z0\nmatch complete accept s2 --> [*] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation**: Each arrow label uses the format `input, stackTop -> newStackTop`. For example, `a, A to AA` means: read input $a$, the top of the stack is $A$, replace it with $AA$ (effectively pushing another $A$). The `\n` creates a line break in Mermaid labels for readability. ## 4.2 Mermaid Functional Architecture — PDA as Block-Level System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputSubsystem["INPUT SUBSYSTEM"] IT["Input Tape Σ*"] IH["Read Head"] end subgraph ControlSubsystem["FINITE CONTROL SUBSYSTEM"] FSM["Finite State Machine Q, q0, F"] TD["Transition Function δ"] end subgraph StackSubsystem["STACK SUBSYSTEM"] ST["Pushdown Stack Γ*"] TOP["Top of Stack X"] end IH -->|"current symbol a"| TD TOP -->|"stack symbol X"| TD TD -->|"new state p"| FSM TD -->|"push pop or replace"| ST ST -->|"updated top"| TOP FSM -->|"next state"| TD IH -->|"advance"| IT ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid ID Trace — Sequence of IDs for $aabb$ Acceptance ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["ID 0\nq0 aabb Z0\nStart configuration"] --> A1["ID 1\nq0 abb AZ0\nRead a, push A"] A1 --> A2["ID 2\nq0 bb AAZ0\nRead a, push A"] A2 --> A3["ID 3\nq1 b AZ0\nRead b, pop A"] A3 --> A4["ID 4\nq1 epsilon Z0\nRead b, pop A"] A4 --> A5["ID 5\nq2 epsilon Z0\nEpsilon move, accept"] A5 --> A6["ACCEPTED\nq2 is final state\nq2 belongs to F"] ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Diagram — FA vs PDA ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FA["FINITE AUTOMATON (FA)"] FA1["Input Tape"] FA2["Read Head"] FA3["Finite Control States Q"] FA1 --> FA2 --> FA3 end subgraph PDA["PUSHDOWN AUTOMATON (PDA)"] PD1["Input Tape"] PD2["Read Head"] PD3["Finite Control States Q"] PD4["Pushdown Stack Gamma"] PD5["Top of Stack Pointer"] PD1 --> PD2 --> PD3 PD3 <--> PD4 PD4 --> PD5 end FA -.augments.-> PDA ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > A **PDA is a strict superset of an FA** in terms of computational power. Every regular language can be accepted by a PDA that simply **ignores its stack**. Therefore, **Regular Languages $\subset$ Context-Free Languages**, with strict inclusion. # 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a Pushdown Automaton (PDA) formally. List all seven components of a PDA.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Pushdown Automaton (PDA) is a 7-tuple mathematical model used to accept Context-Free Languages. Formally, a PDA is defined as: $$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$$ The seven components are: 1. **$Q$**: Finite non-empty set of states. 2. **$\Sigma$**: Finite set of input symbols (input alphabet). 3. **$\Gamma$**: Finite set of stack symbols (stack alphabet). 4. **$\delta$**: Transition function mapping $Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times (\Gamma \cup \{\varepsilon\})$ to finite subsets of $Q \times \Gamma^{*}$. 5. **$q_0$**: The start state, $q_0 \in Q$. 6. **$Z_0$**: The initial stack symbol, $Z_0 \in \Gamma$. 7. **$F$**: Set of final (accepting) states, $F \subseteq Q$. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between Deterministic PDA (DPDA) and Non-Deterministic PDA (NPDA).** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | DPDA | NPDA | |---|---|---| | Transition Choice | At most **one** next move per configuration | **Multiple** next moves possible | | Formal Restriction | $\mid \delta(q, a, X) \mid \leq 1$ and no $a$-move when $\varepsilon$-move exists from $(q, X)$ | No such restrictions | | Power | Accepts only **DCFLs** (Deterministic CFLs) | Accepts all **CFLs** | | Example | $L = \{a^n b^n\}$ is in DCFL | $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^{*}\}$ is CFL but **not** DCFL | | Relation | $\mathcal{L}(\text{DPDA}) \subsetneq \mathcal{L}(\text{NPDA})$ | Strictly more powerful | --- ## 📝 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Pattern**: Each Part B question has **internal choice** between two questions. Both questions below are independent alternatives — either can appear in the exam paper. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Define a Pushdown Automaton formally. Explain the role of the **stack** and the **transition function** in PDA with a suitable example. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** A Pushdown Automaton is formally defined as the 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ where each component is as defined in Part A Question 1. **Role of the Stack**: The stack provides **unbounded LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) memory** to the PDA.
