While a finite automaton has only finite memory (its states), the stack allows the PDA to remember an **arbitrarily large** amount of information, but with the constraint that only the **topmost symbol** can be accessed at any moment. This is precisely the memory model needed to count matched pairs, e.g., verifying that the number of $a$'s equals the number of $b$'s in $a^n b^n$. **Role of the Transition Function $\delta$**: The transition function is the **"program"** of the PDA. It determines, given the current state, the next input symbol (or $\varepsilon$), and the top of the stack, what the next state should be and how the stack-top should be modified. Formally: $$\delta: Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times (\Gamma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \to \mathcal{P}(Q \times \Gamma^{*})$$ **Example** (PDA for $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$): $$M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b\}, \{A, Z_0\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_2\})$$ With transitions: - $\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, AZ_0)\}$ - $\delta(q_0, a, A) = \{(q_0, AA)\}$ - $\delta(q_0, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$ - $\delta(q_1, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$ - $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_2, Z_0)\}$ Here, the stack stores one $A$ for every $a$ read, then pops one $A$ for every $b$ read. The $\varepsilon$-transition at the end ensures acceptance only when all $a$'s are matched by $b$'s. **(b)** Consider the PDA $M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b\}, \{A, Z_0\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_2\})$ with the transitions given above. Show the **complete sequence of Instantaneous Descriptions (IDs)** for the input string $w = aabb$. Does $M$ accept $w$? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The complete sequence of moves is: $$ \begin{aligned} (q_0, aabb, Z_0) &\vdash (q_0, abb, AZ_0) & &\text{[Apply } \delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = (q_0, AZ_0)\text{]} \\ &\vdash (q_0, bb, AAZ_0) & &\text{[Apply } \delta(q_0, a, A) = (q_0, AA)\text{]} \\ &\vdash (q_1, b, AZ_0) & &\text{[Apply } \delta(q_0, b, A) = (q_1, \varepsilon)\text{]} \\ &\vdash (q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) & &\text{[Apply } \delta(q_1, b, A) = (q_1, \varepsilon)\text{]} \\ &\vdash (q_2, \varepsilon, Z_0) & &\text{[Apply } \delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = (q_2, Z_0)\text{]} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion**: The final ID $(q_2, \varepsilon, Z_0)$ has $q_2 \in F$ and the input is fully consumed. Therefore, $M$ **accepts** the string $aabb$. $\checkmark$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Define the **transition function** of a PDA in detail. Discuss the role of **$\varepsilon$-transitions** with an example. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The transition function of a PDA is the heart of its computational behaviour. It is formally defined as: $$\delta: Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times (\Gamma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \to \mathcal{P}(Q \times \Gamma^{*})$$ This function takes three arguments: 1. The current state $q \in Q$ 2. The current input symbol $a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$ (where $\varepsilon$ means "no input consumed") 3. The top symbol of the stack $X \in \Gamma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$ (where $\varepsilon$ means "stack is not consulted") And returns a **finite set of pairs** $(p, \gamma)$ where: - $p \in Q$ is the next state - $\gamma \in \Gamma^{*}$ is the string that **replaces** $X$ on top of the stack **Notation**: $(p, \gamma) \in \delta(q, a, X)$ means the PDA may move from state $q$ to state $p$, consuming $a$ from input and replacing $X$ on stack with $\gamma$. **Role of $\varepsilon$-Transitions**: An $\varepsilon$-transition occurs when the PDA makes a move **without consuming any input symbol**. This is critical for several reasons: 1. **Post-input verification**: After all input is consumed, the PDA may still need to manipulate the stack (e.g., check if it is empty or contains only $Z_0$) to decide acceptance. 2. **Stack-only operations**: The PDA can perform stack operations (push/pop) without being constrained by input timing. 3. **Enabling $\varepsilon$-closure style computation**: Allows the PDA to "guess" without committing to an input read. **Example** (the final acceptance step in the $a^n b^n$ PDA): - $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_2, Z_0)\}$ This transition fires when input is empty but the stack still has $Z_0$ at the bottom. It moves to accepting state $q_2$ without consuming input — the "match complete" signal. **(b)** Construct a PDA that accepts the language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ using the formal 7-tuple notation. Verify acceptance for the input $w = aaabbb$. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Construction of the PDA**: $$M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b\}, \{A, Z_0\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_2\})$$ **Transition Function $\delta$**: | Current State | Input | Stack Top | New State | New Stack | |---|---|---|---|---| | $q_0$ | $a$ | $Z_0$ | $q_0$ | $AZ_0$ | | $q_0$ | $a$ | $A$ | $q_0$ | $AA$ | | $q_0$ | $b$ | $A$ | $q_1$ | $\varepsilon$ | | $q_1$ | $b$ | $A$ | $q_1$ | $\varepsilon$ | | $q_1$ | $\varepsilon$ | $Z_0$ | $q_2$ | $Z_0$ | **Verification for $w = aaabbb$**: $$ \begin{aligned} (q_0, aaabbb, Z_0) &\vdash (q_0, aabbb, AZ_0) & &\text{[push first A]} \\ &\vdash (q_0, abbb, AAZ_0) & &\text{[push second A]} \\ &\vdash (q_0, bbb, AAAZ_0) & &\text{[push third A]} \\ &\vdash (q_1, bb, AAZ_0) & &\text{[pop one A, switch to q1]} \\ &\vdash (q_1, b, AZ_0) & &\text{[pop second A]} \\ &\vdash (q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) & &\text{[pop third A]} \\ &\vdash (q_2, \varepsilon, Z_0) & &\text{[\varepsilon-move, accept]} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion**: Since the computation halts in the final state $q_2 \in F$ with the input fully consumed, $M$ **accepts** the string $aaabbb$. $\checkmark$ --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Stack-Top Convention Mismatch** (loss of 2–3 marks): Forgetting to clarify whether the **leftmost** or **rightmost** symbol of the stack string is the top. KTU uses the convention that the **leftmost symbol is the top**. If you use the opposite without stating it, your entire transition trace is marked wrong. > > 2. **Forgetting the Initial Symbol $Z_0$** (loss of 1–2 marks): The PDA stack is never truly "empty" at the start — it contains $Z_0$. Many students omit $Z_0$ from the initial ID, which causes the first transition to fail. > > 3. **Confusing $\varepsilon$-move with reading a symbol** (loss of 1 mark): When applying $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X)$, **no input is consumed**. The remaining input stays exactly the same in the next ID. > > 4. **Pop vs. Replace Confusion** (loss of 2 marks): When $\gamma = \varepsilon$, the top symbol is **popped** (removed). When $\gamma = \alpha X$ (where $\alpha$ is non-empty), the top is **replaced** by $\alpha$ (a push operation). Writing the wrong direction loses marks. > > 5. **Non-Determinism in Single Transitions** (loss of 1 mark): If $\delta(q, a, X)$ has more than one pair, the PDA **may** take any of them. Acceptance requires only **one** accepting path, not all. --- ## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist** - **PDA = FA + Stack**: A Pushdown Automaton is a finite automaton augmented with an **unbounded LIFO stack**. - **Formal Definition**: $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ — **seven components**. - **Transition Function**: $\delta: Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times (\Gamma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \to \mathcal{P}(Q \times \Gamma^{*})$ - **Stack-Top Convention**: Leftmost symbol of stack string = top of stack (KTU standard). - **$\varepsilon$-moves**: Allow stack manipulation without consuming input — **essential for post-input verification**. - **Instantaneous Description (ID)**: A triple $(q, w, \gamma)$ capturing the complete configuration at a moment. - **Move Notation**: $(q, aw, X\beta) \vdash (p, w, \gamma\beta)$ if $(p, \gamma) \in \delta(q, a, X)$. - **Acceptance by Final State**: Reach $q \in F$ with input exhausted. - **Acceptance by Empty Stack**: Reach configuration with empty stack. - **Power**: $\text{Regular} \subsetneq \text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL} = \text{PDA-acceptable}$. - **DPDA ⊊ NPDA**: Deterministic PDAs are strictly weaker — they cannot accept all CFLs (e.g., palindromes of even length). - **Classic Example**: $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ needs a 3-state PDA with stack alphabet $\{A, Z_0\}$. - **Application**: Compilers, parsers, balanced-parenthesis checking, XML validation, RNA structure analysis. - **Always** show the **complete ID sequence** in KTU answers — partial traces lose easy marks.

topics.title: Design of Deterministic PDA and Nondeterministic PDA, Simplification of Context-Free Languages: Chomsky Normal Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Deterministic PDA and Nondeterministic PDA, Simplification of Context-Free Languages: Chomsky Normal Form

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Pushdown Automaton (PDA) – Recap

A **Pushdown Automaton (PDA)** is a finite-state machine equipped with a **stack** (an unbounded, last-in-first-out memory). It accepts the class of **Context-Free Languages (CFLs)** that finite automata cannot recognize (e.g., $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$).

The stack is what makes the PDA powerful — it can "remember" an arbitrary number of symbols (like a count of $a$'s) that a DFA/NFA cannot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (PDA):** A PDA is a 7-tuple
> $$M = (Q,\ \Sigma,\ \Gamma,\ \delta,\ q_0,\ Z_0,\ F)$$
> where $Q$ is a finite set of states, $\Sigma$ is the input alphabet, $\Gamma$ is the stack alphabet, $\delta$ is the transition function, $q_0 \in Q$ is the start state, $Z_0 \in \Gamma$ is the initial stack symbol, and $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of final (accepting) states.

### 1.2 Deterministic PDA (DPDA)

> [!NOTE]
> **Deterministic PDA (DPDA):** A PDA is **deterministic** if for every configuration, the next move is uniquely determined. Formally, the transition function must be single-valued:
> $$\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma^* \quad \text{(single element)}$$
> Constraints: (i) If $\delta(q, a, X)$ is defined, then $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X)$ must be undefined, and (ii) For each $(q, a, X)$ there is at most one move.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a DPDA as a **vending machine with one coin slot and one button per coin**. When you insert a coin (read input $a$) and look at the current product display (top of stack $X$), there is **exactly one** product (action) that comes out. There is no ambiguity — no "maybe it does this, maybe it does that."

### 1.3 Nondeterministic PDA (NPDA)

> [!NOTE]
> **Nondeterministic PDA (NPDA):** A PDA is nondeterministic when the transition function maps a configuration to a **finite set of possible next moves**:
> $$\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \rightarrow 2^{Q \times \Gamma^*}$$
> This means from one configuration, the machine may branch into several possible computations in parallel.

**Intuitive Analogy:** An NPDA is like a **maze-solving robot that splits into clones** at every junction. Each clone tries a different path, and if **at least one clone** reaches the exit, the string is accepted.

### 1.4 Why the Distinction Matters (DPDA ⊂ NPDA)

| Feature | DPDA | NPDA |
|---|---|---|
| Branching | None (single move) | Multiple parallel moves |
| Power | Recognizes **DCFLs** (Deterministic CFLs) | Recognizes **all CFLs** |
| Closure under complement | **Yes** | **No** |
| Example language | $L = \{a^n b^n\}$ | $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^*\}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Every regular language is a DCFL, but not every CFL is a DCFL. The classic counter-example is $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^*\}$ — it requires an NPDA, **not** a DPDA.

### 1.5 Simplification of CFLs and Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)

> [!NOTE]
> **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF):** A context-free grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is in **Chomsky Normal Form** if every production in $P$ is of one of the following two forms:
> $$A \rightarrow BC \quad \text{where } A,\ B,\ C \in V$$
> $$A \rightarrow a \quad \text{where } a \in T$$
> Special case: $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is permitted **only if** $\varepsilon \in L(G)$.

**Intuitive Analogy:** CNF is like a **standardized LEGO set**. Every rule either says "**one variable becomes two variables**" (binary branching tree) or "**one variable becomes one terminal**" (a leaf). No big rules, no mixed rules — every derivation becomes a uniform binary tree of bounded width, which makes algorithms (parsing, CYK, membership testing) dramatically simpler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** Module 3 explicitly covers *Simplification of CFLs* (removing useless symbols, unit productions, and $\varepsilon$-productions) as the **prerequisite pipeline** to reach CNF. You cannot jump straight to CNF without these cleanups.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a DPDA Transition

A DPDA transition is written as:
$$\delta(q,\ a,\ X) = (p,\ \gamma)$$

**Reading the notation:**
- $q$ — current state
- $a$ — current input symbol (or $\varepsilon$ for an $\varepsilon$-move)
- $X$ — symbol on top of the stack (popped automatically)
- $p$ — next state
- $\gamma$ — string of symbols **pushed** onto the stack (leftmost symbol becomes the new top)

**Why this format?** The stack is **LIFO** (last-in-first-out). To push $\gamma = YZ$ on top of $X$, you effectively replace $X$ with $ZY$ (with $Y$ now on top). So the new stack is: $\text{(rest of stack below)} \cdot ZY$.

### 2.2 DPDA Design Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

To design a DPDA, follow these structured rules:

1. **Read & Match Strategy:** For every input symbol $a_i$, **push** a marker onto the stack.
2. **Boundary Detection:** Use a **special marker** (e.g., $Z_0$ or a delimiter like $\\#$) to detect when the "pushing phase" ends.
3. **Pop & Verify Strategy:** For every input symbol in the "verifying phase," **pop** one marker.
4. **Accept Condition:** If the stack returns to just $Z_0$ and the input is fully consumed, **accept**.

**Key DPDA rules of thumb:**
- DPDA cannot handle ambiguous grammars cleanly (e.g., $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\} \cup \{a^n b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ is **not** a DCFL).
- DPDA has exactly **one initial stack symbol** $Z_0$.

### 2.3 NPDA Design Principles

For NPDA, we **exploit nondeterminism** to "guess" structure:

1. **Guess-then-Verify:** Branch the computation to try all possible parses in parallel.
2. **Push for "remember", Pop for "match":** Same stack discipline as DPDA.
3. **Accept Condition (NPDA accepts by):**
   - **Final state:** Input is fully consumed **and** the machine is in a state $q \in F$ (stack may be non-empty).
   - **Empty stack:** Input is fully consumed **and** the stack is empty (no acceptance states needed, $F = \emptyset$).

### 2.4 CFL Simplification Pipeline (the road to CNF)

To bring any CFL into CNF, we execute a **5-step algorithm**:

| Step | Operation | Removes |
|------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | Eliminate $\varepsilon$-productions | Rules of form $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| 2 | Eliminate unit productions | Rules of form $A \rightarrow B$ (where $A,B \in V$) |
| 3 | Eliminate useless symbols | Non-generating / non-reachable variables |
| 4 | Replace terminals in long rules | Mixed rules like $A \rightarrow aB$ become $A \rightarrow C_a B$ |
| 5 | Break long RHS into binary form | Rules like $A \rightarrow B_1 B_2 B_3$ split into pairs |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| PDA Tuple | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ | 7-tuple definition |
| DPDA transition | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to Q \times \Gamma^*$ | Single-valued |
| NPDA transition | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to 2^{Q \times \Gamma^*}$ | Multi-valued |
| CNF rule type 1 | $A \rightarrow BC$ | $A,B,C \in V$ |
| CNF rule type 2 | $A \rightarrow a$ | $a \in T$ |
| CNF special | $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | Only if $\varepsilon \in L$ |
| Configuration | $(q,\ w,\ \gamma)$ | state, remaining input, stack |
| Stack replacement | $X \to \gamma$ | Replace top $X$ with $\gamma$; new top is leftmost of $\gamma$ |
| Pumping-lemma length | $p \leq 2^{\vert V \vert}$ | Used to prove non-CFLs (not CNF, but related) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** CNF is the **backbone of modern compilers**. The **CYK (Cocke–Younger–Kasami) algorithm** only works on grammars in CNF and runs in $O(n^3)$. Tools like YACC, Bison, and ANTLR internally convert grammars to CNF-like normal forms (e.g., GNF) before generating parse tables. Without CNF, **predictive parsing** and **parser generators** would be vastly more complex.

### 2.6 Decision Problems for CFLs in CNF

Once a grammar is in CNF, many decision problems become polynomial:
- **Membership** ($w \in L(G)$?): $O(n^3)$ via CYK.
- **Emptiness** ($L(G) = \emptyset$?): Check if $S$ is generating.
- **Finiteness** ($\vert L(G) \vert < \infty$?): Check for cycles in the variable dependency graph.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Design a DPDA for $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

**Goal:** Construct a DPDA $M$ that accepts $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$.

#### Step 1 — Define the components
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ (push, pop, accept)
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $\Gamma = \{a, Z_0\}$ ($Z_0$ = bottom-of-stack marker)
- $q_0 = q_0$, $Z_0 = Z_0$
- $F = \{q_2\}$

#### Step 2 — Encode the transition rules

**Phase 1 (reading $a$'s, push $a$ for each):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0,\ a,\ Z_0) &= (q_0,\ aZ_0) \\
\delta(q_0,\ a,\ a) &= (q_0,\ aa)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** While we see $a$'s, stay in $q_0$ and push an $a$ on top of the stack. $Z_0$ stays at the bottom as a marker.

**Phase 2 (reading $b$'s, pop $a$ for each):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0,\ b,\ a) &= (q_1,\ \varepsilon) \\
\delta(q_1,\ b,\ a) &= (q_1,\ \varepsilon)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** On the first $b$, transition to $q_1$ and pop one $a$. Continue in $q_1$ for subsequent $b$'s, popping $a$'s.

**Phase 3 (accept when stack returns to $Z_0$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) &= (q_2,\ Z_0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** When all $b$'s are consumed and only $Z_0$ remains on the stack, do an $\varepsilon$-move to the accepting state $q_2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trace for $aabb$:** $(q_0, aabb, Z_0) \to (q_0, abb, aZ_0) \to (q_0, bb, aaZ_0) \to (q_1, b, aZ_0) \to (q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) \to (q_2, \varepsilon, Z_0)$ ✓ **Accepted.**

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Design an NPDA for $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^+\}$ (palindromes of even length)

**Why NPDA is needed:** The DPDA cannot "guess" the midpoint of the string; an NPDA branches on both guesses in parallel.

#### Step 1 — Define the components
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $\Gamma = \{a, b, Z_0\}$
- $F = \{q_2\}$

#### Step 2 — Transitions (with nondeterministic branching)

**Phase 1 (push every symbol read):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0,\ a,\ X) &= \{(q_0,\ aX)\} \quad \text{for } X \in \Gamma \\
\delta(q_0,\ b,\ X) &= \{(q_0,\ bX)\} \quad \text{for } X \in \Gamma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Phase 2 (non-deterministically "guess" the midpoint via $\varepsilon$-move):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0,\ \varepsilon,\ X) &= \{(q_1,\ X)\} \quad \text{for } X \in \Gamma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Phase 3 (pop and verify mirror symbols):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_1,\ a,\ a) &= \{(q_1,\ \varepsilon)\} \\
\delta(q_1,\ b,\ b) &= \{(q_1,\ \varepsilon)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Phase 4 (accept):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) &= \{(q_2,\ Z_0)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Trace for $abba$:** NPDA clones at every midpoint guess. The "lucky" clone guesses midpoint after $ab$, then matches $b$ with $b$ (pop), $a$ with $a$ (pop), and accepts.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Full CNF Conversion

**Given Grammar $G$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aAb \mid bBa \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Goal:** Convert to CNF.

#### Step 1 — Eliminate $\varepsilon$-productions
There are **no** $\varepsilon$-productions in $G$ (no $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$). ✓ **Skip.**

#### Step 2 — Eliminate unit productions
There are **no** unit productions (no $A \rightarrow B$ where both are variables). ✓ **Skip.**

#### Step 3 — Eliminate useless symbols
- **Generating check:** All $A \rightarrow a$, $B \rightarrow b$ are terminal. $A \rightarrow aA$ generates. $B \rightarrow bB$ generates. $S \rightarrow aAb$ uses $A$. $S \rightarrow bBa$ uses $B$. ✓ All variables are generating.
- **Reachable check:** From $S$, we reach $A$ and $B$. ✓ All variables are reachable.

#### Step 4 — Replace terminals in productions of length $\geq 2$

Introduce new variables for terminals that appear alongside others:
- $C_a$ represents terminal $a$
- $C_b$ represents terminal $b$

Rewrite the long rules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aAb \;\Rightarrow\; S \rightarrow C_a A C_b \\
S &\rightarrow bBa \;\Rightarrow\; S \rightarrow C_b B C_a
\end{aligned}
$$

Add the new terminal rules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_a &\rightarrow a \\
C_b &\rightarrow b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Current grammar:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow C_a A C_b \mid C_b B C_a \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid b \\
C_a &\rightarrow a \\
C_b &\rightarrow b
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Break productions of length $\geq 3$ into binary form

Production $S \rightarrow C_a A C_b$ has length 3. Introduce a new variable $D_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow C_a D_1 \\
D_1 &\rightarrow A C_b
\end{aligned}
$$

Production $S \rightarrow C_b B C_a$ has length 3. Introduce a new variable $D_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow C_b D_2 \\
D_2 &\rightarrow B C_a
\end{aligned}
$$

Productions of length 2 (e.g., $A \rightarrow aA$) are already in CNF form. ✓

#### Final CNF Grammar $G'$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow C_a D_1 \mid C_b D_2 \\
D_1 &\rightarrow A C_b \\
D_2 &\rightarrow B C_a \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid b \\
C_a &\rightarrow a \\
C_b &\rightarrow b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** Every production matches one of:
- $A \rightarrow BC$ (with $A, B, C \in V$), e.g., $D_1 \rightarrow A C_b$
- $A \rightarrow a$ (with $a \in T$), e.g., $C_a \rightarrow a$ ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DPDA vs NPDA vs CNF

| Operation | DPDA | NPDA | CNF |
|---|---|---|---|
| Main use | Recognizing DCFLs | Recognizing general CFLs | Standardizing CFLs |
| Closure under union | No | Yes | N/A |
| Closure under complement | Yes | No | N/A |
| Algorithm complexity | $O(n)$ (single trace) | Exponential (branching) | $O(n^3)$ via CYK |
| Standardized form | Not standardized | Not standardized | **Strictly standardized** |
| KTU 2024 weight | High | High | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — High-Level PDA Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node_input[Input Tape] --> node_ctrl[Finite Control Unit]
    node_stack[Stack Memory] --> node_ctrl
    node_ctrl --> node_output[Accept or Reject]
    node_ctrl -. push.-> node_stack
    node_stack -. pop.-> node_ctrl
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **finite control** reads one input symbol at a time, peeks at the top of the **stack**, decides a transition, and may push/pop on the stack. After the input is exhausted, it outputs **Accept** if in a final state (DPDA) or if the stack is empty (NPDA-empty mode).

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — DPDA for $L = \{a^n b^n\}$

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0 --> q0 : read a, push a
    q0 --> q1 : read b, pop a
    q1 --> q1 : read b, pop a
    q1 --> q2 : epsilon move on Z0
    q2 --> [*] : accept
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flow — CNF Conversion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start with Grammar G] --> stepB[Step 1: Remove epsilon productions]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 2: Remove unit productions]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 3: Remove useless symbols]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 4: Replace terminals in long rules]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 5: Break rules longer than 2]
    stepF --> stepG[Final Grammar G prime in CNF]
    stepG --> stepH[Apply CYK Algorithm or parse]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — DPDA vs NPDA Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DPDA[Deterministic PDA]
        d1[Single transition] --> d2[Trace one path] --> d3[Accept or Reject]
    end
    subgraph NPDA[Nondeterministic PDA]
        n1[Multiple transitions] --> n2[Branch into parallel paths] --> n3[Any one path accepts]
    end
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Visualization — CNF Rule Tree for $S \rightarrow C_a D_1$, $D_1 \rightarrow A C_b$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS[S] --> nodeC1[C a]
    nodeS --> nodeD1[D 1]
    nodeD1 --> nodeA[A]
    nodeD1 --> nodeC2[C b]
    nodeC1 --> nodeT1[Terminal a]
    nodeC2 --> nodeT2[Terminal b]
    nodeA --> nodeA2[A]:::rec
    nodeA --> nodeT3[Terminal a]
    classDef rec fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a binary tree?** Every CNF derivation corresponds to a **full binary tree** of height $\leq n$ (for a string of length $n$). This is exactly the structure the CYK algorithm exploits to do membership testing in $O(n^3)$ via dynamic programming on the tree.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define a Deterministic Pushdown Automaton (DPDA). How does it differ from a Non-deterministic PDA (NPDA)?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A DPDA is a 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ where the transition function is **single-valued**:

$$\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma^*$$

Differences from NPDA:
1. **DPDA** has a single possible move per configuration; **NPDA** maps to a set of possible moves: $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \rightarrow 2^{Q \times \Gamma^*}$.
2. **DPDA** recognizes only **DCFLs** (a proper subset of CFLs); **NPDA** recognizes **all CFLs**.
3. **DPDA** is closed under **complement**; **NPDA** is not.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]** State the conditions for a context-free grammar to be in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A CFG $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is in CNF if every production in $P$ is of one of the following two forms:

1. $A \rightarrow BC$, where $A, B, C \in V$ (i.e., a variable producing exactly **two variables**)
2. $A \rightarrow a$, where $a \in T$ (i.e., a variable producing exactly **one terminal**)

**Special exception:** $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is allowed if and only if $\varepsilon \in L(G)$.

CNF grammars have no $\varepsilon$-productions (except possibly $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$), no unit productions, no useless symbols, and no rules with mixed terminals and variables on the RHS of length $\geq 2$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> **Q-A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** (a) Design a DPDA that accepts the language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$. Provide the transition function and trace its execution for the input string $aaabbb$. (7 marks)
>
> (b) Convert the following CFG into **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**:
> $$S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$$ (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a) — DPDA for $L = \{a^n b^n\}$

**Components:**
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_f\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$, $\Gamma = \{a, Z_0\}$
- Start state $q_0$, initial stack symbol $Z_0$, final state $F = \{q_f\}$

**Transition Function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0,\ a,\ Z_0) &= (q_0,\ aZ_0) \\
\delta(q_0,\ a,\ a) &= (q_0,\ aa) \\
\delta(q_0,\ b,\ a) &= (q_1,\ \varepsilon) \\
\delta(q_1,\ b,\ a) &= (q_1,\ \varepsilon) \\
\delta(q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) &= (q_f,\ Z_0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trace for $aaabbb$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(q_0,\ aaabbb,\ Z_0) \\
\vdash\ &(q_0,\ aabbb,\ aZ_0) \quad \text{[read a, push a]} \\
\vdash\ &(q_0,\ abbb,\ aaZ_0) \quad \text{[read a, push a]} \\
\vdash\ &(q_0,\ bbb,\ aaaZ_0) \quad \text{[read a, push a]} \\
\vdash\ &(q_1,\ bb,\ aaZ_0) \quad \text{[read b, pop a]} \\
\vdash\ &(q_1,\ b,\ aZ_0) \quad \text{[read b, pop a]} \\
\vdash\ &(q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) \quad \text{[read b, pop a]} \\
\vdash\ &(q_f,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) \quad \text{[epsilon move, accept]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** String accepted. ✓

#### Model Solution for (b) — CNF Conversion of $S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$

**Step 1 — Eliminate $\varepsilon$-productions:** None present. ✓

**Step 2 — Eliminate unit productions:** None present. ✓

**Step 3 — Eliminate useless symbols:** $S$ is generating and reachable. ✓

**Step 4 — Replace terminals in rules of length $\geq 2$:**

Introduce $C_a \rightarrow a$ and $C_b \rightarrow b$. Rewrite:

$$S \rightarrow C_a S C_b \mid C_a C_b$$

**Step 5 — Break rules of length $> 2$:**

$S \rightarrow C_a S C_b$ has length 3. Introduce $D \rightarrow S C_b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow C_a D \\
D &\rightarrow S C_b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final CNF Grammar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow C_a D \mid C_a C_b \\
D &\rightarrow S C_b \\
C_a &\rightarrow a \\
C_b &\rightarrow b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** Every production is either $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$. ✓

---

### 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

> **Q-B. [KTU University Exam - June 2022]** (a) Construct an NPDA that accepts $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^+\}$. Clearly state the components and transitions. (7 marks)
>
> (b) Consider the grammar $G$ with productions:
> $$S \rightarrow aA \mid bB,\quad A \rightarrow aA \mid b,\quad B \rightarrow a \mid bB$$
> Convert $G$ into CNF. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a) — NPDA for $L = \{ww^R\}$

**Components:**
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$, $\Gamma = \{a, b, Z_0\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$, $Z_0 = Z_0$, $F = \{q_2\}$

**Transitions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0,\ a,\ X) &= \{(q_0,\ aX)\} \quad \forall X \in \Gamma \\
\delta(q_0,\ b,\ X) &= \{(q_0,\ bX)\} \quad \forall X \in \Gamma \\
\delta(q_0,\ \varepsilon,\ X) &= \{(q_1,\ X)\} \quad \forall X \in \Gamma \quad \text{[guess midpoint]} \\
\delta(q_1,\ a,\ a) &= \{(q_1,\ \varepsilon)\} \\
\delta(q_1,\ b,\ b) &= \{(q_1,\ \varepsilon)\} \\
\delta(q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) &= \{(q_2,\ Z_0)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The NPDA pushes every input symbol until it **nondeterministically guesses** the midpoint. Then it pops and verifies that the remaining input matches the reverse of the stacked symbols.

#### Model Solution for (b) — CNF Conversion of Given $G$

**Original grammar:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aA \mid bB \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid b \\
B &\rightarrow a \mid bB
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — No $\varepsilon$-productions.** ✓

**Step 2 — No unit productions.** ✓

**Step 3 — No useless symbols.** (All generate and are reachable from $S$.) ✓

**Step 4 — Replace terminals in length-2 rules:**

Introduce $C_a \rightarrow a$ and $C_b \rightarrow b$. Rewrite:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow C_a A \mid C_b B \\
A &\rightarrow C_a A \mid b \\
B &\rightarrow a \mid C_b B
\end{aligned}
$$

Update single-terminal rules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow C_a A \mid C_b \quad \text{(replaced } b \text{ with } C_b \text{ only in long rule)}\\
B &\rightarrow C_a \mid C_b B
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — All rules are now of length $\leq 2$.** ✓

**Final CNF Grammar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow C_a A \mid C_b B \\
A &\rightarrow C_a A \mid C_b \\
B &\rightarrow C_a \mid C_b B \\
C_a &\rightarrow a \\
C_b &\rightarrow b
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ exception:** Many students omit the special $\varepsilon$-rule and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Skipping Step 3 (useless symbols):** KTU specifically tests this — failing to remove useless symbols can leave the grammar with variables that block CNF conversion.
> 3. **Confusing "unit production" with "Useless":** $A \rightarrow B$ (both variables) is a **unit production**, NOT a useless symbol. They're removed in different steps.
> 4. **Mixing up the stack order:** In PDA transitions, the string pushed goes **left-to-right on top**. So pushing $aZ_0$ on top of existing $X$ gives stack $aZ_0X$ (not $XaZ_0$). This is a recurring 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Forgetting the DPDA constraint:** If both $\delta(q, a, X)$ and $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X)$ are defined, it is **not** a DPDA.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 (DPDA, NPDA, CNF)**

- [ ] **PDA 7-tuple:** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ — memorize the symbol meanings.
- [ ] **DPDA transitions are single-valued; NPDA transitions are set-valued.** The difference is **determinism vs nondeterminism** of choices.
- [ ] **DPDA ⊂ NPDA in language-recognition power.** Example: $\{a^n b^n\}$ is DCFL; $\{ww^R\}$ needs NPDA.
- [ ] **DPDA is closed under complement; NPDA is not.** This is a **favorite KTU two-mark question**.
- [ ] **Stack discipline:** $X \to \gamma$ replaces the top $X$ with $\gamma$, where leftmost of $\gamma$ becomes the new top.
- [ ] **CNF rules:** Only $A \rightarrow BC$ (with $A, B, C \in V$) and $A \rightarrow a$ (with $a \in T$). Exception: $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ if $\varepsilon \in L$.
- [ ] **5-step CNF algorithm:** (1) $\varepsilon$-removal → (2) unit-removal → (3) useless-removal → (4) terminal-replacement → (5) length-reduction.
- [ ] **Order matters:** Always remove $\varepsilon$-productions **first**, then unit productions, then useless symbols. KTU checks this sequence.
- [ ] **New variables** added in Steps 4 and 5 must themselves be added as proper CNF rules (e.g., $C_a \rightarrow a$).
- [ ] **CYK algorithm** runs in $O(n^3)$ **only** on CNF grammars. This is why CNF matters in practice.
- [ ] **DPDA example to memorize:** $L = \{a^n b^n\}$ — push $a$ for each input $a$, pop $a$ for each input $b$, accept when stack is $Z_0$.
- [ ] **NPDA example to memorize:** $L = \{ww^R\}$ — push symbols, $\varepsilon$-move guesses midpoint, then pop to verify.
- [ ] **Pumping Lemma for CFLs** uses length bound $2^{\vert V \vert}$ (not directly CNF, but related — and a frequent KTU question).
- [ ] **Application:** CNF underpins compiler parsers (YACC, Bison, ANTLR) and is a foundation for **formal verification**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Greibach Normal Form, Properties of Context-Free Languages: The Pumping Lemma for Context-Free Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Greibach Normal Form, Properties of Context-Free Languages: The Pumping Lemma for Context-Free Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Greibach Normal Form (GNF)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A Context-Free Grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is said to be in **Greibach Normal Form (GNF)** if every production in $P$ is of the form:
> $$A \rightarrow a\alpha$$
> where $A \in V$, $a \in T$, and $\alpha \in V^{*}$ (a possibly empty string of variables).

In other words, every production in GNF begins with **exactly one terminal symbol**, followed by **zero or more variables**. There are **no $\varepsilon$-productions** (except possibly $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ if $\varepsilon \in L(G)$), **no unit productions**, and **no left recursions** in GNF.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Lunch Box" Rule 🍱

Think of a grammar as a recipe book for building sentences. In Greibach Normal Form, every rule is like a **lunch box**: it must contain **one fruit (terminal) on top first**, and below that fruit, you may stack **zero or more snacks (variables)** in any order. You can never place a variable on top, never skip the top fruit, and you cannot have an empty lunch box (no $\varepsilon$-productions). This strict structure makes it very easy for a Pushdown Automaton (PDA) to read input and simulate the grammar using exactly **one stack symbol per production**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Properties of GNF:**
> 1. Every string $w \in L(G)$ with $w \neq \varepsilon$ has a derivation tree of **height exactly $|w|$**.
> 2. A CFG in GNF can be converted directly to an equivalent **NPDA with a single state**.
> 3. GNF is **stronger** than Chomsky Normal Form (CNF) — every GNF grammar is also in CNF, but the reverse is **not** true.

---

## 1.2 The Pumping Lemma for Context-Free Languages

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Let $L$ be a Context-Free Language. Then there exists a constant $n$ (the **pumping length**) such that every string $z \in L$ with $|z| \geq n$ can be decomposed as:
> $$z = uvwxy$$
> satisfying the following three conditions:
> 1. $|vwx| \leq n$
> 2. $|vx| \geq 1$ (i.e., $vx \neq \varepsilon$)
> 3. $uv^{i}wx^{i}y \in L$ for **all** $i \geq 0$

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Stretchy Rubber Band" 🧩

Imagine you have a long rubber-band string $z$ that belongs to some CFL. Because the language is built by a context-free grammar, the string contains some small middle portion ($vwx$) that has a **repeatable, nested structure** — like a recursive palindrome inside an English sentence. The Pumping Lemma says: you can grab this nested middle and **pump it up** ($i = 2, 3, 4, \ldots$) or **squeeze it down** ($i = 0, 1$), and the new string will *still* belong to the language, provided the pumped region is not too small and not too far from the start.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the Pumping Lemma Useful?**
> The Pumping Lemma is the **principal tool for proving that a language is NOT context-free**. It works by **proof-by-contradiction**: assume $L$ is CFL, then derive a contradiction by pumping some string to break the language's defining pattern.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conversion Algorithm: CFG → GNF (KTU Board Standard)

The standard KTU-approved algorithm converts any CFG (with no $\varepsilon$- and no unit-productions) into GNF in two major phases:

### **Phase 1 — Convert to Chomsky Normal Form (CNF) first**
- Eliminate $\varepsilon$-productions
- Eliminate unit-productions
- Eliminate useless symbols
- Replace terminals in long RHS by variables (CNF rule)

### **Phase 2 — Eliminate Left Recursion (Variable Ordering Trick)**
Order variables as $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$. Then process rules for $A_i$ using already-processed variables $A_1, \ldots, A_{i-1}$ to eliminate left recursion.

The detailed algorithm:

| **Step** | **Action** | **Justification** |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Order variables: $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$ | Establish fixed processing order |
| 2 | For $i = 1$ to $n$: convert all $A_i$ rules | Use substitution to push terminals forward |
| 3 | If $A_i \rightarrow A_j \gamma$ with $j < i$, substitute $A_j$'s rules | Eliminate left recursion |
| 4 | Apply rule **$A \rightarrow B\alpha \mid C$ becomes $A \rightarrow b\alpha \mid C$** | Replace leftmost variable by terminal-starting rule |
| 5 | Repeat until every production starts with a terminal | Achieves GNF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Formula for GNF Conversion:**
> If $A \rightarrow A\alpha_1 \mid A\alpha_2 \mid \ldots \mid A\alpha_m \mid \beta_1 \mid \beta_2 \mid \ldots \mid \beta_n$, then introduce a new variable $Z$ and replace with:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> A &\rightarrow \beta_i \mid \beta_i Z && \text{for } i = 1, \ldots, n \\
> Z &\rightarrow \alpha_j \mid \alpha_j Z && \text{for } j = 1, \ldots, m
> \end{aligned}$$

This eliminates left recursion while preserving the language generated.

---

## 2.2 Pumping Lemma — Theoretical Structure

### **Proof Sketch (Why It Works)**

Given $z \in L$ with $|z| \geq n$:
- Take the parse tree of $z$.
- A binary branching tree of height $\geq n$ has **at least $n+1$ leaves on a path**.
- By the **Pigeonhole Principle**, some variable repeats along this path.
- The **upper occurrence** of the repeated variable $R$ has subtree that yields $w$, and the **lower occurrence** yields $w'$.
- Decompose: $z = uvwxy$ where $v, x$ come from the upper $R$-subtree's yield (excluding the inner $w'$-yield) and $w$ is the inner yield.

### **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet**

| **Concept** | **Formula / Property** | **Notes / Boundary Conditions** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| GNF Production Form | $A \rightarrow a\alpha$ | $A \in V$, $a \in T$, $\alpha \in V^{*}$ |
| GNF Derivation Tree Height | $h = \vert w \vert$ | For $w \neq \varepsilon$ |
| Pumping Lemma Decomposition | $z = uvwxy$ | Five-part split |
| Upper Bound on Pump Region | $\vert vwx \vert \leq n$ | "Pumping length" $n$ depends on $L$ |
| Non-emptiness of Pump | $\vert vx \vert \geq 1$ | $v$ and $x$ together cannot be empty |
| Pump for all iterations | $uv^{i}wx^{i}y \in L$, $\forall i \geq 0$ | Both $v$ and $x$ pumped equally |
| CFL Pumping Length | $n = b^{k+1}$ where $b = \max$ RHS length, $k = \vert V \vert$ | Standard upper bound (Hopcroft/Ullman style) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Compiler Design:** GNF-based parsers (e.g., recursive descent) parse any input symbol in exactly **one derivation step**, enabling linear-time parsing.
> - **XML/HTML Validation:** The Pumping Lemma guarantees that valid markup has **nested, recursive structure** — anything that violates nesting is provably non-CFL.
> - **Programming Language Design:** Languages requiring nested constructs (e.g., balanced parentheses in `LISP`, block-scoped `{ }` in `C`) are inherently context-free, and the Pumping Lemma provides the underlying formal justification.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Converting a CFG to GNF (Full KTU Board-Style Solution)

**Convert the following CFG to Greibach Normal Form:**
$$S \rightarrow AB \mid b$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid bB \mid a$$
$$B \rightarrow b \mid bA$$

### **Step 1: Eliminate $\varepsilon$-productions and unit-productions**
- No $\varepsilon$-productions present ✓
- No unit-productions present ✓
- All symbols ($S, A, B$) generate terminals and reach $S$ ✓

### **Step 2: Order variables**
$$A_1 = S, \quad A_2 = A, \quad A_3 = B$$

### **Step 3: Process $A_1 = S$**
Original rules:
$$S \rightarrow AB \mid b$$
- The first rule starts with variable $A$ (not in GNF form yet), and second rule starts with terminal $b$ (✓ already in GNF).
- We delay processing $S \rightarrow AB$ until $A$ is in GNF.

### **Step 4: Convert $A_2 = A$ to GNF form**
Current rules:
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid bB \mid a$$
- All three rules start with a terminal ($a$ or $b$) ✓ → **$A$ is already in GNF!**

### **Step 5: Convert $A_3 = B$ to GNF form**
Current rules:
$$B \rightarrow b \mid bA$$
- Both rules start with terminal $b$ ✓ → **$B$ is already in GNF!**

### **Step 6: Revisit $S \rightarrow AB$**
Now substitute $A$'s rules into $S$:
$$\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aA \cdot B \mid bB \cdot B \mid a \cdot B \mid b \\
&\Rightarrow S \rightarrow aAB \mid bBB \mid aB \mid b
\end{aligned}$$

### **Step 7: Final GNF**
$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aAB \mid bBB \mid aB \mid b \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid bB \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow b \mid bA
\end{aligned}}$$

All productions begin with a terminal symbol followed by zero or more variables — **GNF achieved!**

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Pumping Lemma to Prove Non-CFL

**Prove that the language $L = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$ is NOT context-free.**

### **Step 1: Assume for contradiction**
Suppose $L$ is context-free. Then by the Pumping Lemma, there exists a pumping length $n$ (let us call it $p$ to avoid clash) such that every string $z \in L$ with $|z| \geq p$ can be decomposed as $z = uvwxy$ satisfying the three conditions.

### **Step 2: Choose the string to pump**
Choose:
$$z = a^{p}\,b^{p}\,c^{p}$$
Clearly $z \in L$ and $|z| = 3p \geq p$.

### **Step 3: Analyze the decomposition**
By Condition 1: $|vwx| \leq p$, so $vwx$ contains **at most $p$ symbols in total**. This means $vwx$ **cannot contain all three character types** $a, b, c$ simultaneously — it can touch at most two of them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> We now consider **three exhaustive cases** depending on which symbols appear in $vx$:

### **Case A: $vx$ contains only $a$'s (and possibly no $b$'s or $c$'s)**
Pump up with $i = 2$:
$$uv^{2}wx^{2}y = a^{p + |vx|}\,b^{p}\,c^{p}$$
Since $|vx| \geq 1$, the number of $a$'s now exceeds the number of $b$'s and $c$'s. Hence $uv^{2}wx^{2}y \notin L$ — **contradiction**.

### **Case B: $vx$ contains only $b$'s**
By symmetric reasoning, $uv^{2}wx^{2}y$ has unequal counts of $a, b, c$ — **contradiction**.

### **Case C: $vx$ contains $b$'s and $c$'s (or only $c$'s, or spans $b$ and $c$ but no $a$'s)**
Pump up with $i = 2$:
- If $vx$ contains $b$ but no $a$ and no $c$: $a$-count unchanged, $b$-count increased, $c$-count unchanged → mismatch.
- If $vx$ contains $c$ but no $a$ and no $b$: $c$-count increased → mismatch.
- If $vx$ contains both $b$ and $c$: $b$ and $c$ counts change together but $a$ stays at $p$. For $uv^{2}wx^{2}y$ to be in $L$, all three counts must be equal — which requires $a$-count to increase too, but it does not.

### **Step 4: Conclusion**
In every case, we derive a contradiction. Therefore our assumption that $L$ is context-free is **false**.
$$\boxed{L = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 0\} \text{ is NOT context-free.}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Non-CFL Proofs at KTU Exams

| **Language $L$** | **String Chosen** | **Pump Constant** | **Contradiction Trigger** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| $\{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n}\}$ | $a^{p}b^{p}c^{p}$ | $p$ | Mismatch in symbol counts |
| $\{ww \mid w \in \{a,b\}^{*}\}$ | $a^{p}b^{p}a^{p}b^{p}$ | $p$ | Length of $vx$ cannot span the middle boundary symmetrically |
| $\{a^{i}b^{j}c^{k} \mid i < j < k\}$ | $a^{p}b^{p+1}c^{p+2}$ | $p$ | Pumping $v$ or $x$ violates the strict ordering |
| $\{a^{n^{2}} \mid n \geq 0\}$ | $a^{p^{2}}$ | $p$ | $p^{2} + \vert vx \vert$ is not a perfect square for large enough pump |
| $\{a^{n!} \mid n \geq 0\}$ | $a^{p!}$ | $p$ | Factorial gaps cannot be preserved by pumping |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — GNF Conversion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Input CFG] --> stepB[Remove epsilon productions]
    stepB --> stepC[Remove unit productions]
    stepC --> stepD[Remove useless symbols]
    stepD --> stepE[Convert to CNF]
    stepE --> stepF[Order variables A1 to An]
    stepF --> stepG{Left recursion present?}
    stepG -- Yes --> stepH[Apply left recursion elimination rule]
    stepG -- No --> stepI[Substitute processed variable rules]
    stepH --> stepJ[Replace leftmost variable by terminal using substitution]
    stepJ --> stepK{All rules start with terminal?}
    stepI --> stepK
    stepK -- No --> stepF
    stepK -- Yes --> endL[GNF Achieved]

    style startA fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style endL fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style stepK fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pumping Lemma Structural Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sIn[String z in L with length greater than or equal n] --> sDec[Decompose z equals uvwxy]
    sDec --> c1[Condition 1: vwx length within n]
    sDec --> c2[Condition 2: vx nonempty]
    sDec --> c3[Condition 3: Pump for all i greater than or equal 0]
    c3 --> sUp[Pump up i equals 2]
    c3 --> sDown[Pump down i equals 0]
    sUp --> sCheck{New string still in L?}
    sDown --> sCheck
    sCheck -- No --> sFail[Contradiction: L is not CFL]
    sCheck -- Yes --> sOk[L is potentially CFL - need further test]

    style sIn fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style sFail fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style sOk fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — GNF Production Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lhsA[Variable A] --> termA[Terminal a]
    termA --> varB[Optional variable sequence B1 B2 dot dot dot Bk]

    style lhsA fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style termA fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style varB fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Architecture Flow — Pumping Lemma Application Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    t1[Identify target language L] --> t2{Is L defined by counting, nesting or matching?}
    t2 -- Yes --> t3[Likely non CFL - apply pumping lemma]
    t2 -- No --> t4[Try closure properties first]
    t3 --> t5[Assume L is CFL with pumping length n]
    t5 --> t6[Choose z in L with length much greater than n]
    t6 --> t7[Decompose z equals uvwxy]
    t7 --> t8[Case analysis on vx content]
    t8 --> t9{Pump i equals 2 and check membership}
    t9 -- Stays in L --> t10[Try i equals 0 or larger i]
    t9 -- Exits L --> t11[Contradiction found - L is not CFL]
    t10 --> t11

    style t1 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style t11 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style t9 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define Greibach Normal Form (GNF). State any two properties of a grammar in GNF.

**Model Answer:**
A Context-Free Grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is in **Greibach Normal Form** if every production has the form $A \rightarrow a\alpha$ where $A \in V$, $a \in T$, and $\alpha \in V^{*}$.

**Two properties:**
1. There are no $\varepsilon$-productions (except possibly $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$).
2. The derivation tree height for any string $w \in L(G)$ of length $n$ is exactly $n$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** State the Pumping Lemma for Context-Free Languages. What is its significance?

**Model Answer:**
If $L$ is a CFL, then there exists a constant $n$ such that every string $z \in L$ with $|z| \geq n$ can be written as $z = uvwxy$ where:
- $|vwx| \leq n$
- $|vx| \geq 1$
- $uv^{i}wx^{i}y \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$

**Significance:** It is used to **prove that certain languages are NOT context-free**, by deriving a contradiction when a chosen long string is pumped.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Convert the following grammar into Greibach Normal Form: **(7 marks)**
$$S \rightarrow AA \mid a$$
$$A \rightarrow SA \mid b$$

**(b)** Prove that $L = \{a^{n}b^{n}a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$ is **not** a context-free language using the Pumping Lemma. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1: Verify prerequisites.** No $\varepsilon$-productions, no unit-productions ✓. Variables are ordered: $A_1 = S$, $A_2 = A$.

**Step 2: Process $A_2 = A$ first.**
$$A \rightarrow SA \mid b$$
This rule starts with $S$, not a terminal. Substitute $S$'s rules:
$$\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow (AA) A \mid aA \mid b \\
&= AAA \mid aA \mid b
\end{aligned}$$
Now $A$ still has rules starting with $A$ — **left recursion exists**.

**Step 3: Apply left-recursion elimination.**
Grammar for $A$:
$$A \rightarrow AAA \mid aA \mid b$$
Rewrite as $A \rightarrow aA \mid b \mid AAA$. Introduce new variable $Z$:
$$\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow aA \mid b \mid aAZ \mid bZ \\
Z &\rightarrow AA \mid AAAZ
\end{aligned}$$
Now $A$ is in GNF (starts with terminal).

**Step 4: Revisit $S$.**
Substitute $A$'s GNF rules into $S \rightarrow AA$:
$$\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow (aA)A \mid bA \mid (aAZ)A \mid (bZ)A \mid a \\
&= aAA \mid bA \mid aAZA \mid bZA \mid a
\end{aligned}$$
All start with terminal → **GNF Achieved**.

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aAA \mid bA \mid aAZA \mid bZA \mid a \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid b \mid aAZ \mid bZ \\
Z &\rightarrow AA \mid AAAZ
\end{aligned}}$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Assume** $L$ is CFL with pumping length $p$. Choose $z = a^{p}b^{p}a^{p}b^{p} \in L$ with $|z| = 4p \geq p$.

By Pumping Lemma: $z = uvwxy$ with $|vwx| \leq p$.

Since $|vwx| \leq p$, the substring $vwx$ **cannot span all four blocks** of $z$. It lies within at most two adjacent blocks.

**Case analysis on which blocks $vx$ lies in:**
- If $vx$ lies entirely within the first $a^{p}$ block: pump $i=2$ → $a^{p+|vx|}b^{p}a^{p}b^{p}$. The first $a$-block no longer equals the second → contradiction.
- If $vx$ spans first $a^{p}$ and first $b^{p}$: pumping disturbs only one $a$-block and one $b$-block → mismatch with other two blocks → contradiction.
- Similar contradictions for all other cases by symmetry.

Hence $L$ is **not context-free**. ∎

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** Convert the following grammar into Greibach Normal Form: **(7 marks)**
$$S \rightarrow aB \mid bA$$
$$A \rightarrow a \mid aS \mid bAA$$
$$B \rightarrow b \mid bS \mid aBB$$

**(b)** Using the Pumping Lemma, prove that $L = \{a^{2^{n}} \mid n \geq 0\}$ is not context-free. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** No $\varepsilon$-productions or unit-productions. Order variables: $A_1 = S$, $A_2 = A$, $A_3 = B$.

**Step 2: Process $A_2 = A$.**
$$A \rightarrow a \mid aS \mid bAA$$
All rules start with a terminal → **$A$ is already in GNF**.

**Step 3: Process $A_3 = B$.**
$$B \rightarrow b \mid bS \mid aBB$$
All rules start with a terminal → **$B$ is already in GNF**.

**Step 4: Revisit $S$.**
Substitute processed variables into $S \rightarrow aB \mid bA$:
- $S \rightarrow aB$: $B$ already starts with $a$ or $b$ — substitute:
$$\begin{aligned}
aB &\rightarrow ab \mid abS \mid a(aBB) = aaBB \\
\Rightarrow S &\rightarrow ab \mid abS \mid aaBB \mid ba \mid baS \mid bbAA
\end{aligned}$$

Final GNF:
$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow ab \mid abS \mid aaBB \mid ba \mid baS \mid bbAA \\
A &\rightarrow a \mid aS \mid bAA \\
B &\rightarrow b \mid bS \mid aBB
\end{aligned}}$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Assume** $L$ is CFL with pumping length $p$. Choose:
$$z = a^{2^{p}} \in L$$
with $|z| = 2^{p} \geq p$.

Decompose $z = uvwxy$ with $|vx| \geq 1$ and $|vwx| \leq p$.

Pump up: $uv^{2}wx^{2}y = a^{2^{p} + |vx|}$.

For this string to belong to $L$, we need:
$$2^{p} + |vx| = 2^{m} \text{ for some } m$$

But $|vx| \leq |vwx| \leq p$, so:
$$2^{p} < 2^{p} + |vx| \leq 2^{p} + p$$

For $p \geq 1$, the next power of 2 after $2^{p}$ is $2^{p+1} = 2 \cdot 2^{p}$. So we need:
$$|vx| \geq 2^{p}$$

But $|vx| \leq p$, which contradicts $|vx| \geq 2^{p}$ for $p \geq 1$.

Hence $L$ is **not context-free**. ∎

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Order of variable processing matters!** Always process $A_2$ before $A_1$ (i.e., lower-indexed variables get their rules fixed first, then substituted upward). Reversing the order yields incorrect GNF.
> 2. **Forgetting to substitute back.** After processing $A_2, A_3, \ldots, A_n$, you **must** revisit $A_1$'s rules and substitute the newly GNF-formatted variables. Many students skip this step and lose 3–4 marks.
> 3. **In Pumping Lemma proofs,** do NOT forget to verify all three conditions in your contradiction. Examiners specifically check $|vwx| \leq n$, $|vx| \geq 1$, and the pumped string's membership.
> 4. **Choosing a wrong test string.** The string $z$ MUST be of length at least $n$ and must be carefully constructed so that pumping it *necessarily* breaks the language's defining property.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GNF Definition:** Every production is $A \rightarrow a\alpha$ where $a$ is a single terminal and $\alpha$ is a (possibly empty) string of variables.
- **GNF is stricter than CNF** — it forbids left recursion entirely.
- **Conversion Pipeline:** CFG → (remove $\varepsilon$, unit, useless) → CNF → Order variables → Eliminate left recursion → Substitute back → GNF.
- **Left recursion formula:**
  $$A \rightarrow A\alpha_1 \mid \ldots \mid A\alpha_m \mid \beta_1 \mid \ldots \mid \beta_n$$
  becomes
  $$A \rightarrow \beta_i \mid \beta_i Z, \quad Z \rightarrow \alpha_j \mid \alpha_j Z$$
- **Pumping Lemma:** $z = uvwxy$ with $|vwx| \leq n$, $|vx| \geq 1$, $uv^{i}wx^{i}y \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$.
- **Pumping Lemma is for proving NON-CFL only** — not every non-CFL can be proven non-CFL by pumping, but it is the standard KTU tool.
- **Typical non-CFL test cases:** $\{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n}\}$, $\{ww\}$, $\{a^{n^2}\}$, $\{a^{n!}\}$, $\{a^{i}b^{j}c^{k} \mid i < j < k\}$.
- **GNF advantage:** Enables single-state PDA construction and efficient linear-time parsing — a cornerstone of compiler theory.
- **Always revisit the start symbol's rules** after processing other variables in GNF conversion.
- **Use the Pigeonhole Principle** implicitly when explaining why a repeated variable must appear in any parse tree of depth $> |V|$.
- **For 14-mark exam questions,** spend roughly **equal time** on GNF conversion (algorithm walk-through) and the Pumping Lemma proof (case analysis + contradiction).
- **KTU 2024 Bloom's Tag:** This topic spans **Apply** (GNF conversion), **Analyze** (pumping lemma application), and **Evaluate** (non-CFL proof).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Closure and Decision Properties of Context-Free Languages, Linear Bounded Automata
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Closure and Decision Properties of Context-Free Languages, Linear Bounded Automata

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Closure Properties of Context-Free Languages (CFLs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A closure property of a language family $\mathcal{L}$ under an operation $\circ$ states that if $L_1, L_2 \in \mathcal{L}$, then $L_1 \circ L_2 \in \mathcal{L}$. In other words, performing the operation on languages within the family yields another language still inside the family.

A language family is **closed** under an operation if performing that operation on any language(s) in the family always produces a language that is also in the family. Closure properties are foundational because they tell us *what kinds of languages we can build* from known CFLs using simple operations.

### 1.2 Decision Properties of CFLs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A decision property is an algorithm that takes a formal-language description (such as a CFG or PDA) as input and answers a yes/no question about a specific language-theoretic property — such as emptiness, finiteness, or membership.

> [!NOTE]
> **Revisit Your COs:**
> In KTU's Outcome-Based framework, you must distinguish between a property being **Decidable** (an algorithm always halts with a correct answer) versus **Undecidable** (no such algorithm can exist for the general case).

### 1.3 Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus — Sipser, Hopcroft-Ullman Standard):**
> A **Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA)** is a restricted Turing Machine $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ in which the tape head is constrained so it cannot move past the leftmost or rightmost cell of the input. Equivalently, the usable tape is bounded by $c \cdot \vert w \vert$ cells for some constant $c$, where $\vert w \vert$ is the input length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Three Tiers of Computing:**
> Imagine a librarian with three desks:
> 1. **PDA (Pushdown Automaton)** — A librarian with a *single tall stack of books*: can match parentheses, build nested structures, but struggles with two related lists.
> 2. **Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA)** — A librarian with a *long table whose size is exactly proportional to the number of pages on the desk* (input). Powerful, but cannot exploit unbounded scratch paper.
> 3. **Turing Machine** — A librarian with an *infinite warehouse*: the most powerful model.

The **LBA sits exactly between a PDA and a TM** in the Chomsky hierarchy and recognizes precisely the **Context-Sensitive Languages (CSL)**.

### 1.4 Real-World Engineering Context

- **Closure properties** are used in *compiler construction* — when you compose language recognizers, you must know whether the resulting language is still parseable by the same parser class.
- **Decision properties** drive tools like `yacc`/`bison` and `ANTLR` — the parser generator itself decides emptiness/ambiguity of the grammar.
- **LBAs** model practical systems with bounded memory such as *embedded controllers*, *network protocol verifiers*, and *string-rewriting databases* — all have memory linearly proportional to input size.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Closure Properties of CFLs — Complete Breakdown

#### A. Operations Under Which CFLs ARE Closed

| # | Operation | Construction Hint | Closed? |
|---|-----------|------------------|---------|
| 1 | Union: $L_1 \cup L_2$ | New start symbol $S \to S_1 \mid S_2$ | ✅ Yes |
| 2 | Concatenation: $L_1 L_2$ | $S \to S_1 S_2$ | ✅ Yes |
| 3 | Kleene Star: $L^{\star}$ | $S \to S_1 S \mid \varepsilon$ | ✅ Yes |
| 4 | Reversal: $L^{R}$ | Reverse every production's RHS | ✅ Yes |
| 5 | Homomorphism: $h(L)$ | Apply $h$ to each terminal | ✅ Yes |
| 6 | Inverse Homomorphism: $h^{-1}(L)$ | Pre-image construction | ✅ Yes |
| 7 | Substitution: $\sigma(L)$ | Replace non-terminals by CFGs | ✅ Yes |
| 8 | Intersection with Regular: $L \cap R$ | Product construction $PDA \times DFA$ | ✅ Yes |
| 9 | Reverse Homomorphism | String-mapping inverse | ✅ Yes |
| 10 | Quotient by Regular: $L / R$ | Track suffix recognition | ✅ Yes |

#### B. Operations Under Which CFLs are NOT Closed

> [!WARNING]
> **HIGH-YIELD — These are favorite KTU questions:**

| # | Operation | Counter-Example |
|---|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Intersection**: $L_1 \cap L_2$ | $L_1 = \{a^n b^n c^m\}$, $L_2 = \{a^m b^n c^n\}$; intersection gives $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$, which is **NOT** context-free (proved via pumping lemma). |
| 2 | **Complement**: $\overline{L}$ | CFLs are not closed under complement, but note that DCFLs ARE closed under complement. |
| 3 | **Difference**: $L_1 - L_2$ | Since $L^c = \Sigma^{\star} - L$, non-closure under complement directly implies non-closure under difference. |
| 4 | **Intersection of CFL with CFL** | Reduces to case (1). |

#### C. Proof Strategy Recap

For each closure property, the standard KTU proof technique is:
1. Given CFGs $G_1 = (V_1, \Sigma, R_1, S_1)$ and $G_2 = (V_2, \Sigma, R_2, S_2)$.
2. Construct a new CFG $G'$ (or PDA $M'$, or combined machine) using a fresh start symbol.
3. Show **every string derivation in $G'$ corresponds to a derivation in $G_1$ and/or $G_2$** (soundness).
4. Show **every string accepted by $L_1 \circ L_2$ has a valid derivation in $G'$** (completeness).

### 2.2 Decision Properties of CFLs

| Property | Question | Decidable? | Algorithm |
|----------|----------|------------|-----------|
| Membership: $w \in L(G)$? | Is a specific string in the CFL? | ✅ **Yes** | CYK Algorithm (Cocke-Younger-Kasami) — $O(\vert w \vert^3)$ |
| Emptiness: $L(G) = \emptyset$? | Does the grammar generate any string? | ✅ **Yes** | Mark reachable & generating symbols; check if $S$ is generating. |
| Finiteness: $\vert L(G) \vert < \infty$? | Is the language finite? | ✅ **Yes** | Detect cycles among generating symbols (no cycle on a viable path). |
| Infiniteness: $\vert L(G) \vert = \infty$? | Complement of finiteness | ✅ **Yes** | Same cycle-detection algorithm. |
| Equivalence: $L(G_1) = L(G_2)$? | Do two CFGs generate the same language? | ❌ **No** | Undecidable — reduced from Post Correspondence Problem. |
| Ambiguity: Is $G$ ambiguous? | Does some string have two parse trees? | ❌ **No** | Undecidable — proved by Ginsburg. |
| Subset: $L(G_1) \subseteq L(G_2)$? | CFL inclusion | ❌ **No** | Undecidable in general. |
| Regularity: Is $L$ regular? | Is this CFL actually regular? | ❌ **No** | Undecidable. |
| $L = \Sigma^{\star}$? | Universality | ❌ **No** | Undecidable. |

### 2.3 Linear Bounded Automaton — Deep Dive

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Linear Bounded"?**
> Because the tape usable for computation grows **at most linearly** with the input length — bounded by $c \cdot \vert w \vert$ for some constant $c$. In the standard formulation $c = 1$, using exactly $\vert w \vert$ cells.

**Formal Definition:**

An LBA is a 7-tuple:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

where:

- $Q$ — finite set of states
- $\Sigma$ — input alphabet (subset of $\Gamma$)
- $\Gamma$ — finite tape alphabet
- $\delta$ — transition function: $Q \times \Gamma \to Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ (deterministic LBA) or $2^{Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}}$ (non-deterministic LBA)
- $q_0 \in Q$ — start state
- $B \in \Gamma - \Sigma$ — blank symbol
- $F \subseteq Q$ — set of final states

**Critical Constraints:**

1. The input $w$ is placed between two sentinel markers, often $\vdash$ (left) and $\dashv$ (right).
2. The read-write head **cannot cross** these sentinels — this is the linear bound.
3. The LBA halts and accepts if it reaches a state in $F$.

**Languages Recognized by LBA:**

$$\text{LBA} \equiv \text{Context-Sensitive Languages (CSL)} \equiv \text{NSPACE}(n)$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | KTU Context |
|------------------|---------|-------------|
| $\Sigma^{\star}$ | Set of all strings over $\Sigma$ | Used in complement proofs |
| $L_1 \cdot L_2$ | Concatenation | Closure proof by $S \to S_1 S_2$ |
| $L^R$ | Reversal | Replace each production $A \to \alpha$ by $A \to \alpha^R$ |
| $h^{-1}(L)$ | Inverse homomorphism | $h^{-1}(L) = \{w : h(w) \in L\}$ |
| $L_1 \cap L_2 = \{a^n b^n c^n\}$ | Counter-example | Pumps only in one block — NOT CF |
| $O(\vert w \vert^3)$ | CYK complexity | Standard membership test |
| $c \cdot \vert w \vert$ | LBA tape bound | Linear space complexity |
| $\text{NSPACE}(n)$ | LBA complexity class | Equals CSL |
| $\text{NSPACE}(n) = \text{DSPACE}(n)$ | Open? | **Savitch's Theorem** says $\text{NSPACE}(S(n)) \subseteq \text{DSPACE}(S(n)^2)$ — for LBA, $\text{DSPACE}(n^2)$ |
| $A_{\text{LBA}} = \{\langle M, w \rangle : M \text{ is LBA and } M \text{ accepts } w\}$ | Acceptance problem | **Decidable** |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Topic | Engineering Application |
|-------|------------------------|
| CFL closure | Compiler parser composition (LL + LR modules) |
| CFL decision (emptiness) | Dead-code elimination in compiler optimizers |
| CFL decision (membership) | Syntactic analysis in IDEs (syntax highlighting) |
| LBA | Embedded firmware verification, bounded-buffer protocol checking |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Proof — CFL Closed Under Union

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-asked closure proof** in KTU examinations.

**Theorem:** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are context-free languages, then $L_1 \cup L_2$ is context-free.

**Given:**

$$L_1 \text{ generated by } G_1 = (V_1, \Sigma, R_1, S_1)$$
$$L_2 \text{ generated by } G_2 = (V_2, \Sigma, R_2, S_2)$$

**Proof (Construction):**

We construct a new CFG $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ such that $L(G) = L_1 \cup L_2$.

**Step 1.** Define $V = V_1 \cup V_2 \cup \{S\}$ where $S$ is a fresh start symbol not in $V_1 \cup V_2$.

**Step 2.** Define the production set $R = R_1 \cup R_2 \cup \{S \to S_1 \mid S_2\}$.

**Step 3.** Show that $L(G) = L(G_1) \cup L(G_2)$.

$(\supseteq)$: Take any $w \in L(G_1)$. Then $S \Rightarrow_G S_1 \Rightarrow^{\star}_G w$ (using the production $S \to S_1$ and then only rules from $R_1$). Hence $w \in L(G)$.

Similarly, $w \in L(G_2) \implies w \in L(G)$.

$(\subseteq)$: Take any $w \in L(G)$. The first step in any derivation must be $S \Rightarrow S_1$ or $S \Rightarrow S_2$. From there, since $S_1$'s productions never produce $S_2$ and vice versa, the rest of the derivation uses only rules from $R_1$ or $R_2$. Thus $w \in L(G_1)$ or $w \in L(G_2)$.

**Conclusion:** $L(G) = L(G_1) \cup L(G_2)$, hence CFL is closed under union. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Detailed Proof — CFL NOT Closed Under Intersection

**Theorem:** CFL is NOT closed under intersection.

**Proof (by counter-example):**

Consider:
$$L_1 = \{a^n b^n c^m : n \geq 1, m \geq 1\}$$
$$L_2 = \{a^m b^n c^n : n \geq 1, m \geq 1\}$$

**Step 1.** Show $L_1$ is context-free. Use grammar $S \to AC$, $A \to aAb \mid ab$, $C \to cC \mid c$. ✓

**Step 2.** Show $L_2$ is context-free. Use grammar $S \to BC$, $B \to aB \mid a$, $C \to bCc \mid bc$. ✓

**Step 3.** Compute the intersection:

$$L_1 \cap L_2 = \{a^n b^n c^n : n \geq 1\}$$

**Step 4.** Prove $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$ is NOT context-free using the **pumping lemma for CFL**.

Assume $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$ is context-free with pumping length $p$. Choose $w = a^p b^p c^p$.

Decompose $w = uvxyz$ with $\vert vxy \vert \leq p$, $\vert vy \vert \geq 1$.

**Case A:** $vxy$ is contained entirely within $a^p$. Pumping up gives $a^{p+k} b^p c^p$ — the $b^p$ and $c^p$ are no longer equal in count to the $a$'s. Not in language.

**Case B:** $vxy$ spans $a^p$ and $b^p$. Pumping up breaks the balance between $b$ and $c$ blocks.

**Case C:** $vxy$ is contained within $b^p$. Similar to Case A.

**Case D:** $vxy$ spans $b^p$ and $c^p$. Breaks balance.

**Case E:** $vxy$ within $c^p$. Breaks balance.

In every case, $uv^2 xy^2 z \notin L$ — contradiction.

**Conclusion:** $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$ is not context-free, hence CFL is not closed under intersection. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Detailed Proof — CFL Closed Under Intersection with Regular Languages

**Theorem:** If $L_1$ is context-free and $R$ is regular, then $L_1 \cap R$ is context-free.

**Proof (Construction):**

Let $L_1$ be accepted by PDA $P = (Q_P, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta_P, q_P, Z_0, F_P)$.
Let $R$ be accepted by DFA $D = (Q_D, \Sigma, \delta_D, q_D, F_D)$.

**Step 1.** Construct the product PDA $M = (Q_P \times Q_D, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, (q_P, q_D), Z_0, F_P \times F_D)$.

**Step 2.** Define $\delta$:

For each $\big((q_1, q_2), a, X\big)$ where $a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$ and $X \in \Gamma$:

$$\delta\big((q_1, q_2), a, X\big) = \big\{((q_1', \delta_D(q_2, a)), X') : (q_1', X') \in \delta_P(q_1, a, X)\big\}$$

(For $a = \varepsilon$, the DFA state $q_2$ does not change.)

**Step 3.** Show $L(M) = L(P) \cap L(D)$.

A string $w$ is accepted by $M$ iff after reading $w$ the PDA-component is in $F_P$ AND the DFA-component is in $F_D$. Since the DFA tracks $w$ precisely, $w \in L(D)$. Since the PDA-component tracks the original pushdown behavior, $w \in L(P)$. Conversely, if $w \in L(P) \cap L(D)$, the synchronized construction succeeds.

**Conclusion:** $L(M) = L(P) \cap L(D)$ is context-free. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Detailed Proof — Decision Property: Finiteness of CFL

**Theorem:** Given a CFG $G$, the question "$L(G)$ is finite" is DECIDABLE.

**Algorithm:**

**Step 1.** Eliminate $\varepsilon$-productions and unit productions from $G$ (standard CNF transformation).

**Step 2.** Identify the set of **generating symbols** $G_{\text{gen}}$ via fixed-point iteration:
- Base: every terminal is generating.
- Inductive step: $A$ is generating if some production $A \to \alpha$ has all symbols of $\alpha$ already known to be generating.

**Step 3.** Identify the set of **reachable symbols** $G_{\text{reach}}$:
- Base: $S$ is reachable.
- Inductive step: $A$ is reachable if a production $B \to \alpha$ exists where $B$ is reachable and $A$ appears in $\alpha$.

**Step 4.** Restrict to useful symbols: $U = G_{\text{gen}} \cap G_{\text{reach}}$.

**Step 5.** Build the **dependency graph** $D = (U, E)$ where $(A, B) \in E$ iff there is a production $A \to \alpha_1 B \alpha_2$ in $G$.

**Step 6.** Check for cycles in $D$. If a cycle exists on a path from $S$ that traverses only useful symbols, then $L(G)$ is infinite. Otherwise, $L(G)$ is finite.

**Decidability:** Each step is a finite computation (finite number of symbols, finite productions), so the algorithm always terminates. Hence finiteness is **decidable**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.5 Detailed Proof — Decidability of $A_{\text{LBA}}$

**Theorem:** $A_{\text{LBA}} = \{\langle M, w \rangle : M \text{ is an LBA that accepts } w\}$ is DECIDABLE.

**Proof Idea:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a frequently-asked KTU question — the trick is the **finite configuration space**.

**Step 1.** A deterministic LBA on input $w$ has tape of size exactly $\vert w \vert$. The tape alphabet $\Gamma$ is finite, with $k = \vert \Gamma \vert$.

**Step 2.** Number of distinct configurations:
- States: $\vert Q \vert = m$ (finite).
- Head position: $\vert w \vert$ choices.
- Tape content: $k^{\vert w \vert}$ possibilities.
- Total: $C = m \cdot \vert w \vert \cdot k^{\vert w \vert}$ configurations.

**Step 3.** If $M$ has more than $C$ moves without accepting, by the pigeonhole principle, some configuration must repeat, meaning $M$ is in a loop.

**Step 4.** The decider $D$ simulates $M$ on $w$ for up to $C + 1$ moves:
- If $M$ accepts within $C$ moves — **accept**.
- If $M$ rejects within $C$ moves — **reject**.
- If $M$ makes $C + 1$ moves without halting — **reject** (must be looping).

**Conclusion:** $D$ always halts. Hence $A_{\text{LBA}}$ is decidable. $\blacksquare$

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — CFL Decision Properties

| Property | Decidable? | Algorithm Complexity | Key Idea |
|----------|------------|----------------------|----------|
| Membership ($w \in L$?) | ✅ Yes | $O(\vert w \vert^3)$ | CYK — dynamic programming on CNF |
| Emptiness | ✅ Yes | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert R \vert)$ | Fixed-point on generating symbols |
| Finiteness | ✅ Yes | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | Cycle detection in dependency graph |
| Infiniteness | ✅ Yes | Same as Finiteness | Complement of Finiteness |
| Equivalence ($L_1 = L_2$?) | ❌ No | — | Reduction from PCP |
| Ambiguity | ❌ No | — | Reduction from PCP |
| Subset ($L_1 \subseteq L_2$?) | ❌ No | — | Reduction from universality |
| Regularity | ❌ No | — | Reduction from PCP |
| Universality ($L = \Sigma^{\star}$?) | ❌ No | — | Complement-of-emptiness reduction |

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — LBA vs PDA vs TM

| Feature | PDA | LBA | TM |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|
| Memory | Stack (unbounded, LIFO) | Tape (linear in input) | Tape (unbounded) |
| Language Class | CFL | CSL | Recursively Enumerable |
| Configuration Count | Infinite (stack can grow) | Finite ($\leq m \cdot \vert w \vert \cdot k^{\vert w \vert}$) | Infinite |
| Halting Problem | Decidable for DPDA | Decidable for DLBA | Undecidable |
| Equivalence Problem | Undecidable | Undecidable | Undecidable |
| Acceptance | Stack empty OR final state | Final state (typically) | Final state |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LBA Tape Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef boundary fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tape fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef head fill:#f94144,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef label fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    L[[Left Sentinel ⊢]]:::boundary
    R[[Right Sentinel ⊣]]:::boundary

    C1[w1]:::tape
    C2[w2]:::tape
    C3[w3]:::tape
    C4[w4]:::tape
    C5[w5]:::tape
    C6[w6]:::tape
    C7[w7]:::tape

    HD{{Read Write Head}}:::head

    L --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> C6 --> C7 --> R

    HD -. scans only w1..w7 .-> C4

    NOTE["Head cannot cross ⊢ or ⊣ — bounded by input length"]:::label

    style NOTE fill:#fff,stroke:#999,stroke-dasharray:5 5
```

### 4.2 Closure Property Tree for CFLs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef closed fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef notclosed fill:#f94144,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    classDef neutral fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,color:#000

    A[CFL Operations]:::neutral

    A --> B[CLOSED UNDER]:::neutral
    A --> C[NOT CLOSED UNDER]:::neutral

    B --> B1[Union]:::closed
    B --> B2[Concatenation]:::closed
    B --> B3[Kleene Star]:::closed
    B --> B4[Reversal]:::closed
    B --> B5[Homomorphism]:::closed
    B --> B6[Inverse Homomorphism]:::closed
    B --> B7[Substitution]:::closed
    B --> B8[Intersection with Regular]:::closed

    C --> C1[Intersection of two CFLs]:::notclosed
    C --> C2[Complement of CFL]:::notclosed
    C --> C3[Difference of two CFLs]:::notclosed
```

### 4.3 Decision Property Decidability Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef dec fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef undec fill:#f94144,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    classDef root fill:#4361ee,stroke:#333,color:#fff

    ROOT[CFL Decision Question]:::root

    ROOT --> D[DECIDABLE]:::root
    ROOT --> U[UNDECIDABLE]:::root

    D --> D1[Is w in L? - CYK]:::dec
    D --> D2[Is L empty?]:::dec
    D --> D3[Is L finite?]:::dec
    D --> D4[Is L infinite?]:::dec

    U --> U1[Is L1 equal to L2?]:::undec
    U --> U2[Is G ambiguous?]:::undec
    U --> U3[Is L1 subset of L2?]:::undec
    U --> U4[Is L regular?]:::undec
    U --> U5[Is L equal to Sigma star?]:::undec
```

### 4.4 LBA Acceptance Decider Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef input fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#4361ee,stroke:#333,color:#fff

    I[/Input: ⟨M, w⟩/]:::input
    P1[Simulate M on w step by step]:::proc
    P2[Track number of moves]:::proc
    P3{Bound Check: moves ≤ m·|w|·k^|w| ?}:::proc
    P4[M accepts? -> ACCEPT]:::out
    P5[M rejects? -> REJECT]:::out
    P6[Pigeonhole: loop detected -> REJECT]:::out

    I --> P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P3 -- Yes, accept observed --> P4
    P3 -- Yes, reject observed --> P5
    P3 -- Yes, exceeds bound --> P6
```

### 4.5 Chomsky Hierarchy Positioning of LBA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef lvl fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef lba fill:#f94144,stroke:#333,color:#fff

    L3[Type 0: Recursively Enumerable Languages - Turing Machine]:::top
    L1[Type 1: Context-Sensitive Languages - LBA]:::lba
    L2[Type 2: Context-Free Languages - PDA]:::lvl
    L4[Type 3: Regular Languages - DFA]:::lvl

    L3 -- contains --> L1
    L1 -- contains --> L2
    L2 -- contains --> L4
```
