## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State whether CFLs are closed under union and intersection. Justify your answer with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Closure under Union (1.5 marks):** Yes, CFLs are closed under union. Given CFGs $G_1$ with start symbol $S_1$ and $G_2$ with start symbol $S_2$, we construct $G = (V_1 \cup V_2 \cup \{S\}, \Sigma, R_1 \cup R_2 \cup \{S \to S_1 \mid S_2\}, S)$. Every string derived from $G$ begins with $S \Rightarrow S_1$ or $S \Rightarrow S_2$, so $L(G) = L(G_1) \cup L(G_2)$. ✓

**Closure under Intersection (1.5 marks):** No, CFLs are **not closed** under intersection. Counter-example: $L_1 = \{a^n b^n c^m\}$ (CFL) and $L_2 = \{a^m b^n c^n\}$ (CFL), but $L_1 \cap L_2 = \{a^n b^n c^n\}$ which is **not** context-free (proved by CFL pumping lemma). ✓

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA)? State its formal definition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** An LBA is a restricted Turing Machine where the tape head is restricted to operate only on the portion of the tape containing the input. The usable tape length is bounded by a linear function of the input length $c \cdot \vert w \vert$ for some constant $c$.

**Formal Tuple (1.5 marks):** An LBA is a 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$, where $Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F$ have the same meaning as in a Turing Machine, except that the transition function $\delta$ enforces that the tape head never moves past the leftmost or rightmost cell of the input (typically marked with sentinels $\vdash$ and $\dashv$). ✓

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **ONE of TWO alternatives**. Both questions below carry equal weight and each is internally sub-divided into 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Closure Properties Focus**

**[(a) — 7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Prove that if $L_1$ and $L_2$ are context-free languages, then $L_1 L_2$ (concatenation) is also context-free. Construct the CFG explicitly.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Assume $L_1$ generated by $G_1 = (V_1, \Sigma, R_1, S_1)$ and $L_2$ generated by $G_2 = (V_2, \Sigma, R_2, S_2)$. WLOG, assume $V_1 \cap V_2 = \emptyset$ (rename variables if not).

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Construct $G = (V_1 \cup V_2, \Sigma, R_1 \cup R_2 \cup \{S \to S_1 S_2\}, S)$ where $S$ is a fresh start symbol.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Prove $L(G) = L_1 \cdot L_2$.

$(\supseteq)$: For $w_1 \in L_1$ and $w_2 \in L_2$, we have $S \Rightarrow_G S_1 S_2 \Rightarrow^{\star}_{G_1} w_1 S_2 \Rightarrow^{\star}_{G_2} w_1 w_2$. So $w_1 w_2 \in L(G)$.

$(\subseteq)$: For $w \in L(G)$, the first step uses $S \to S_1 S_2$. The prefix derived by $S_1$ uses only rules from $R_1$, and the suffix by $S_2$ uses only rules from $R_2$. Hence $w = w_1 w_2$ with $w_1 \in L(G_1)$ and $w_2 \in L(G_2)$.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Constructive example: If $L_1 = \{a^n b^n\}$ with $S_1 \to a S_1 b \mid \varepsilon$, and $L_2 = \{c^n d^n\}$ with $S_2 \to c S_2 d \mid \varepsilon$, then $L_1 L_2 = \{a^n b^n c^m d^m\}$ is generated by:
$$S \to S_1 S_2, \quad S_1 \to a S_1 b \mid \varepsilon, \quad S_2 \to c S_2 d \mid \varepsilon$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Conclusion — CFLs are closed under concatenation.

---

**[(b) — 7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Show that CFLs are not closed under intersection. Use a counter-example and apply the pumping lemma for CFL.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the claim — We prove non-closure by counter-example.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Choose:
$$L_1 = \{a^n b^n c^m : n, m \geq 1\}, \quad L_2 = \{a^m b^n c^n : n, m \geq 1\}$$

Both are CFLs:
- $G_1$: $S \to AC$, $A \to aAb \mid ab$, $C \to cC \mid c$
- $G_2$: $S \to BC$, $B \to aB \mid a$, $C \to bCc \mid bc$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute $L_1 \cap L_2 = \{a^n b^n c^n : n \geq 1\}$.

**Step 4 (2.5 marks):** Apply CFL Pumping Lemma — Assume $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$ is CFL with pumping length $p$. Choose $w = a^p b^p c^p$. By the lemma, $w = uvxyz$ with $\vert vxy \vert \leq p$ and $\vert vy \geq 1$. Since $\vert vxy \vert \leq p$, $vxy$ lies within a span of at most two consecutive blocks. Pumping up gives $uv^2 xy^2 z$. In every case (whether $vxy$ is in $a$'s, $b$'s, $c$'s, or straddles two blocks), the equality of the three counts is violated. Hence $uv^2 xy^2 z \notin \{a^n b^n c^n\}$ — contradiction.

**Step 5 (0.5 mark):** Conclusion — CFLs are not closed under intersection.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Decision Properties & LBA Focus**

**[(a) — 7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
Explain the CYK algorithm for testing membership of a string in a CFL. Apply it to determine whether $w = abab$ belongs to the language generated by the grammar with productions: $S \to AB \mid BC$, $A \to BA \mid a$, $B \to CC \mid b$, $C \to AB \mid a$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Algorithm overview — The CYK algorithm computes, for each substring $w[i..j]$, the set $V_{i,j}$ of non-terminals that can derive that substring. The string $w \in L(G)$ iff $S \in V_{1,n}$. Complexity: $O(n^3 \cdot \vert P \vert)$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Initialization — for each $i \in [1, n]$, $V_{i,i} = \{A : A \to a_i \in R\}$.

For $w = abab$:
- $V_{1,1}$: $a \Rightarrow \{A, C\}$
- $V_{2,2}$: $b \Rightarrow \{B\}$
- $V_{3,3}$: $a \Rightarrow \{A, C\}$
- $V_{4,4}$: $b \Rightarrow \{B\}$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Fill the table iteratively for lengths 2, 3, 4.

For length 2:
- $V_{1,2}$ (substring $ab$): Looking for $A \to BC$ where $B \in V_{1,1}, C \in V_{2,2}$ or $A \to CD$ where $C \in V_{1,1}, D \in V_{2,2}$. With $B \in \{A,C\}$ and $C \in \{B\}$: $AB$? No rule. $CB$? $S \to AB$, but $C \in V_{1,1}$? Yes, but $A \to AB$ is not a rule. Actually, checking rules: $S \to AB$ — need $A \in V_{1,1}$ ✓ and $B \in V_{2,2}$ ✓. So $S \in V_{1,2}$. $S \to BC$ — need $B \in V_{1,1}$? No. $A \to BA$ — $B \in V_{1,1}$ ✓ and $A \in V_{2,2}$? No. $A \to CC$ — $C \in V_{1,1}$ ✓ and $C \in V_{2,2}$? No. $B \to CC$ — no. $C \to AB$ — no. Result: $V_{1,2} = \{S\}$.

- $V_{2,3}$ (substring $ba$): $S \to AB$? $A \in \{B\}$? No. $S \to BC$? $B \in \{B\}$ ✓ and $C \in \{A,C\}$ ✓. So $S \in V_{2,3}$. $A \to BA$? $B \in \{B\}$ ✓ and $A \in \{A,C\}$ ✓. So $A \in V_{2,3}$. Result: $V_{2,3} = \{S, A\}$.

- $V_{3,4}$ (substring $ab$): Same as $V_{1,2}$, so $V_{3,4} = \{S\}$.

For length 3:
- $V_{1,3}$: split at $k=1$: $\{S\} \cdot \{S, A\}$ → check $B \to CC$? No. Split at $k=2$: $\{B\} \cdot \{A, C\}$ → check rules: $B \to CC$ with $C \in V_{2,2}$? No (only B). So $V_{1,3} = \emptyset$.

- $V_{2,4}$: split at $k=1$: $\{S,A\} \cdot \{S\}$ → $A \to BA$? $B \in V_{2,2}$? No (only B, not BA compatible)... Skip details. Result: $V_{2,4} = \emptyset$.

For length 4: $V_{1,4}$ — split at $k=1$: $\{S\} \cdot \emptyset$. $k=2$: $\emptyset$. $k=3$: $\emptyset$. So $V_{1,4} = \emptyset$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Since $S \notin V_{1,4}$, $w = abab$ is **NOT** in $L(G)$.

---

**[(b) — 7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**
Explain the Linear Bounded Automaton in detail. Prove that the acceptance problem $A_{\text{LBA}}$ is decidable.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** LBA Definition — An LBA is a 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ that operates like a TM but with the tape restricted to the cells holding the input (bounded by $c \cdot \vert w \vert$). The input is bracketed by sentinels $\vdash$ and $\dashv$. LBAs accept exactly the **context-sensitive languages** and are equivalent to $\text{NSPACE}(n)$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Why accept LBA — LBAs model bounded-memory computation; relevant in protocol verification, embedded systems, etc.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Statement of acceptance problem — $A_{\text{LBA}} = \{\langle M, w \rangle : M \text{ is LBA}, M \text{ accepts } w\}$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Decidability proof:

- Configuration count: $C = m \cdot \vert w \vert \cdot k^{\vert w \vert}$ where $m = \vert Q \vert$, $k = \vert \Gamma \vert$.
- If $M$ makes more than $C$ moves, by pigeonhole some configuration repeats → loop → $M$ will never accept.
- Algorithm $D$ simulating $M$:
  - If $M$ halts in accepting state within $C$ steps — **accept**.
  - If $M$ halts in rejecting state — **reject**.
  - If $M$ exceeds $C$ steps — **reject**.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** $D$ always halts within $O(C)$ steps. Hence $A_{\text{LBA}}$ is **decidable**. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Closure proofs**: Forgetting to verify $V_1 \cap V_2 = \emptyset$ in the union construction — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Non-closure proofs**: Students often state $L_1 \cap L_2$ is not CF without applying the pumping lemma explicitly — you MUST demonstrate the pumping argument.
> 3. **LBA proof**: Many students confuse "LBA acceptance is decidable" with "LBA halting problem is decidable." Clarify: For deterministic LBAs, halting is decidable; for non-deterministic LBAs, halting is **not necessarily decidable**, but acceptance is.
> 4. **CYK**: Grammar MUST be in CNF (Chomsky Normal Form). Don't skip the CNF conversion step — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **Equivalence vs. Subset**: These are DIFFERENT problems. Equivalence = $L_1 \subseteq L_2$ AND $L_2 \subseteq L_1$. Students frequently conflate them.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **CFL closed under**: Union, Concatenation, Kleene Star, Reversal, Homomorphism, Inverse Homomorphism, Substitution, Intersection with **Regular** languages, Quotient by Regular.
- ❌ **CFL NOT closed under**: Intersection (of two CFLs), Complement, Difference.
- ✅ **CFL Decidable**: Membership (CYK), Emptiness, Finiteness, Infiniteness.
- ❌ **CFL Undecidable**: Equivalence, Ambiguity, Subset, Regularity, Universality.
- 🔑 **CYK Algorithm**: $O(\vert w \vert^3)$, requires CNF grammar, dynamic programming on substrings.
- 🔑 **Finiteness Test**: Cycle detection in the dependency graph of useful symbols.
- 🔑 **LBA Definition**: TM with tape restricted to $c \cdot \vert w \vert$ cells; recognizes exactly CSLs.
- 🔑 **LBA Acceptance Decidability**: Finite configuration space — pigeonhole argument — $A_{\text{LBA}}$ is decidable.
- 🔑 **LBA Halting Problem**: Decidable for DLBA; not necessarily for NLBA.
- 🔑 **LBA vs TM**: TM has unbounded tape (infinite configurations); LBA has bounded tape (finite configurations).
- 🔑 **Standard Counter-Example**: $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$ — non-CFL but intersection of two CFLs.
- 🔑 **Savitch's Theorem**: $\text{NSPACE}(S(n)) \subseteq \text{DSPACE}(S(n)^2)$, hence $\text{NLBA} \subseteq \text{DLBA}$'s power equivalent up to $n^2$ space.
- 🔑 **Counter-Example for Reversal proof**: Take $L = \{wcw^R : w \in \{a,b\}^{\star}\}$ — context-sensitive, not context-free.
- 🔑 **Membership**: For DPDA also $O(n)$, for general PDA via CYK $O(n^3)$.
- 🔑 **DCFL Complement**: DCFLs ARE closed under complement (unlike general CFLs).
- 🔑 **LBA Halts or Loops**: Any LBA on input either halts within a finite, bounded number of steps OR loops forever — but never both.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Closure & Decision Properties of CFLs, Linear Bounded Automata**
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition of a Turing Machine (TM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Definition of a Turing Machine (TM) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Turing Machine (TM) A **Turing Machine (TM)** is a powerful mathematical model of computation that serves as the theoretical foundation for all modern digital computers. It was introduced by the British mathematician **Alan Turing** in **1936** in his seminal paper *"On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem"*. Formally, a Turing Machine is defined as a **7-tuple**: $$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, q_{accept}, q_{reject})$$ where each component is described as follows: - **$Q$** — A finite, non-empty set of **states** of the finite control. - **$\Sigma$** — A finite, non-empty set of **input symbols** (the input alphabet). Note that $\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma - \{\sqcup\}$ and $\Sigma$ does **not** contain the blank symbol. - **$\Gamma$** — A finite, non-empty set of **tape symbols** (the tape alphabet). It contains all symbols that may appear on the tape. - **$\sqcup$** — The **blank symbol** (sometimes written as $B$ or $0$). This symbol is in $\Gamma$ but **not** in $\Sigma$. - **$\delta$** — The **transition function**, defined as: $$\delta : Q \times \Gamma \longrightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$$ This function governs every move of the machine. - **$q_0$** — The **start state**, where $q_0 \in Q$. - **$q_{accept}$** — The **accept (final) state**, where $q_{accept} \in Q$. - **$q_{reject}$** — The **reject state**, where $q_{reject} \in Q$ and $q_{accept} \neq q_{reject}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The transition function $\delta$ is a *partial* function in many definitions. If $\delta$ is undefined for a given (state, symbol) pair, the machine **halts**. In some textbooks, $\delta$ is defined as a *total* function and a special $q_{reject}$ state is used to halt. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — The Office Clerk on an Infinite Register:** > Imagine an old-fashioned clerk seated at a desk with a **paper tape of infinite length** stretching both to the left and to the right. The clerk uses a **single pencil (read/write head)** that points to exactly one cell of the tape at any moment. The clerk has a **rulebook (finite control)** specifying what to do based on: > 1. Their current **mood (state)**. > 2. The **letter** they currently see on the tape. > > After consulting the rulebook, the clerk may: > - **Erase** the current letter and **write** a new one. > - Change their **mood (state)**. > - **Slide** the tape **one cell Left (L)** or **Right (R)**. > > The clerk **accepts** the input if they ever enter the "ACCEPT" mood, and **rejects** if they enter the "REJECT" mood or get stuck (no rule applies). This analogy directly captures every component of the formal definition. The clerk's moods = **$Q$**, the rulebook = **$\delta$**, the paper tape = **the tape**, and the pencil = **the tape head**. ### 1.3 Physical Components of a Turing Machine A TM consists of **three** fundamental physical components: 1. **An infinitely long tape** — Divided into discrete **cells**, each capable of holding exactly one symbol from $\Gamma$. At any finite time, only a finite number of cells are non-blank. 2. **A tape head** — A read/write device positioned over exactly one cell. It can read the symbol, write a new symbol, and move one cell left or right per step. 3. **A finite control** — A finite-state machine that determines the next move based on the current state and the symbol being read. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction from Finite Automata & Pushdown Automata:** Unlike DFAs/NFAs/PDAs, a TM has a **read/write head** (not just a read head), an **infinite** (not just a stack) memory, and can move in **both directions** on its storage. This extra power makes TMs capable of recognizing **recursively enumerable** languages — the largest class in the Chomsky hierarchy. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Instantaneous Description (ID) — A Snapshot of the TM To mathematically describe the configuration of a TM at any instant, we use a notation called an **Instantaneous Description (ID)**. If the tape content is $u$, the current state is $q$, the head is reading the symbol $a$, and the rest of the tape to the right is $v$, then the ID is written as: $$ID = u \, q \, a \, v$$ Here, $u \in \Gamma^*$ is the string to the left of the head, $a \in \Gamma$ is the symbol under the head, and $v \in \Gamma^*$ is the string to the right of the head (extending infinitely with blanks $\sqcup$ if necessary). ### 2.2 Operational Semantics (The "How" of Each Step) For each step of the TM, given a current ID of the form $u \, q_i \, a \, v$ and a transition rule: $$\delta(q_i, a) = (q_j, b, D)$$ where $D \in \{L, R\}$ is the direction of movement, the next ID is computed as: **Case 1: Move Right ($D = R$)** $$u \, q_i \, a \, v \;\vdash\; u \, b \, q_j \, v$$ **Case 2: Move Left ($D = L$)** $$u \, c \, q_i \, a \, v \;\vdash\; u \, q_j \, c \, b \, v$$ where $c$ is the symbol immediately to the left of the head (which becomes the new symbol under the head after the move). ### 2.3 Acceptance and Rejection — The Halting Problem A TM $M$ is said to **accept** an input string $w$ if there exists a sequence of configurations: $$q_0 w \;\vdash^*\; u \, q_{accept} \, v$$ for some strings $u, v \in \Gamma^*$. Similarly, $M$ **rejects** $w$ if it halts in $q_{reject}$. > [!WARNING] > **A TM need not always halt!** Unlike a DFA, a TM may enter an **infinite loop** for certain inputs. This is precisely the reason the language recognized by a TM is called **recursively enumerable (RE)** rather than **recursive (decidable)**. If the TM halts on **every** input (accepting or rejecting), then the language is called **recursive** or **decidable**. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Mathematical Element | Symbol / Notation | Meaning / Engineering Interpretation | |---|----------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------| | 1 | TM as a tuple | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, q_{accept}, q_{reject})$ | Complete specification of the machine | | 2 | Set of states | $Q$ | Finite set of internal configurations | | 3 | Input alphabet | $\Sigma$ | Symbols allowed in the input string | | 4 | Tape alphabet | $\Gamma$ | All symbols ever appearing on the tape | | 5 | Blank symbol | $\sqcup$ or $B$ | Marks unused tape cells | | 6 | Transition function | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \to Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ | Determines one computational step | | 7 | Movement directions | $\{L, R\}$ | Left or Right movement of the head | | 8 | Start state | $q_0 \in Q$ | Initial state before any input is read | | 9 | Accept state | $q_{accept} \in Q$ | Halting state for acceptance | | 10 | Reject state | $q_{reject} \in Q$ | Halting state for rejection | | 11 | Instantaneous Description | $u \, q \, a \, v$ | Snapshot of a TM at a specific time | | 12 | Yields in one step | $\vdash$ | One computational transition | | 13 | Yields in zero or more steps | $\vdash^*$ | Reflexive, transitive closure of $\vdash$ | | 14 | Language accepted by $M$ | $L(M)$ | Set of all strings accepted by $M$ | | 15 | Recursive Language | $L$ is decidable | A TM halts on **every** input | | 16 | Recursively Enumerable | $L$ is RE | A TM accepts but may loop | ### 2.5 Real-World Utility of Turing Machines | Application Area | Engineering Significance | |------------------|--------------------------| | **Compiler Design** | Lexical analysis and parsing can be formally modeled as TM computations. | | **Algorithm Complexity** | The classes **P** and **NP** (computational complexity) are defined over deterministic and non-deterministic TMs. | | **Decidability Theory** | Determines which problems are *solvable* by any computer (e.g., Halting Problem is undecidable). | | **Cryptography** | TMs underpin the notion of *efficient* computation, which is the basis of secure encryption. | | **Operating Systems** | Virtual memory and process scheduling rely on TM-equivalent models of computation. | | **Artificial Intelligence** | Foundation for understanding the limits of what algorithms can achieve. | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Standard Notation for a TM Configuration A Turing Machine operates on a string of symbols on an infinite tape. To fully describe a *configuration*, we must capture three things at every moment: (i) the state of the finite control, (ii) the contents of the tape, and (iii) the position of the head. Let us derive the standard ID notation step by step. **Step 1 — Represent the tape contents.** Suppose the tape contains symbols (from left to right) as the sequence $a_1 a_2 a_3 \ldots a_n$, with blanks $\sqcup$ extending to the right. This can be written formally as: $$Tape = a_1 a_2 a_3 \ldots a_n \sqcup \sqcup \sqcup \ldots$$ **Step 2 — Mark the head position.** The head is over some cell containing symbol $a_k$. We insert the current state symbol $q$ (subscripted to the cell) at that position: $$a_1 a_2 \ldots a_{k-1} \, q \, a_k \, a_{k+1} \ldots a_n \sqcup \sqcup \ldots$$ **Step 3 — Split into left and right strings.** Define $u = a_1 a_2 \ldots a_{k-1}$, the substring to the left of the head, and $v = a_k a_{k+1} \ldots a_n$, the substring starting at the head. Then: $$ID = u \, q \, v$$ This is the canonical "u-q-v" representation used in Hopcroft, Ullman, and Motwani's classic textbook. **Step 4 — Formalize the "yields" relation.** A single step of the TM corresponds to the relation $\vdash$ ("yields"). We define it as follows. Given a transition: $$\delta(q_i, X) = (q_j, Y, D)$$ the yields relation is: $$ u \, q_i \, X \, v \;\vdash\; \begin{cases} u \, Y \, q_j \, v & \text{if } D = R \\ u' \, q_j \, Y' \, v' & \text{if } D = L \text{ and } u = u'X' \text{ for some } X' \in \Gamma \end{cases} $$ **Step 5 — Extend to multiple steps.** Define $\vdash^*$ as the reflexive, transitive closure of $\vdash$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Base case: } & C \vdash^* C \text{ for every configuration } C \\ \text{Inductive step: } & \text{If } C_1 \vdash^* C_2 \text{ and } C_2 \vdash C_3, \text{ then } C_1 \vdash^* C_3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Define the language of a TM.** The language accepted by a TM $M$ is: $$L(M) = \{ w \in \Sigma^* \mid q_0 w \vdash^* u \, q_{accept} \, v \text{ for some } u, v \in \Gamma^* \}$$ ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: TM vs Other Automata | Property | DFA | PDA | Turing Machine | |----------|-----|-----|----------------| | Memory | None (stateless) | One Stack | Infinite Tape | | Head | Read-only, fixed position | Read/Write, top of stack only | Read/Write, bi-directional | | Movement | None (input consumed L→R) | Push/Pop only | L or R (one cell per step) | | Power | Regular Languages | Context-Free Languages | Recursively Enumerable | | Halting Guarantee | Yes | Yes | **No** (may loop) | | Transition Function | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \to Q$ | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to \mathcal{P}(Q \times \Gamma^*)$ | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \to Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ | | Storage Size | $O(1)$ | Unbounded but limited access | $O(n)$ for any $n$ | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Constructing a Simple TM **Problem:** Design a TM that accepts the language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$. **Solution Approach (Marking Strategy):** We use a *marking technique* — replace each 'a' with 'X' and each 'b' with 'Y', checking that the counts match. **Transition Function Design:** $$ \begin{aligned} \delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, X, R) \\ \delta(q_1, a) &= (q_1, a, R) \\ \delta(q_1, Y) &= (q_1, Y, R) \\ \delta(q_1, b) &= (q_2, Y, L) \\ \delta(q_2, Y) &= (q_2, Y, L) \\ \delta(q_2, a) &= (q_2, a, L) \\ \delta(q_2, X) &= (q_0, X, R) \\ \delta(q_0, Y) &= (q_3, Y, R) \\ \delta(q_3, Y) &= (q_3, Y, R) \\ \delta(q_3, \sqcup) &= (q_{accept}, \sqcup, R) \end{aligned} $$ **Trace for Input $w = aabb$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Start:} & \quad q_0 \, a \, a \, b \, b \\ \text{After 1st pass (mark first 'a' and first 'b'):} & \quad X \, a \, Y \, b \\ \text{After 2nd pass:} & \quad X \, X \, Y \, Y \\ \text{Final:} & \quad q_3 \, \sqcup \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{ACCEPT} \end{aligned} $$ This concrete example demonstrates the operational power of a TM and how its transition function is built systematically. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Turing Machine The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a Turing Machine, showing how the three primary components — the finite control, the tape, and the read/write head — interact with one another. ```mermaid flowchart LR FC["FINITE CONTROL
Set of States Q
Start q0, Accept qAccept, Reject qReject"] TAPE["INFINITE TAPE
Cells 0, 1, 2, ...
Each cell holds one symbol from Gamma"] HEAD["READ WRITE HEAD
Reads current cell
Writes new symbol
Moves L or R"] FC -- "Current State qi" --> HEAD HEAD -- "Symbol Read a in Gamma" --> FC FC -- "New State qj" --> HEAD FC -- "Symbol to Write b in Gamma" --> HEAD FC -- "Direction D in L R" --> HEAD HEAD -- "Reads current cell" --> TAPE HEAD -- "Writes symbol to current cell" --> TAPE HEAD -- "Shifts position by 1 cell" --> TAPE ``` ### 4.2 State Transition Diagram of a Simple TM (Accepting $a^n b^n$) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> q0 q0 --> q1 : delta q0 a equals X R q1 --> q1 : delta q1 a equals a R q1 --> q1 : delta q1 Y equals Y R q1 --> q2 : delta q1 b equals Y L q2 --> q2 : delta q2 Y equals Y L q2 --> q2 : delta q2 a equals a L q2 --> q0 : delta q2 X equals X R q0 --> q3 : delta q0 Y equals Y R q3 --> q3 : delta q3 Y equals Y R q3 --> qAccept : delta q3 blank equals blank R qAccept --> [*] ``` ### 4.3 Flowchart of a Single TM Computational Step ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Step Begins: TM in state qi, head reads symbol a"] --> B["Lookup delta qi a in transition function"] B --> C{"Is transition defined?"} C -- "No" --> D["HALT Machine may reject or loop"] C -- "Yes" --> E["Retrieve triple qj, b, D from delta"] E --> F["WRITE symbol b on current tape cell"] F --> G["UPDATE state from qi to qj"] G --> H{"Direction D"} H -- "R Move Right" --> I["Shift head one cell to the RIGHT"] H -- "L Move Left" --> J["Shift head one cell to the LEFT"] I --> K["Step Ends Configuration updated"] J --> K["Step Ends Configuration updated"] ``` ### 4.4 Tape-Head Snapshot (ID Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TAPE["Infinite Tape with Head at Position 3"] C0["Cell 0: a"] C1["Cell 1: b"] C2["Cell 2: a"] C3["Cell 3: b HEAD"] C4["Cell 4: blank"] C5["Cell 5: blank"] C6["Cell 6: blank"] end ID["ID equals a b a q1 b blank blank blank"] TAPE --- ID ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define a Turing Machine. List its components.* **Model Answer:** A Turing Machine is a 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, q_{accept}, q_{reject})$ where: - $Q$ is a finite set of states. - $\Sigma$ is the input alphabet. - $\Gamma$ is the tape alphabet, $\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$ and $\sqcup \in \Gamma$. - $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \to Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ is the transition function. - $q_0$ is the start state. - $q_{accept}$ and $q_{reject}$ are the accept and reject states respectively. The components are: (1) an infinite tape, (2) a read/write head, and (3) a finite control. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** *What is the difference between a Pushdown Automaton and a Turing Machine in terms of memory?* **Model Answer:** A Pushdown Automaton (PDA) has only a **stack** as its auxiliary memory, which allows access only to the **top symbol** at any time. The stack is also inherently unbounded in growth but bounded in *direct* accessibility. A Turing Machine, on the other hand, has an **infinite tape** as memory. Its read/write head can access *any* cell on the tape and can move **bi-directionally (left or right)**, providing a far more flexible and powerful memory model. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a)** Define a Turing Machine formally as a 7-tuple. Explain the role of each component with an example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a Turing Machine that accepts the language $L = \{w \in \{a, b\}^* \mid w \text{ is a palindrome}\}$. Show the transition function and trace its execution on the input "aba". **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Formal Definition of a TM:** A Turing Machine is defined as a 7-tuple: $$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, q_{accept}, q_{reject})$$ **Component Roles:** 1. **$Q$ (States):** Encodes the current "mode" of operation. Example: $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_{accept}\}$. 2. **$\Sigma$ (Input Alphabet):** The set of symbols the user supplies. Example: $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$. 3. **$\Gamma$ (Tape Alphabet):** All symbols that can appear on the tape. Example: $\Gamma = \{a, b, X, Y, \sqcup\}$. 4. **$\delta$ (Transition Function):** The "program" of the TM. Example: $\delta(q_0, a) = (q_1, X, R)$. 5. **$q_0$ (Start State):** Initial state of the finite control. 6. **$q_{accept}$ (Accept State):** Halting state indicating acceptance. 7. **$q_{reject}$ (Reject State):** Halting state indicating rejection. **(7 marks — 1 for definition + 6 for explanations)** --- **(b) TM for Palindromes:** We use a *match-and-reduce* strategy. The TM marks the leftmost unmarked symbol, moves to the rightmost unmarked symbol, and checks if they match. **State Descriptions:** - $q_0$: Start state, find the leftmost unmarked symbol. - $q_1$: Move right to find the rightmost unmarked symbol. - $q_2$: Check match; if it matches, mark both and repeat. - $q_3$: If mismatch, reject. - $q_4$: If all symbols matched, accept. **Transition Function:** $$ \begin{aligned} \delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, X, R) \\ \delta(q_0, b) &= (q_1, Y, R) \\ \delta(q_0, X) &= (q_0, X, R) \\ \delta(q_0, Y) &= (q_0, Y, R) \\ \delta(q_0, \sqcup) &= (q_{accept}, \sqcup, R) \\ \delta(q_1, a) &= (q_1, a, R) \\ \delta(q_1, b) &= (q_1, b, R) \\ \delta(q_1, X) &= (q_1, X, R) \\ \delta(q_1, Y) &= (q_1, Y, R) \\ \delta(q_1, \sqcup) &= (q_2, \sqcup, L) \\ \delta(q_2, a) &= (q_3, X, L) \\ \delta(q_2, b) &= (q_3, Y, L) \\ \delta(q_2, X) &= (q_4, X, L) \\ \delta(q_2, Y) &= (q_4, Y, L) \\ \delta(q_3, \sqcup) &= (q_0, \sqcup, R) \\ \delta(q_4, X) &= (q_4, X, L) \\ \delta(q_4, Y) &= (q_4, Y, L) \\ \delta(q_4, a) &= (q_{reject}, a, R) \\ \delta(q_4, b) &= (q_{reject}, b, R) \end{aligned} $$ **Trace for $w = aba$:** | Step | Configuration | Action | |------|--------------|--------| | 0 | $q_0 \, a \, b \, a$ | Start | | 1 | $X \, q_1 \, b \, a$ | Mark leftmost 'a' | | 2 | $X \, b \, a \, q_2 \, \sqcup$ | Move to rightmost | | 3 | $X \, b \, q_3 \, X \, \sqcup$ | Match, mark rightmost 'a' | | 4 | $X \, q_0 \, b \, X$ | Return to leftmost | | 5 | $X \, Y \, q_1 \, X$ | Mark 'b' | | 6 | $X \, Y \, X \, q_2 \, \sqcup$ | Move right | | 7 | $X \, Y \, q_4 \, X$ | All matched, move left | | 8 | $X \, Y \, q_4 \, X \, \sqcup$ | Read blank — **ACCEPT** | **(7 marks — 3 for transition function + 4 for trace)** --- **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024] — ALTERNATIVE** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a standard Turing Machine. Clearly label all components. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the following terms with examples: (i) Instantaneous Description, (ii) Recursively Enumerable Language, (iii) Decidable Language. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) TM Architecture (Block Diagram):** A TM consists of three main components connected as follows: 1. **Finite Control:** A finite-state machine that holds the current state of the TM and uses the transition function $\delta$ to determine the next move. 2. **Tape:** An infinite sequence of cells, each holding one symbol from $\Gamma$. The tape is divided into two halves: the *used* portion (containing non-blank symbols) and the *unused* portion (filled with $\sqcup$). 3. **Read/Write Head:** Positioned over one cell at a time. It reads the symbol, writes a new symbol (possibly the same), and moves one cell to the left or right. **Data Flow:** - The head reads the symbol from the tape → sends it to the finite control. - The finite control consults $\delta$ → produces a triple (new state, new symbol, direction). - The head writes the new symbol on the tape and moves in the specified direction. A clean block diagram (as drawn in Section 4.1 above) should include labeled boxes for each component and arrows for data flow. **(7 marks)** --- **(b) Definitions with Examples:** **(i) Instantaneous Description (ID):** An ID is a complete snapshot of the TM at a single moment, written as $u \, q \, v$ where $u, v \in \Gamma^*$ and $q \in Q$. It captures the state, the tape contents, and the head position. *Example:* The ID $X Y q_3 a$ means the tape has $X Y$ to the left, the head is on $a$ in state $q_3$, and the rest of the tape is blank. **(ii) Recursively Enumerable Language:** A language $L$ is recursively enumerable if there exists a TM $M$ such that for every $w \in L$, $M$ eventually accepts $w$. (It may, however, loop forever on inputs not in $L$.) *Example:* The language $L = \{w \mid w \text{ is the encoding of a valid C program}\}$ is RE. **(iii) Decidable (Recursive) Language:** A language $L$ is decidable if there exists a TM $M$ that **halts on every input** — accepting if $w \in L$ and rejecting if $w \notin L$. *Example:* The language $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ is decidable. The key difference: **RE may not halt on inputs outside $L$, but Decidable always halts.** **(7 marks — 2+2+3)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not forget the blank symbol $\sqcup$** in $\Gamma$ — it is the single most common reason students lose 1 mark in the formal definition. > 2. **Do not confuse $q_{accept}$ and $q_{reject}$ with halting** — these are *specific* states, not a general notion of halting. A TM can halt in a *non-accepting, non-rejecting* state if $\delta$ is partially defined. > 3. **Trace tables must include the head position and current state at every step** — examiners deduct marks for traces that only show tape contents. > 4. **Movement notation:** $L$ = left, $R$ = right. Do not write "L/R" ambiguously; specify one. > 5. **For design problems (7-mark sub-parts):** Always include the **state description** (purpose of each state) along with the transition function. Marks are split: ~3 for transitions, ~4 for trace. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam** - **A TM is a 7-tuple** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, q_{accept}, q_{reject})$ — memorize this verbatim. - **Tape alphabet $\Gamma$ always contains the blank symbol $\sqcup$**, but input alphabet $\Sigma$ does **not**. - **Transition function** $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \to Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ — the codomain has three parts: new state, symbol to write, and direction. - **A TM accepts** by entering $q_{accept}$ and **rejects** by entering $q_{reject}$ or halting without an applicable transition. - **TM may not halt** — this is the defining property of **recursively enumerable** (RE) languages. - **Recursive (decidable) language** = TM halts on **every** input. - **ID notation** $u \, q \, v$ captures state + tape + head position in one expression. - **Single-step transition** is denoted by $\vdash$; multi-step is $\vdash^*$. - **Comparison shortcut:** DFA = regular, PDA = context-free, TM = recursively enumerable. - **Tape is bi-directional, head is read/write** — these two features separate TM from PDA. - **Movement options:** $L$ (left) and $R$ (right) only — TMs cannot "stay" in place. - **Chomsky Hierarchy placement:** TM recognizes **Type-0** (unrestricted) languages. - **Real-world use:** TMs define the theoretical limits of computation (Halting Problem, P vs NP, computability). - **Standard exam trick:** "Design a TM for $a^n b^n$" or "$a^n b^n c^n$" — memorize the marking strategy pattern. - **Notation caution:** Some textbooks use $B$ instead of $\sqcup$ for the blank — both are acceptable, but be **consistent** in your answer. - **Multiple start configurations:** Always begin trace from $q_0 w$ where $w$ is the input string. ---

topics.title: Design of Turing Machines - TM as Language Acceptors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Turing Machines - TM as Language Acceptors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Turing Machine

A **Turing Machine (TM)** is the most powerful computational model in the Chomsky hierarchy. Formally, it is defined as a **7-tuple**:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

Where every component carries a precise mathematical meaning:

| Component | Symbol | Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Finite set of states | $Q$ | The internal control configurations of the machine |
| Input alphabet | $\Sigma$ | The set of symbols allowed on the input tape |
| Tape alphabet | $\Gamma$ | The complete set of symbols writable on the tape, $\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$ |
| Transition function | $\delta$ | $Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ |
| Start state | $q_0$ | The initial state, $q_0 \in Q$ |
| Blank symbol | $B$ | The default symbol filling every unused tape cell, $B \in \Gamma \setminus \Sigma$ |
| Set of final/accepting states | $F$ | $F \subseteq Q$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Tone):** A Turing Machine is a mathematical model of computation that manipulates symbols on an infinite tape according to a transition table. Unlike finite automata, the TM has a **read/write head** that can both **move** (Left/Right) and **modify** the tape contents, giving it memory unbounded by its finite description.

## 1.2 TM as a Language Acceptor — Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **dedicated librarian** standing in front of an **infinitely long conveyor belt** of wooden cards. Each card has a letter written on it. The librarian:

1. Can look at **only one card at a time** (the read head).
2. Can **erase** the letter and write a **new one** in its place.
3. Can **walk one step left or right** to the next card.
4. Has a small **notebook (finite states)** that tells them what to do based on what they see and their current note.
5. Has a special **"ACCEPTED" sign** they can hang when satisfied, and a **"REJECTED"** sign otherwise.

If the librarian hangs the "ACCEPTED" sign after reading the entire message, the **string is in the language**. Otherwise, it is rejected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a TM and not a DFA/PDA?** A DFA has **finite memory** (no stack, no extra tape). A PDA has **one stack**, but the stack is *last-in-first-out* and access to the *bottom* of the stack is limited. A TM has an **infinite tape with random access**, which makes it equivalent in power to any real computer (Church-Turing Thesis).

## 1.3 Language Acceptance: The Two Halting Categories

A TM can halt in two semantically distinct ways:

- **Accepting Halting (Halt-Accept):** The TM enters a state $q \in F$ and halts. The string is **accepted**.
- **Rejecting Halting (Halt-Reject):** The TM enters a state $q \notin F$ and halts, or a transition is undefined. The string is **rejected**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A string is accepted if and only if the TM halts in an accepting state.** The mere act of stopping is not enough — the state must belong to $F$.

## 1.4 The Recursive vs Recursively Enumerable Distinction

| Class | Definition | Halts on Non-Input? |
|-------|------------|---------------------|
| **Recursive Language (Decidable)** | TM **always halts** on every input, accepting or rejecting. | Yes — always halts |
| **Recursively Enumerable (RE) Language** | TM halts and accepts every string in the language; on strings *not* in the language, it may **loop forever**. | Not guaranteed |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students confuse "TM accepts a language" with "TM decides a language." *Accepting* only requires halting on accepted strings; *deciding* requires halting on **all** strings. This distinction is a 14-mark favourite in university exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Semantics: Reading One Step of the TM

At every clock tick, the TM reads the current symbol $X$ under the head, consults the current state $q$, and applies the transition:

$$\delta(q, X) = (q', Y, D)$$

Where:
- $q'$ is the **next state**.
- $Y$ is the symbol **written** on the tape, replacing $X$.
- $D \in \{L, R\}$ is the **direction** the head moves: **L**eft or **R**ight.

> **Why this matters for design:** A TM designer must specify, for **every possible (state, symbol)** pair, what to do — or explicitly declare it undefined (which causes a **reject-halting**).

## 2.2 Why a TM Outclasses a DFA and PDA

The hierarchy is: **DFA ⊂ PDA ⊂ TM**, meaning every regular language is context-free, and every context-free language is accepted by some TM.

| Model | Memory | Power |
|-------|--------|-------|
| DFA | None (state only) | Regular languages |
| PDA | One Stack (LIFO) | Context-free languages |
| TM | Infinite Tape (R/W) | Recursively Enumerable languages |

**Key Insight:** The TM's infinite tape is **not** "more memory" in the colloquial sense — it is **persistent, two-way, and modifiable**, which makes it equivalent to a general-purpose computer.

## 2.3 Design Philosophy for TMs as Acceptors

The KTU board pattern for designing a TM acceptor follows a **3-phase strategy**:

1. **Phase 1 — Match:** Scan the input to find a "matching pair" (e.g., one `a` and one `b`).
2. **Phase 2 — Mark:** Replace the matched symbols with special markers (e.g., `X` and `Y`) so the head does not re-process them.
3. **Phase 3 — Verify:** When no more pairs are found, ensure the remaining input is consistent (e.g., all symbols already marked, or only blanks remain).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This 3-phase pattern is the exact foundation of compilers, garbage collectors, and balanced-parentheses checkers. The TM is the formal template; real code is an implementation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Notation | Notes |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | TM definition | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ | 7-tuple |
| 2 | Transition | $\delta(q, X) = (q', Y, D)$ | $D \in \{L, R\}$ |
| 3 | Instantaneous Description (ID) | $X_1 X_2 \dots X_{i-1} q X_i X_{i+1} \dots X_n$ | State is between symbols |
| 4 | Yields in one step | $\vdash_M$ | Single step |
| 5 | Yields in zero or more steps | $\vdash_M^{\star}$ | Reflexive transitive closure |
| 6 | Language accepted | $L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^{\star} \mid q_0 w \vdash_M^{\star} \alpha q \beta,\, q \in F\}$ | Final state reached |
| 7 | Recursive language | $L \in \mathcal{R}$ if some TM **always halts** on every input | Decidable |
| 8 | Recursively Enumerable | $L \in \mathcal{RE}$ if some TM **halts on accepted** inputs | Semi-decidable |
| 9 | Church-Turing Thesis | Every effective procedure is computable by some TM | Not a theorem — a thesis |
| 10 | Configuration count bound | Configurations $\leq \vert Q \vert \cdot n \cdot \vert \Gamma \vert^n$ | Where $n$ is the tape length used |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU exams:** Memorise the **ID notation**. Board examiners award full marks only when the ID is written with the state $q$ positioned at the head's current cell.

## 2.5 Engineering & Real-World Utility

TM acceptors are the theoretical bedrock of:

- **Compiler Design** — Lexical and syntactic analysis (decidability of matching brackets).
- **Verification Tools** — Model checkers use TM-equivalent procedures to verify hardware/software.
- **Complexity Theory** — The TM is the unit of measurement for $\mathbf{P}$, $\mathbf{NP}$, and beyond.
- **Decidability Proofs** — Reducing a problem to a known TM-acceptable language is the standard technique.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: TM for $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

**Intuition:** The machine must match every `a` with a `b` in a one-to-one fashion. If the count of `a`s and `b`s differs, reject.

**Phase Logic:**
- Phase 1: Read the leftmost `a`, replace with `X`, move right past all `a`s and `Y`s, find a `b`, replace with `Y`, return left.
- Phase 2: When no more `a` is found, verify all remaining symbols are `Y` or blank.

### Transition Table

| State | Read $a$ | Read $b$ | Read $X$ | Read $Y$ | Read $B$ |
|-------|----------|----------|----------|----------|----------|
| $\to q_0$ | $(q_1, X, R)$ | — | $(q_3, X, R)$ | — | — |
| $q_1$ | $(q_1, a, R)$ | — | $(q_1, X, R)$ | $(q_1, Y, R)$ | — |
| $q_2$ | — | $(q_2, Y, L)$ | — | $(q_2, Y, L)$ | $(q_0, X, R)$ |
| $q_3$ | — | — | — | $(q_3, Y, R)$ | $(q_4, B, R)$ |
| $\ast q_4$ | — | — | — | — | — |

> State $q_4$ is the **accepting state** (marked $\ast$).

### Step-by-Step Trace for Input `aabb`

| Step | Tape | State | Head Position | Action |
|------|------|-------|---------------|--------|
| 1 | $\underline{a}abb$ | $q_0$ | 0 | Read `a`, write `X` |
| 2 | $X\underline{a}bb$ | $q_1$ | 1 | Skip `a` |
| 3 | $Xa\underline{b}b$ | $q_1$ | 2 | See `b`, transition to $q_2$, write `Y` |
| 4 | $XaY\underline{b}$ | $q_2$ | 3 | Skip `Y` going left |
| 5 | $X\underline{a}Yb$ | $q_2$ | 1 | Reach `a`, transition to $q_1$ |
| 6 | $Xa\underline{Y}b$ | $q_1$ | 2 | Skip `Y` |
| 7 | $XaY\underline{b}$ | $q_1$ | 3 | See `b`, go to $q_2$, write `Y` |
| 8 | $XaYY\underline{B}$ | $q_2$ | 4 | Hit blank, return left |
| 9 | $X\underline{a}YY$ | $q_2$ | 1 | Skip left |
| 10 | $\underline{X}aYY$ | $q_0$ | 0 | See `X`, move to $q_3$ |
| 11 | $XaY\underline{Y}$ | $q_3$ | 3 | Skip `Y` |
| 12 | $XaYY\underline{B}$ | $q_3$ | 4 | Hit blank, go to $q_4$, **ACCEPT** |

**Verdict:** String `aabb` is accepted because $n=2$ `a`s and $n=2$ `b`s.

> [!NOTE]
> **String `aaab` would be rejected:** After matching two `a`s to two `b`s, the head would see an unmatched `a` and find no `b` to match, leading to a **reject-halting** state.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: TM for $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{\star}\}$ (Even-Length Palindromes)

**Intuition:** A palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards. The TM must match the **outermost symbols** by comparing the first and last, then recurse inward.

### Transition Table

| State | Read $a$ | Read $b$ | Read $X$ | Read $Y$ | Read $B$ |
|-------|----------|----------|----------|----------|----------|
| $\to q_0$ | $(q_1, X, R)$ | $(q_2, Y, R)$ | $(q_5, X, R)$ | $(q_5, Y, R)$ | $(q_3, B, R)$ |
| $q_1$ | $(q_1, a, R)$ | $(q_1, b, R)$ | $(q_1, X, R)$ | $(q_1, Y, R)$ | $(q_4, B, L)$ |
| $q_2$ | $(q_2, a, R)$ | $(q_2, b, R)$ | $(q_2, X, R)$ | $(q_2, Y, R)$ | $(q_4, B, L)$ |
| $q_3$ | — | — | — | — | — |
| $\ast q_5$ | — | — | — | — | — |
| $q_4$ | — | $(q_4, B, L)$ | $(q_0, X, R)$ | $(q_0, Y, R)$ | — |

### Step-by-Step Trace for Input `abba`

We begin with $q_0$ reading `a` (leftmost), marking it `X`, scanning right through `bb`, hitting the blank, transitioning to $q_4$, then reading rightmost `a` → writing `X`, moving left to $q_0$. The pair `X` ... `X` is now matched. Next, $q_0$ reads `b`, marks `Y`, scans right, sees the next `b`, matches it. Both pairs cleared, head returns to $q_0$, sees only `XYXY`, moves to $q_3$ on blank — but the **accepting path** is $q_5$ reached when *only* `X`/`Y` markers and blank remain in matched pattern. In the standard construction, $q_5$ is reached from $q_0$ when the entire tape is consumed (no `a` or `b` left).

**Acceptance Condition:** The string $w w^R$ is accepted iff every symbol is matched and the machine reaches $q_5$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: TM for $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

This language is **not context-free** — a PDA cannot accept it. But a TM can. The strategy is **3-way matching**:

1. Replace the leftmost `a` with `X`.
2. Scan right past all `a`s, `X`s, `b`s, and `Y`s to find the first `c`.
3. Replace that `c` with `Z`, move left, find the leftmost unmarked `b`, replace with `Y`.
4. Repeat until all `a`s are exhausted.
5. Verify only `Y`s and `Z`s remain.

### Transition Table (Skeleton)

| State | Read $a$ | Read $b$ | Read $c$ | Read $X$ | Read $Y$ | Read $Z$ | Read $B$ |
|-------|----------|----------|----------|----------|----------|----------|----------|
| $\to q_0$ | $(q_1, X, R)$ | — | — | $(q_5, X, R)$ | $(q_5, Y, R)$ | $(q_5, Z, R)$ | — |
| $q_1$ | $(q_1, a, R)$ | $(q_1, b, R)$ | — | $(q_1, X, R)$ | $(q_1, Y, R)$ | — | $(q_2, B, L)$ |
| $q_2$ | — | — | $(q_3, Z, L)$ | — | — | — | — |
| $q_3$ | — | $(q_4, Y, L)$ | — | — | $(q_3, Y, L)$ | $(q_3, Z, L)$ | — |
| $q_4$ | — | — | — | $(q_4, X, L)$ | $(q_4, Y, L)$ | $(q_4, Z, L)$ | $(q_0, B, R)$ |
| $\ast q_5$ | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> The 3-way nested matching is the **canonical example** of TM power exceeding PDA. This problem appears in nearly every KTU board paper.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU TM Design Problems

| Language | Strategy | # States | Key Insight |
|----------|----------|----------|-------------|
| $a^n b^n$ | 2-way match (a↔b) | 4–5 | Mark `a`→`X`, mark `b`→`Y` |
| $w w^R$ | Outermost match | 5–6 | Mirror-palindrome |
| $a^n b^n c^n$ | 3-way nested match | 6–8 | Beyond CFG power |
| $a^{2^n}$ | Multiply count, halt | 7+ | Non-linear — needs tape scratch |
| $\{wc w \mid w \in \Sigma^{\star}\}$ | Find `c`, copy, compare | 8+ | Pattern: "double word" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question:** A student is asked to design a TM for $a^n b^n c^n$ using a PDA. **Impossible.** A PDA's stack cannot enforce three-way balance. This is a classic *show why TM is needed* question.

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation: Why Halting in a Non-Final State Equals Rejection

Consider the ID notation. The TM halts when **no transition is defined** for the current $(q, X)$ pair, or it enters an explicit $q_{halt}$.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$w \in L(M) \iff \exists\,\alpha, \beta \in \Gamma^{\star},\, q \in F \text{ such that } q_0 w \vdash_M^{\star} \alpha q \beta$$

**Derivation:**
1. $M$ starts with input $w$ on the tape, head at the leftmost symbol, in state $q_0$. Formally, the starting ID is $q_0 w$.
2. Each step of computation produces a new ID. The relation $\vdash_M$ is the *one-step yields* relation.
3. $\vdash_M^{\star}$ is the reflexive-transitive closure — zero or more steps.
4. If the machine ever enters a state $q \in F$, the input is **accepted** and $M$ halts.
5. If the machine halts in $q \notin F$ (no transition defined), the input is **rejected**.

This defines the **language accepted by $M$**, denoted $L(M)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing $L(M) = \{w \mid q_0 w \vdash_M^{\star} \alpha\}$. The final state $q \in F$ is **mandatory**. Forgetting it costs 2 marks.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid State Diagram: TM for $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0 --> q1 : a to X move R
    q1 --> q1 : a or X or Y move R
    q1 --> q2 : b to Y move L
    q2 --> q2 : Y move L
    q2 --> q2 : a move L
    q2 --> q0 : X to X move R
    q0 --> q3 : X or Y move R
    q3 --> q3 : Y move R
    q3 --> q4 : B move R
    q4 --> [*] : ACCEPT
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Circle nodes = states; arrows = transitions.
- Double circle would denote the final state $q_4$.
- The loop on $q_1$ represents scanning past all `a`/`X`/`Y` symbols going right.
- The loop on $q_2$ represents returning left past all marked symbols.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a TM Acceptor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input String on Tape] --> B[Start State q0]
    B --> C{Read Symbol Under Head}
    C -->|a or b| D[Apply Transition delta]
    C -->|X or Y| D
    C -->|Blank B| D
    D --> E{New State in F?}
    E -->|Yes| F[ACCEPT Halt]
    E -->|No| G{Transition Defined?}
    G -->|Yes| C
    G -->|No| H[REJECT Halt]
```

**Block Functions:**
- **Input String on Tape:** Initial configuration, head at leftmost cell.
- **Start State $q_0$:** The control unit's initial configuration.
- **Read Symbol Under Head:** The read/write head observes the current cell.
- **Apply Transition $\delta$:** Lookup $(q, X) \to (q', Y, D)$ and execute.
- **ACCEPT Halt:** Enter a state in $F$ and stop — the string is in $L(M)$.
- **REJECT Halt:** No transition defined — the string is not in $L(M)$.

## 4.3 TM Tape Movement Schematic (Trace Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Step0[Step 0: Initial]
        T0["a a b b B B B B"]
        H0[HEAD]
    end
    subgraph Step1[Step 1: Mark First a]
        T1["X a b b B B B B"]
        H1[HEAD]
    end
    subgraph Step2[Step 2: Scan Right]
        T2["X a b b B B B B"]
        H2[HEAD]
    end
    subgraph Step3[Step 3: Mark First b]
        T3["X a Y b B B B B"]
        H3[HEAD]
    end
    subgraph Step4[Step 4: Return Left]
        T4["X a Y b B B B B"]
        H4[HEAD]
    end
    Step0 --> Step1 --> Step2 --> Step3 --> Step4
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- The HEAD block shows the head's current cell.
- $B$ = blank.
- The 4 steps illustrate the **match-and-return** pattern for the first `a`/`b` pair in `aabb`.

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram: TM for $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0 --> q1 : a to X move R
    q1 --> q1 : a b X Y move R
    q1 --> q2 : B move L
    q2 --> q3 : c to Z move L
    q3 --> q3 : Y Z move L
    q3 --> q4 : b to Y move L
    q4 --> q4 : X Y Z move L
    q4 --> q0 : B move R
    q0 --> q5 : X Y Z move R
    q5 --> q6 : B move R
    q6 --> [*] : ACCEPT
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- $q_0 \to q_1$: Mark leftmost `a` and scan right.
- $q_1 \to q_2$: Hit blank, prepare to backtrack.
- $q_2 \to q_3$: Find and mark the rightmost `c`.
- $q_3 \to q_4$: Find leftmost unmarked `b` and mark.
- $q_4 \to q_0$: Return to the leftmost cell and restart.
- $q_0 \to q_5 \to q_6$: Verify only markers remain, then accept.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define a Turing Machine. List its components and explain the role of the transition function.**

**Model Answer:**
A Turing Machine $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ is a 7-tuple computational model consisting of a finite control, an infinite tape, and a read/write head. The **transition function** $\delta: Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ defines the next state, the symbol to write, and the head's movement direction (Left or Right) for every (state, symbol) pair. It is the heart of the TM, dictating its entire behaviour.

*(3 marks — definition 1, components 1, transition function 1)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between a Recursive and a Recursively Enumerable language. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Recursive language** $L$ is one for which there exists a TM that **always halts** on every input, accepting strings in $L$ and rejecting strings not in $L$. Example: $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ — a TM can be constructed that always halts.

A **Recursively Enumerable (RE) language** is one accepted by a TM that halts on accepted inputs but may **loop forever** on rejected inputs. Example: $L = \{w \mid w \text{ is a valid C program}\}$ — semantically, the TM may never halt on an infinite-looping program.

*(3 marks — definition of recursive 1, definition of RE 1, examples 1)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**Q. (a)** Design a Turing Machine to accept the language $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$. Draw the transition diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Strategy:** The machine performs 3-way nested matching. It marks one `a` as `X`, one `b` as `Y`, and one `c` as `Z` per iteration.

**Transition Function:**

| State | $a$ | $b$ | $c$ | $X$ | $Y$ | $Z$ | $B$ |
|-------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| $\to q_0$ | $(q_1, X, R)$ | — | — | $(q_5, X, R)$ | $(q_5, Y, R)$ | $(q_5, Z, R)$ | — |
| $q_1$ | $(q_1, a, R)$ | $(q_1, b, R)$ | — | $(q_1, X, R)$ | $(q_1, Y, R)$ | — | $(q_2, B, L)$ |
| $q_2$ | — | — | $(q_3, Z, L)$ | — | — | — | — |
| $q_3$ | — | $(q_4, Y, L)$ | — | — | $(q_3, Y, L)$ | $(q_3, Z, L)$ | — |
| $q_4$ | — | — | — | $(q_4, X, L)$ | $(q_4, Y, L)$ | $(q_4, Z, L)$ | $(q_0, B, R)$ |
| $q_5$ | — | — | — | — | — | — | $(q_6, B, R)$ |
| $\ast q_6$ | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |

**Trace for $aaabbbccc$:** The TM marks $a \to X$, scans right to find $c \to Z$, returns left to find $b \to Y$, then loops. After 3 iterations, only `XYXYXY` remain, the head reaches $q_5$ on a blank, then $q_6$ → **ACCEPT**.

*(7 marks — transition table 3, strategy explanation 2, trace 2)*

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the concept of a recursively enumerable language. Prove that the language $L = \{w w \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{\star}\}$ is recursively enumerable. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

A language $L$ is **recursively enumerable (RE)** if there exists a TM $M$ such that:
- If $w \in L$, then $M$ halts in an accepting state.
- If $w \notin L$, then $M$ either halts in a non-accepting state **or loops forever**.

**Construction of TM for $L = \{w w \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{\star}\}$:**

The TM uses a **divide-and-compare** strategy:

1. **Phase 1:** Find the **midpoint** of the string. Use a marker to find the middle by pairing symbols from both ends.
2. **Phase 2:** Compare the first half symbol-by-symbol with the second half. Replace matched pairs with blanks.
3. **Phase 3:** If all comparisons succeed, accept; otherwise, reject.

**Transition Highlights:**
- Use two markers: `M` (left half pointer) and `N` (right half pointer).
- For each character under `M`, scan right to find the corresponding character under `N`.
- If they match, mark both as `Z` and recurse. If not, reject.

**Conclusion:** Since a TM exists that halts and accepts every $ww$ string, the language is **recursively enumerable**. (Note: It is actually **recursive** — decidable — because we can also construct a halting rejection path for non-$ww$ strings.)

*(7 marks — RE definition 2, TM construction idea 3, conclusion 2)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**Q. (a)** Design a Turing Machine to accept palindromes over $\{a, b\}$. Draw the transition diagram and explain with a trace for input `abba`. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Strategy:** A palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards. The TM:
1. Reads the leftmost symbol, marks it `X` (if `a`) or `Y` (if `b`).
2. Scans right to the end of the string.
3. Compares the rightmost unmarked symbol with the marked one.
4. If match, marks the rightmost and recurses.
5. If no more symbols remain (only `X`/`Y` and blank), accept.

**Transition Table:**

| State | $a$ | $b$ | $X$ | $Y$ | $B$ |
|-------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| $\to q_0$ | $(q_1, X, R)$ | $(q_2, Y, R)$ | $(q_4, X, R)$ | $(q_4, Y, R)$ | — |
| $q_1$ | $(q_1, a, R)$ | $(q_1, b, R)$ | $(q_1, X, R)$ | $(q_1, Y, R)$ | $(q_3, B, L)$ |
| $q_2$ | $(q_2, a, R)$ | $(q_2, b, R)$ | $(q_2, X, R)$ | $(q_2, Y, R)$ | $(q_3, B, L)$ |
| $q_3$ | $(R, X, L)$ | $(R, Y, L)$ | — | — | — |
| $\ast q_4$ | — | — | — | — | $(q_4, B, R)$ |

> Where $R$ denotes the reject state (undefined transition) and $\ast$ marks acceptance.

**Trace for `abba`:**

| Step | Tape | State | Head | Action |
|------|------|-------|------|--------|
| 1 | $\underline{a}bba$ | $q_0$ | 0 | Mark `a`→`X`, go $q_1$ |
| 2 | $X\underline{b}ba$ | $q_1$ | 1 | Scan right |
| 3 | $Xb\underline{b}a$ | $q_1$ | 2 | Scan right |
| 4 | $Xbb\underline{a}$ | $q_1$ | 3 | Scan right |
| 5 | $Xbba\underline{B}$ | $q_1$ | 4 | Hit blank, go $q_3$ |
| 6 | $Xb\underline{b}a$ | $q_3$ | 2 | Mark `b`→`Y`, go left |
| 7 | $X\underline{b}Ya$ | $q_3$ | 1 | Move left to $q_0$ |
| 8 | $\underline{X}bYa$ | $q_0$ | 0 | See `X`, go $q_4$ |
| 9 | $X\underline{b}Ya$ | $q_4$ | 1 | Skip `b`/`Y` |
| 10 | $XbY\underline{a}$ | $q_4$ | 3 | Skip `a` |
| 11 | $XbYa\underline{B}$ | $q_4$ | 4 | Hit blank, **ACCEPT** |

*(7 marks — table 2, strategy 2, trace 3)*

---

**Q. (b)** Construct a TM that accepts the language $L = \{a^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ (even-count of `a`s). Show the transition diagram and trace for input `aaaa`. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Strategy:** Pair up `a`s from left to right. Replace pairs with `X`. If an odd number of `a`s remains, reject.

**Transition Table:**

| State | $a$ | $X$ | $B$ |
|-------|-----|-----|-----|
| $\to q_0$ | $(q_1, X, R)$ | $(q_3, X, R)$ | — |
| $q_1$ | $(q_2, X, R)$ | $(q_1, X, R)$ | — |
| $q_2$ | — | — | $(q_0, B, R)$ |
| $q_3$ | — | $(q_3, X, R)$ | $(q_4, B, R)$ |
| $\ast q_4$ | — | — | — |

**Trace for `aaaa`:**

1. $q_0$ reads `a`, marks `X`. Tape: `Xaaa`. State: $q_1$.
2. $q_1$ scans right, finds `a` at position 2, marks `X`. Tape: `XXaX`? Wait — re-trace: position 1 is the first `a`, marked `X`. Position 2 is second `a`, in $q_1$ → mark `X`. Tape becomes `XXaa`.
3. $q_1$ scans right over `aa`, hits blank at position 4. Goes to $q_0$ via $q_2$.
4. $q_0$ sees `X` at position 0, goes to $q_3$.
5. $q_3$ scans right over `XXaa`, hits blank at position 4. Goes to $q_4$ → **ACCEPT**.

*(7 marks — table 2, logic 2, trace 3)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Missing the Final State:** Students often forget to mark $q_f$ as the accepting state using $\ast$ or a double circle. **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Undefined Transitions:** Leaving entries blank in the transition table without specifying that they lead to a reject state. Always **explicitly** say "undefined → reject" for the no-transition case.
> 3. **Wrong Head Movement:** Confusing $L$ (Left) with $R$ (Right). In a leftward scan, use $L$; in a rightward scan, use $R$. Mixing them up causes the TM to miss critical symbols.
> 4. **Forgetting the Blank $B$ Transitions:** The TM must be defined on $B$ too — especially at the "scan to the end" phase. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not Drawing the Transition Diagram:** The KTU 2024 paper pattern allocates 2–3 marks specifically for the state diagram. Always draw it alongside the table.
> 6. **Confusing Accept vs Decide:** Writing "TM accepts $L$" when asked to "decide $L$" is a definitional error. **−1 mark**.
> 7. **ID Notation Errors:** Writing $q_0 w$ instead of $q_0 w$ at the start (correct) or omitting the state symbol from the ID loses 1 mark.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TM is a 7-tuple:** $(Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ — memorise the symbol for each component.
- **Acceptance condition:** $w \in L(M) \iff q_0 w \vdash_M^{\star} \alpha q \beta$ with $q \in F$.
- **Recursive ⊂ Recursively Enumerable:** Every recursive language is RE, but not vice versa.
- **TM > PDA > DFA:** The TM's infinite tape gives it strictly more power than the PDA's stack.
- **3-phase design pattern:** **Match → Mark → Verify** is the universal template for TM construction.
- **Nested matching:** $a^n b^n c^n$ requires **3-way** matching — a PDA **cannot** do this. The TM succeeds by using **two marker symbols** ($Y$ for $b$, $Z$ for $c$).
- **Palindromes ($w w^R$):** Mirror-match outermost symbols, recurse inward.
- **Double word ($ww$):** Find midpoint, compare halves. The midpoint-finding phase is the tricky part.
- **Halting is critical:** A TM that loops forever on rejected inputs gives an RE language; one that always halts gives a recursive (decidable) language.
- **ID Notation:** Always write the state symbol $q$ at the head's current position: $X_1 X_2 \dots X_{i-1} q X_i X_{i+1} \dots X_n$.
- **Direction symbols:** $L$ = Left, $R$ = Right. Never use $\leftarrow / \rightarrow$ in the formal definition.
- **Blank $B$:** A symbol, not a hole. Treat it as a regular tape symbol that the TM can write or match.
- **Church-Turing Thesis:** Every effectively computable function is computable by a TM — the theoretical foundation of computer science.
- **Typical KTU 14-mark design question:** "Design a TM to accept $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ / $\{w w^R\}$ / $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$ / palindromes." Always provide: **transition table + state diagram + trace for one example string**.
- **Distinguishing input and tape alphabets:** $\Sigma \subsetneq \Gamma$ — the input alphabet excludes $B$; the tape alphabet includes $B$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TM as Computers of Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** TM as Computers of Functions # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition: Turing Machine as a Computer of Functions > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > A **Turing Machine (TM)** is said to *compute* a (partial) function $f : \Sigma^{\star} \rightarrow \Sigma^{\star}$ if, for every input string $w \in \Sigma^{\star}$ on which $f$ is defined, the TM halts in a unique final accepting configuration with exactly $f(w)$ written on its work tape, starting from the initial configuration $q_0 w$. A TM $M$ is a **7-tuple** $$M = (Q,\ \Sigma,\ \Gamma,\ \delta,\ q_0,\ B,\ F)$$ where every component has the standard meaning. When $M$ is used **as a function computer** (rather than as a language acceptor), the *output* replaces the role of the *accepting state*. The function-computation paradigm demands: 1. **Total halting on valid inputs** — $M$ must halt on every $w \in \text{dom}(f)$. 2. **Output on the tape** — the final tape content (ignoring trailing blanks) must equal $f(w)$. 3. **Determinism of result** — exactly one final configuration exists for every legal input. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Total vs. Partial Functions** > - If $M$ halts on **every** $w \in \Sigma^{\star}$, then $M$ computes a **total function**. > - If $M$ halts only on the inputs where $f(w)$ is defined and loops otherwise, then $M$ computes a **partial function**. > This distinction is fundamental to **Recursion Theory** and the theory of **Recursive & Recursively Enumerable** sets. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The TM as a Mechanical Calculator Imagine a **mechanical adding machine from the 1800s**. You feed it tally marks (ones and zeros separated by a marker), turn the crank (execute transitions), and it stops with the answer written in the result tray. You never see the machine "accept" or "reject"; you simply see the final number. This is precisely how a TM computes a function: | Component | Real-World Counterpart | TM Equivalent | |-----------|------------------------|----------------| | Input tally marks | Slide-rule input tape | Initial tape $q_0 w$ | | Crank rotations | Mechanical steps | Applications of $\delta$ | | Result tray | Final display | Final tape $f(w)$ | | "Done" lever | Bell / brake | Entry to halting state | > [!TIP] > **Why numbers are written in unary ($0^n$) for TM computation:** > Unary is the *easiest* representation to manipulate symbolically. Each $0$ acts like a single bead on an abacus. The TM only needs to erase a separator (`1`) or duplicate a block — operations that are trivial in unary but cumbersome in binary or decimal. --- ## 1.3 Numerical Representation Conventions (KTU Convention) Throughout KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4, the following conventions are followed: $$\text{Number } n \;\longleftrightarrow\; \underbrace{0\,0\,0\,\cdots\,0}_{n \text{ zeros}}$$ A pair $(m,n)$ is encoded as: $$(m,n) \;\longleftrightarrow\; 0^{m}\,1\,0^{n}$$ where the symbol `1` is the **separator**. After computation, the separator is erased and the resulting tape contains a single block of $0$'s representing the result. # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Formal Computational Semantics Let $M$ be a deterministic TM. $M$ **computes** a function $f : \Sigma^{\star} \to \Sigma^{\star}$ iff: $$\forall\, w \in \Sigma^{\star},\ \ \big[\, (q_0\,w) \vdash^{\star}_{M} \,(q_f\,f(w)) \,\big] \quad \text{for some } q_f \in F$$ Equivalently, in *ID notation* where an *Instantaneous Description* $C$ represents $(q, \text{tape}, \text{head position})$: $$\text{Initial ID: } C_0 = (q_0,\ w,\ 1) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{Final ID: } C_f = (q_f,\ f(w),\ k)$$ with the read/write head somewhere over $f(w)$ (conventionally at the leftmost symbol of the output, but position is immaterial for the tape content). > [!NOTE] > **Difference between "Computing a Function" and "Accepting a Language"** > - *Accepting a language* $L$ → TM halts in $F$ **iff** $w \in L$; otherwise it may loop forever. > - *Computing a function* $f$ → TM **must halt** on every input in the domain, and the **tape content** (not just the state) carries the result. --- ## 2.2 Why Numbers Are Easier Than Strings for TM Computation Strings require a TM to *understand structure* (balanced parentheses, well-formed formulas), whereas numbers in unary are simply **countable tokens**. The TM only needs primitive operations: 1. **Erase a symbol** (replace by $B$). 2. **Scan right to end** (look for first $B$). 3. **Scan left** to a marked position. 4. **Append a $0$** at the rightmost non-blank position. These four primitives suffice to build adders, multipliers, and even compilers for arithmetic expressions. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — TM as Function Computer > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this table for the ESE — it condenses every formula you will need.** | # | Concept | Formal Statement | Symbols / Notes | |---|---------|-----------------|-----------------| | 1 | TM computes $f$ | $(q_0 w) \vdash^{\star}_M (q_f f(w))$ | $q_f \in F$ | | 2 | Unary encoding of $n$ | $n \leftrightarrow 0^n$ | one-to-one | | 3 | Pair encoding | $(m,n) \leftrightarrow 0^m 1 0^n$ | `1` = separator | | 4 | Successor $f(n)=n+1$ | $0^n \rightarrow 0^{n+1}$ | append one $0$ | | 5 | Addition $f(m,n)=m+n$ | $0^m 1 0^n \rightarrow 0^{m+n}$ | erase separator | | 6 | Multiplication $f(m,n)=m\cdot n$ | $0^m 1 0^n \rightarrow 0^{m\cdot n}$ | repeated copy | | 7 | Proper subtraction $m \div n$ | $0^m 1 0^n \rightarrow 0^{\max(m-n,0)}$ | guarded erase | | 8 | Halting condition | No $\delta$ defined at current $(q, X)$ | TM halts | | 9 | Total function | $M$ halts on **all** $w \in \Sigma^{\star}$ | $f$ is recursive | | 10 | Partial function | $M$ may loop on some $w$ | $f$ is partial recursive | > [!NOTE] > **Notation warning — KTU paper valuation**: Examiners expect the transition $\delta(q, X) = (q', Y, D)$ to be written with $D \in \{L, R\}$. Direction is **never** "stay" for a standard deterministic TM. --- ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of TM-as-Function-Computer Although physical TMs do not exist, the *function-computer view* is the conceptual ancestor of every modern computing device: - **CPU arithmetic logic units (ALUs)** — binary addition is the direct descendant of TM unary addition, optimised for hardware. - **Compiler design** — A TM's tape mirrors the assembly-time *symbol table* where variables are computed and stored. - **Algorithm analysis** — The *time complexity* of a TM computing $f$ corresponds to the number of steps $\delta$ applied; this yields the formal **complexity class** $\mathrm{DTIME}(f(n))$. - **Formal verification** — Proving a program correct reduces to showing that the program TM computes the *intended* mathematical function. # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Successor Function $f(n) = n + 1$ ### 3.1.1 Specification We design a TM $S$ that maps $0^n \mapsto 0^{n+1}$ — i.e., it appends a single $0$ to the input block. ### 3.1.2 Formal 7-tuple $$S = \big(\{q_0, q_1, q_2\},\ \{0\},\ \{0, B\},\ \delta,\ q_0,\ B,\ \{q_2\}\big)$$ with the transition table: $$ \begin{aligned} \delta(q_0,\,0) &= (q_0,\,0,\,R) \\ \delta(q_0,\,B) &= (q_1,\,0,\,L) \\ \delta(q_1,\,0) &= (q_2,\,0,\,R) \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Trace for $n = 3$ (input $0^3$) | Move | Instantaneous Description (ID) | Transition Applied | |------|-------------------------------|--------------------| | 0 | $q_0\, 0\,0\,0\,B$ | Initial ID | | 1 | $0\, q_0\,0\,0\,B$ | $\delta(q_0,0)=(q_0,0,R)$ | | 2 | $0\,0\, q_0\,0\,B$ | $\delta(q_0,0)=(q_0,0,R)$ | | 3 | $0\,0\,0\, q_0\,B$ | $\delta(q_0,0)=(q_0,0,R)$ | | 4 | $0\,0\,0\,0\, q_1$ | $\delta(q_0,B)=(q_1,0,L)$ | | 5 | $0\,0\,0\, q_2\,0$ | $\delta(q_1,0)=(q_2,0,R)$ — **HALT** | **Output tape content**: $0\,0\,0\,0\,B = 0^4$. $\;\checkmark\; 0^3 \mapsto 0^{3+1} = 0^4$. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Addition $f(m, n) = m + n$ (Full KTU Model Trace) ### 3.2.1 Specification We design a TM $A$ that maps $0^m\,1\,0^n \mapsto 0^{m+n}$. The strategy is **erase the separator** — the remaining $m+n$ zeros form the result. ### 3.2.2 Formal 7-tuple $$ A = \big(\{q_0, q_1, q_2\},\ \{0,1\},\ \{0,1,B\},\ \delta,\ q_0,\ B,\ \{q_2\}\big) $$ Transitions: $$ \begin{aligned} \delta(q_0,\,0) &= (q_0,\,0,\,R) \quad &\text{(skip leading block)}\\ \delta(q_0,\,1) &= (q_1,\,B,\,R) \quad &\text{(erase separator, enter right block)}\\ \delta(q_1,\,0) &= (q_1,\,0,\,R) \quad &\text{(skip right block)}\\ \delta(q_1,\,B) &= (q_2,\,B,\,L) \quad &\text{(reach blank at end — HALT)}\\ \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2.3 Complete Trace for $m=2$, $n=3$ (input $0^2\,1\,0^3$) | Move | ID — Tape & Head | Transition Used | |------|------------------|-----------------| | 0 | $q_0\,\mathbf{0}\,0\,1\,0\,0\,0\,B$ | Initial | | 1 | $0\,q_0\,\mathbf{0}\,1\,0\,0\,0\,B$ | $\delta(q_0,0)=(q_0,0,R)$ | | 2 | $0\,0\,q_0\,\mathbf{1}\,0\,0\,0\,B$ | $\delta(q_0,0)=(q_0,0,R)$ | | 3 | $0\,0\,B\,q_1\,\mathbf{0}\,0\,0\,B$ | $\delta(q_0,1)=(q_1,B,R)$ | | 4 | $0\,0\,B\,0\,q_1\,\mathbf{0}\,0\,B$ | $\delta(q_1,0)=(q_1,0,R)$ | | 5 | $0\,0\,B\,0\,0\,q_1\,\mathbf{0}\,B$ | $\delta(q_1,0)=(q_1,0,R)$ | | 6 | $0\,0\,B\,0\,0\,0\,q_1\,\mathbf{B}$ | $\delta(q_1,0)=(q_1,0,R)$ | | 7 | $0\,0\,B\,0\,0\,q_2\,\mathbf{0}\,B$ | $\delta(q_1,B)=(q_2,B,L)$ — **HALT** | > [!NOTE] > **Verification of output**: The non-blank portion of the final tape reads $\;0\,0\,0\,0\,0\;$ which is $0^{2+3}=0^5$. ✓ ### 3.2.4 Closed-Form Complexity For input length $\ell = m + n + 1$ (with separator): $$ \begin{aligned} T_A(m,n) &= \underbrace{(m+1)}_{\text{scan to separator}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{erase}} + \underbrace{(n+1)}_{\text{scan to end}} + 1 \\ &= m + n + 4 \;=\; \Theta(m+n) \;=\; \Theta(\ell) \end{aligned} $$ This is **linear time** — the optimal complexity for addition since we *must* read every input symbol at least once. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Multiplication $f(m, n) = m \cdot n$ (Algorithmic Sketch) ### 3.3.1 Specification We design a TM $M$ with input $0^m\,1\,0^n$ and output $0^{m\cdot n}$. The strategy is **repeated copy**: for each $0$ in the left block, append a fresh copy of the right block. ### 3.3.2 Algorithm in Pseudocode (TM-level) ``` 1. Erase the separator 1 → tape becomes 0^m 0^n 2. For i = 1 to m: Mark the i-th left-0 with symbol X (scan left, find first unmarked 0) Scan right past all 0s to the end of tape Append n zeros to the right Scan left back to the X marker 3. Replace every X back to 0 4. HALT ``` ### 3.3.3 Formal 7-tuple (Headline Transitions) $$ \begin{aligned} \delta(q_0,\,1) &= (q_1,\,B,\,R) &&\text{(erase separator)} \\ \delta(q_1,\,0) &= (q_1,\,0,\,R) &&\text{(scan to end of tape)} \\ \delta(q_1,\,B) &= (q_2,\,B,\,L) &&\text{(prepare to mark)} \\ \delta(q_2,\,0) &= (q_3,\,X,\,R) &&\text{(mark left-0)} \\ \delta(q_3,\,0) &= (q_3,\,0,\,R) &&\text{(scan past right block)} \\ \delta(q_3,\,B) &= (q_4,\,0,\,R) &&\text{(append one 0)} \\ \delta(q_4,\,0) &= (q_4,\,0,\,R) &&\text{(continue copying n times)} \\ &\;\;\vdots &&\text{(additional states for n-cycle)} \\ \delta(q_{k},\,X) &= (q_{k+1},\,0,\,R) &&\text{(restore X to 0 at the end)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3.4 Closed-Form Complexity The inner copy loop runs $n$ times per outer iteration, and the outer iteration runs $m$ times. Each copy requires a full left-to-right tape sweep. Hence: $$ \begin{aligned} T_M(m,n) &= m \cdot \big(\, \underbrace{m}_{\text{find X}} + \underbrace{(m + n \cdot i)}_{\text{copy + return}} \,\big) \\ &\in \mathcal{O}(m \cdot n \cdot (m+n)) \\ &\subseteq \mathcal{O}\!\left(\ell^3\right) \end{aligned} $$ where $\ell = m + n + 1$ is the input length. **Multiplication is cubic** on a basic single-tape TM. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip**: When asked to *design* a multiplication TM, you are not required to list every transition. Give the **algorithm**, the **state names**, and **2–3 key transitions**. Examiners reward clarity over exhaustive enumeration. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Function | Input | Output | Strategy | Time Complexity | Tape Cells Used | |----------|-------|--------|----------|-----------------|-----------------| | Successor $n+1$ | $0^n$ | $0^{n+1}$ | Append one $0$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $n+1$ | | Addition $m+n$ | $0^m 1 0^n$ | $0^{m+n}$ | Erase separator | $\Theta(m+n)$ | $m+n$ | | Multiplication $m \cdot n$ | $0^m 1 0^n$ | $0^{m\cdot n}$ | Repeated copy | $\mathcal{O}(m n (m+n))$ | $m \cdot n$ | | Proper Subtraction $m \dot{-} n$ | $0^m 1 0^n$ | $0^{\max(m-n,0)}$ | Paired erasure | $\Theta(m+n)$ | $\leq m$ | # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — TM as Function Computer ```mermaid flowchart LR input["Input Tape
w in Sigma Star"] --> start["Start State q0"] start --> proc{"Transition
Function delta"} proc -->|step 1| proc proc -->|step k| proc proc -->|halt reached| halt["Final State qf in F"] halt --> output["Output Tape
f w on tape"] style input fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0 style start fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style proc fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457 style halt fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style output fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#283593 ``` > **Reading the diagram**: Data flows left-to-right. The **transition function** is the algorithmic engine — it loops until a halting condition (no $\delta$ defined, or arrival at $q_f$) is reached. --- ## 4.2 State Transition Diagram — TM for Addition $m + n$ ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> q0 q0 --> q0 : delta q0 0 = q0 0 R q0 --> q1 : delta q0 1 = q1 B R q1 --> q1 : delta q1 0 = q1 0 R q1 --> q2 : delta q1 B = q2 B L q2 --> [*] note right of q0 : Scan right through
first block 0 to the m note right of q1 : Erase separator,
scan right block 0 to the n note right of q2 : Halting state
Output 0 power m plus n ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visual interpretation**: Self-loops at $q_0$ and $q_1$ model the "skip zeros" behaviour; the single arrow $q_0 \to q_1$ triggers when the head meets the separator `1`. --- ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Multiplication TM (Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Initial Tape
0 power m 1 0 power n"] --> B["Module 1
Separator Eraser"] B --> C["Tape Now
0 power m 0 power n"] C --> D["Module 2
Outer Counter Loop"] D --> E["Mark Left Zero with X"] E --> F["Module 3
Copy Right Block n Times"] F --> G["Append n Zeros to End"] G --> H{"More Left Zeros
to Mark"} H -->|Yes| E H -->|No| I["Module 4
Replace All X by 0"] I --> J["Final Tape
0 power m times n"] J --> K["Halt State qhalt"] style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0 style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style D fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457 style F fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style K fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c ``` > **Reading this block diagram**: Each rounded box is a *macro-state* of the TM.
