# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Recursive Language. Give one example and one non-example.**

**Model Answer:**

A language $L \subseteq \Sigma^{*}$ is **recursive** if there exists a Turing Machine $M$ such that $M$ halts on every input string $w \in \Sigma^{*}$, and $M$ accepts $w$ if and only if $w \in L$. Equivalently, $L$ is decided by a decider TM.

- **Example of recursive language:** $L = \{w \in \{a,b\}^{*} \mid w \text{ has an equal number of } a\text{s and }b\text{s}\}$. A TM can count, compare, and halt with a definite answer.
- **Non-example:** $A_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ accepts } w\}$ is **not** recursive. It is r.e. but its complement is not even r.e.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** Definition = 2 marks, example + non-example = 1 mark combined. Do not skip the "halts on **every** input" clause — that is the differentiator from r.e.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the difference between a Universal Turing Machine and an ordinary Turing Machine? Why is the UTM significant?**

**Model Answer:**

An **ordinary TM** has a fixed, hardwired transition function $\delta$ and solves one specific problem. A **Universal TM (UTM)** takes as input a *description* $\langle M \rangle$ of *any* TM $M$ along with a string $w$, and simulates $M$ running on $w$. The UTM is itself a single, fixed TM.

**Significance:**
1. It establishes that one fixed machine can compute everything that is computable.
2. It introduces the concept of a **stored program** — a precursor to modern computers.
3. It enables the proof that $A_{TM}$ is r.e. (the UTM itself is the recognizer).
4. It underlies the Church-Turing thesis and all of computability theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** Clear distinction = 1.5 marks, three valid significance points = 1.5 marks. Avoid vague statements like "it is general purpose" without elaboration.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 marks]** Prove that a language $L$ is recursive if and only if both $L$ and its complement $\overline{L}$ are recursively enumerable.

**(b) [7 marks]** Show that the Halting Problem $H_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w\}$ is recursively enumerable but not recursive.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Statement.** $L$ is recursive $\iff$ $L \in \text{RE}$ and $\overline{L} \in \text{RE}$.

**Proof ($\Rightarrow$):** Suppose $L$ is recursive. Then there exists a TM $M$ that halts on every input. Construct $E_L$:

$E_L$ = "Ignore input. For $i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$:
  - Generate the $i$-th string $x_i$ in lexicographic order.
  - Run $M$ on $x_i$.
  - If $M$ accepts $x_i$, output $x_i$."

Since $M$ always halts, $E_L$ eventually produces every $x \in L$ — so $L$ is enumerated. Similarly, modify $E_L$ to output $x_i$ when $M$ rejects $x_i$ to enumerate $\overline{L}$. Thus both are r.e. ✓

**Proof ($\Leftarrow$):** Suppose $L$ and $\overline{L}$ are both r.e., via TMs $M_1$ and $M_2$ respectively. Construct decider $D$:

$D$ = "On input $x$:
  1. Repeat for $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$:
      (a) Run $M_1$ on $x$ for $k$ steps.
      (b) Run $M_2$ on $x$ for $k$ steps.
      (c) If $M_1$ accepted, **accept and halt**.
      (d) If $M_2$ accepted, **reject and halt**."

**Correctness.** If $x \in L$, then $M_1$ accepts $x$ in some finite number of steps $T_1$. For $k \geq T_1$, step (a) sees the acceptance, and $D$ halts with "accept". Similarly for $x \in \overline{L}$, $M_2$ accepts and $D$ halts with "reject". Since $L \cup \overline{L} = \Sigma^{*}$, every $x$ triggers one of the two halts. Therefore $D$ decides $L$, so $L$ is recursive. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — $H_{TM}$ is RE.** Construct TM $U_H$:

$U_H$ = "On input $\langle M, w \rangle$:
  1. Simulate $M$ on $w$ step by step using a UTM.
  2. If $M$ ever enters $q_{\text{accept}}$ or $q_{\text{reject}}$ (i.e., halts), **accept** $\langle M, w \rangle$.
  3. If $M$ never halts, $U_H$ never halts (loops)."

If $M$ halts on $w$, $U_H$ accepts. If $M$ does not halt on $w$, $U_H$ does not accept. This matches the definition of r.e. Therefore $H_{TM} \in \text{RE}$. ✓

**Step 2 — $H_{TM} \notin \text{REC}$.** Use reduction from $A_{TM}$. Define reduction $f$:

$$f(\langle M, w \rangle) = \langle M', w \rangle \quad \text{where } M' \text{ behaves as:}$$

$M'$ = "On input $x$:
  1. Simulate $M$ on $w$.
  2. If $M$ accepts $w$, **accept** $x$ (halt).
  3. If $M$ rejects $w$, **loop** forever on $x$."

**Properties of $f$:**
- $f$ is computable (we can construct $M'$ mechanically from $\langle M, w \rangle$).
- $\langle M, w \rangle \in A_{TM} \implies M' \text{ halts on every } x \implies \langle M', x \rangle \in H_{TM}$.
- $\langle M, w \rangle \notin A_{TM} \implies M' \text{ loops on every } x \implies \langle M', x \rangle \notin H_{TM}$.

So $A_{TM} \leq_m H_{TM}$ (many-one reducible). If $H_{TM}$ were recursive, $A_{TM}$ would be recursive (decide $A_{TM}$ by reducing and then deciding $H_{TM}$). But $A_{TM} \notin \text{REC}$ (proved in Section 3.2). Hence $H_{TM} \notin \text{REC}$. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (lose up to 3 marks):** Students often forget to verify that the reduction $f$ is **computable** — the constructor TM must be explicitly described, not just handwaved. Also, the "$\leq_m$" symbol should be explained as "many-one reducible" for full marks.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the Chomsky Hierarchy with a neat diagram. State the grammar type, language class, and recognizing automaton for each level.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the Universal Turing Machine with a block diagram. Show that the language $L_U$ accepted by the UTM is recursively enumerable but not recursive.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

The **Chomsky Hierarchy** classifies formal grammars into four nested types:

| Type | Grammar Form | Language Class | Recognizing Automaton |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **Type 0** | Unrestricted: $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, no length restriction | Recursively Enumerable (RE) | Turing Machine |
| **Type 1** | Context-Sensitive: $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ with $\vert\alpha\vert \leq \vert\beta\vert$ (non-contracting) | Context-Sensitive (CSL) | Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA) |
| **Type 2** | Context-Free: $A \rightarrow \beta$, $A$ a single non-terminal | Context-Free (CFL) | Pushdown Automaton (PDA) |
| **Type 3** | Regular: $A \rightarrow aB \mid a$ (right-linear) or $A \rightarrow Ba \mid a$ (left-linear) | Regular (REG) | Finite Automaton (DFA / NFA) |

**Strict containment chain:**

$$\text{REG} \subsetneq \text{CFL} \subsetneq \text{CSL} \subsetneq \text{REC} \subsetneq \text{RE}$$

**Witness languages** (proving strictness):
- $\{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$ is CFL but not REG.
- $\{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$ is CSL but not CFL.
- $A_{TM}$ is RE but not REC (proved via diagonalization).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (7 marks):** Correct grammar form for each type = 1 mark each (4 total), automaton = 0.5 marks each (2 total), strict-containment witnesses = 1 mark.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Universal Turing Machine — Architecture:**

The UTM is a TM $U$ that takes $\langle M, w \rangle$ as input — the encoding of an arbitrary TM $M$ together with an input string $w$ — and simulates $M$ on $w$. The block diagram (refer to Section 4.2) has three conceptual tapes:
- **Tape 1:** Simulated tape of $M$.
- **Tape 2:** Current state of $M$.
- **Tape 3:** Encoded transition table of $M$.

**Showing $L_U$ is RE but not recursive:**

The language $L_U$ accepted by the UTM is:

$$L_U = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM and } M \text{ accepts } w\} = A_{TM}$$

**RE proof:** The UTM itself is a TM that accepts $\langle M, w \rangle$ if and only if $M$ accepts $w$. By definition, $L_U$ is r.e. ✓

**Not recursive proof:** Assume for contradiction that $L_U = A_{TM}$ is recursive. Then $A_{TM}$ has a decider $D$. Construct a new TM $T$:

$T$ = "On input $\langle M \rangle$:
  1. Run $D$ on $\langle M, \langle M \rangle \rangle$.
  2. If $D$ accepts, **reject**.
  3. If $D$ rejects, **accept**."

Now consider $T$ on its own description $\langle T \rangle$:

- If $T(\langle T \rangle) = \text{accept}$, then by construction of $T$, $D$ rejected $\langle T, \langle T \rangle \rangle$, meaning $T$ does **not** accept $\langle T \rangle$. Contradiction.
- If $T(\langle T \rangle) = \text{reject}$, then $D$ accepted $\langle T, \langle T \rangle \rangle$, meaning $T$ **does** accept $\langle T \rangle$. Contradiction.

Both cases are contradictory. Hence $D$ cannot exist, $A_{TM}$ is not recursive, and $L_U$ is r.e. but not recursive. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (lose up to 4 marks):** Students frequently forget to specify that the UTM accepts on the **accept state** and ignores the **reject state** during the simulation — only the accept triggers a halt. Also, the encoding $\langle M, w \rangle$ is **two** strings concatenated, not just $M$. This is a 1-mark deduction in many valuation scripts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 Final Notes**

### Recursive vs Recursively Enumerable
- **Recursive** = TM that **always halts** with accept or reject. Decidable.
- **Recursively Enumerable** = TM that halts on accept, may loop on reject. Semi-decidable.
- **Inclusion:** $\text{REC} \subsetneq \text{RE}$. Witness: $A_{TM}$ (r.e. but not recursive).
- **Complement of r.e.:** Not necessarily r.e. (cf. $\overline{A_{TM}}$). $\text{REC}$ is closed under complement.
- **Equivalence:** $L \in \text{REC} \iff L \in \text{RE} \text{ and } \overline{L} \in \text{RE}$ (proved via dovetailing).

### Chomsky Hierarchy
- 4 levels: Type 0 (Unrestricted, RE) → Type 1 (Context-Sensitive, CSL) → Type 2 (Context-Free, CFL) → Type 3 (Regular, REG).
- Each inclusion is **strict**.
- Automaton correspondence: TM → LBA → PDA → FA.
- Type 1 rule: $\vert\alpha\vert \leq \vert\beta\vert$ (non-contracting).
- Type 3 rule: $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ (right-linear) — single non-terminal at the right end.

### Universal Turing Machine
- A **single TM** $U$ that simulates **any other TM** $M$ on **any input** $w$.
- Input format: $\langle M, w \rangle$ — encoding of $M$ concatenated with $w$.
- $L_U = A_{TM}$ is **RE but not recursive**.
- Significance: foundation of stored-program computers; central to Church-Turing thesis.
- Simulation cost: $O(T(n) \cdot \vert\langle M\rangle\vert)$ time, $O(\vert\langle M\rangle\vert)$ extra space.

### Diagonalization Template (for any "X is not recursive" proof)
1. Assume $X$ is recursive → construct decider $D$.
2. Build a self-referential machine $D'$ that inverts $D$ on its own description.
3. Show $D'(\langle D' \rangle)$ leads to a contradiction.
4. Conclude $X$ is not recursive.

### Must-Know Languages
- $A_{TM}, H_{TM}$: **r.e. but not recursive**.
- $\overline{A_{TM}}$: **not r.e.**
- $E_{TM}, EQ_{TM}$: **not r.e., not co-r.e.**
- $A_{DFA}, E_{DFA}, A_{CFG}, E_{CFG}$: **recursive**.

### Examiner's Mental Model
> [!NOTE]
> When KTU asks "Is $L$ recursive?", always ask: *Can a TM be built that halts on every input?* If yes → recursive. If you can show it halts only on YES-instances (r.e. behavior) but cannot prove the NO-instances halt → r.e. If the diagonal argument applies → not even r.e.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Church-Turing Thesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Church-Turing Thesis # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Church-Turing Thesis (CTT):** A foundational hypothesis in theoretical computer science, independently formulated by **Alonzo Church (1936)** and **Alan Turing (1936)**, which asserts that **every function that is "effectively computable" (intuitively calculable by a finite algorithmic procedure) is also computable by a Turing machine.** In the precise KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the Church-Turing Thesis is stated as: > *"Any computational process that can be carried out by a human mathematician using a paper-and-pencil procedure, following a well-defined algorithm, can be simulated by a Turing Machine."* Formally, this is expressed as the set-theoretic equivalence: $$\mathcal{C}_{\text{Church}} \;=\; \mathcal{C}_{\text{Turing}} \;=\; \mathcal{C}_{\text{algorithm}}$$ where $\mathcal{C}_{\text{Turing}}$ is the class of functions computable by some Turing Machine, $\mathcal{C}_{\text{Church}}$ is the class of $\lambda$-definable functions, and $\mathcal{C}_{\text{algorithm}}$ is the class of "effectively calculable" functions. > [!NOTE] > The Church-Turing Thesis is **NOT a mathematical theorem**. It is an empirical hypothesis about the nature of computation. It cannot be proven by formal deduction because it equates an informal notion ("effective computability") with a formal one (Turing machine computability). All supporting evidence is *corroborative* — no counterexample has ever been found. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a **universal recipe book** — a book containing *every possible dish* that any chef in the world could ever cook, given enough time and ingredients. The Church-Turing Thesis essentially claims: > **"There exists ONE master machine (a Turing Machine) that can simulate the behavior of EVERY other computational device ever conceived — past, present, or future."** A more relatable analogy: - **Turing Machine** = a very simple cashier who can only add, subtract, compare, and move tokens left/right on an infinite paper tape. - **Modern Computer** = a high-powered chef with ovens, blenders, and microwaves. - **Church-Turing Thesis** = the surprising claim that, no matter how sophisticated the chef's kitchen becomes, the *simple cashier* can still reproduce *every dish* the chef can make — it just might take the cashier a longer time. This is why CTT is the **cornerstone of computability theory** — it tells us that the Turing Machine is the correct *yardstick* for measuring what is computable. ## 1.3 Historical Context | Year | Contributor | Contribution | |------|-------------|--------------| | **1936** | Alonzo Church | Proposed $\lambda$-calculus as a model of computation (Princeton) | | **1936** | Alan Turing | Introduced the "a-machine" (Turing Machine) as a model of computation (Cambridge) | | **1936** | Turing (Appendix) | Proved $\lambda$-definable functions $\equiv$ Turing-computable functions | | **1936** | Kleene | Showed $\lambda$-definable functions $\equiv$ general recursive functions | | **1939** | Post | Introduced Post canonical systems, proved equivalence | > [!NOTE] > All three independent formalisms (Turing Machines, $\lambda$-calculus, General Recursive Functions) converged on the **same class of computable functions**, providing massive empirical support for CTT. No other model has yet been shown to compute more functions than a TM. ## 1.4 Definition of "Effective Computability" A function $f : \mathbb{N}^k \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ is **effectively computable** if there exists a *finite*, *definite* procedure that: 1. **Always halts** for valid inputs (decidable procedure). 2. Produces the **correct output** for every valid input. 3. Can be carried out by a **human** (or machine) following **mechanical rules** — no intuition, no creativity, no "insight" required. The function must be specified in a way that is **completely unambiguous** — every step is predetermined. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Logical Anatomy of the Thesis The Church-Turing Thesis can be decomposed into **four logical components**: 1. **The Informal Notion (LHS):** "Effectively computable function" — a function whose values can be computed by a human following a finite, mechanical procedure. 2. **The Formal Notion (RHS):** "Turing-computable function" — a function recognized by some Turing Machine. 3. **The Equivalence (Bridge):** Every informal algorithm has a corresponding Turing Machine, and every Turing Machine corresponds to a realizable algorithm. 4. **The Universality Claim:** No physically realizable computational device can compute a function that lies *outside* the class of Turing-computable functions. ## 2.2 The Three Equivalent Formalisms (The "Holy Trinity") The strongest evidence for the Church-Turing Thesis is that **three independently developed models of computation converge on the exact same function class**: > [!IMPORTANT] > $$\mathcal{F}_{\text{Turing}} \;=\; \mathcal{F}_{\lambda\text{-calc}} \;=\; \mathcal{F}_{\mu\text{-recursive}}$$ where: - $\mathcal{F}_{\text{Turing}}$ = functions computable by Turing Machines - $\mathcal{F}_{\lambda\text{-calc}}$ = $\lambda$-definable functions (Church, 1936) - $\mathcal{F}_{\mu\text{-recursive}}$ = general recursive functions (Gödel–Herbrand–Kleene, 1936) ### 2.2.1 Turing Machines A 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ that manipulates symbols on an infinite tape. Computes exactly the class of *recursive* and *recursively enumerable* languages. ### 2.2.2 Lambda Calculus ($\lambda$-calculus) A formal system based on **function abstraction** and **application**. The notation $\lambda x . M$ denotes the function $f(x) = M$. A function is *$\lambda$-definable* if it can be expressed using $\lambda$-terms. ### 2.2.3 General (μ-)Recursive Functions Built from a small set of **initial functions** (zero, successor, projection) using three **operators**: - **Composition** - **Primitive Recursion** - **Minimization (μ-operator / unbounded search)** ## 2.3 Variations of the Thesis | Variant | Statement | Status | |---------|-----------|--------| | **Church-Turing Thesis (CTT)** | Every effectively computable function is Turing-computable. | Hypothesis (unprovable) | | **Extended CTT (ECTT)** | Every *physically realizable* computational model can be *efficiently* simulated by a probabilistic TM with at most polynomial overhead. | Hypothesis (now doubted due to quantum computing) | | **Strong CTT (SCTT)** | Any two "reasonable" computational models that compute the same functions do so with polynomially related resources. | Hypothesis (refuted in strict form by quantum computing) | | **Physical CTT** | Any physical process can be simulated by a TM. | Open philosophical question | | **Turing's Thesis** | The class of TM-computable functions coincides with the class of "human-computable" functions. | Equivalent to CTT | > [!NOTE] > The **Extended Church-Turing Thesis** is the most practically relevant variant for algorithm design — it justifies the use of standard complexity classes (P, NP) as machine-independent. However, **Shor's algorithm (1994)** for quantum integer factorization challenges the strong form of ECTT, as it suggests quantum computers may offer super-polynomial speedup over classical TMs for certain problems. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept / Symbol | Definition / Formula | Use Case in KTU | |---|------------------|----------------------|------------------| | 1 | $\mathcal{F}_{\text{TM}}$ | Class of Turing-computable functions | Reference set for CTT | | 2 | $\lambda x.M$ | Lambda abstraction defining a function | Church's formalism | | 3 | $\mu y [g(x,y)=0]$ | Minimization (unbounded search) operator | Kleene's general recursion | | 4 | $\text{Comp}(f, g) = \lambda x . f(g(x))$ | Function composition operator | Building recursive functions | | 5 | $\text{Rec}(f, g) = h$ where $h(0,x)=f(x); h(n{+}1,x)=g(h(n,x),n,x)$ | Primitive recursion operator | Building complex recursive fns | | 6 | $f(n) = \mu m [p(n,m)=1]$ | Search for smallest $m$ satisfying predicate $p$ | Defines partial recursive fns | | 7 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{RE}}$ | Recursively enumerable languages | Computably enumerable sets | | 8 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{REC}}$ | Recursive (decidable) languages | Always-halting computations | | 9 | $\langle M, w \rangle$ | Encoding of TM $M$ with input $w$ | Used in universality proofs | | 10 | $H_{\text{alt}} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w\}$ | The halting problem language | Canonical undecidable problem | > ⚠️ **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** When writing absolute value or "such that" in your answer sheet, use $\mid$ or $\vert$ to avoid breaking markdown tables. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering The Church-Turing Thesis has profound engineering implications: 1. **Compiler Design & Programming Language Theory:** The $\lambda$-calculus (a foundation of CTT) directly inspired **Lisp**, **Haskell**, **ML**, and modern **functional programming**. Every programming language in use today is **Turing-complete** — meaning it can compute exactly the same class of functions as a TM. 2. **Decidability Testing:** When designing a real system (e.g., a verification tool for hardware or a static analyzer for code), CTT tells us: *if the problem is undecidable for a TM, it is undecidable for ANY computer*. This saves engineers from attempting impossible tasks (e.g., perfect malware detection). 3. **Computational Complexity:** CTT provides the philosophical foundation for why we use **Turing Machines as the reference model** for defining complexity classes like P, NP, PSPACE, etc. — they are considered the canonical "reasonable" model. 4. **Quantum Computing Debate:** The ECTT is currently being tested — **Shor's algorithm** suggests quantum computers may compute certain functions (like factoring) *asymptotically faster* than classical TMs, prompting research into new computational models. # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Equivalence of $\lambda$-Calculus and Turing Machines We prove the key equivalence $\mathcal{F}_{\lambda\text{-calc}} = \mathcal{F}_{\text{TM}}$ in two directions. ### Direction 1: $\lambda$-definable $\Rightarrow$ Turing-computable **Claim:** Every $\lambda$-definable function can be computed by some Turing Machine. **Step 1:** Recall the syntax of $\lambda$-calculus. A $\lambda$-term is built from variables $x, y, z, \ldots$ using: - **Abstraction:** If $M$ is a $\lambda$-term and $x$ is a variable, then $(\lambda x . M)$ is a $\lambda$-term. - **Application:** If $M$ and $N$ are $\lambda$-terms, then $(M N)$ is a $\lambda$-term. **Step 2:** Define $\beta$-reduction. The expression $(\lambda x . M) N$ reduces to $M[N/x]$ — i.e., substitute $N$ for every free occurrence of $x$ in $M$. $$ (\lambda x . \text{plus}\; x\; 1)\; 5 \;\longrightarrow_{\beta}\; \text{plus}\; 5\; 1 \;\longrightarrow_{\beta}\; 6 $$ **Step 3:** Construction of the Turing Machine. Design a deterministic TM $M_{\lambda}$ with a multi-tape configuration: - Tape 1: stores the input $\lambda$-term in a fully parenthesized prefix notation. - Tape 2: stores the working memory (current redex being reduced). - Tape 3: output buffer. **Step 4:** $M_{\lambda}$ simulates $\beta$-reduction by repeatedly scanning the term, locating the leftmost redex $(\lambda x . M) N$, performing substitution, and continuing until the term reaches **normal form** (no further $\beta$-reduction possible). **Step 5:** When normal form is reached, the result is decoded as a Church numeral or symbol and emitted on the output tape. $$\boxed{\therefore \text{ Every } \lambda\text{-definable function is computable by some TM } M_{\lambda}}$$ ### Direction 2: Turing-computable $\Rightarrow$ $\lambda$-definable **Claim:** Every TM-computable function is $\lambda$-definable. **Step 1:** Recall that Turing Machine configurations are finite sequences of the form: $$C_i = q_i \; a_{i,1} \; a_{i,2} \; \cdots \; a_{i,k}$$ where $q_i$ is the current state and $a_{i,j}$ are tape symbols. **Step 2:** Represent configurations using **Church numerals and lists in $\lambda$-calculus**. Church numerals encode natural numbers: $$\underline{n} = \lambda f . \lambda x . f^n(x) = \lambda f . \lambda x . \underbrace{f(f(\cdots(f}_{n \text{ times}}(x))\cdots))$$ **Step 3:** Define the **list constructor** in $\lambda$-calculus: $$\text{cons} = \lambda h . \lambda t . \lambda z . z\; h\; t$$ $$\text{nil} = \lambda z . z \quad (\text{or use the false value } \lambda x . \lambda y . y)$$ **Step 4:** Define a $\lambda$-term $T$ that simulates the transition function $\delta$ of the Turing Machine: $$ T \;=\; \lambda c . \text{(apply } \delta \text{ to the head of configuration } c \text{ and produce the next configuration)} $$ **Step 5:** Define the iteration operator that repeats $T$ until a halting configuration is reached. Since the TM may not halt, this requires the **fixed-point combinator** $Y$: $$ Y \;=\; \lambda f . (\lambda x . f\;(x\;x))\;(\lambda x . f\;(x\;x)) $$ This satisfies $Y\; g = g\;(Y\; g)$, enabling unbounded recursion in pure $\lambda$-calculus. **Step 6:** The complete simulation is then: $$ \text{Sim}_{\text{TM}} = Y(\lambda F . \lambda c . \text{if } \text{HALT}(c) \text{ then } c \text{ else } F\;(T\; c)) $$ $$\boxed{\therefore \text{ Every TM-computable function is } \lambda\text{-definable.}}$$ Combining both directions: $$\boxed{\mathcal{F}_{\lambda\text{-calc}} \;=\; \mathcal{F}_{\text{TM}}}$$ ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Equivalence to General Recursive Functions ### Definition of General (μ-)Recursive Functions A function $f : \mathbb{N}^k \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ is **general recursive** if it can be built from the following three **initial functions** using the three **operators** below. **Initial Functions:** $$ \text{Zero}(x) \;=\; 0 $$ $$ \text{Succ}(x) \;=\; x + 1 $$ $$ \text{Proj}_i^n(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \;=\; x_i \quad (\text{for } 1 \le i \le n) $$ **Operators:** **(a) Composition:** If $g : \mathbb{N}^m \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ and $h_1, \ldots, h_m : \mathbb{N}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ are recursive, then so is: $$ f(x_1, \ldots, x_n) \;=\; g(h_1(x_1, \ldots, x_n), \ldots, h_m(x_1, \ldots, x_n)) $$ **(b) Primitive Recursion:** If $g : \mathbb{N}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ and $h : \mathbb{N}^{n+2} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ are recursive, then so is $f : \mathbb{N}^{n+1} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ defined by: $$ \begin{aligned} f(0, x_1, \ldots, x_n) \;&=\; g(x_1, \ldots, x_n) \\ f(y{+}1, x_1, \ldots, x_n) \;&=\; h(y, f(y, x_1, \ldots, x_n), x_1, \ldots, x_n) \end{aligned} $$ **(c) Minimization (μ-operator):** If $g : \mathbb{N}^{n+1} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ is recursive, then the partial function $\mu y [g(y, \vec{x}) = 0]$ is also recursive: $$ \mu y [g(y, \vec{x}) = 0] \;=\; \text{the smallest } y \text{ such that } g(y, \vec{x}) = 0 $$ (If no such $y$ exists, the function is undefined — hence "partial".) ### Proof Sketch: Every General Recursive Function is Turing-Computable **Step 1:** Each initial function is trivially computable by a small TM. - $\text{Zero}$: write $0$ and halt. - $\text{Succ}$: increment the symbol and halt. - $\text{Proj}_i^n$: ignore all inputs except the $i$-th and halt. **Step 2:** Composition is preserved — given TMs for $g$ and $h_1, \ldots, h_m$, compose them sequentially on multiple tapes. **Step 3:** Primitive recursion is preserved — given TMs for $g$ and $h$, construct a TM that iterates: copy state, call $h$, decrement counter, repeat until counter is zero. **Step 4:** Minimization is preserved — given a TM for $g$, construct a TM that enumerates $y = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ and tests $g(y, \vec{x}) = 0$. The first $y$ that succeeds is the output. (If no such $y$ exists, the TM runs forever — this is the source of *partial* recursive functions.) **Step 5:** Since every general recursive function is built from initial functions using composition, primitive recursion, and minimization — and each of these operations preserves Turing-computability — every general recursive function is Turing-computable. ### Proof Sketch: Every Turing-Computable Function is General Recursive **Step 1:** Encode TM configurations and tape contents as natural numbers (Gödel numbering). **Step 2:** Show that the *next-configuration* function $\text{Next}(C, C') = 1$ iff $C'$ is reachable from $C$ in one step is **primitive recursive**. **Step 3:** Show that the *reachable-in-$n$-steps* function $\text{Reach}(n, C, C') = 1$ iff $C'$ reachable from $C$ in exactly $n$ steps is **primitive recursive** (built by primitive recursion over $n$). **Step 4:** Define the result function as: $$ f(x) \;=\; \text{content of output tape in the unique halting configuration reachable from } q_0 x $$ **Step 5:** Express $f$ using $\mu$-operator: $$ f(x) \;=\; \mu y \left[\text{HALT}(\text{StateAt}(y, q_0 x)) = 1\right] $$ where $\text{StateAt}(y, C_0)$ extracts the $y$-th configuration reachable from $C_0$. **Step 6:** Since $\mu$-operator applied to a primitive recursive predicate yields a general recursive function, $f$ is general recursive. $$\boxed{\therefore \mathcal{F}_{\mu\text{-recursive}} \;=\; \mathcal{F}_{\text{TM}}}$$ ## 3.3 Combined Result Combining both equivalences: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathcal{F}_{\text{TM}} \;&=\; \mathcal{F}_{\lambda\text{-calc}} \\ \mathcal{F}_{\text{TM}} \;&=\; \mathcal{F}_{\mu\text{-recursive}} \\ \hline \therefore \quad \mathcal{F}_{\text{TM}} \;&=\; \mathcal{F}_{\lambda\text{-calc}} \;=\; \mathcal{F}_{\mu\text{-recursive}} \;=\; \mathcal{C}_{\text{effectively computable}} \end{aligned} $$ This triple coincidence is the **strongest possible empirical evidence** for the Church-Turing Thesis. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Properties of Equivalent Models | Property | Turing Machine | $\lambda$-Calculus | $\mu$-Recursive Functions | |----------|----------------|--------------------|----------------------------| | **Inventor / Year** | Turing, 1936 | Church, 1936 | Gödel–Herbrand–Kleene, 1936 | | **Basic Elements** | States, symbols, tape, head | Variables, abstraction, application | Initial functions, operators | | **Computational Mechanism** | State transitions | $\beta$-reduction | Function composition + search | | **Decidability of Equivalence** | Undecidable | Undecidable | Undecidable | | **Halting Decidable?** | No (Halting Problem) | No | No | | **Turing-Complete?** | Yes (by definition) | Yes (equivalent) | Yes (equivalent) | | **Strength** | Intuitive, visual | Elegant, mathematical | Logical, constructive | # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Equivalence Diagram: The Church-Turing Thesis This Mermaid diagram shows the equivalence of the three formal models and their relationship to the informal notion of "effectively computable". ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Informal["Informal Domain"] A1["Human Algorithm
(Paper Pencil Procedure)"] A2["Effectively Computable
Function Intuition"] end subgraph Formal["Formal Computational Models"] B1["Turing Machine
1936"] B2["Lambda Calculus
Church 1936"] B3["General Recursive
Functions Godel Kleene"] B4["Post Canonical
Systems 1936"] B5["Register Machines
Minsky 1961"] B6["Random Access
Machines"] end subgraph Implications["CTT Implications"] C1["Class of Recursive
Languages DEC"] C2["Class of Recursively
Enumerable Languages RE"] C3["Undecidable Problems
Halting Problem"] C4["Complexity Classes
P NP PSPACE"] end subgraph Extensions["Modern Extensions"] D1["Probabilistic TM"] D2["Quantum TM
Shor 1994"] D3["Cellular Automata
Conway Wolfram"] end A1 --> A2 A2 -- "CTT" --> B1 A2 -- "CTT" --> B2 A2 -- "CTT" --> B3 A2 -- "CTT" --> B4 A2 -- "CTT" --> B5 A2 -- "CTT" --> B6 B1 <-->|"equiv"| B2 B1 <-->|"equiv"| B3 B2 <-->|"equiv"| B3 B4 <-->|"equiv"| B1 B5 <-->|"equiv"| B1 B6 <-->|"equiv"| B1 B1 --> C1 B1 --> C2 B2 --> C3 B3 --> C4 D1 -.->|"extends"| B1 D2 -.->|"challenges ECTT"| B1 D3 -.->|"extends"| B1 classDef informal fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef formal fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef impl fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ext fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF class A1,A2 informal class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,B6 formal class C1,C2,C3,C4 impl class D1,D2,D3 ext ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Simulation Between Models This diagram depicts *how* a function defined in one model is *converted* into the other equivalent models. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input["User Defines Function F"] I1["Algorithm in
Pseudocode"] end subgraph TMside["Turing Machine Path"] T1["Step 1 Encode
as TM transitions"] T2["Step 2 Design

as lambda terms"] L2["Step 2 Apply

initial functions"] G2["Step 2 Apply
composition"] G3["Step 3 Apply
prim recursion mu"] end subgraph Equiv["Equivalence Verifier"] E1["All three paths
produce same
output on input x"] end subgraph Output["Final Result"] O1["f of x computed
consistently"] end I1 --> T1 --> T2 --> T3 I1 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 I1 --> G1 --> G2 --> G3 T3 --> E1 L3 --> E1 G3 --> E1 E1 --> O1 classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px classDef tm fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px classDef lc fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px classDef gr fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px classDef eq fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:3px classDef out fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px class I1 input class T1,T2,T3 tm class L1,L2,L3 lc class G1,G2,G3 gr class E1 eq class O1 out ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchy of Computational Models (Strength Comparison) ```mermaid flowchart TB R1["Finite Automata
Regular Languages"] R2["Pushdown Automata

Context Sensitive Languages"] R4["Turing Machines

Beyond RE Languages"] R1 -->|"more power"| R2 R2 -->|"more power"| R3 R3 -->|"more power"| R4 R4 -->|"more power"| R5 R4 -.->|"CTT says
MAX for any
real computer"| R4 classDef fa fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px classDef tm fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:3px classDef oracle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px class R1,R2,R3 fa class R4 tm class R5 oracle ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **CTT Interpretation:** The red box (Turing Machines) is the **upper bound** for any physically realizable computer. Oracle TMs (purple) exceed this bound by definition but cannot be physically constructed. # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (2 × 3 marks = 6 marks) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(3 marks)* **State the Church-Turing Thesis. Why is it considered a "thesis" and not a theorem?** **Model Answer:** The **Church-Turing Thesis**, proposed independently by Alonzo Church and Alan Turing in 1936, states that: > *"Every function that is effectively computable (i.e., can be calculated by a finite mechanical procedure) is also computable by some Turing Machine."* It is called a **"thesis"** and not a "theorem" because: 1. **It cannot be proven formally** — one side of the equation is an *informal, intuitive* concept ("effectively computable"), which has no rigorous mathematical definition. 2. It is an **empirical hypothesis** — supported by the convergence of multiple independent models (TMs, $\lambda$-calculus, recursive functions), but never formally proved. 3. **No counterexample** has ever been found in nearly 90 years of research, which gives strong *corroborative evidence*. ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(3 marks)* **List any three formal models of computation that are equivalent to Turing Machines.** **Model Answer:** Three formal models equivalent to Turing Machines under the Church-Turing Thesis are: 1. **Lambda Calculus** (Alonzo Church, 1936) — based on function abstraction and application via $\beta$-reduction. 2. **General (μ-)Recursive Functions** (Gödel–Herbrand–Kleene, 1936) — built from initial functions (zero, successor, projection) using composition, primitive recursion, and the $\mu$-operator. 3. **Post Canonical Systems** (Emil Post, 1936) — string-rewriting systems based on production rules of the form $x_1 \ldots x_n \rightarrow y_1 \ldots y_m$. All three compute exactly the same class of functions as the Turing Machine. --- ## 📝 PART B QUESTIONS — INTERNAL CHOICE (Answer ANY ONE — 14 marks) ### ❓ QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(14 marks)* **(a)** Define the Church-Turing Thesis. Explain the three equivalent formalisms — Turing Machines, $\lambda$-calculus, and general recursive functions — and discuss why their convergence provides strong evidence for the Thesis. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Construct a general recursive function definition for the multiplication function $\text{mult}(x, y) = x \times y$ using primitive recursion. Show all intermediate steps. *(7 marks)* --- ### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION A #### Part (a) — Solution *(7 marks)* **Definition of CTT (2 marks):** The Church-Turing Thesis (CTT) states: > *A function $f : \mathbb{N}^k \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ is "effectively computable" if and only if there exists a Turing Machine that computes $f$.* Equivalently: $$\mathcal{F}_{\text{effective}} \;=\; \mathcal{F}_{\text{TM}}$$ where $\mathcal{F}_{\text{TM}}$ is the class of Turing-computable functions. **The Three Equivalent Formalisms (4 marks):** | Formalism | Inventor / Year | Core Idea | |-----------|----------------|-----------| | **Turing Machine** | Alan Turing, 1936 | A finite-state controller + unbounded tape; computes by reading/writing/moving on tape. | | **$\lambda$-Calculus** | Alonzo Church, 1936 | Computation = repeated $\beta$-reduction of $\lambda$-terms. | | **General Recursive Functions** | Gödel–Herbrand–Kleene, 1936 | Computation = building functions from initial functions using composition, primitive recursion, and $\mu$-search. | **Proof Sketch of Equivalence (already detailed in Section 3):** - $\lambda \Rightarrow$ TM: simulate $\beta$-reduction on a multi-tape TM. - TM $\Rightarrow \lambda$: encode configurations as $\lambda$-terms; use fixed-point combinator $Y$ for iteration. - $\mu$-recursive $\Rightarrow$ TM: simulate each operator with a small TM; compose them. - TM $\Rightarrow \mu$-recursive: Gödel-encode configurations; express reachability as primitive recursion over step count; extract result using $\mu$-operator. **Why this convergence is strong evidence (1 mark):** Three mathematicians, working independently, using completely different conceptual frameworks (state machines, function algebra, equation-based recursion), arrived at the **exact same class of computable functions**. No extension or variant of any of the three models has ever been shown to compute a function outside this class. This unprecedented convergence across paradigms is the strongest possible empirical support for a hypothesis. #### Part (b) — Solution *(7 marks)* We want to define $\text{mult}(x, y) = x \cdot y$ using **primitive recursion** over the second argument $y$. **Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the recursive structure. We use the identities: - $x \cdot 0 = 0$ - $x \cdot (y+1) = x \cdot y + x$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Identify the base and step functions. - Base case $g(x) = x \cdot 0 = 0$: choose $g(x) = \text{Zero}(x)$. - Step case $h(y, z, x) = z + x$: this is the function that adds the previous result $z$ to the original $x$. **Step 3 (2 marks):** Define $g$ and $h$ using initial functions. $$ g(x) \;=\; \text{Zero}(x) \;=\; 0 $$ $$ h(y, z, x) \;=\; \text{Sum}(z, x) $$ where $\text{Sum}$ is itself defined by primitive recursion: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Sum}(z, 0) \;&=\; z \\ \text{Sum}(z, n{+}1) \;&=\; \text{Succ}(\text{Sum}(z, n)) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Write the formal primitive recursion definition. Using the primitive recursion operator $\mathcal{R}$: $$ \text{mult} \;=\; \mathcal{R}\big(g, h\big) $$ where: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{mult}(0, x) \;&=\; g(x) \;=\; \text{Zero}(x) \;=\; 0 \\ \text{mult}(y{+}1, x) \;&=\; h\big(y, \text{mult}(y, x), x\big) \;=\; \text{Sum}\big(\text{mult}(y, x), x\big) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Verification — check small cases. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{mult}(0, 5) \;&=\; 0 \quad \checkmark \\ \text{mult}(1, 5) \;&=\; \text{mult}(0, 5) + 5 = 0 + 5 = 5 \quad \checkmark \\ \text{mult}(2, 5) \;&=\; \text{mult}(1, 5) + 5 = 5 + 5 = 10 \quad \checkmark \\ \text{mult}(3, 5) \;&=\; 10 + 5 = 15 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\therefore \text{mult}(x, y) = x \cdot y \text{ is general recursive.}}$$ --- ### ❓ QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(14 marks)* **(a)** What are the different variations of the Church-Turing Thesis (CTT, Extended CTT, Strong CTT)? Discuss how quantum computing challenges the Extended CTT. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Define the $\mu$-operator (minimization operator). Using it, formally define the function $f(x) = $ "the smallest prime factor of $x$" and explain why this is a partial recursive function. *(7 marks)* --- ### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION B #### Part (a) — Solution *(7 marks)* **Variations of CTT (4 marks):** **1. Original Church-Turing Thesis (CTT):** > Every effectively computable function is Turing-computable. This is the *basic* form. It addresses only *what is computable*, not *how efficiently*. **2. Extended Church-Turing Thesis (ECTT):** > Any *physically realizable* computational device can be simulated by a *probabilistic Turing Machine* with at most a *polynomial overhead* in time and space. This is the form most relevant to algorithm design. It justifies using standard TMs as the reference model for defining complexity classes. **3. Strong Church-Turing Thesis (SCTT):** > Any two "reasonable" computational models that compute the *same function class* must do so with *polynomially related* resources. This is the strongest claim — it ties together the function class *and* the resource bounds. **Quantum Computing Challenge (3 marks):** In **1994, Peter Shor** developed a quantum algorithm that factors an $n$-bit integer in time $O(n^3)$, whereas the best known classical algorithm runs in time $O(e^{n^{1/3} \cdot (\log n)^{2/3}})$ (sub-exponential). For factoring, this gap is **super-polynomial**. **Implications for ECTT:** - If large-scale quantum computers are physically realizable, they can compute *the same functions* as classical TMs but *exponentially faster* in some cases. - This **does NOT refute CTT itself** — quantum TMs still compute exactly the same class of *functions* as classical TMs. - However, it **challenges the ECTT**, because the simulation of a quantum TM by a classical TM is believed to require *exponential* overhead (this is the famous conjecture that **BQP $\not\subseteq$ BPP**). **Conclusion:** The original CTT remains unscathed. The Extended/Strong forms are now considered *empirically questionable* and are active areas of research. #### Part (b) — Solution *(7 marks)* **Definition of $\mu$-Operator (2 marks):** Given a total computable function $g : \mathbb{N}^{n+1} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$, the **minimization operator** $\mu y$ is defined as: $$ \mu y [g(y, x_1, \ldots, x_n) = 0] \;=\; \text{the smallest } y \in \mathbb{N} \text{ such that } g(y, x_1, \ldots, x_n) = 0 $$ If no such $y$ exists, the function is **undefined** for that input — hence the resulting function is generally a **partial** recursive function. **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the predicate $\text{IsPrime}(p)$. $$ \text{IsPrime}(p) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } p \ge 2 \text{ and } p \text{ has no divisor in } [2, p{-}1] \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ This is **primitive recursive** (can be built from initial functions using bounded quantifiers). **Step 2 (1 mark):** Define the predicate $\text{Divides}(p, x)$. $$ \text{Divides}(p, x) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } p \text{ divides } x \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ This is also **primitive recursive**. **Step 3 (2 marks):** Define the smallest-prime-factor function. $$ f(x) \;=\; \mu y \big[\text{IsPrime}(y) = 1 \;\land\; \text{Divides}(y, x) = 1\big] $$ Read as: "the smallest $y$ such that $y$ is prime AND $y$ divides $x$." **Step 4 (1 mark):** Why this is a partial recursive function. - The inner predicate $P(y, x) = \text{IsPrime}(y) \land \text{Divides}(y, x)$ is **primitive recursive** (conjunction of primitive recursive predicates is primitive recursive). - Applying the $\mu$-operator to a primitive recursive predicate yields a **general recursive** function. - The function is **partial** because: - For $x = 0$: no prime divides $0$, so $f(0)$ is **undefined**. - For $x = 1$: no prime divides $1$, so $f(1)$ is **undefined**. - For $x \ge 2$: a smallest prime factor always exists, so $f(x)$ is defined. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Verification with small inputs. $$ \begin{aligned} f(2) \;&=\; \mu y [\text{IsPrime}(y) \land \text{Divides}(y, 2)] = 2 \\ f(6) \;&=\; \mu y [\text{IsPrime}(y) \land \text{Divides}(y, 6)] = 2 \\ f(15) \;&=\; \mu y [\text{IsPrime}(y) \land \text{Divides}(y, 15)] = 3 \\ f(49) \;&=\; \mu y [\text{IsPrime}(y) \land \text{Divides}(y, 49)] = 7 \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\therefore f(x) = \text{smallest prime factor of } x \text{ is a partial general recursive function.}}$$ --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in CTT Questions:** > > 1. **Calling CTT a "theorem"** — It's a *thesis/hypothesis*. Examiners specifically deduct marks if students claim it is "proven". Always use the words *hypothesis*, *empirical claim*, or *conjecture*. > > 2. **Confusing CTT with ECTT/SCTT** — Students often write "CTT is disproved by quantum computers". This is WRONG. CTT itself is *unscathed*; only the *Extended* form is challenged. > > 3. **Forgetting the initial functions** — When asked to construct a general recursive function, students often forget to start from the three initial functions (Zero, Succ, Projection). Always mention these as the *building blocks*. > > 4. **Mixing up $\mu$ and $\Sigma$ operators** — The bounded $\mu$-operator gives a *total* (primitive recursive) function, while the unbounded $\mu$-operator may give a *partial* function. Be explicit about which one you use. > > 5. **Skipping $\beta$-reduction steps** — When simulating $\lambda$-terms, always show the reduction sequence step-by-step. Each $\beta$-step earns a partial mark. > > 6. **Not distinguishing partial vs. total recursive** — Always state whether your constructed function is **partial** (uses unbounded $\mu$) or **total** (only composition + primitive recursion). Examiners reward this clarity. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist for Church-Turing Thesis:** > > - ✅ CTT = informal "effectively computable" $\equiv$ Turing Machine computable. > - ✅ Proposed by **Alonzo Church** and **Alan Turing** independently in **1936**. > - ✅ It is a **thesis**, NOT a theorem — cannot be formally proved. > - ✅ Three equivalent models: **Turing Machines, $\lambda$-Calculus, $\mu$-Recursive Functions**. > - ✅ **CTT** itself is about *what* is computable; **ECTT/SCTT** are about *how efficiently*. > - ✅ Every modern programming language is **Turing-complete** (computes the same function class as a TM). > - ✅ **Shor's algorithm** challenges **ECTT** but **not CTT** itself. > - ✅ General recursive functions are built from **Zero, Succ, Projection** using **Composition, Primitive Recursion, Minimization (μ)**. > - ✅ The **$\mu$-operator** applied to a primitive recursive predicate yields a **general (partial) recursive** function. > - ✅ **Church numerals** encode natural numbers: $\underline{n} = \lambda f.\lambda x. f^n(x)$. > - ✅ The **fixed-point combinator** $Y = \lambda f . (\lambda x . f (x x))(\lambda x . f (x x))$ enables unbounded recursion in pure $\lambda$-calculus. > - ✅ The convergence of three independent models on the same function class is the **strongest empirical evidence** for CTT. > - ✅ **Decidable = Recursive = Turing-computable function that always halts**. > - ✅ **Recognizable = Recursively Enumerable = TM that halts on YES inputs but may loop on NO inputs**. > - ✅ **Halting Problem** is the canonical undecidable problem — no TM can decide it for all inputs.

topics.title: Decidable and Undecidable Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT525 - FORMAL LANGUAGES AND AUTOMATA THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Decidable and Undecidable Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# DECIDABLE AND UNDECIDABLE PROBLEMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Decision Problems

In the theory of computation, a **Decision Problem** is a problem that asks a yes-or-no question about a given input. Every language $L$ can be viewed as encoding a decision problem: the problem asks "Is string $w$ in $L$?" A decision problem is **Decidable** if there exists a Turing machine that always halts and correctly answers the yes/no question for every possible input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** A problem is called **Decidable** (or **Solvable**) if there exists a Turing machine that halts on every input and accepts if the answer is "yes," and rejects if the answer is "no." A problem is called **Undecidable** if no such Turing machine exists.

$$\text{Decision Problem} = \begin{cases} \text{Decidable} & \exists \text{ TM } M : M(w) = \text{accept} \text{ if } w \in L,\; M(w) = \text{reject} \text{ if } w \notin L \\ \text{Undecidable} & \text{No such TM exists} \end{cases}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Courtroom Verdict**
> 
> Imagine a courtroom where a judge (the Turing machine) is asked to rule "Yes" or "No" on every case (the input). 
> - **Decidable** = The judge **always** gives a final verdict, no matter how complicated the case. (e.g., "Is this person over 18?" — Just check the birth certificate.)
> - **Undecidable** = There exist cases where the judge can think **forever** and never reach a verdict, or worse — no judge in existence can solve it. (e.g., "Will this program ever stop running?" — Sometimes you simply cannot tell!)

### 1.3 Classification of Languages

Languages are classified into the following hierarchy based on decidability:

| Class | Description | TM Behavior |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| **Recursive (Decidable)** | TM always halts | Accepts or Rejects |
| **Recursively Enumerable (RE)** | TM halts on "yes" inputs | Accepts, may loop on "no" |
| **Co-RE** | Complement is RE | Rejects, may loop on "yes" |
| **Non-RE** | Neither RE nor co-RE | No general procedure |

$$\text{Recursive} \subset \text{RE} \subset \text{All Languages}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decidable Problems About Regular Languages

Let $A_{DFA}$, $A_{NFA}$, $A_{REX}$, $E_{DFA}$, $EQ_{DFA}$ denote standard decision problems. The following are **decidable**:

### Theorem 1: $A_{DFA} = \{\langle B, w \rangle \mid B \text{ is a DFA that accepts input string } w\}$

**Why Decidable:** Simulate $B$ on input $w$. If the simulation ends in an accept state, accept; if it ends in a non-accept state, reject. Simulation halts in at most $|w|$ steps.

### Theorem 2: $A_{NFA} = \{\langle B, w \rangle \mid B \text{ is an NFA that accepts } w\}$

**Why Decidable:** Convert NFA to equivalent DFA using subset construction, then run $A_{DFA}$ decider.

### Theorem 3: $A_{REX} = \{\langle R, w \rangle \mid R \text{ is a regular expression generating } w\}$

**Why Decidable:** Convert $R$ to equivalent NFA, then apply $A_{NFA}$ decider.

### Theorem 4: $E_{DFA} = \{\langle B \rangle \mid B \text{ is a DFA and } L(B) = \emptyset\}$

**Why Decidable:** Mark the start state. Mark any state reachable via transitions from a marked state. If no accept state gets marked, $L(B) = \emptyset$, accept; else reject.

### Theorem 5: $EQ_{DFA} = \{\langle A, B \rangle \mid A, B \text{ are DFAs and } L(A) = L(B)\}$

**Why Decidable:** Construct DFA $C$ for symmetric difference $L(A) \triangle L(B)$, then use $E_{DFA}$ decider on $C$.

## 2.2 Decidable Problems About Context-Free Languages

### Theorem 6: $A_{CFG} = \{\langle G, w \rangle \mid G \text{ is a CFG generating } w\}$

**Why Decidable:** Convert $G$ to Chomsky Normal Form (CNF). The number of derivation steps is bounded by $2|w| - 1$. Try all derivations up to this bound.

### Theorem 7: $E_{CFG} = \{\langle G \rangle \mid G \text{ is a CFG and } L(G) = \emptyset\}$

**Why Decidable:** Iteratively mark all terminal-generating variables. If the start variable is marked, $L(G) \neq \emptyset$, reject; else accept.

## 2.3 Undecidable Problems

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The following problems are **UNDECIDABLE** and frequently appear in examinations:
> - $A_{TM}$ — Acceptance problem (Halting Problem)
> - $HALT_{TM}$ — Halting problem
> - $E_{TM}$ — Emptiness for Turing Machines
> - $EQ_{TM}$ — Equivalence for Turing Machines
> - $REGULAR_{TM}$ — Is $L(M)$ regular?
> - $PCP$ — Post Correspondence Problem

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Problem | Symbol | Status | Decider Outline |
|---------|--------|--------|----------------|
| DFA acceptance | $A_{DFA}$ | **Decidable** | Simulate DFA on $w$ |
| NFA acceptance | $A_{NFA}$ | **Decidable** | Convert to DFA, simulate |
| Regex generation | $A_{REX}$ | **Decidable** | Convert to NFA |
| DFA emptiness | $E_{DFA}$ | **Decidable** | Reachability of accept states |
| DFA equivalence | $EQ_{DFA}$ | **Decidable** | Symmetric difference |
| CFG generation | $A_{CFG}$ | **Decidable** | Bounded derivation in CNF |
| CFG emptiness | $E_{CFG}$ | **Decidable** | Mark terminals up |
| TM acceptance | $A_{TM}$ | **Undecidable** | Diagonalization proof |
| TM halting | $HALT_{TM}$ | **Undecidable** | Reduction from $A_{TM}$ |
| TM emptiness | $E_{TM}$ | **Undecidable** | Reduction from $A_{TM}$ |
| TM equivalence | $EQ_{TM}$ | **Undecidable** | Reduction from $E_{TM}$ |
| Post Correspondence | $PCP$ | **Undecidable** | Reduction from $A_{TM}$ |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Relevance:** Undecidability results are foundational in:
> - **Software Verification:** It is provably impossible to write a program that detects all infinite loops in arbitrary code (Halting Problem).
> - **Compiler Design:** Decidability of $A_{CFG}$ enables the existence of syntax checkers for programming languages.
> - **Cybersecurity:** Virus detection faces fundamental limits due to $A_{TM}$ undecidability.
> - **Formal Methods:** Hardware verification tools (model checkers) operate within decidable sub-logics precisely *because* general verification is undecidable.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Proofs

## 3.1 Proof that $A_{DFA}$ is Decidable

We construct a Turing machine $M$ that decides $A_{DFA}$.

**Construction of Decider $M$:**

$$M = \text{``On input } \langle B, w \rangle \text{ where } B \text{ is a DFA and } w \text{ is a string:}$$

**Step 1:** Simulate the DFA $B$ on input $w$. Let the current state be $q_0$ (start state). For each symbol of $w$, transition according to $B$'s transition function $\delta$.

**Step 2:** After all symbols of $w$ are consumed, check the final state.

**Step 3:** If the final state is an accept state $q \in F$, **accept**; otherwise, **reject**.

**Proof of Correctness:**

Let $n = |w|$. The simulation requires exactly $n$ steps of the Turing machine. Since $n$ is finite and each step is deterministic, $M$ **always halts** within $n+1$ steps.

- If $w \in L(B)$, then following the transition function $\delta$ from $q_0$ through $n$ transitions ends in a state $q_f \in F$. Hence $M$ accepts.
- If $w \notin L(B)$, then the final state $q_f \notin F$. Hence $M$ rejects.

$$\therefore M \text{ decides } A_{DFA} \implies A_{DFA} \text{ is Decidable} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Proof that $EQ_{DFA}$ is Decidable

**Goal:** Decide whether $L(A) = L(B)$ for two DFAs $A$ and $B$.

**Construction of Decider $M$:**

$$M = \text{``On input } \langle A, B \rangle \text{ where } A, B \text{ are DFAs:}$$

**Step 1:** Construct a new DFA $C$ such that:
$$L(C) = (L(A) \cap \overline{L(B)}) \cup (\overline{L(A)} \cap L(B))$$

This is the **symmetric difference** $L(A) \triangle L(B)$. $C$ is constructible because:
- DFAs are closed under complement.
- DFAs are closed under union.
- DFAs are closed under intersection.

**Step 2:** Run the decider for $E_{DFA}$ on input $\langle C \rangle$.

**Step 3:** If the decider accepts (i.e., $L(C) = \emptyset$), then $L(A) = L(B)$, so $M$ accepts. Otherwise, $M$ rejects.

**Mathematical Justification:**

$$L(A) = L(B) \iff L(A) \triangle L(B) = \emptyset$$

The key equation governing this reduction is:

$$\begin{aligned} L(A) \triangle L(B) &= (L(A) \cup L(B)) \setminus (L(A) \cap L(B)) \\ &= (L(A) \cap \overline{L(B)}) \cup (\overline{L(A)} \cap L(B)) \end{aligned}$$

If this symmetric difference is empty, then $L(A) = L(B)$. Since all operations preserve regular languages and closure under complement, intersection, and union hold for DFAs, $C$ exists. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Proof that $A_{TM}$ is Undecidable (Diagonalization)

This is the most celebrated proof in the theory of computation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** $A_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM and } M \text{ accepts } w\}$ is **Undecidable**.

**Proof by Contradiction:**

**Assumption:** Suppose $A_{TM}$ is decidable. Then there exists a decider $H$ such that:
$$H(\langle M, w \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } M \text{ accepts } w \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } M \text{ does not accept } w \end{cases}$$

**Step 1: Construct a new TM $D$.**

$D$ takes input $\langle M \rangle$ (the description of any TM) and behaves as follows:

$$D = \text{``On input } \langle M \rangle \text{ where } M \text{ is a TM:}$$

**Step 2:** Run $H$ on input $\langle M, \langle M \rangle \rangle$.

**Step 3:** Output the opposite of $H$'s output:
- If $H$ accepts, then $D$ **rejects**.
- If $H$ rejects, then $D$ **accepts**.

**Step 4: Examine $D(\langle D \rangle)$ — the diagonal case.**

Since $D$ is a TM, $\langle D \rangle$ is a valid description. We can feed $\langle D \rangle$ to itself:

$$\begin{aligned} D(\langle D \rangle) \text{ accepts} &\iff H(\langle D, \langle D \rangle \rangle) \text{ rejects} \quad \text{(by construction of } D\text{)} \\ &\iff D(\langle D \rangle) \text{ does not accept} \quad \text{(by definition of } H\text{)} \end{aligned}$$

This produces a contradiction:

$$D(\langle D \rangle) \text{ accepts} \iff D(\langle D \rangle) \text{ does not accept} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Contradiction!}$$

**Conclusion:** Our assumption that $H$ exists is false. Therefore, $A_{TM}$ is **undecidable**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Reduction: $HALT_{TM}$ is Undecidable

**Theorem:** $HALT_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM and } M \text{ halts on } w\}$ is undecidable.

**Proof by Reduction from $A_{TM}$:**

**Assumption:** Suppose decider $R$ for $HALT_{TM}$ exists.

**Step 1:** Construct TM $S$ that decides $A_{TM}$ using $R$:

$$S = \text{``On input } \langle M, w \rangle:$$

**Step 2:** Run $R$ on $\langle M, w \rangle$.

**Step 3:** If $R$ rejects, then $M$ does not halt on $w$, so $S$ **rejects**.

**Step 4:** If $R$ accepts, simulate $M$ on $w$ until it halts.
- If $M$ accepts, $S$ **accepts**.
- If $M$ rejects, $S$ **rejects**.

**Justification:**

$$\begin{aligned} \langle M, w \rangle \in A_{TM} &\iff M \text{ accepts } w \\ &\iff M \text{ halts on } w \text{ AND accepts} \\ &\iff R(\langle M, w \rangle) = \text{accept AND } M(w) = \text{accept} \end{aligned}$$

Since $S$ decides $A_{TM}$, and $A_{TM}$ is undecidable, our assumption that $R$ exists is false. Hence $HALT_{TM}$ is undecidable. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 The Post Correspondence Problem (PCP)

**Definition:** Given two lists of strings $A = (a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n)$ and $B = (b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n)$ over some alphabet $\Sigma$, find a sequence of indices $i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_k$ (with $k \geq 1$) such that:

$$a_{i_1} a_{i_2} \cdots a_{i_k} = b_{i_1} b_{i_2} \cdots b_{i_k}$$

**Theorem:** PCP is undecidable.

The proof proceeds by reducing $A_{TM}$ to a Modified PCP (MPCP), then showing MPCP reduces to PCP. The key idea: encode TM computation histories as pairs of strings in the PCP instance.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Language Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[All Languages over Sigma] --> B[Recursively Enumerable RE]
    A --> C[Non RE Languages]
    B --> D[Decidable Recursive]
    D --> E[Context Sensitive]
    E --> F[Context Free]
    F --> G[Regular]
    B --> H[Co RE]
    C --> I[Outside RE and Co RE]
    
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Decidability Reduction Chain

```mermaid
graph LR
    ATM[A sub TM Undecidable] -->|reduction| HALT[HALT sub TM Undecidable]
    ATM -->|reduction| ETM[E sub TM Undecidable]
    ATM -->|reduction| EQTM[EQ sub TM Undecidable]
    ETM -->|reduction| EQTM
    ATM -->|reduction| REG[REGULAR sub TM Undecidable]
    ATM -->|reduction| PCP[Post Correspondence Problem]
    
    HALT -->|reduction| ATMB[ATM complement]
    
    style ATM fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style HALT fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style ETM fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style EQTM fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style REG fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style PCP fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style ATMB fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Diagonalization Table (Turing Machine Behavior Matrix)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Table of M sub i on input w sub j]
    P --> P1[Row 1 M1 on w1 accept]
    P --> P2[Row 2 M2 on w2 reject]
    P --> P3[Row 3 M3 on w3 accept]
    P --> PN[Row N MN on wN loop]
    P --> PD[Diagonal D constructed to oppose all]
    PD --> DC[D of D of D causes contradiction]
    
    style P fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PD fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DC fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Decider Architecture for $A_{DFA}$

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[Input: Angle B, w] --> PARSE[Parse DFA Description]
    PARSE --> SIM[Simulate B on w Symbol by Symbol]
    SIM --> CHK{Final State in F?}
    CHK -->|Yes| ACC[Accept]
    CHK -->|No| REJ[Reject]
    
    style IN fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SIM fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CHK fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ACC fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style REJ fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```
